**Step 1 — Start with the electromechanical power equality.**
The electrical power converted to mechanical form in the armature equals the mechanical power developed at the shaft (before rotational losses):
$$
P_m = E_b\,I_a = T_a\,\omega
$$
where $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ is the angular velocity in rad/s.

**Step 2 — Solve the equality for torque.**
$$
T_a = \frac{E_b\,I_a}{\omega}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $E_b$ from the EMF equation.**
$$
T_a = \frac{\left(\dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}\right)\,I_a}{\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator.**
The angular velocity in rad/s:
$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the $N$ and 60 terms.**
$$
T_a = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z\,I_a}{60\,A} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi N}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{60\,A} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} \\[4pt]
T_a &= \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi\,A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Reorder to KTU standard form.**
$$
T_a = \frac{P\,Z}{2\pi\,A}\,\Phi\,I_a \quad \text{[N·m]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The remarkable fact: **$T_a$ does NOT depend on N**. The developed torque is determined purely by flux and armature current. This is why DC motors are called "constant-torque variable-speed" machines.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed Equation $N = \dfrac{V - I_aR_a}{\Phi}\cdot\dfrac{60\,A}{P\,Z}$

**Step 1 — Write the steady-state armature KVL (Motor action).**
When the motor runs at constant speed, applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop:
$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$
(The supply voltage must overcome both the back EMF and the ohmic drop.)

**Step 2 — Solve for the back EMF.**
$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a
$$

**Step 3 — Equate to the EMF equation derived in §3.1.**
$$
\frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A} = V - I_a R_a
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the speed N.**
$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60\,A}{P\,Z}
$$

**Step 5 — Observe the two-term structure.**
- **Numerator term** $(V - I_a R_a)$: the effective "back-EMF voltage" available for rotation.
- **Denominator term** $\Phi$: the magnetic loading that resists the rotation through induction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **N ∝ 1 / Φ** (inverse proportionality). Halving the flux → doubling the speed. This is the **principle of field-weakening speed control** used in every modern EV traction drive above ~80 km/h.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Developed Equation $P_m = T_a\,\omega$

> [!NOTE]
> While the form $P_m = T_a\,\omega$ is elementary, the KTU board expects the **explicit use of Faraday's coupling**: $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$ must be shown together, not in isolation.

$$
P_m = E_b \cdot I_a
$$

Substituting both sides:
$$
T_a \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A} \cdot I_a
$$

Cancelling the common $N/60$ factor:
$$
T_a \cdot 2\pi = \frac{P\Phi Z}{A} \cdot I_a
$$

Re-solving for $T_a$ returns the §3.2 result, confirming internal consistency.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Equation Inter-Dependence Map

| If you are given … | Use this equation first … | Then derive … |
|:------------------:|:------------------------:|:-------------:|
| $N, \Phi, P, Z, A$ | $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$ | Back EMF |
| $\Phi, I_a, P, Z, A$ | $T_a = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}\Phi I_a$ | Developed torque |
| $V, I_a, R_a, \Phi, P, Z, A$ | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}\dfrac{60A}{PZ}$ | Steady-state speed |
| $T_a$ and $\omega$ | $P_m = T_a \omega$ | Mechanical power |
| $E_b$ and $I_a$ | $P_m = E_b I_a$ | Electromechanical power |
| $E_b, I_a, R_a$ | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Terminal voltage (motor) |
| $V, I_a, R_a$ | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back EMF (motor) |
| $V, I_a, R_a$ | $E_b = V + I_a R_a$ | Generated EMF (generator) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Electromechanical Energy-Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The diagram below maps the five-block functional architecture through which electrical input at the armature terminals becomes rotational mechanical output at the shaft. Every arrow corresponds to one of the §3 derivations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[DC Supply V] --> blk1[Block 1 Armature Loop V minus Eb equals IaRa]
    blk1 --> curA[Armature Current Ia]
    curA --> blk2[Block 2 Flux Current Interaction Phi times Ia]
    blk2 --> torq[Electromagnetic Torque Ta]
    torq --> blk3[Block 3 Mechanical Conversion Ta times omega]
    blk3 --> mechP[Mechanical Power Pm]
    mechP --> shaft[Rotating Shaft N rpm]
    rot[Armature Rotation N] --> blk4[Block 4 Faraday Induction Phi times N]
    blk4 --> eb[Back EMF Eb]
    eb --> blk1
    rot -.-> angV[Angular velocity omega equals 2pi N over 60]
    angV --> blk3
    curA -.-> power[Electrical Power P equals V times Ia]
    eb -.-> power
    mechP -.-> power
    classDef elec fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef cross fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class srcA,curA,blk1,eb,power elec
    class torq,blk3,mechP,shaft,angV,rot mech
    class blk2,blk4 cross
```

### 4.2 Equation-Interdependence Map (Mermaid)

This second diagram shows the *mathematical* dependencies — which equation feeds into which — for the three KTU high-yield equations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    flux[Phi Flux per pole] --> emfEq[EMF Equation Eb equals P Phi N Z over 60A]
    poles[P Number of poles] --> emfEq
    condZ[Z Total conductors] --> emfEq
    pathsA[A Parallel paths] --> emfEq
    speedN[N Speed in rpm] --> emfEq
    emfEq --> kvlEq[Armature KVL V equals Eb plus Ia Ra]
    voltV[Supply V] --> kvlEq
    resistR[Armature R] --> kvlEq
    armI[Ia Armature current] --> kvlEq
    kvlEq --> speedEq[Speed Equation N equals V minus IaRa over Phi times 60A over PZ]
    flux --> torqEq[Torque Equation Ta equals PZ over 2pi A times Phi times Ia]
    armI --> torqEq
    emfEq --> powerEq[Power Developed Pm equals Eb times Ia]
    armI --> powerEq
    torqEq --> powerEq
    angV[omega equals 2pi N over 60] --> powerEq
    classDef key fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class emfEq,kvlEq,speedEq,torqEq,powerEq key
```

### 4.3 DC Motor Armature Winding Topology — Lap vs Wave

A physical schematic is not feasible in Mermaid, so we render the **functional architecture** of the two winding choices, since this choice directly changes $A$ in the three equations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAP[Lap Winding A equals P]
        lapIn[Armature Terminal] --> lapP1[Brush Pair 1]
        lapP1 --> lapP2[Brush Pair 2]
        lapP2 --> lapP3[Brush Pair 3]
        lapP3 --> lapPN[Brush Pair P]
    end
    subgraph WAV[Wave Winding A equals 2]
        wavIn[Armature Terminal] --> wavP1[Brush Pair 1]
        wavP1 --> wavP2[Brush Pair 2]
    end
    noteL[Lap gives more current capacity lower voltage]
    noteW[Wave gives higher voltage lower current]
    LAP --- noteL
    WAV --- noteW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU practical takeaway:**
> - Choose **Lap** for **low-voltage, high-current** machines (e.g., traction motors, rolling mills).
> - Choose **Wave** for **high-voltage, low-current** machines (e.g., ordinary industrial shunt motors up to ~250 V).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define *Back EMF* in a DC motor. Why is it called "back" EMF, and what role does it play in the steady-state operation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Back EMF ($E_b$) is the EMF induced in the rotating armature conductors of a DC motor, given by $E_b = P\Phi NZ / (60A)$. (1 mark)
>
> It is called "back" EMF because, by **Lenz's Law**, the induced EMF always **opposes the cause of induction** — i.e., the applied supply voltage $V$. (1 mark)
>
> **Role:** In steady state, $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. The back EMF acts as a **self-regulating feedback element**: if the motor speed $N$ rises (mechanical load drops), $E_b$ rises, which reduces the net voltage $V - E_b$ and hence reduces $I_a$, reducing torque, which counteracts the speed rise. This negative feedback is what gives a DC motor its **natural stable operating point**. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
State the torque equation of a DC motor. What is the **significance** of the term $PZ/(2\pi A)$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The torque equation of a DC motor is:
> $$T_a = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A}\,\Phi\,I_a \quad \text{[N·m]} \quad (1.5\text{ marks})$$
>
> The term $K_T = PZ/(2\pi A)$ is the **torque constant** of the machine — a *constructional* constant that depends only on the geometry of the armature (number of poles, conductors, and the type of winding). It does **not** depend on operating conditions like flux or current. (1 mark)
>
> **Physical significance:** $K_T$ tells us "how much torque per Weber of flux per Ampere of armature current the machine can deliver." A higher $K_T$ means a more torque-dense motor. (0.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–1 mark):** Using $A = 2$ for a *Lap* winding problem. Always re-read the question: if it says "Lap wound", $A = P$. If it says "Wave wound", $A = 2$.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s when applying $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Examiners see this error in ~30% of answer sheets.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Mixing up the **developed torque** ($T_a$) and the **shaft torque** ($T_{\text{sh}}$). Developed torque is at the armature surface; shaft torque is reduced by rotational losses.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Not stating the *units* of the final numerical answer. Always write "N·m" for torque, "rpm" for speed, "V" for EMF. Examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

A **4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt motor** has **500 armature conductors**. The armature resistance is $0.2\ \Omega$ and the shunt field resistance is $200\ \Omega$. The motor takes a supply of **250 V**, and the flux per pole is **30 mWb**. The armature current drawn is **50 A**.

**Compute:**

**(a)** The **back EMF** and the **total armature copper loss**. (7 marks)

**(b)** The **developed torque** $T_a$ and the **no-load speed** of the motor (assume no armature reaction). (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question A**

**Given Data Extraction:**
- $P = 4$
- Lap wound → $A = P = 4$
- $Z = 500$
- $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 200\ \Omega$
- $V = 250\ \text{V}$
- $\Phi = 30\ \text{mWb} = 30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Wb}$
- $I_a = 50\ \text{A}$

---

**(a) Back EMF and Armature Copper Loss [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Write the EMF equation.**
$$
E_b = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the armature KVL to find the *operating* back EMF.**
Wait — to compute $E_b$ numerically we need $N$, which is asked in part (b). We instead use the KVL *first*:
$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$
$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= 250 - (50)(0.2) \\
E_b &= 250 - 10 \\
E_b &= 240\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Compute the armature copper loss.**
$$
P_{a,\text{cu}} = I_a^2 R_a
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a,\text{cu}} &= (50)^2 \times 0.2 \\
&= 2500 \times 0.2 \\
&= 500\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Compute the total armature copper loss (just the armature in this case).**
Since the question asks for "total armature copper loss", and the field copper loss is separate:
$$
P_{\text{cu,armature}} = 500\ \text{W} = 0.5\ \text{kW}
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — State the units and physical interpretation.**
The armature draws 50 A through 0.2 Ω, dissipating 500 W as heat — this is the loss that limits continuous-duty operation. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) Developed Torque and No-Load Speed [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the developed torque $T_a$.**
$$
T_a = \frac{P\,Z}{2\pi\,A}\,\Phi\,I_a
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= \frac{(4)(500)}{2\pi(4)} \cdot (30 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (50) \\
T_a &= \frac{2000}{8\pi} \cdot 30 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 50 \\
T_a &= \frac{250}{\pi} \cdot 1.5 \\
T_a &= \frac{375}{\pi} \\
T_a &\approx 119.37\ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — Cross-verify using $P_m = T_a \omega$.**
First, mechanical power developed:
$$
P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 240 \times 50 = 12{,}000\ \text{W}
$$
Then $\omega = P_m / T_a = 12{,}000 / 119.37 \approx 100.5\ \text{rad/s}$
Then $N = 60\omega / (2\pi) = 60 \times 100.5 / (2\pi) \approx 960\ \text{rpm}$.
**(0.5 mark, for the verification logic)**

**Step 3 — Find the *operating* speed N (load speed) from §3.3.**
$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z} = \frac{240}{30 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot \frac{60 \times 4}{4 \times 500}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 8000 \cdot \frac{240}{2000} \\
N &= 8000 \times 0.12 \\
N &= 960\ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Find the *no-load* speed $N_0$ (assume $I_a \approx 0$ at no-load).**
At no-load, $I_a R_a$ is negligible, so $E_b \approx V = 250$ V.
Since $\Phi$ and machine constants are unchanged:
$$
\frac{N_0}{N_{\text{load}}} = \frac{E_{b,0}}{E_{b,\text{load}}} = \frac{250}{240}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_0 &= 960 \times \frac{250}{240} \\
N_0 &= 1000\ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — State final answers clearly.**
- Developed torque $T_a \approx 119.37\ \text{N·m}$
- Operating speed $N = 960\ \text{rpm}$
- No-load speed $N_0 = 1000\ \text{rpm}$
- Speed regulation $= (N_0 - N)/N \times 100 = (1000 - 960)/960 \times 100 \approx 4.17\%$
**(0.5 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A **2-pole, wave-wound DC series motor** is connected to a **220 V** DC supply. The armature has **600 conductors** and the total armature + series-field resistance is **0.5 Ω**. At a certain load, the armature current is **40 A** and the flux per pole is **25 mWb**.

**Compute:**

**(a)** The **back EMF**, the **speed in rpm**, and the **shaft power developed** in the armature. (7 marks)

**(b)** The **electromagnetic torque** developed, and the **new speed** if the flux is weakened to **20 mWb** with the same armature current. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question B**

**Given Data Extraction:**
- $P = 2$, Wave → $A = 2$
- $Z = 600$
- $R_a + R_{se} = 0.5\ \Omega$ (treat as total series resistance $R$)
- $V = 220\ \text{V}$
- $I_a = 40\ \text{A}$, $\Phi_1 = 25\ \text{mWb} = 25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Wb}$

---

**(a) Back EMF, Speed, and Shaft Power [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute back EMF from armature KVL.**
$$
E_b = V - I_a R
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= 220 - (40)(0.5) \\
E_b &= 220 - 20 \\
E_b &= 200\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Compute the speed from the EMF equation.**
$$
E_b = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = \frac{E_b}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= \frac{200}{25 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot \frac{60 \times 2}{2 \times 600} \\
N_1 &= 8000 \cdot \frac{120}{1200} \\
N_1 &= 8000 \times 0.1 \\
N_1 &= 800\ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Compute the developed (armature) power.**
$$
P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 200 \times 40 = 8000\ \text{W} = 8\ \text{kW}
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — State the shaft power as approximately equal to developed power (rotational losses neglected unless specified).**
$$
P_{\text{shaft}} \approx 8000\ \text{W}
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Cross-verify using torque approach.**
$\omega = 2\pi(800)/60 \approx 83.78\ \text{rad/s}$, so $T = P/\omega = 8000/83.78 \approx 95.49\ \text{N·m}$ (this matches part b). **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Developed Torque and New Speed at Reduced Flux [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the developed torque at $\Phi_1 = 25\ \text{mWb}$.**
$$
T_{a1} = \frac{P\,Z}{2\pi A}\,\Phi_1\,I_a
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{a1} &= \frac{(2)(600)}{2\pi(2)} \cdot (25 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (40) \\
T_{a1} &= \frac{1200}{4\pi} \cdot 1.0 \\
T_{a1} &= \frac{300}{\pi} \\
T_{a1} &\approx 95.49\ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — Compute the new torque at $\Phi_2 = 20\ \text{mWb}$ (same $I_a = 40\ \text{A}$).**
Since $T_a \propto \Phi$ for constant $I_a$:
$$
\frac{T_{a2}}{T_{a1}} = \frac{\Phi_2}{\Phi_1} = \frac{20}{25} = 0.8
$$
$$
T_{a2} = 0.8 \times 95.49 = 76.39\ \text{N·m}
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Compute the new back EMF at $\Phi_2$.**
The current is unchanged at 40 A, so:
$$
E_{b2} = V - I_a R = 220 - 20 = 200\ \text{V}
$$
(same as before, since the resistance drop is unchanged)
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Compute the new speed $N_2$ at reduced flux.**
$$
N_2 = \frac{E_{b2}}{\Phi_2} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_2 &= \frac{200}{20 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot \frac{60 \times 2}{2 \times 600} \\
N_2 &= 10{,}000 \times 0.1 \\
N_2 &= 1000\ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Conclude with physical interpretation.**
Reducing flux from 25 to 20 mWb (a 20% reduction) **increased speed from 800 to 1000 rpm** (a 25% increase). This is the **field-weakening principle** — speed rises *more* than flux falls, because of the inverse proportionality. **(Bonus insight, no marks deducted if missing.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall before the exam.)**
> - **EMF equation:** $E_b = P\Phi NZ / (60A)$ — units of V when $\Phi$ in Wb, N in rpm.
> - **Torque equation:** $T_a = (PZ / 2\pi A) \cdot \Phi I_a$ — units of N·m; **independent of N**.
> - **Speed equation:** $N = (V - I_a R_a)/\Phi \cdot 60A/(PZ)$ — N is *inversely* proportional to $\Phi$.
> - **Armature KVL (motor):** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. Always use this to find $E_b$ when $V$ and load are given.
> - **Power developed:** $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$ — the single bridge between the electrical and mechanical sides.
> - **Winding rule:** Lap → $A = P$, Wave → $A = 2$. **Re-check the question** every time.
> - **Field weakening:** Halve $\Phi$ → double $N$ (for the same load current).
> - **Generator mode:** $E_b = V + I_a R_a$ — the I·R drop adds to the terminal voltage.
> - **No-load:** $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V$, and $N_0$ is the maximum safe speed.
> - **Unit conversions:** $1\ \text{mWb} = 10^{-3}\ \text{Wb}$, $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$, $1\ \text{hp} = 746\ \text{W}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the three equations — "EFTS"**
> **E**MF — *E*quation (relates $E_b$ to $N$ and $\Phi$).
> **F**lux-Torque — *F*lux times current gives torque.
> **S**peed — *S*peed depends on $(V - I_aR_a)/\Phi$.
>
> Remember: **Torque needs current AND flux, but Speed needs Voltage AND inverse flux.** This asymmetry is the soul of every DC-machine problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of DC Motor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Characteristics of DC Motor

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of DC Motor

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a DC Motor?

A **DC Motor** is a rotary electromechanical energy conversion device that converts **Direct Current (DC) electrical energy** into **mechanical energy** (rotational motion), based on the principle of interaction between current-carrying conductors and a magnetic field (Lorentz Force: $F = BIL$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "A DC machine is an electromechanical energy conversion device which operates on the principle of Faraday's Laws of Electromagnetic Induction, where the interaction between the field flux and the armature current produces a unidirectional torque, governed by the equation $T_a = k \phi I_a$."

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Water Wheel

Imagine a giant water wheel in a river. The water current (analogous to **armature current $I_a$**) pushes the paddles. The strength of the magnetic pull on each paddle (analogous to **field flux $\phi$**) determines how hard the wheel turns. The faster the wheel spins, the more water passes through, and the more power is generated for grinding. By controlling either the water flow or the paddle magnets, we control the wheel's behavior. A DC motor works similarly — controlling either $I_a$ or $\phi$ gives us different performance characteristics for different applications.

### 1.3 Classification of DC Motors

DC motors are classified based on the **field winding excitation** method:

| Type | Field Winding Configuration | Key Property |
|------|----------------------------|--------------|
| **DC Shunt Motor** | Field winding connected in **parallel** with armature | Constant field flux, nearly constant speed |
| **DC Series Motor** | Field winding connected in **series** with armature | High starting torque, variable speed |
| **DC Compound Motor** | Combination of both shunt and series field | Mix of shunt and series properties |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The **characteristics** of a DC motor are the graphical relationships between fundamental quantities such as **Speed ($N$)**, **Torque ($T$)**, **Armature Current ($I_a$)**, and **Field Flux ($\phi$)**. The KTU 2024 scheme places significant emphasis on plotting and interpreting these curves.

### 1.4 The Three Fundamental Characteristics

Every DC motor exhibits three essential characteristics curves:

1. **Torque vs. Armature Current ($T_a$ vs $I_a$)** — also called the **Electrical Characteristic**
2. **Speed vs. Armature Current ($N$ vs $I_a$)** — also called the **Mechanical / Speed Characteristic**
3. **Speed vs. Torque ($N$ vs $T_a$)** — also called the **Load Characteristic**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board exams, students are **expected** to:
> - Draw the characteristic curves with correct shapes and labeling.
> - Identify the type of motor from the nature of its curve.
> - Derive the slope equations of these curves using the EMF/Torque equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equations of a DC Motor

Every DC motor is governed by **two fundamental equations** that form the analytical foundation of all its characteristics:

### Equation 1: Back EMF Equation
The rotating armature conductors cut the magnetic flux, inducing a counter-voltage that opposes the applied voltage:

$$E_b = \frac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

where:

- $E_b$ = Back EMF (in Volts, **V**)
- $\phi$ = Average flux per pole (in Webers, **Wb**)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of the motor (in RPM)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths

For a **wave-wound** motor, $A = 2$. For a **lap-wound** motor, $A = P$.

A simpler engineering form is:

$$E_b = k \phi \omega = k' \phi N$$

where $k$ and $k'$ are machine-dependent constants, and $\omega$ is angular speed in rad/s.

### Equation 2: Torque Equation
The interaction of armature current with the field flux produces electromagnetic torque:

$$T_a = \frac{E_b I_a}{\omega} = k \phi I_a \quad \text{(in N·m)}$$

or, in terms of machine constants:

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \frac{\phi Z I_a P}{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two equations are the **bedrock** of every DC motor analysis. Every characteristic curve you plot is derived directly from these two fundamental relationships combined with the **voltage equation**: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.

## 2.2 The Three Core DC Motor Types — Analytical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 DC Shunt Motor

**Construction Logic:** The shunt field winding has a large number of turns of thin wire, connected directly across the supply voltage. Hence, field current $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ is **constant** (assuming constant supply voltage), which means flux $\phi$ is **approximately constant** (ignoring armature reaction).

**Voltage Equation:**
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Field Current (constant):**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \text{constant}$$

**Armature Current:**
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$$

**Step-by-Step Analysis:**
- Since $I_{sh}$ is constant, $\phi \approx \text{constant}$
- From Torque Equation: $T_a = k \phi I_a \Rightarrow T_a \propto I_a$ → **Linear (straight line through origin)**
- From Back EMF Equation: $E_b = k' \phi N \Rightarrow N \propto E_b$
- And $E_b = V - I_a R_a \Rightarrow E_b$ **decreases linearly** as $I_a$ increases
- Therefore: $N$ **decreases slightly** (linearly) as $I_a$ increases → **Drooping straight line**

### 2.2.2 DC Series Motor

**Construction Logic:** The series field winding has a few turns of thick wire, connected in series with the armature. Hence, the **field flux is proportional to armature current** (before saturation).

**Voltage Equation:**
$$V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

**Field Flux (before magnetic saturation):**
$$\phi \propto I_a$$

**Step-by-Step Analysis:**
- Before saturation: $\phi = k_1 I_a$
- Torque: $T_a = k \phi I_a = k (k_1 I_a) I_a = k k_1 I_a^2 \Rightarrow T_a \propto I_a^2$ → **Parabolic curve**
- After saturation: $\phi \approx \text{constant}$, then $T_a \propto I_a$ → **Linear at high currents**
- Speed: $N \propto E_b / \phi$, and $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$
- Since $\phi$ rises faster than $E_b$ falls, $N$ **decreases sharply** as $I_a$ increases (i.e., as load increases) → **Inverse / Hyperbolic curve**

### 2.2.3 DC Compound Motor

A compound motor has **both** shunt and series field windings on each pole. Two subtypes exist:

- **Cumulative Compound:** Series field flux **aids** shunt field flux → $\phi_{total} = \phi_{sh} + \phi_{se}$
- **Differential Compound:** Series field flux **opposes** shunt field flux → $\phi_{total} = \phi_{sh} - \phi_{se}$

The cumulative compound motor is the most commonly used. Its characteristics lie **between** those of shunt and series motors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Shunt Motor | Series Motor | Compound Motor (Cumulative) |
|---|-----------|-------------|--------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Field Current $I_{sh}$ | $V / R_{sh}$ (constant) | — | $V / R_{sh}$ (constant) |
| 2 | Flux $\phi$ | Constant (approx.) | $\propto I_a$ (linear region) | $\phi_{sh} + \phi_{se}$ (additive) |
| 3 | Back EMF $E_b$ | $V - I_a R_a$ | $V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | $V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ |
| 4 | Torque $T_a$ | $k \phi I_a \propto I_a$ | $\propto I_a^2$ (linear region) | $k(\phi_{sh}+\phi_{se})I_a$ (between) |
| 5 | Speed $N$ | $N \propto E_b / \phi$ (drops slightly) | $N \propto 1/I_a$ (drops sharply) | Between shunt and series |
| 6 | $N$ vs $T_a$ Slope | Almost horizontal line | Sharp hyperbolic drop | Moderate drop |
| 7 | Starting Torque | Low to medium | **Very high** (highest of all) | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Check (Must Remember):**
> - Speed $N$ → **rpm (revolutions per minute)**
> - Torque $T_a$ → **N·m (Newton-meter)**
> - Flux $\phi$ → **Wb (Weber)**
> - EMF $E_b$ → **V (Volt)**
> - Current $I_a$ → **A (Ampere)**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Motor Type | Primary Applications |
|------------|----------------------|
| **DC Shunt** | Constant speed loads: lathes, machine tools, fans, blowers, line-shaft drives |
| **DC Series** | High starting torque: electric traction (trains, trams), cranes, hoists, conveyors, electric vehicles |
| **Cumulative Compound** | Intermediate: elevators, rolling mills, printing presses, punches, shears |
| **Differential Compound** | Rarely used (unstable); used in small servo applications |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed Equation of a DC Motor

Starting from the voltage equation of a DC motor:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the back EMF equation $E_b = k' \phi N$:

$$V = k' \phi N + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2:** Isolate the speed $N$ on one side:

$$k' \phi N = V - I_a R_a$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $k' \phi$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \phi}$$

**Step 4 (Final Master Equation):**

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V}{k' \phi} - \frac{R_a}{k' \phi} I_a}$$

This equation is of the form $y = c - mx$ (a **straight line with negative slope**), which is the basis of the $N$ vs $I_a$ characteristic.

**Key Interpretation:**
- The y-intercept (when $I_a = 0$) is $N_0 = V / (k' \phi)$ = **No-load speed**
- The slope of the line is $-R_a / (k' \phi)$, which is **negative** (speed falls as current rises)
- For shunt motors, $\phi$ is constant → straight line; for series motors, $\phi$ varies → curve

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Speed Regulation of a DC Shunt Motor

**Definition:** Speed regulation is the change in speed from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load speed.

$$\text{Speed Regulation (\%)} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$$

**Step 1:** Write no-load speed (using $I_a \approx 0$):

$$N_{NL} = \frac{V}{k' \phi}$$

**Step 2:** Write full-load speed (using $I_a = I_{a,FL}$):

$$N_{FL} = \frac{V - I_{a,FL} R_a}{k' \phi}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the regulation formula:

$$\text{Speed Regulation (\%)} = \frac{\frac{V}{k' \phi} - \frac{V - I_{a,FL} R_a}{k' \phi}}{\frac{V - I_{a,FL} R_a}{k' \phi}} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Simplify (the $k' \phi$ denominators cancel):

$$\text{Speed Regulation (\%)} = \frac{I_{a,FL} R_a}{V - I_{a,FL} R_a} \times 100$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque Equation from Power Balance

The electrical power converted to mechanical form is called the **developed power** or **air-gap power**:

$$P_g = E_b I_a$$

This power is also equal to mechanical power developed:

$$P_m = T_a \omega$$

where $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ is angular speed in rad/s.

**Step 1:** Equate the two power expressions:

$$T_a \omega = E_b I_a$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $E_b = k \phi \omega$ (from the EMF equation rewritten using angular speed):

$$T_a \omega = (k \phi \omega) I_a$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $\omega$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{T_a = k \phi I_a}$$

This is the master torque equation. Multiplying both sides by $\omega$ gives total developed power in watts.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Speed vs Torque Characteristic of a DC Shunt Motor

**Goal:** Express $N$ as a function of $T_a$ (eliminate $I_a$).

**Step 1:** From the torque equation: $T_a = k \phi I_a \Rightarrow I_a = T_a / (k \phi)$ (since $\phi$ is constant in shunt motor).

**Step 2:** Substitute this $I_a$ into the speed equation $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \phi}$:

$$N = \frac{V - \left(\frac{T_a}{k \phi}\right) R_a}{k' \phi}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange into the $y = c - mx$ form:

$$N = \frac{V}{k' \phi} - \frac{R_a}{k k' \phi^2} T_a$$

**Step 4 (Final):**

$$\boxed{N = N_0 - K \cdot T_a \quad \text{where} \quad K = \frac{R_a}{k k' \phi^2}}$$

This is a **straight line with negative slope**, confirming that for a DC shunt motor, the speed falls very slightly (almost constantly) as torque load increases. This is the highly desirable **constant-speed** property.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Speed vs Torque of a DC Series Motor (Before Saturation)

**Goal:** Find $N$ as a function of $T_a$, assuming $\phi \propto I_a$.

**Step 1:** Let $\phi = k_1 I_a$ (linear region, pre-saturation).

**Step 2:** Substitute into the torque equation:

$$T_a = k \phi I_a = k (k_1 I_a) I_a = k k_1 I_a^2$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $I_a$:

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T_a}{k k_1}}$$

**Step 4:** Now use the speed equation. The voltage equation for a series motor is:

$$V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

$$E_b = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

**Step 5:** Speed is $N = E_b / (k' \phi) = E_b / (k' k_1 I_a)$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})}{k' k_1 I_a}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $I_a = \sqrt{T_a / (k k_1)}$:

$$N = \frac{V - (R_a + R_{se}) \sqrt{\frac{T_a}{k k_1}}}{k' k_1 \sqrt{\frac{T_a}{k k_1}}}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify to obtain the inverse-square-root relationship:

$$\boxed{N \approx \frac{V}{k' k_1 I_a} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_a}}}$$

This is the famous **inverse / hyperbolic** $N$ vs $T_a$ curve — the speed falls sharply as the load torque increases. This is why series motors are unsuitable for constant-speed applications but ideal for traction drives.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Slope Equations for All Three Characteristics

| Characteristic | Shunt Motor | Series Motor (Pre-Saturation) | Cumulative Compound |
|----------------|-------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| $T_a$ vs $I_a$ | $T_a = k \phi I_a$ (linear) | $T_a = k k_1 I_a^2$ (parabola) | Between (slightly curved) |
| $N$ vs $I_a$ | $N = \frac{V}{k'\phi} - \frac{R_a}{k'\phi} I_a$ (linear) | $N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k' k_1 I_a}$ (inverse) | Between (gentle curve) |
| $N$ vs $T_a$ | $N = N_0 - K T_a$ (linear) | $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T_a}$ (hyperbolic) | Between (mild droop) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the characteristic curves of a DC motor. Mention the three important characteristics. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The characteristic curves of a DC motor are the **graphical relationships** between fundamental motor variables: **Torque ($T_a$)**, **Speed ($N$)**, **Armature Current ($I_a$)**, and **Field Flux ($\phi$)**. These curves help in selecting the appropriate DC motor for a given application.

The **three important characteristics** of a DC motor are:

1. **Torque vs. Armature Current ($T_a$ vs $I_a$)** — also called the *Electrical Characteristic*.
2. **Speed vs. Armature Current ($N$ vs $I_a$)** — also called the *Speed / Mechanical Characteristic*.
3. **Speed vs. Torque ($N$ vs $T_a$)** — also called the *Load Characteristic* or *Performance Characteristic*.

### Question 2: Why is a DC series motor never started on no-load? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A DC series motor has its field winding connected in **series** with the armature, so the field flux $\phi$ is proportional to armature current $I_a$. On no-load, the armature current is very small, leading to a very weak field flux. According to the speed equation $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \phi}$, when $\phi \to 0$, the speed tends to **infinity**. This causes the motor to **run away at dangerously high speeds**, potentially damaging the armature due to centrifugal forces. Hence, a DC series motor must **always be started with at least 20% to 30% of its rated load** to prevent this destructive self-speeding condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the **Torque–Armature Current ($T_a$ vs $I_a$)** and **Speed–Armature Current ($N$ vs $I_a$)** characteristics of a **DC Shunt Motor**. Sketch the curves clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.3 Ω and a field winding resistance of 250 Ω. The motor draws an armature current of 50 A at a certain load and runs at 1000 rpm. Compute: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Developed Torque, and (iii) Speed at no-load (assuming armature reaction is negligible). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Torque Equation:** From the fundamental torque equation of a DC machine:

$$T_a = k \phi I_a$$

In a **shunt motor**, the field winding is connected in parallel to the supply, hence:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2 — Constant Flux:** Since the field current is constant, the field flux $\phi$ is also **approximately constant** (ignoring armature reaction). Therefore:

$$T_a = (\text{constant}) \cdot I_a \Rightarrow T_a \propto I_a$$

This is a **straight line passing through the origin** with slope $k \phi$. The curve is linear up to the saturation point.

**Step 3 — Speed Equation:** Starting from the voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a \Rightarrow E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

The back EMF is also:

$$E_b = k' \phi N \Rightarrow N = \frac{E_b}{k' \phi} = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \phi}$$

**Step 4 — Linear Speed Equation:** Since $\phi$ is constant in a shunt motor:

$$N = \frac{V}{k' \phi} - \frac{R_a}{k' \phi} I_a$$

This is of the form $y = c - mx$ — a **straight line with negative slope**. The y-intercept (no-load speed, when $I_a = 0$) is $N_0 = V / (k' \phi)$.

**Step 5 — Sketch Description:**
- The $T_a$ vs $I_a$ curve is a **straight line through the origin**.
- The $N$ vs $I_a$ curve is a **drooping straight line** starting from $N_0$ and decreasing linearly.
- The drop in speed from no-load to full-load is **very small** (typically 3–5%), confirming the constant-speed nature of the shunt motor.

**Marks Distribution:** Voltage equation (1.5) → Constant flux justification (1) → Torque proportionality (1) → Speed equation derivation (2) → Curve description and labels (1.5).

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Supply voltage $V = 250$ V
- Armature resistance $R_a = 0.3$ Ω
- Shunt field resistance $R_{sh} = 250$ Ω
- Armature current $I_a = 50$ A
- Speed at load $N = 1000$ rpm

**Step 1 — Compute Back EMF $E_b$:**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (50)(0.3) = 250 - 15 = 235 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Developed Torque $T_a$:**

Angular speed:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

Developed torque (from power equation $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$):

$$T_a = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega} = \frac{235 \times 50}{104.72} = \frac{11750}{104.72} \approx 112.22 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute No-Load Speed $N_0$:**

At no-load, the armature current is very small (just enough to overcome no-load losses). For a first approximation, $I_a \approx 0$:

$$N_0 = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \phi} \approx \frac{V}{k' \phi}$$

Since $E_b = k' \phi N$ at load:

$$k' \phi = \frac{E_b}{N} = \frac{235}{1000} = 0.235 \text{ V/rpm}$$

Therefore:

$$N_0 = \frac{250}{0.235} = 1063.83 \text{ rpm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $E_b = 235$ V
- (ii) $T_a = 112.22$ N·m
- (iii) $N_0 = 1063.83$ rpm

**Marks Distribution:** Formula identification (1) → $E_b$ calculation (1.5) → $\omega$ and $T_a$ (2) → Speed calculation (2.5).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and derive the **Torque–Armature Current ($T_a$ vs $I_a$)** and **Speed–Torque ($N$ vs $T_a$)** characteristics of a **DC Series Motor**. Sketch the curves. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC series motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a series field resistance of 0.3 Ω. The motor takes an armature current of 50 A at a speed of 1200 rpm. Find: (i) The developed torque, (ii) The speed when the armature current is 75 A (assuming the flux is proportional to the armature current), and (iii) The speed at no-load theoretically. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Construction:** In a DC series motor, the field winding is connected in **series** with the armature, hence it carries the **full armature current**. The field winding has a **small number of turns of thick wire** (low resistance, high current capacity) since it must withstand the entire armature current.

**Step 2 — Flux Relationship:** Before magnetic saturation:

$$\phi \propto I_a \Rightarrow \phi = k_1 I_a$$

**Step 3 — Torque Equation:**

$$T_a = k \phi I_a = k (k_1 I_a) I_a = (k k_1) I_a^2$$

Hence $T_a \propto I_a^2$ — a **parabolic curve through the origin** in the pre-saturation region. After magnetic saturation, $\phi$ becomes constant, and $T_a \propto I_a$ (linear).

**Step 4 — Speed vs Torque:** From the speed equation:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})}{k' \phi} = \frac{V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})}{k' k_1 I_a}$$

Using $I_a = \sqrt{T_a / (k k_1)}$ from the torque equation:

$$N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_a}}$$

This is the **characteristic hyperbolic / inverse curve** — speed falls sharply as torque increases.

**Step 5 — Sketch:** $T_a$ vs $I_a$ is a **parabola** (curving upward); $N$ vs $T_a$ is a **hyperbolic curve** falling rapidly.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω, $R_{se} = 0.3$ Ω, $I_{a1} = 50$ A, $N_1 = 1200$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Back EMF at given load:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 50(0.5 + 0.3) = 220 - 40 = 180 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Developed Torque:**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{a1} = \frac{E_{b1} \cdot I_{a1}}{\omega_1} = \frac{180 \times 50}{125.66} = \frac{9000}{125.66} \approx 71.62 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Speed at $I_{a2} = 75$ A (assuming $\phi \propto I_a$):**

Back EMF at new load:

$$E_{b2} = 220 - 75(0.8) = 220 - 60 = 160 \text{ V}$$

New flux $\phi_2 = \phi_1 \cdot (I_{a2} / I_{a1}) = \phi_1 \cdot (75/50) = 1.5 \phi_1$

Since $E_b = k' \phi N$:

$$\frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} = \frac{\phi_2 N_2}{\phi_1 N_1} \Rightarrow N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \cdot \frac{\phi_1}{\phi_2}$$

$$N_2 = 1200 \cdot \frac{160}{180} \cdot \frac{1}{1.5} = 1200 \cdot 0.8889 \cdot 0.6667 = 711.11 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical No-Load Speed:**

As $I_a \to 0$, $\phi \to 0$, hence $N \to \infty$ — the **theoretical no-load speed is infinite** (which is why the motor is dangerous on no-load).

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $T_{a1} = 71.62$ N·m
- (ii) $N_2 = 711.11$ rpm
- (iii) $N_0 = \infty$ (theoretical)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units of speed** — many students use $\omega$ (rad/s) and $N$ (rpm) interchangeably, leading to wrong answers. Always convert $N$ to $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ before substituting into power equations.
> 2. **Neglecting the series field resistance in the voltage equation** — for a series motor, the voltage equation is $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$, NOT $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. Missing $R_{se}$ will give wrong back EMF.
> 3. **Confusing cumulative and differential compound motors** — always check whether the series flux aids or opposes the shunt flux. **Cumulative** is the default unless stated otherwise.
> 4. **Plotting curves without proper labels and origin** — KTU examiners award **at least 1 mark** specifically for the *shape, axis labels, and trend* of the characteristic curve.
> 5. **Forgetting the proportionality constant** — when writing $T_a \propto I_a^2$ for a series motor, explicitly mention the assumption $\phi \propto I_a$ (linear region, pre-saturation) to earn full credit.
> 6. **Not distinguishing the three characteristics** — students often mix up $N$ vs $I_a$ with $N$ vs $T_a$. Always specify which curve you are drawing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ The **two master equations** of every DC motor are: $E_b = k' \phi N$ and $T_a = k \phi I_a$.
- ⚡ A **shunt motor** has **constant field flux**, hence:
  - $T_a$ vs $I_a$ → **Straight line through origin**
  - $N$ vs $I_a$ → **Slight droop (nearly constant speed)**
  - $N$ vs $T_a$ → **Nearly horizontal line**
- ⚡ A **series motor** has $\phi \propto I_a$ (pre-saturation), hence:
  - $T_a$ vs $I_a$ → **Parabolic curve**
  - $N$ vs $I_a$ → **Inverse / hyperbolic drop**
  - $N$ vs $T_a$ → **Sharp inverse relationship**
- ⚡ A **cumulative compound motor** has characteristics **between** shunt and series.
- ⚡ A **differential compound motor** has the series flux opposing the shunt flux — it has **poor starting torque** and is rarely used.
- ⚡ A **series motor must never be started on no-load** — its speed tends to infinity theoretically.
- ⚡ The **speed regulation** of a shunt motor is **very good (3–5%)**, of a series motor is **very poor**, and of a compound motor is **moderate**.
- ⚡ **Applications to remember for KTU exam:**
  - Shunt → Lathes, Fans, Blowers (constant speed)
  - Series → Traction, Cranes, Hoists (high starting torque)
  - Compound → Elevators, Rolling Mills, Punches
- ⚡ For numerical problems, always **convert rpm to rad/s** using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ before computing torque.
- ⚡ **Slope formulas** to memorize:
  - Shunt $N$ vs $I_a$ slope: $-R_a / (k' \phi)$
  - Shunt $N$ vs $T_a$ slope: $-R_a / (k k' \phi^2)$
  - Series $N \propto 1 / I_a$ (pre-saturation)
- ⚡ **Sign convention:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ for a motor (back EMF opposes supply voltage).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformer (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **transformer** is a static (stationary), electromagnetic device that transfers electrical energy from one alternating-current (AC) circuit to another through the principles of **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency. It typically consists of two or more electrically isolated windings (primary and secondary) magnetically coupled through a common ferromagnetic core.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, transformers are classified as the **backbone of modern power systems** because they enable efficient transmission, distribution, and utilization of electrical energy at different voltage levels. The operating frequency remains **constant** (e.g., **50 Hz** in India), while the voltage level is stepped up or stepped down.

## 1.2 Classification of Transformers

| Classification Basis | Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Level** | Step-Up / Step-Down | Power transmission, distribution |
| **Construction** | Core-Type / Shell-Type | Distribution / Power transformers |
| **Phase** | Single-Phase / Three-Phase | Domestic / Industrial loads |
| **Cooling** | ONAN, ONAF, OFAF, OFWF | Varies with kVA rating |
| **Use** | Power, Distribution, Instrument (CT/PT) | Grid, substation, metering |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pressure" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine electricity flowing through wires like water flowing through pipes.
> - **Voltage (V)** is like **water pressure** — higher pressure can push water farther.
> - **Current (I)** is like the **flow rate** of water.
> - A **transformer** is like a **pressure-regulating valve** that takes high-pressure, low-flow water from a reservoir and converts it into low-pressure, high-flow water for household use — *without any water leaking out*.
> - The "magnetic core" is the **pump-chamber** that channels the energy, and the "windings" are the **inlet and outlet pipes** of different diameters.

The power delivered (Pressure × Flow Rate) remains almost the same — only the *form* of the energy is re-shaped.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> - **Frequency in India:** $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$
> - **Flux Density (B)** in core: $1.1 \text{ T}$ to $1.4 \text{ T}$ for CRGO steel
> - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
> - **Standard kVA ratings:** 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000, 2500 kVA

## 1.5 Why Transformers Are Indispensable

- **Long-distance transmission** requires *high voltage / low current* to minimize $I^{2}R$ losses.
- **Domestic appliances** require *low voltage* for safety (230 V single-phase in India).
- Transformers bridge this gap with efficiencies of **98% to 99.5%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Single-Phase Transformer

A transformer has three essential constructional parts:

1. **Magnetic Core** — Made of laminated silicon steel (CRGO). Lamination thickness is typically **0.35 mm** to reduce **eddy current losses**.
2. **Primary Winding** — The input winding, connected to the AC source.
3. **Secondary Winding** — The output winding, connected to the load.

### Core-Type vs. Shell-Type Construction

| Feature | Core-Type | Shell-Type |
|---|---|---|
| Magnetic path | Single path enclosing both windings | Double path around the windings |
| Winding placement | Around two limbs | Around the central limb |
| Cooling | Poorer | Better (more surface area) |
| Typical use | Distribution transformers | Power transformers (low voltage, high current) |

## 2.2 Working Principle — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When an alternating voltage $V_{1}$ is applied to the primary winding, it draws a small **magnetizing current** $I_{m}$ that produces an **alternating flux** $\Phi$ in the core. This flux links the secondary winding and, by **Faraday's Law**, induces an EMF $E_{2}$ in it.

The induced EMF is given by the general expression:

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

## 2.3 Derivation of the EMF Equation (Logical Steps)

1. The flux varies **sinusoidally** as: $\Phi = \Phi_{m} \sin(\omega t)$
2. The rate of change is: $\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \omega \Phi_{m} \cos(\omega t)$
3. In RMS form, the average EMF per turn is $4.44 \, f \, \Phi_{m}$.
4. Multiplying by total turns $N$, we obtain the RMS EMF equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step reasoning is provided in Section 3 of these notes.**

## 2.4 Ideal Transformer — The Perfect Reference Model

An **ideal transformer** has:
- Zero winding resistance
- Infinite core permeability (no magnetizing current needed)
- Zero leakage flux
- Zero losses (core + copper)

The defining equations of an ideal transformer are:

$$\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}} = \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} = K$$

where **K** is called the **Transformation Ratio**.

## 2.5 Practical Transformer — Real-World Departures

A practical transformer deviates from the ideal model because of:
- **Copper losses** ($I^{2}R$ losses) in windings
- **Core (iron) losses** — Hysteresis + Eddy current losses
- **Leakage flux** from primary and secondary
- Finite magnetizing current

These are accounted for using an **Equivalent Circuit** consisting of:
- $R_{1}, R_{2}$ — winding resistances
- $X_{1}, X_{2}$ — leakage reactances
- $R_{c}$ (or $R_{0}$) — represents core loss
- $X_{m}$ (or $X_{0}$) — magnetizing reactance

## 2.6 Losses in a Transformer

| Loss Type | Cause | Formula | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hysteresis Loss** | Reversal of magnetic domains | $P_{h} = \eta \, B_{m}^{1.6} \, f \, V$ | Depends on $f$ |
| **Eddy Current Loss** | Circulating currents in core | $P_{e} = K_{e} \, B_{m}^{2} \, f^{2} \, t^{2}$ | $\propto f^{2}$ |
| **Copper Loss (Full Load)** | Resistance of windings | $P_{cu} = I^{2}R$ | Variable with load |

> [!TIP]
> Hysteresis loss is **frequency-dependent but voltage-independent** (at constant V/f ratio), while eddy current loss is **highly frequency-sensitive**.

## 2.7 Efficiency and Regulation

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Output Power} + P_{cu} + P_{core}} \times 100\%$$

**Voltage Regulation:**

$$\text{Regulation} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

**Condition for Maximum Efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\text{Copper Loss at full load} = \text{Core Loss (constant loss)}}$$

This occurs at a fractional load $x$ given by:

$$x = \sqrt{\frac{P_{i}}{P_{cu(FL)}}}$$

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for guaranteed marks.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$ | RMS EMF per winding | Volts |
| 2 | $\Phi_{m} = \frac{E}{4.44 \, f \, N}$ | Maximum flux | Weber |
| 3 | $K = \frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} = \frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}$ | Transformation ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{1} I_{1} = V_{2} I_{2}$ | Power balance (ideal) | VA |
| 5 | $P_{h} = \eta \, B_{m}^{1.6} \, f \, V$ | Hysteresis loss | Watts |
| 6 | $P_{e} = K_{e} \, B_{m}^{2} \, f^{2} \, t^{2}$ | Eddy current loss | Watts |
| 7 | $\eta = \frac{x \, S \, \cos\phi}{x \, S \cos\phi + P_{i} + x^{2} P_{cu}}$ | Efficiency at fractional load $x$ | % |
| 8 | $\% \text{Reg} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation | % |
| 9 | $x_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{i}}{P_{cu(FL)}}}$ | Load fraction for max efficiency | — |
| 10 | $I_{0} = \sqrt{I_{w}^{2} + I_{\mu}^{2}}$ | No-load current | Amps |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Grid Substations:** Step-up transformers (11 kV → 400 kV) at generating stations.
- **Distribution:** Pole-mounted step-down transformers (11 kV → 230 V) for households.
- **Electronics:** SMPS (Switched-Mode Power Supply) uses high-frequency transformers.
- **Instrumentation:** Current Transformers (CT) and Potential Transformers (PT) for metering.
- **Renewable Energy:** Isolation transformers in solar PV and wind installations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation

**Given:** A sinusoidal alternating flux $\Phi(t) = \Phi_{m} \sin(\omega t)$ links a winding of $N$ turns.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Induced EMF (per turn):**

By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the EMF induced in a single turn is:

$$e_{\text{turn}} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the flux expression:**

Substituting $\Phi = \Phi_{m} \sin(\omega t)$:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \left[ \Phi_{m} \sin(\omega t) \right] = \Phi_{m} \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t)$$

Therefore, the magnitude of the instantaneous EMF per turn is:

$$e_{\text{turn}} = \Phi_{m} \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — EMF in the entire N-turn winding:**

Multiplying by $N$:

$$e = N \, \Phi_{m} \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t)$$

The **peak (maximum) value** $E_{m}$ occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$:

$$E_{m} = N \, \Phi_{m} \, \omega$$

**Step 4 — Convert angular frequency to ordinary frequency:**

Recall that $\omega = 2\pi f$. Substituting:

$$E_{m} = 2\pi f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$$

**Step 5 — Convert peak value to RMS value:**

Using $E_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{E_{m}}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$E_{\text{rms}} = \frac{2\pi f \, N \, \Phi_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \, \pi \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify using the constant 4.44:**

Since $\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \approx 4.44$:

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The constant 4.44 is derived purely from $2\pi / \sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2} \pi$.** It is **NOT** a universal constant of nature — it applies *only* to sinusoidal flux waveforms. For square waves, the constant is **4.0**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Transformation Ratio

**For an ideal transformer**, the voltages induced in the two windings share the same flux $\Phi_{m}$ and the same frequency $f$:

$$\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} = \frac{4.44 \, f \, N_{1} \, \Phi_{m}}{4.44 \, f \, N_{2} \, \Phi_{m}}$$

The common terms cancel:

$$\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} = \frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}$$

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the ideal windings ($V = E$):

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}} = \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} = K}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

The efficiency at a fractional load $x$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{x \, S \cos\phi}{x \, S \cos\phi + P_{i} + x^{2} P_{cu}}$$

Where:
- $S$ = Apparent power rating (VA)
- $P_{i}$ = Iron loss (constant)
- $P_{cu}$ = Full-load copper loss
- $\cos\phi$ = Load power factor

**Differentiating $\eta$ with respect to $x$ and setting $\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$** gives the condition for maximum efficiency:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0 \implies P_{i} = x^{2} P_{cu}$$

At full load, $x = 1$, so $P_{cu(FL)} = P_{cu}$:

$$\boxed{x = \sqrt{\frac{P_{i}}{P_{cu(FL)}}}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** A transformer achieves maximum efficiency when its **variable (copper) loss equals its constant (iron) loss**. This is why transformers are designed to operate near full load most of the time.

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Style Problem

**Problem:** A 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has 200 turns on the primary and 50 turns on the secondary. The cross-sectional area of the core is $250 \text{ cm}^{2}$. If the primary is connected to a 2000 V, 50 Hz supply, calculate:
- (a) The maximum flux density in the core
- (b) The secondary EMF
- (c) The transformation ratio

**Solution:**

**(a) Maximum flux $\Phi_{m}$:**

Using $E_{1} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{1} \, \Phi_{m}$:

$$\Phi_{m} = \frac{E_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N_{1}} = \frac{2000}{4.44 \times 50 \times 200}$$

$$\Phi_{m} = \frac{2000}{44400} = 0.04505 \text{ Wb}$$

**Maximum flux density:**

$$B_{m} = \frac{\Phi_{m}}{A} = \frac{0.04505}{250 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.802 \text{ T}$$

**(b) Secondary EMF:**

$$E_{2} = \frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \times E_{1} = \frac{50}{200} \times 2000 = 500 \text{ V}$$

**(c) Transformation ratio:**

$$K = \frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always include **units** in the final answer and clearly state the formula used. This earns easy "method marks" even if the final computation has a minor slip.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transformer

The diagram below maps the **functional energy flow** through a transformer, from the input AC source to the connected load, showing where losses and magnetic coupling occur.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Source 50 Hz] --> B[Primary Winding N1]
    B --> C[Magnetizing Branch Rc and Xm]
    C --> D[Core Flux Phi sine omega t]
    D --> E[Mutual Coupling]
    E --> F[Secondary Winding N2]
    F --> G[Induced EMF E2]
    G --> H[Load Impedance ZL]
    H --> I[Output Power]

    B -.-> J[Copper Loss Pcu1 equals I1 squared R1]
    F -.-> K[Copper Loss Pcu2 equals I2 squared R2]
    D -.-> L[Core Loss Pi equals Ph plus Pe]
    B -.-> M[Leakage Reactance X1]
    F -.-> N[Leakage Reactance X2]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Flow (Approximate)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[V1 Applied] --> R1[R1]
    R1 --> X1[jX1]
    X1 --> MERGE[Common Node]
    MERGE --> Rc[Rc Core Loss Branch]
    MERGE --> Xm[jXm Magnetizing]
    MERGE --> R2[R2 Referred]
    R2 --> X2[jX2 Referred]
    X2 --> V2[V2 Output]
    V2 --> ZL[Load ZL]
```

## 4.3 Operational Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as AC Supply
    participant P as Primary Winding
    participant C as Magnetic Core
    participant SC as Secondary Winding
    participant L as Load
    S->>P: Apply Voltage V1
    P->>C: Magnetizing Current produces Flux
    C->>SC: Alternating Flux links via Mutual Induction
    SC->>L: Induced EMF E2 drives Load Current I2
    L-->>P: Reflected Load Current I2 slash K flows in Primary
```

## 4.4 Key Notes on the Diagrams

- **Solid arrows** represent the **main power-flow path** (active energy transfer).
- **Dashed arrows** represent **loss components** (parasitic, non-ideal).
- The **magnetizing branch** ($R_{c}$ and $X_{m}$) is shown in **parallel** because core losses and magnetizing reactance are shunt (parallel) phenomena in the equivalent circuit.
- **Leakage reactances** $X_{1}$ and $X_{2}$ are placed in **series** with their respective windings because they represent series voltage drops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board exam answers, the **approximate equivalent circuit** (shifting the shunt branch to the primary or secondary terminals) is preferred over the exact T-model for simplicity and to save time.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a transformer. Why is the core laminated?*

**Model Answer:**

A transformer is a static electromagnetic device that transfers AC electrical energy from one circuit to another through mutual electromagnetic induction, operating at a constant frequency.

The core is **laminated** to reduce **eddy current losses**. The thin laminations (typically 0.35 mm thick) are insulated from each other, which restricts the path of eddy currents and reduces the $I^{2}R$ loss caused by them. The formula $P_{e} \propto t^{2}$ shows that lamination thickness $t$ has a squared effect on loss reduction.

> [!TIP]
> **3-Mark Strategy:** Definition (1 mark) + Lamination reason (1 mark) + Formula (1 mark).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Write the EMF equation of a transformer and explain the significance of the constant 4.44.*

**Model Answer:**

The RMS EMF equation of a transformer is:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$$

The constant **4.44** originates from the product $\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi$, which arises from two physical assumptions:
1. The flux varies **sinusoidally** (giving a form factor of $1.11$, and $4 \times 1.11 = 4.44$).
2. The RMS-to-peak conversion (factor of $\sqrt{2}$).

It is **not** a universal constant — for square or triangular waveforms, a different constant is used.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer starting from Faraday's Law. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 kVA, 1100/220 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has 80 turns on the secondary winding. Calculate:
- (i) Number of primary and secondary turns
- (ii) Maximum flux in the core
- (iii) Full-load primary and secondary currents **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation of EMF Equation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Flux varies sinusoidally with time: $\Phi = \Phi_{m} \sin(\omega t)$.
2. Windings are tightly coupled (no leakage).
3. Winding resistance is negligible.

**Derivation:**

By Faraday's Law, the EMF induced in $N$ turns is:

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

Differentiating the flux expression:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \Phi_{m} \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Substituting back:

$$e = N \, \Phi_{m} \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

The peak EMF is:

$$E_{m} = 2\pi f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$$

Converting peak to RMS ($E = E_{m}/\sqrt{2}$):

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $S = 5000 \text{ VA}$, $V_{1} = 1100 \text{ V}$, $V_{2} = 220 \text{ V}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$, $N_{2} = 80$.

**(i) Turns Calculation:**

Transformation ratio:

$$K = \frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} = \frac{220}{1100} = 0.2$$

$$N_{1} = \frac{N_{2}}{K} = \frac{80}{0.2} = 400 \text{ turns}$$

$$N_{2} = 80 \text{ turns}$$

**(ii) Maximum Flux:**

$$\Phi_{m} = \frac{E_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N_{1}} = \frac{1100}{4.44 \times 50 \times 400}$$

$$\Phi_{m} = \frac{1100}{88800} = 0.01239 \text{ Wb} \approx 12.39 \text{ mWb}$$

**(iii) Full-Load Currents:**

$$I_{1(FL)} = \frac{S}{V_{1}} = \frac{5000}{1100} = 4.545 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{2(FL)} = \frac{S}{V_{2}} = \frac{5000}{220} = 22.727 \text{ A}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a single-phase core-type transformer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 kVA, 3300/240 V, 50 Hz transformer has full-load copper loss of 1.2 kW and core loss of 800 W. Calculate:
- (i) Efficiency at full load at 0.8 power factor lagging
- (ii) Efficiency at half load at unity power factor
- (iii) The load at which maximum efficiency occurs **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Construction & Working:**

*Construction:* A core-type transformer has a rectangular laminated core with two vertical limbs. The primary and secondary windings are concentrically wound on each limb. The core is built from CRGO steel laminations of thickness 0.35 mm, insulated with varnish.

*Working:* When AC voltage $V_{1}$ is applied to the primary, an alternating flux $\Phi$ is set up in the core. This flux links the secondary winding and induces an EMF $E_{2}$ according to Faraday's Law. If a load is connected, current $I_{2}$ flows in the secondary, and a corresponding current $I_{1}' = I_{2}/K$ is reflected back to the primary.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $S = 100000 \text{ VA}$, $P_{cu(FL)} = 1200 \text{ W}$, $P_{i} = 800 \text{ W}$.

**(i) Full-load efficiency at 0.8 PF lag:**

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{S \cos\phi}{S \cos\phi + P_{i} + P_{cu}} \times 100$$

Output at full load:

$$P_{out} = 100000 \times 0.8 = 80000 \text{ W}$$

Total losses = 800 + 1200 = 2000 W

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{80000}{80000 + 2000} \times 100 = \frac{80000}{82000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FL} = 97.56\%}$$

**(ii) Half-load efficiency at unity PF:**

At half load, $x = 0.5$, so $P_{cu} = x^{2} P_{cu(FL)} = 0.25 \times 1200 = 300 \text{ W}$.

Output power = $0.5 \times 100000 \times 1.0 = 50000$ W

$$\eta_{HL} = \frac{50000}{50000 + 800 + 300} \times 100 = \frac{50000}{51100} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{HL} = 97.85\%}$$

**(iii) Maximum efficiency load fraction:**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_{i}}{P_{cu(FL)}}} = \sqrt{\frac{800}{1200}} = \sqrt{0.6667} = 0.8165$$

$$\boxed{x_{max} \approx 0.816 \text{ (or 81.6\% of full load)}}$$

Output at this load (unity PF) = $0.8165 \times 100000 = 81.65$ kW

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{81650}{81650 + 800 + 800} = 98.08\%$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Transformer Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Flux area must be in $\text{m}^{2}$ and $\Phi$ in Weber, not $\text{cm}^{2}$ or milliweber without conversion. (*Lose 1–2 marks*)
> 2. **Confusing peak and RMS values** — The formula $E = 4.44 f N \Phi_{m}$ gives **RMS**. If the problem says "peak EMF," multiply by $\sqrt{2}$.
> 3. **Wrong transformation ratio** — Students often write $K = N_{1}/N_{2}$ instead of $N_{2}/N_{1}$. Re-read the definition: $K = V_{2}/V_{1}$ for step-down.
> 4. **Missing the constant 4.44 explanation** — Examiners award 1 mark for *deriving* $4.44 = \sqrt{2} \pi$, not just stating it.
> 5. **In efficiency problems** — Do not confuse *iron loss* with *eddy current loss*. Iron loss = Hysteresis + Eddy current (combined).
> 6. **No diagram** — A 14-mark answer without a labelled circuit or core diagram loses at least **2 marks** as per KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transformers**

- **Definition:** Static AC device, transfers power via mutual induction, **frequency unchanged**.
- **EMF Equation:** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$ (RMS) — derived from $\sqrt{2} \pi = 4.44$.
- **Construction:** Core-type (single magnetic path) vs Shell-type (double path).
- **Lamination:** Reduces **eddy current loss**; thickness $\approx 0.35 \text{ mm}$; material: **CRGO steel**.
- **Ideal Transformer:** $\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}} = \frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}$; no losses, no leakage.
- **Practical Transformer:** Has winding resistance, leakage reactance, and core losses.
- **Equivalent Circuit:** $R_{1} + jX_{1}$ (series) + $[R_{c} \parallel jX_{m}]$ (shunt) + $R_{2} + jX_{2}$ (series).
- **Hysteresis Loss:** $P_{h} \propto f \, B_{m}^{1.6}$ — Reduced by using **silicon steel**.
- **Eddy Current Loss:** $P_{e} \propto f^{2} \, B_{m}^{2} \, t^{2}$ — Reduced by **lamination**.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Output} + P_{i} + P_{cu}}$.
- **Maximum Efficiency Condition:** $P_{i} = P_{cu(FL)} \cdot x^{2}$.
- **Voltage Regulation:** $\% \text{Reg} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$.
- **Transformation Ratio $K$:** $K = N_{2}/N_{1}$ (Step-Down if $K < 1$).
- **Phasor Diagram (Resistive Load):** $V_{2}$ in phase with $I_{2}$; $E_{2}$ leads $V_{2}$ by small angle.
- **Key Frequency:** India uses **50 Hz**; USA uses **60 Hz** (irrelevant for formula, important for design).
- **Units to remember:** Flux in **Wb**, $B$ in **Tesla**, $f$ in **Hz**, Area in **m²**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid (Mnemonic): "E = FANC"**
> **E**MF = 4.44 × **F**requency × turns **N** × Maximum flux **C** (Phi_m).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Working Principle of a DC Generator

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **DC Generator** is a rotating electrical machine that converts **mechanical energy** into **direct current (DC) electrical energy** based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When a conductor (armature) rotates in a uniform magnetic field, an alternating EMF is induced in it, which is converted to unidirectional (DC) EMF using a **mechanical commutator-brush assembly**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water wheel** placed in a flowing river. As the river pushes the wheel, it rotates and lifts water up to a higher level. In the same way, in a DC generator:
- The **mechanical prime mover** (e.g., a diesel engine or turbine) acts like the flowing river.
- It pushes the **armature conductors** to rotate through a **magnetic field** created by field poles.
- This rotation **induces an EMF** in the conductors — this is the lifting of "electrical potential."
- The **commutator and brushes** ensure the current always flows in the **same direction** in the external circuit (just like a one-way valve).

The magnitude of the induced EMF is governed by Faraday's Law:

$$e = -\frac{d\psi}{dt}$$

where the rate of change of flux linkage $\psi$ is created mechanically by rotation.

---

## 1.2 Working Principle of a DC Motor

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **DC Motor** is a rotating electrical machine that converts **direct current (DC) electrical energy** into **mechanical energy** based on the **Lorentz Force Principle**. When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a mechanical force, causing the armature to rotate.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **sailboat** navigating using a rudder. The wind (electricity) pushes against the sail, and the rudder's orientation (magnetic field) determines the direction of motion. In a DC motor:
- **Electrical input (current)** is the wind.
- **Magnetic field from field poles** is the rudder.
- The interaction produces a **mechanical force** that rotates the shaft (motion of the boat).

The force on a current-carrying conductor is governed by the Lorentz Law:

$$\vec{F} = I(\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$

This is the **dual counterpart** of the DC generator — the same construction, but with input and output swapped.

---

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Laws Governing DC Machines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of DC Machine Operation**
> 1. **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** — Governs the DC Generator (induces EMF).
> 2. **Biot-Savart / Lorentz Force Law** — Governs the DC Motor (produces torque).
> 3. **Lenz's Law** — Determines the **direction** of induced EMF and back-EMF (opposes cause).

| Law | Physical Quantity Produced | Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Law | Induced EMF $(E_g)$ | DC Generator |
| Lorentz Force Law | Mechanical Torque $(T)$ | DC Motor |
| Lenz's Law | Direction of Induced EMF / Back-EMF | Both |

The standard values of **flux per pole $\Phi$** in practical DC machines range from **$\mathbf{0.005\ Wb}$ to $\mathbf{0.5\ Wb}$**, with operating speeds typically **$\mathbf{600\ rpm}$ to $\mathbf{3000\ rpm}$** depending on application.

---

## 1.4 Basic Construction Elements (Foundation for Principle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Major Parts of a DC Machine**
> - **Yoke**: Outer cylindrical frame — provides mechanical support and carries the magnetic flux.
> - **Field Poles (Pole Core & Pole Shoe)**: Carry the field winding; create the main magnetic flux $\Phi$.
> - **Field Winding**: Copper coils wound around pole cores; carry the field current $I_f$ to produce flux.
> - **Armature Core**: Laminated cylindrical structure that houses the armature conductors.
> - **Armature Winding**: Where EMF is induced (generator) or torque is produced (motor).
> - **Commutator**: Cylindrical segment of copper bars; converts AC to DC (generator) or DC to AC (motor).
> - **Brushes**: Graphite/carbon blocks that maintain sliding contact with the commutator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Principle of a DC Generator

The principle of operation of a DC generator can be broken down into **four logical stages**:

1. **Excitation of Field Poles**: When DC current is supplied to the field winding, it produces a uniform **stationary magnetic flux** $\Phi$ across the air gap between the poles and the armature.

2. **Rotation of Armature Conductors**: The prime mover (engine/turbine) rotates the armature. Each conductor cuts the magnetic flux lines.

3. **Induction of Alternating EMF**: Per Faraday's Law, an EMF is induced in each conductor. As the conductor moves from one pole to the other, the direction of induced EMF alternates — it is **AC within the individual coil**.

4. **Commutation (Rectification)**: The commutator segments rotate with the armature. The brushes are positioned such that the connections in the external circuit reverse at the precise instant the EMF in the coil reverses. This results in **unidirectional (DC) EMF** in the external circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule (Generator Rule)**
> - **Thumb** → Direction of Motion of Conductor
> - **Forefinger** → Direction of Magnetic Field (N → S)
> - **Middle Finger** → Direction of Induced Current (EMF)

---

## 2.2 Detailed Principle of a DC Motor

The principle of operation of a DC motor:

1. **Field Excitation**: Field winding is energized to produce a uniform magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the air gap.

2. **Current Supply to Armature**: The DC supply is given to the armature conductors through the brushes and commutator.

3. **Force Production**: Each armature conductor carries a current $I_a$ and is placed in the magnetic field $\Phi$. Per Lorentz force, it experiences a tangential force $F = BIL$.

4. **Torque Production**: These forces, acting at the armature radius, combine to produce a **net unidirectional torque** $T$, rotating the armature. The commutator continuously reverses the armature current direction so that torque is always unidirectional.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule (Motor Rule)**
> - **Thumb** → Direction of Force / Motion
> - **Forefinger** → Direction of Magnetic Field (N → S)
> - **Middle Finger** → Direction of Current

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities use **SI Units**. Standard symbol: $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $P$ = number of poles, $A$ = number of parallel paths, $N$ = speed (rpm), $I_a$ = armature current (A).

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMF Equation (Generator) | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$ | Generated EMF in volts |
| 2 | Back-EMF (Motor) | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$ | Opposes supply voltage |
| 3 | Armature Torque | $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi \, Z \, I_a \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Developed torque in N-m |
| 4 | Power Developed (Mech.) | $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$ | Converted power in watts |
| 5 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = (9.55) \cdot \dfrac{P_{out}}{N}$ | Useful output torque |
| 6 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s | Mechanical speed |
| 7 | Wave Winding: Paths | $A = 2$ | For all $P$ |
| 8 | Lap Winding: Paths | $A = P$ | Equals no. of poles |
| 9 | Lorentz Force | $F = B \, I \, L$ | Force on single conductor |
| 10 | Faraday's EMF | $e = B \, L \, v$ | EMF in single conductor |

**Boundary Conditions & Important Notes:**
- For a **Lap-wound** machine: $A = P$, so EMF simplifies to $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60}$.
- For a **Wave-wound** machine: $A = 2$, so EMF becomes $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{120}$.
- **Back-EMF** in a motor can never equal supply voltage $V$ (else current → ∞).
- The ratio $\dfrac{P}{A}$ is called the **machine constant** $k$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles described are used in:
- **Automotive** (starter motors, alternators, dynamos).
- **Steel rolling mills** (separately excited DC motors for precise speed control).
- **Electric traction** (tram and metro DC series motors).
- **Battery charging** (DC shunt generators as battery chargers).
- **Servo systems** (small DC motors for robotics and CNC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation of a DC Generator

Let the following standard machine parameters be defined:
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in Webers, Wb)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature (in revolutions per minute, rpm)
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature winding

**Step 1: Compute flux cut by one conductor in one revolution**

Each conductor sweeps past $P$ poles per revolution (one full magnetic cycle). Therefore, the flux cut by a single conductor in one complete revolution is:

$$\text{Flux per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \cdot P$$

> *Logic*: In one revolution, a conductor moves past $P$ pole-pairs of flux, each of magnitude $\Phi$.

**Step 2: Convert revolutions to time**

The number of revolutions per second is $\dfrac{N}{60}$. Hence, the time taken for **one revolution** is:

$$t_1 = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

> *Logic*: rpm → rps conversion: $1 \text{ rev} = \dfrac{60}{N} \text{ sec}$.

**Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law to a single conductor**

Per Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced in a single conductor is:

$$e_{\text{avg}} = \frac{d\psi}{dt} = \frac{\Phi \cdot P}{t_1}$$

Substituting $t_1$:

$$e_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot P}{\left(\frac{60}{N}\right)} = \frac{\Phi \, P \, N}{60} \text{ volts}$$

> *Logic*: Faraday's law relates rate of change of flux linkage to induced EMF.

**Step 4: Combine all $Z$ conductors**

The $Z$ armature conductors are distributed in $A$ parallel paths. Therefore, the **number of conductors in series per parallel path** is:

$$Z_{\text{series}} = \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 5: Compute total EMF across the brushes**

Since the parallel paths are connected in parallel between the brushes, the voltage between the brushes equals the EMF across one parallel path. Each path contains $\dfrac{Z}{A}$ conductors in series. So:

$$E_g = e_{\text{avg}} \cdot Z_{\text{series}} = \left(\frac{\Phi \, P \, N}{60}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right)$$

$$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} \text{ volts}}$$

> *Logic*: The brushes are connected at the parallel-path terminals; voltages of all paths add in parallel, not in series, so we only sum conductors in one path.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation of a DC Motor

**Step 1: Force on a single armature conductor**

A conductor of active length $L$ (in metres), carrying current $I$ (in Amperes), in a magnetic flux density $B$ (in Tesla) experiences a force:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \text{ Newtons}$$

> *Logic*: This is the Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor.

**Step 2: Relate $B$ to $\Phi$**

The average flux density over one pole pitch is:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_{\text{pole}}}$$

where $A_{\text{pole}}$ is the cross-sectional area per pole (m²). For simplicity, we retain $B$ as flux density.

**Step 3: Torque due to one conductor**

The conductor lies at the armature surface at a radius $r$ (in metres). The torque produced by one conductor is:

$$T_{\text{one}} = F \cdot r = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot r$$

**Step 4: Current in each conductor**

The total armature current $I_a$ is shared among $A$ parallel paths. The current per conductor is:

$$I = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

**Step 5: Sum over all $Z$ conductors**

Total armature torque is contributed by all $Z$ conductors acting at radius $r$:

$$T_a = Z \cdot B \cdot \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) \cdot L \cdot r$$
