$$T_a = B \cdot L \cdot r \cdot \left(\frac{Z \, I_a}{A}\right)$$

**Step 6: Substitute $B \cdot L \cdot r = \dfrac{\Phi \, P}{2\pi}$**

This geometric substitution arises from the fact that the air-gap flux $\Phi$ spans $P$ pole-pairs, and the conductor sweeps a circumference of $2\pi r$:

$$T_a = \left(\frac{\Phi \, P}{2\pi}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{Z \, I_a}{A}\right)$$

$$\boxed{T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi \, Z \, I_a \text{ N-m}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$T_a = 0.159 \cdot \frac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi \, Z \, I_a \text{ N-m}$$

> *Logic*: The factor $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ converts the spatial magnetic geometry to torque units.

**Step 7: Derive the relation between $T_a$ and $E_b$**

The mechanical power developed equals electrical power absorbed by back-EMF:

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega = T_a \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)$$

Substituting $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$:

$$T_a \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right) = \left(\frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\right) \cdot I_a$$

Solving for $T_a$:

$$T_a = \frac{\Phi Z I_a P}{60 A} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi Z I_a$$

This **confirms consistency** with our derived torque equation — a powerful sanity check.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Validation Example

> **Given**: A 4-pole, wave-wound DC machine, $Z = 600$ conductors, $\Phi = 0.05\ \text{Wb}$, $N = 1000\ \text{rpm}$, $I_a = 50\ \text{A}$.

For wave winding: $A = 2$.

**Generated EMF:**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = \frac{0.05 \times 600 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 2} = \frac{120000}{120} = 1000 \text{ V}$$

**Armature Torque:**

$$T_a = 0.159 \times \frac{P}{A} \times \Phi Z I_a = 0.159 \times \frac{4}{2} \times 0.05 \times 600 \times 50 = 477 \text{ N-m}$$

**Power Developed:**

$$P_m = E_g \cdot I_a = 1000 \times 50 = 50000 \text{ W} = 50 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These equations form the **analytical foundation** for every numerical problem in the KTU Module 2 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Operating Principle of a DC Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Mechanical Prime Mover] --> A2[Rotating Armature Conductors]
    A2 --> A3{Faradays Law: dpsi/dt}
    A3 --> A4[Induced AC EMF in Coil]
    A4 --> A5[Commutator Assembly]
    A5 --> A6[Brushes Output Terminals]
    A6 --> A7[Unidirectional DC Voltage to Load]
    F1[DC Field Excitation Supply] --> F2[Field Winding on Poles]
    F2 --> F3[Uniform Magnetic Flux Phi]
    F3 --> A2
    A7 --> A8[Electrical Load Lamp Motor]
    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Operating Principle of a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[DC Supply Source] --> B2[Brushes and Commutator]
    B2 --> B3[Armature Conductors Carry Current Ia]
    F1[Field Supply] --> F2[Field Winding Excited]
    F2 --> F3[Magnetic Flux Phi in Air Gap]
    B3 --> B4{Lorentz Force F = BIL}
    F3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5[Tangential Mechanical Force]
    B5 --> B6[Unidirectional Torque Ta]
    B6 --> B7[Rotating Shaft Output]
    B7 --> B8[Mechanical Load Fan Pump]
    B1 -.Back EMF Eb.-> B2
    style B1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Generator vs Motor (Dual Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph gen[DC Generator Mode]
        G1[Mechanical Input Torque T] --> G2[Armature Rotation N]
        G2 --> G3[Induced EMF Eg]
        G3 --> G4[DC Electrical Output]
    end
    subgraph mot[DC Motor Mode]
        M1[DC Electrical Input V] --> M2[Armature Current Ia]
        M2 --> M3[Developed Torque Ta]
        M3 --> M4[Mechanical Output]
    end
    G3 -.Lenzs Law.- M2
    M3 -.Back EMF Eb.- G3
    style G1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Power Stages in a DC Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Input Electrical or Mechanical] --> P1{Conversion Process}
    P1 -->|Generator| P2[Mechanical Power Pm]
    P2 --> P3[Armature Copper Loss I2a Ra]
    P2 --> P4[Iron Loss Pi]
    P2 --> P5[Mechanical Friction Loss]
    P4 --> P6[Gross Electrical Output Eg Ia]
    P3 --> P6
    P5 --> P7[Net Shaft Power Output Pout]
    P1 -->|Motor| P8[Input Electrical Power V Ia]
    P8 --> P3
    P3 --> P2
    P2 --> P7
    style P0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** State Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction and explain its application in a DC generator. **(3 Marks)**

### Model Answer
Faraday's Law states that the EMF induced in a conductor is proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage:

$$e = -\frac{d\psi}{dt} = -N\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Application in DC Generator:** When the armature conductors are rotated by a prime mover, they continuously cut the magnetic flux $\Phi$ produced by the field poles. The change in flux linkage induces an EMF in each conductor. By arranging the conductors in a closed winding and using a commutator-brush assembly, the alternating EMF is rectified to give a unidirectional DC output at the terminals. **(3 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between the operating principles of a DC generator and a DC motor with reference to the energy conversion law. **(3 Marks)**

### Model Answer
The **DC Generator** converts **mechanical energy to electrical energy** using **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** — the relative motion between conductors and magnetic flux induces an EMF. The governing law is $e = B L v$.

The **DC Motor** converts **electrical energy to mechanical energy** using the **Lorentz Force Principle** — a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force. The governing law is $F = B I L$. 

Both machines are **dual** in operation, and physically identical; the difference lies only in the direction of power flow and the law applied. The **back-EMF** in a motor and the **generated EMF** in a generator are mathematically the same: $E = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$. **(3 Marks)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Select ONE)

---

### ✅ **OPTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**
> **Q3 (a).** Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator. A 4-pole, lap-wound DC generator has 800 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.04 Wb. Find the generated EMF when running at 1500 rpm. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Derivation:**

Let $\Phi$ = flux per pole, $Z$ = total conductors, $N$ = speed in rpm, $P$ = poles, $A$ = parallel paths.

- Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $\Phi P$
- Time for one revolution = $\dfrac{60}{N}$ sec
- Average EMF per conductor = $\dfrac{\Phi P}{60 / N} = \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}$ V
- Conductors in series per path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$
- Total EMF across brushes:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \text{ V}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $P = 4$, lap-wound $\Rightarrow A = P = 4$, $Z = 800$, $\Phi = 0.04$ Wb, $N = 1500$ rpm.

$$E_g = \frac{0.04 \times 800 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 4} = \frac{0.04 \times 800 \times 1500}{60}$$

$$E_g = \frac{48000}{60} = 800 \text{ V}$$

**Answer: $E_g = 800\ \text{V}$** **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Q3 (b).** Derive the torque equation of a DC motor. A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω, runs at 1000 rpm, and draws an armature current of 20 A. The flux per pole is 0.05 Wb and the machine has 4 poles, 1000 conductors, lap-wound. Calculate the developed torque. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Derivation:** (Refer Section 3.2)
- Force on conductor: $F = BIL$
- Torque per conductor: $T_1 = F \cdot r$
- Sum over $Z$ conductors: $T_a = Z \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{I_a}{A} \cdot L \cdot r$
- Using $B L r = \dfrac{\Phi P}{2\pi}$:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi Z I_a$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $P = 4$, lap-wound $\Rightarrow A = 4$, $Z = 1000$, $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb, $I_a = 20$ A.

$$T_a = 0.159 \times \frac{4}{4} \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 20$$

$$T_a = 0.159 \times 1 \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 20 = 159 \text{ N-m}$$

**Answer: $T_a = 159\ \text{N-m}$** **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ **OPTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**
> **Q4 (a).** Explain with a neat sketch, the construction and working principle of a DC generator. Also derive its EMF equation. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Construction:** A DC generator consists of:
- A **yoke** (outer frame) made of cast iron or steel.
- **Pole cores and pole shoes** bolted to the yoke, carrying the **field winding**.
- A cylindrical **armature core** built of silicon steel laminations, slotted to house the **armature winding**.
- A **commutator** (cyl [copper segments insulated by mica]) mounted on the shaft.
- **Brushes** (carbon/graphite) sliding on the commutator to tap the output.

**Working Principle:**
1. The prime mover rotates the armature at speed $N$.
2. Field winding is energized, producing flux $\Phi$ per pole.
3. As conductors cut the flux, an alternating EMF is induced in them per Faraday's Law: $e = \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}$ V per conductor.
4. The **commutator** rectifies this alternating EMF into a unidirectional DC EMF at the brushes.
5. Per Fleming's Right-Hand Rule, the induced current direction reverses every half-cycle within the coil, but commutator action ensures constant polarity at the brushes.

**EMF Equation Derivation:**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \text{ volts}$$

where $A = P$ for lap winding, $A = 2$ for wave winding. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Q4 (b).** A 6-pole, wave-wound DC machine has 600 conductors, flux per pole of 30 mWb, and runs at 1200 rpm. If it operates as a motor with armature current of 40 A, calculate: (i) the generated EMF, (ii) the developed torque, and (iii) the gross mechanical power developed. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Given:** $P = 6$, wave-wound $\Rightarrow A = 2$, $Z = 600$, $\Phi = 0.030$ Wb, $N = 1200$ rpm, $I_a = 40$ A.

**(i) Generated / Back EMF:**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = \frac{0.030 \times 600 \times 1200 \times 6}{60 \times 2} = \frac{129600}{120} = 1080 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Developed Torque:**

$$T_a = 0.159 \times \frac{P}{A} \times \Phi Z I_a = 0.159 \times \frac{6}{2} \times 0.030 \times 600 \times 40$$

$$T_a = 0.159 \times 3 \times 0.030 \times 600 \times 40 = 343.44 \text{ N-m}$$

**(iii) Gross Mechanical Power:**

$$P_m = E_b \times I_a = 1080 \times 40 = 43200 \text{ W} = 43.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Alternative verification via $T_a \omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = 343.44 \times 125.66 \approx 43160 \text{ W} \approx 43.2 \text{ kW} \;\checkmark$$

**Answers: $E_b = 1080$ V, $T_a = 343.44$ N-m, $P_m = 43.2$ kW** **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Forgetting to use $A = 2$ (wave) or $A = P$ (lap) — students often write $A = 1$ which is meaningless.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (3 marks lost):** Unit inconsistency — $\Phi$ must be in **Wb**, not mWb. Convert $30\ \text{mWb} = 0.030\ \text{Wb}$ first.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (2 marks lost):** Confusing **Back-EMF** (motor) with **Generated EMF** (generator) — they are mathematically the same, but conceptually different. State the law being applied.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost):** Not mentioning **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule (generator)** and **Left-Hand Rule (motor)** in descriptive answers.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (1 mark lost):** Skipping the unit in the final numerical answer.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **DC Generator Principle**: Mechanical → Electrical, governed by **Faraday's Law** ($e = -d\psi/dt$); direction by **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule**.
> - **DC Motor Principle**: Electrical → Mechanical, governed by **Lorentz Force Law** ($F = BIL$); direction by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**.
> - **Commutator role**:
>   - In generator → **Rectifier** (AC to DC).
>   - In motor → **Inverter** (DC to AC internally for continuous unidirectional torque).
> - **EMF Equation**: $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — valid for both motor (back-EMF $E_b$) and generator (generated EMF $E_g$).
> - **Torque Equation**: $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi Z I_a = 0.159 \cdot \dfrac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi Z I_a$.
> - **Wave winding** always has $A = 2$, **Lap winding** has $A = P$.
> - **Mechanical Power Developed**: $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$ (watts).
> - **Angular speed**: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s.
> - The **constant** $\dfrac{PZ}{60 A}$ is the machine design constant — KTU questions often split it into separate steps.
> - **DC machine is reversible** — the same physical machine can act as generator or motor based on energy input/output.
> - For numerical clarity, always convert **mWb → Wb** before substitution.
> - For **shunt motor**: $I_a = I_L - I_f$; for **series motor**: $I_a = I_{se} = I_L$.
> - **Back-EMF** always opposes the applied voltage — Lenz's Law in action.
> - KTU 2024 expects **unit consistency** in every calculation step — partial credit is given for correct formulas even if the final number is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EMF Equation, Losses and Efficiency
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** EMF Equation, Losses and Efficiency

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The EMF Equation — The Heartbeat of a Transformer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **EMF Equation** of a transformer is the mathematical expression that relates the **RMS value of the induced electromotive force (EMF)** in a transformer winding to the **supply frequency**, the **number of turns** in the winding, and the **maximum value of the alternating flux** linking the winding. It is derived from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** integrated with the geometric shape of the induced EMF waveform.

The governing equation for a sinusoidally excited, single-phase, two-winding transformer is:

$$E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi_{m}$$

Where:
- **E** = RMS value of the induced EMF in volts (**V**)
- **f** = Supply frequency in Hertz (**Hz**)
- **N** = Number of turns in the winding (dimensionless)
- **Φ_m** = Maximum value of the mutual flux in Webers (**Wb**)
- **4.44** = The form factor constant (= $\pi \cdot \sqrt{2} / \sqrt{2}$ effectively) — arises when we convert average flux to RMS EMF

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 4.44?**
> The constant 4.44 is derived as the product of **4** (from the average value calculation over a half cycle, i.e., $4 f N \Phi_m$ for the average EMF) multiplied by the **form factor 1.11** (ratio of RMS to average for a sine wave = $\pi/2\sqrt{2} \approx 1.11$). Thus $4 \times 1.11 = 4.44$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pump and Turbine" Picture

Imagine a **water pump** (primary winding) pushing water (magnetic flux) through a shared pipe (magnetic core). The water flows in pulses because the pump pushes back and forth (AC supply). As the water rushes through, it spins a **turbine** (secondary winding) at the other end.

- The **speed of the pump** = frequency $f$ (more pushes per second = more water per second)
- The **size of the pipe** (cross-section) = $\Phi_m$ (more flux per push)
- The **number of turbine blades** = $N_2$ (more blades = more energy captured)
- The **electrical voltage generated** = induced EMF $E$

Just like the turbine's output power depends on blade count, water flow, and pump speed — the induced EMF depends on turns, flux, and frequency. **This is precisely what the EMF equation quantifies.**

## 1.3 Losses and Efficiency — The Energy Accounting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Losses** are the **unavoidable conversion of useful electrical energy into heat** within a transformer during its operation. No real transformer is 100% efficient because of fundamental physical phenomena like magnetic hysteresis, eddy currents, and conductor resistance.

> **Efficiency** is the ratio of **useful output power** delivered to the load to the **total input power** drawn from the source, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} \times 100\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Transformer efficiency is typically **95% to 99%** for well-designed power transformers. Efficiency is **NOT measured under no-load** because output would be zero — making efficiency trivially zero. It is always evaluated at a specific load and power factor.

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic — Why the EMF Equation Has the Form It Has

The derivation flows through **Faraday's Law** combined with the geometry of sinusoidal flux. Let us break the logic into four structured steps:

### Step 1 — Express the Instantaneous Flux
The AC supply creates a sinusoidal varying flux in the core:

$$\phi(t) = \Phi_{m} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

Where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency in **rad/s**.

### Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law
The induced EMF in a coil of N turns, by Faraday's Law, is:

$$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate
Differentiating $\phi(t)$:

$$e = -N \cdot \Phi_{m} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t) = N \cdot \Phi_{m} \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(\omega t - 90^\circ)$$

The peak (maximum) value is:

$$E_{max} = N \cdot \Phi_{m} \cdot \omega = 2\pi f N \Phi_{m}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Peak to RMS
For a sinusoidal wave, $E_{rms} = E_{max} / \sqrt{2}$:

$$E_{rms} = \frac{2\pi f N \Phi_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi_{m} = 4.44 \, f N \Phi_{m}$$

## 2.2 The Two EMFs in a Transformer

For a two-winding transformer:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_{m} \quad \text{(Primary EMF)}$$

$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_{m} \quad \text{(Secondary EMF)}$$

**Transformation Ratio (K):**

$$K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{E_2}{E_1}$$

- If $N_2 > N_1$: **Step-Up** transformer ($K > 1$)
- If $N_2 < N_1$: **Step-Down** transformer ($K < 1$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The EMF equation is the foundational tool used by **transformer design engineers** to size the core cross-sectional area, select lamination thickness, choose conductor gauge, and determine the **voltage regulation** characteristics before physical prototyping.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Induced EMF (RMS) | $E = 4.44 \, f N \Phi_{m}$ | $E$ in V, $f$ in Hz, $N$ dimensionless, $\Phi_m$ in Wb | Voltage induced per winding |
| Transformation Ratio | $K = N_2/N_1 = E_2/E_1$ | Dimensionless | Voltage step-up/down factor |
| Voltage Ratio | $V_1/V_2 = N_1/N_2$ | Dimensionless | Inverse relation |
| Hysteresis Loss | $W_h = \eta \, B_{max}^{1.6} \, f \, V$ | $W_h$ in W, $B_{max}$ in T, $V$ in m³, $\eta$ = Steinmetz constant | Loss due to molecular friction in core |
| Eddy Current Loss | $W_e = K_e \, B_{max}^{2} \, f^{2} \, t^{2} \, V$ | $t$ = lamination thickness in m, $K_e$ = constant | Loss due to circulating currents in core |
| Total Iron Loss | $P_i = W_h + W_e$ | $P_i$ in W | Constant loss (independent of load) |
| Full-load Cu Loss | $P_{cu(fl)} = I_2^2 R_{02} = I_1^2 R_{01}$ | In W | Load-dependent loss |
| Efficiency (general) | $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}}$ | Dimensionless | Output over input |
| Efficiency (fractional load) | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cos\phi + P_i + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)}}$ | $x$ = load fraction, $S$ = rating in VA | At any load $x$ times full load |
| Max Efficiency Condition | $P_i = x^{2} \, P_{cu(fl)}$ | At maximum efficiency, iron loss = fractional cu loss | Critical balance point |
| Load at Max Efficiency | $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i \, / \, P_{cu(fl)}}$ | Fraction of full load | Load fraction for $\eta_{max}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The EMF equation and loss analysis are used extensively in:

- **Power utility companies** for designing distribution transformers
- **Audio equipment manufacturers** for designing output transformers in amplifiers
- **Renewable energy systems** (solar inverters, wind turbine step-up transformers)
- **Electric vehicle (EV) charging stations** for isolation and voltage conversion
- **Industrial motor drives** for galvanic isolation and noise filtering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Questions on the **EMF equation derivation (5 marks)** and **efficiency calculation with maximum efficiency condition (7-8 marks)** are among the **most frequently asked** topics in Module 2. Mastering the formula sheet above is non-negotiable for scoring above 80% in this module.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation

### Starting Point — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction

According to Faraday's Law, the EMF induced in a coil of N turns is equal to the negative rate of change of flux linkages:

$$e = -N \frac{d\psi}{dt}$$

Where $\psi$ is the flux per turn. In SI units, $e$ is in **volts**, $\psi$ in **webers**, and $t$ in **seconds**.

### Step 1 — Express the Time-Varying Flux

For a transformer connected to a sinusoidal AC supply, the alternating flux in the core varies sinusoidally with respect to time:

$$\phi(t) = \Phi_{m} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

Here:
- $\Phi_m$ = maximum (peak) value of the flux in the core, measured in **Wb**
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency, measured in **rad/s**
- $f$ = supply frequency, measured in **Hz**

### Step 2 — Substitute Into Faraday's Law

Substituting $\phi(t)$ into the EMF expression:

$$e = -N \frac{d}{dt}\left[\Phi_{m} \sin(\omega t)\right]$$

### Step 3 — Perform the Differentiation

The derivative of $\sin(\omega t)$ with respect to $t$ is $\omega \cos(\omega t)$:

$$e = -N \cdot \Phi_{m} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Expression

Rearranging the expression to make the sinusoidal nature explicit:

$$e = N \cdot \Phi_{m} \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(\omega t - 90^\circ)$$

The negative cosine can be expressed as a sine shifted by $-90^\circ$. This shows that the induced EMF **lags** the flux by 90° in a purely inductive system.

### Step 5 — Identify the Maximum (Peak) Value of EMF

The maximum value of EMF occurs when the sine term equals unity:

$$E_{max} = N \cdot \Phi_{m} \cdot \omega$$

### Step 6 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$

$$E_{max} = 2\pi f N \Phi_{m}$$

### Step 7 — Convert Peak to RMS Value

For a pure sinusoidal waveform, the relationship between RMS and peak values is:

$$E_{rms} = \frac{E_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{rms} = \frac{2\pi f N \Phi_{m}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### Step 8 — Simplify to the Standard Form

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{rms} &= \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi_{m} \\
&= \sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi_{m} \\
&= 1.414 \times 3.1416 \times f \cdot N \cdot \Phi_{m} \\
&= 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m} \quad \text{volts}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification of 4.44:** The factor 4.44 = $4 \times 1.11$, where 4 arises from $2 \times 2\pi$ (full cycle averaging) and 1.11 is the form factor (RMS/average) of a pure sine wave. This is **NOT** a universal constant — for square or triangular waves, different constants apply.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Efficiency Equation

### Step 1 — Define Output Power
For a transformer delivering load current $I_2$ at terminal voltage $V_2$ with power factor $\cos\phi$:

$$P_{out} = V_2 \, I_2 \, \cos\phi$$

### Step 2 — Define Total Losses
The total losses comprise **iron (core) losses** $P_i$ (constant) and **copper losses** $P_{cu}$ (variable with load):

$$P_{losses} = P_i + P_{cu}$$

### Step 3 — Express Input Power
By conservation of energy:

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses} = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}$$

### Step 4 — Form the Efficiency Ratio

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}}$$

### Step 5 — Generalize to Fractional Load
Let $x$ = fraction of full load. Then load current becomes $x I_{2(fl)}$, and copper loss becomes $x^2 P_{cu(fl)}$ (since $P_{cu} \propto I^2$):

$$\eta = \frac{x \, V_2 I_{2(fl)} \cos\phi}{x \, V_2 I_{2(fl)} \cos\phi + P_i + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)}}$$

Recognizing $S = V_2 I_{2(fl)}$ as the apparent power rating:

$$\eta = \frac{x \, S \cos\phi}{x \, S \cos\phi + P_i + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)}}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the Maximum Efficiency Condition

### Step 1 — Differentiate Efficiency with Respect to Load $x$
To find the load at which efficiency is maximum, take the derivative of $\eta$ with respect to $x$ and set it to zero. For simplicity, use a single-variable efficiency expression (treating $\cos\phi$ and $S$ as constants):

$$\eta(x) = \frac{x \, S \cos\phi}{x \, S \cos\phi + P_i + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Quotient Rule
Let $u = x S \cos\phi$ and $v = x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu(fl)}$. Then:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = \frac{v \cdot \frac{du}{dx} - u \cdot \frac{dv}{dx}}{v^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Derivatives

$$\frac{du}{dx} = S \cos\phi$$

$$\frac{dv}{dx} = S \cos\phi + 2x \, P_{cu(fl)}$$

### Step 4 — Set Numerator to Zero
At maximum efficiency, $d\eta/dx = 0$, so the numerator must vanish:

$$v \cdot (S \cos\phi) - u \cdot (S \cos\phi + 2x P_{cu(fl)}) = 0$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify

$$\begin{aligned}
&\left[x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)}\right] (S \cos\phi) \\
&\quad - (x S \cos\phi) (S \cos\phi + 2x P_{cu(fl)}) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding:

$$x S^{2} \cos^{2}\phi + P_i S \cos\phi + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)} S \cos\phi - x S^{2} \cos^{2}\phi - 2x^{2} S \cos\phi \cdot P_{cu(fl)} = 0$$

The $x S^2 \cos^2\phi$ terms cancel, leaving:

$$P_i S \cos\phi + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)} S \cos\phi - 2x^{2} S \cos\phi \cdot P_{cu(fl)} = 0$$

### Step 6 — Factor and Solve

$$S \cos\phi \left[ P_i + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)} - 2x^{2} P_{cu(fl)} \right] = 0$$

$$P_i - x^{2} P_{cu(fl)} = 0$$

### Step 7 — Final Result — Maximum Efficiency Condition

$$\boxed{P_i = x^{2} \, P_{cu(fl)}}$$

The load fraction at which maximum efficiency occurs:

$$\boxed{x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu(fl)}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Maximum efficiency occurs precisely when the **constant iron losses** equal the **variable copper losses** at that operating load. This is the "break-even" point where the two loss mechanisms balance.

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Flow in a Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Supply V1 f Hz] --> B[Primary Winding N1 turns]
    B --> C[Magnetic Core Mutual Flux Phi_m]
    C --> D[Secondary Winding N2 turns]
    D --> E[Induced EMF E2 = 4.44 f N2 Phi_m]
    E --> F[Load Z_L Power Factor cos phi]
    
    B --> G[Primary Copper Loss I1^2 R1]
    C --> H[Iron Core Losses Hysteresis + Eddy Current]
    D --> I[Secondary Copper Loss I2^2 R2]
    F --> J[Stray Load Losses]
    
    G --> K[Total Loss Pi + Pcu]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    
    F --> L[Useful Output Power V2 I2 cos phi]
    K --> M[Efficiency Calculator eta = Pout / Pin]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Output Efficiency Percent]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Loss Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Transformer Losses] --> B[No-Load Losses Constant]
    A --> C[Load Losses Variable]
    
    B --> B1[Hysteresis Loss W_h]
    B --> B2[Eddy Current Loss W_e]
    
    B1 --> B1a[Proportional to f]
    B1 --> B1b[Proportional to B_max^1.6]
    
    B2 --> B2a[Proportional to f^2]
    B2 --> B2b[Proportional to t^2 lamination thickness squared]
    
    C --> C1[I^2 R Copper Loss]
    C --> C2[Stray Load Loss]
    C --> C3[Dielectric Loss]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Efficiency vs Load Characteristic Curve — Functional Description

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Load Fraction x on x-axis] --> Q[Efficiency eta on y-axis]
    Q --> R[Region 1 - Low Load - Iron Loss Dominant]
    Q --> S[Region 2 - Maximum Efficiency Peak - Pi = x^2 Pcu]
    Q --> T[Region 3 - High Load - Copper Loss Dominant]
    
    R --> R1[Efficiency rises steeply]
    S --> S1[Eta max occurs at x_max = sqrt Pi by Pcu fl]
    T --> T1[Efficiency falls gradually as x increases]
    
    style P fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style S fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style S1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The **red path** in the first diagram traces the useful power flow (supply → primary → core → secondary → load). The **red loss nodes** show where energy is dissipated as heat. In a **well-designed transformer**, these loss paths should carry as little energy as possible.

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the EMF equation of a transformer and explain the significance of the constant 4.44 used in it.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **EMF equation** of a transformer is the mathematical relationship that expresses the RMS value of the voltage induced in a transformer winding due to the alternating flux linking with it. It is given by:
>
> $$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$$
>
> Where $E$ = RMS EMF in volts, $f$ = frequency in Hz, $N$ = number of turns, and $\Phi_m$ = maximum flux in Webers.
>
> **Significance of 4.44:** The constant 4.44 is the product of **4** and **1.11**. The factor 4 arises from the average EMF calculation ($E_{avg} = 4 f N \Phi_m$), and **1.11** is the **form factor** of a sinusoidal wave (ratio of RMS to average value). Together, they convert the average EMF to the **RMS EMF** which is the practically measurable quantity. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between hysteresis loss and eddy current loss in a transformer core.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hysteresis Loss ($W_h$) | Eddy Current Loss ($W_e$) |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Cause | Molecular friction in core material during magnetization reversal | Circulating currents induced in core laminations |
| Formula | $W_h = \eta B_{max}^{1.6} f V$ | $W_e = K_e B_{max}^{2} f^{2} t^{2} V$ |
| Frequency dependence | $\propto f$ | $\propto f^{2}$ |
| Reduction method | Use silicon steel with low Steinmetz coefficient | Use thin laminations with high-resistivity coating |
| Nature | Atomic-level loss | Macroscopic current loss |
| **(3 Marks)** | | |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the factor 4.44** and writing $E = N \Phi_m \omega$ directly **without converting to RMS** is a 1-mark deduction. KTU strictly expects the **RMS form** as the final answer.
> - **Confusing $E_1$ and $E_2$ with $V_1$ and $V_2$**: In an ideal transformer $E_1 = V_1$ and $E_2 = V_2$, but in a practical transformer, the difference represents voltage drops across winding resistance and leakage reactance. Writing $V_1 = 4.44 f N_1 \Phi_m$ without qualifier is **penalized by 1 mark** if the question specifies a practical transformer.
> - **For maximum efficiency problems**: Students often write $P_i = P_{cu(fl)}$, but the **correct condition is $P_i = x^2 P_{cu(fl)}$** at the load $x$ where $\eta$ is maximum. Writing the wrong version costs **3 marks** outright.
> - **Unit mistakes**: Mixing up Wb and mWb, or V and kV, can cost 0.5 to 1 mark each occurrence. Always convert units first.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**A 50 kVA, 2400/240 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 80 turns on the secondary winding. Calculate:**

**(a)** The number of turns on the primary winding, the maximum flux in the core, and the rated primary and secondary currents. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The full-load copper loss is 600 W and the iron loss is 400 W. Calculate: (i) the efficiency at full load and 0.8 power factor lagging, (ii) the efficiency at half load and unity power factor, and (iii) the load at which maximum efficiency occurs. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given Data:**
- Rating: $S = 50\,\text{kVA} = 50000\,\text{VA}$
- Primary Voltage: $V_1 = 2400\,\text{V}$
- Secondary Voltage: $V_2 = 240\,\text{V}$
- Frequency: $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$
- Secondary turns: $N_2 = 80$

**Step 1 — Calculate Transformation Ratio K:**

$$K = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{240}{2400} = 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Primary Turns $N_1$:**

$$N_1 = \frac{N_2}{K} = \frac{80}{0.1} = 800\,\text{turns}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Maximum Flux $\Phi_m$ Using EMF Equation:**

Using the secondary side: $V_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m$

$$\Phi_m = \frac{V_2}{4.44 \, f \, N_2} = \frac{240}{4.44 \times 50 \times 80}$$

$$\Phi_m = \frac{240}{17760} = 0.01351\,\text{Wb} = 13.51\,\text{mWb}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Rated Currents:**

$$I_1 = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{50000}{2400} = 20.83\,\text{A}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{50000}{240} = 208.33\,\text{A}$$

**Answer Summary (a):** $N_1 = 800$ turns, $\Phi_m = 13.51$ mWb, $I_1 = 20.83$ A, $I_2 = 208.33$ A. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Full-load copper loss: $P_{cu(fl)} = 600\,\text{W}$
- Iron loss: $P_i = 400\,\text{W}$

**Step 1 — Efficiency at Full Load and 0.8 PF Lagging:**

Output power at full load:

$$P_{out} = S \cos\phi = 50000 \times 0.8 = 40000\,\text{W}$$

Total losses = $400 + 600 = 1000\,\text{W}$

$$\eta_{fl} = \frac{40000}{40000 + 1000} \times 100 = \frac{40000}{41000} \times 100 = 97.56\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency at Half Load ($x = 0.5$) and Unity PF:**

At half load, output power = $0.5 \times 50000 \times 1.0 = 25000\,\text{W}$

Copper loss at half load = $x^2 \times P_{cu(fl)} = 0.5^2 \times 600 = 150\,\text{W}$

Total losses = $400 + 150 = 550\,\text{W}$

$$\eta_{0.5} = \frac{25000}{25000 + 550} \times 100 = \frac{25000}{25550} \times 100 = 97.85\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Load at Maximum Efficiency:**

Using the condition $P_i = x^2 \, P_{cu(fl)}$:

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu(fl)}}} = \sqrt{\frac{400}{600}} = \sqrt{0.6667} = 0.8165$$

$$\boxed{x_{max} \approx 0.816 \text{ or } 81.6\% \text{ of full load}}$$

**Answer Summary (b):** (i) $\eta_{fl} = 97.56\%$, (ii) $\eta_{0.5} = 97.85\%$, (iii) Maximum efficiency at $x_{max} = 0.816$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**A single-phase transformer has 400 primary turns and 1000 secondary turns. The primary is connected to a 2000 V, 50 Hz supply. The iron losses at this rated voltage are 500 W. The full-load secondary current is 100 A at 0.85 power factor lagging. If the total copper loss at full load is 800 W, calculate:**

**(a)** The secondary EMF, the transformation ratio, the maximum flux in the core, and the full-load efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The output kVA at which maximum efficiency occurs, the maximum efficiency at unity power factor, and the all-day efficiency if the transformer operates on the following load cycle: 4 hours at full load, 6 hours at half load (unity PF), and 14 hours at no load. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given Data:**
- $N_1 = 400$ turns, $N_2 = 1000$ turns
- $V_1 = 2000$ V, $f = 50$ Hz
- $P_i = 500$ W
- $I_{2(fl)} = 100$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ lagging
- $P_{cu(fl)} = 800$ W

**Step 1 — Transformation Ratio:**

$$K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1000}{400} = 2.5$$

This is a **step-up transformer**.

**Step 2 — Secondary EMF (assuming ideal conditions, $E_2 \approx V_2$):**

$$E_2 = K \times V_1 = 2.5 \times 2000 = 5000\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Flux in the Core:**

Using $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m$ (assuming $V_1 \approx E_1$):

$$\Phi_m = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \frac{2000}{4.44 \times 50 \times 400}$$

$$\Phi_m = \frac{2000}{88800} = 0.02252\,\text{Wb} = 22.52\,\text{mWb}$$

**Step 4 — Full-Load Efficiency:**

Full load rating: $S = V_2 \times I_2 = 5000 \times 100 = 500000\,\text{VA} = 500\,\text{kVA}$

Output at full load: $P_{out} = S \cos\phi = 500 \times 0.85 = 425\,\text{kW}$

Total losses = $P_i + P_{cu(fl)} = 500 + 800 = 1300\,\text{W} = 1.3\,\text{kW}$

$$\eta_{fl} = \frac{425}{425 + 1.3} \times 100 = \frac{425}{426.3} \times 100 = 99.69\,\%$$

**Answer Summary (a):** $E_2 = 5000$ V, $K = 2.5$, $\Phi_m = 22.52$ mWb, $\eta_{fl} = 99.69\%$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Load at Maximum Efficiency:**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu(fl)}}} = \sqrt{\frac{500}{800}} = \sqrt{0.625} = 0.7906$$

Output kVA at max efficiency:

$$S_{max\,\eta} = x_{max} \times S_{fl} = 0.7906 \times 500 = 395.3\,\text{kVA}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Efficiency at Unity Power Factor:**

At max efficiency, $P_i = x^2 P_{cu(fl)} = 500$ W. So copper loss at this load is also 500 W.

Output at unity PF:

$$P_{out} = 395.3 \times 1.0 = 395.3\,\text{kW}$$

Total losses = $500 + 500 = 1000\,\text{W} = 1\,\text{kW}$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{395.3}{395.3 + 1} \times 100 = \frac{395.3}{396.3} \times 100 = 99.75\,\%$$

**Step 3 — All-Day Efficiency:**

All-day efficiency considers energy over 24 hours:

$$\eta_{ad} = \frac{\text{Energy output in 24 hrs}}{\text{Energy input in 24 hrs}} \times 100$$

**Energy output:**
- 4 hrs at full load, 0.85 PF: $425 \times 4 = 1700\,\text{kWh}$
- 6 hrs at half load, unity PF: $(500 \times 0.5 \times 1.0) \times 6 = 1500\,\text{kWh}$
- 14 hrs at no load: $0\,\text{kWh}$
- **Total energy output = 3200 kWh**

**Energy losses (in 24 hrs):**
- Iron loss is constant (present 24 hrs): $0.5 \times 24 = 12\,\text{kWh}$
- Copper losses: $800 \times (1)^2 \times 4 + 800 \times (0.5)^2 \times 6 + 800 \times 0^2 \times 14 = 3200 + 1200 + 0 = 4400\,\text{W·hr} = 4.4\,\text{kWh}$
- **Total energy losses = 12 + 4.4 = 16.4 kWh**

$$\eta_{ad} = \frac{3200}{3200 + 16.4} \times 100 = \frac{3200}{3216.4} \times 100 = 99.49\,\%$$

**Answer Summary (b):** $S_{max\,\eta} = 395.3$ kVA, $\eta_{max} = 99.75\%$, $\eta_{all-day} = 99.49\%$. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EMF Equation, Losses and Efficiency**

- **EMF Equation:** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ — always present RMS, not peak.
- **Transformation Ratio:** $K = N_2 / N_1 = E_2 / E_1 = V_2 / V_1$ (ideal case).
- **Constant 4.44:** = $4 \times 1.11$ (form factor) — only for **pure sine waves**.
- **Primary EMF:** $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m$; **Secondary EMF:** $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m$.
- **Two types of core (iron) losses:** **Hysteresis** ($W_h \propto f$, $B_{max}^{1.6}$) and **Eddy Current** ($W_e \propto f^2$, $t^2$, $B_{max}^{2}$).
- **Iron losses are CONSTANT** (independent of load) — they depend on $V$ and $f$ only.
- **Copper losses are VARIABLE** — proportional to $I^2$, hence scale as $x^2$ of full-load value.
- **Efficiency formula (fractional load $x$):**
  $$\eta = \frac{x \, S \cos\phi}{x \, S \cos\phi + P_i + x^{2} P_{cu(fl)}}$$
- **Max Efficiency Condition:** $P_i = x^{2} \, P_{cu(fl)}$ — **NOT** $P_i = P_{cu(fl)}$ unless load = 1.
- **Load for Max Efficiency:** $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu(fl)}}$ — typically between **0.5 and 0.9** for well-designed transformers.
- **All-Day Efficiency** uses **energy (kWh) over 24 hours** instead of instantaneous power.
- **Voltage Ratio vs Transformation Ratio:** $V_1/V_2 = N_1/N_2$ (inverse), $K = N_2/N_1$ (direct).
- **Units to remember:** Flux in **Wb** (or mWb), frequency in **Hz**, EMF in **V** (or kV), area in **m²** (or cm²).
- **Reducing eddy currents:** Use **thin laminations** (0.35 mm or 0.5 mm) coated with insulating varnish.
- **Reducing hysteresis loss:** Use **silicon steel** (grain-oriented CRGO steel) with low Steinmetz coefficient.
- **For KTU problems:** Always state the **power factor** explicitly; efficiency is meaningless without it.
- **Practical range:** Well-designed power transformers have efficiency **> 98%**; distribution transformers **95-98%**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OC and SC Tests, Equivalent Circuits, Efficiency Calculations, Maximum Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** OC and SC Tests, Equivalent Circuits, Efficiency Calculations, Maximum Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Open Circuit (OC) Test — Formal Definition

The **Open Circuit Test (OC Test)**, also called the **No-Load Test**, is a low-voltage side test performed on a transformer to determine its **core (iron) losses** and the **no-load branch parameters** (shunt branch resistance $R_0$ and magnetising reactance $X_0$) of the equivalent circuit. One winding (usually the **Low-Voltage (LV) winding**) is left open-circuited, and the other winding (usually the **High-Voltage (HV) winding**) is connected to a variable AC source at its **rated voltage** and **rated frequency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LV side is not preferred for OC test?** Because the no-load current is small (typically 2–10% of rated current), and measuring this small current on the HV side would require very delicate, low-range instruments. The LV side requires lower voltage and easier to handle. Hence, the **HV side is open** and instruments are placed on the **LV (energised) side**.

## 1.2 Short Circuit (SC) Test — Formal Definition

The **Short Circuit Test (SC Test)** is a low-current side test performed to determine the **full-load copper losses** and the **equivalent series impedance** ($R_{01}$, $X_{01}$ or referred values $R_{02}'$, $X_{02}'$) of the transformer. One winding (usually the **HV winding**) is **short-circuited through an ammeter**, and the other winding (the **LV winding**) is connected to a variable AC source. The applied voltage is **gradually raised from zero** until the ammeter reads the **rated full-load current**. The applied voltage at this point is typically **5–12% of the rated voltage** and is called the **Short Circuit Voltage ($V_{sc}$)** or **impedance voltage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **SC Test is called the "Reduced Voltage Test"** because only a small fraction of rated voltage is sufficient to circulate full-load current. This is because the impedance of the transformer is small (Z ≈ 5–10% of rated).

## 1.3 Equivalent Circuit — Formal Definition

The **Equivalent Circuit of a Transformer** is a lumped-parameter electrical network that models the behaviour of a real transformer using ideal circuit elements: a **resistance** to represent copper losses, a **reactance** to represent leakage fluxes, and a **shunt branch (R₀ ∥ jX₀)** to represent the magnetising current and core losses. The equivalent circuit is fundamental for calculating **voltage regulation**, **efficiency**, and for **fault analysis**.

## 1.4 Efficiency — Formal Definition

**Efficiency ($\eta$)** of a transformer is the ratio of **output power** to **input power**, expressed as a percentage. Because a transformer has **no rotating parts**, its efficiency is generally **very high (95–99%)**. The efficiency at any loading fraction $x$ (per-unit load) and power factor $\cos\phi$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}}$$

where $S$ is the rated apparent power in kVA, $P_i$ is the iron loss (from OC test), and $P_{cu,fl}$ is the full-load copper loss (from SC test).

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **The Two Doctors Analogy for OC and SC Tests:**
> 
> Imagine a transformer is a **patient** and you are a **doctor diagnosing a disease**:
> 
> 🩺 **OC Test (The Heart Test):** You "open" the output side (like opening a vein) and let the patient "breathe" at normal voltage. You measure the **no-load current (I₀)**, **core loss (Pᵢ)**, and **power factor (cos φ₀)**. This tells you the **health of the magnetic core** — like checking the patient's heartbeat. The instruments (wattmeter, voltmeter, ammeter) on the energised side are like the **stethoscope**.
> 
> 💉 **SC Test (The Blood Test):** You "short" the output side (like clamping a vein) and inject a small voltage. The full-load current flows, and you measure **short-circuit voltage ($V_{sc}$)**, **short-circuit power ($P_{sc}$)**, and **short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$)**. This tells you the **health of the copper windings** — the resistance and leakage reactance. Like a blood test, you need only a **small stimulus** to get the **full information**.

## 1.6 Why These Tests Matter in Engineering

- They are **low-power tests** — no need to load the transformer at full rating, saving energy and avoiding the need for large load banks.
- They allow complete parameter determination for the **per-phase equivalent circuit**.
- They provide direct measurement of losses, enabling **efficiency calculation** at **any load and any power factor**.
- They are used in **transformer commissioning tests** in substations and are mandated by **IS 2026 / IEC 60076** standards.
- The OC test gives the **no-load branch parameters** ($R_0$, $X_0$), and the SC test gives the **equivalent series impedance** ($R_{01}$, $X_{01}$).
- From these, the **percentage impedance (%Z)**, **voltage regulation**, and **maximum efficiency point** are computed.

---

## 1.7 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **Rated frequency**: **50 Hz** (India, KTU syllabus standard)
- **No-load current ($I_0$)**: typically **2% to 10%** of full-load current
- **No-load power factor ($\cos\phi_0$)**: typically **0.1 to 0.3** (lagging) — highly inductive
- **Full-load copper loss ($P_{cu,fl}$)**: typically **0.5% to 2%** of rated kVA
- **Iron/Core loss ($P_i$)**: typically **0.2% to 1.5%** of rated kVA
- **Transformer efficiency range**: **95% to 99.5%**
- **%Z (percentage impedance)**: typically **4% to 12%**
- **Permeability of free space ($\mu_0$)**: **$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open Circuit (OC) Test — Procedure & Logic

### 🔧 Step-by-Step Procedure:
1. Keep the **HV winding open-circuited** (no load connected).
2. Connect the **LV winding** to a **variable autotransformer (Variac)**.
3. Connect measuring instruments on the **LV side**:
   - **Voltmeter (V)** — across the LV terminals.
   - **Ammeter (A)** — in series with the LV winding (reads $I_0$).
   - **Wattmeter (W)** — measures input power (reads $P_i$, the iron loss).
4. Gradually increase the applied voltage to **rated LV voltage** at **rated frequency**.
5. Record readings: **$V_0$, $I_0$, $P_0$ (≈ $P_i$)**.

### 📐 Working Out the No-Load Equivalent Circuit Parameters:

The no-load input power equals the **core loss** (copper loss at no-load is negligible because $I_0$ is very small):

$$P_i = V_0 \cdot I_0 \cdot \cos\phi_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_i}{V_0 \cdot I_0}$$

The no-load current has two components:
- **Working (or core-loss) component**: $I_w = I_0 \cdot \cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_i}{V_0}$
- **Magnetising component**: $\mu = I_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0$

The **shunt branch resistance** (core-loss resistance) and **magnetising reactance** are:

$$R_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{V_0^2}{P_i}$$

$$X_0 = \frac{V_0}{\mu} = \frac{V_0}{I_0 \sin\phi_0}$$

### 🔍 Why is copper loss neglected in OC test?
At no-load, the current $I_0$ is small (2–10% of full-load). Since copper loss $\propto I^2$, the no-load copper loss is $\le 1\%$ of the full-load copper loss, hence negligible. All input power goes to core loss.

---

## 2.2 Short Circuit (SC) Test — Procedure & Logic

### 🔧 Step-by-Step Procedure:
1. **Short-circuit the LV winding** through an ammeter (a thick, low-resistance link).
2. Connect the **HV winding** to a variable AC source via a Variac.
3. Connect instruments on the **HV side**:
   - **Voltmeter** — measures applied voltage ($V_{sc}$, typically 5–12% of rated).
   - **Ammeter** — reads full-load current ($I_{sc} = I_{fl}$).
   - **Wattmeter** — reads input power ($P_{sc}$ ≈ $P_{cu,fl}$, since core loss is negligible at low voltage).
4. Gradually increase voltage from zero until the ammeter reads **rated full-load current**.
5. Record: **$V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{sc}$**.

### 📐 Working Out the Equivalent Series Impedance:

The applied voltage is small, so the flux in the core is small → **core loss is negligible** (since $P_i \propto V^2$). All input power equals **full-load copper loss**:

$$P_{cu,fl} = V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot \cos\phi_{sc} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{P_{cu,fl}}{V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

The **equivalent impedance referred to the HV side**:

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{cu,fl}}{I_{sc}^2} = Z_{01} \cos\phi_{sc}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = Z_{01} \sin\phi_{sc}$$

### 🔍 Why is core loss neglected in SC test?
The applied voltage is only 5–12% of rated, so flux density is small (since $\phi \propto V/f$). Since core loss $P_i \propto B_{max}^{1.6}$ to $B_{max}^2$, it becomes negligible.

---

## 2.3 Equivalent Circuit of Transformer — Detailed Analysis

### 🔁 Exact Equivalent Circuit (Referred to Primary):

```
        R1   jX1                    R2'   jX2'
  o──/\/\/\──UUUU──┬──────────────┬──/\/\/\──UUUU──o
                   │              │
                   │     R0 ∥ jX0 │
                   └──/\/\/⌇⌇⌇/──┘
```

Where:
- $R_1, X_1$ → Primary winding resistance and leakage reactance.
- $R_2', X_2'$ → Secondary winding resistance and leakage reactance **referred to primary** ($R_2' = R_2/k^2$, where $k = N_1/N_2$).
- $R_0$ → Represents core (iron) loss.
- $X_0$ → Represents magnetising reactance.
- $I_0$ → No-load current = $\sqrt{I_w^2 + \mu^2}$.

### 📏 Approximate Equivalent Circuits (Used in KTU problems):

**Approximate Equivalent Circuit (Shift the shunt branch to the input terminals):**

$$R_{01} = R_1 + R_2' \quad ; \quad X_{01} = X_1 + X_2'$$

This is the **standard approximate equivalent circuit** where all series elements are lumped on one side.

**Further Approximation (For short-circuit calculations and efficiency):**

Sometimes, the entire shunt branch is **neglected** for SC test analysis, giving $V_{sc} \approx I_{sc} \cdot Z_{01}$.

---

## 2.4 Efficiency Calculation — The Core Formula

For a transformer delivering a load of $x$ times its full-load rating (where $x$ is the **per-unit load**, $0 \le x \le 1.5$ typically) at power factor $\cos\phi$:

**Output Power:**
$$P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi \quad \text{(where } S = \text{rated kVA, and } \cos\phi = \text{load power factor)}$$

**Losses at load fraction $x$:**
- **Iron loss** ($P_i$): **Constant** — independent of load (since voltage is constant).
- **Copper loss** at load $x$: $P_{cu} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}$ — varies with **square** of load.

**Total Losses:**
$$P_{loss} = P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}$$

**Efficiency Formula:**
$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}} \times 100\%}$$

### ⚡ All-Day Efficiency (Special Case — Distribution Transformers):

Because distribution transformers are **energised 24 hours** but supply load only for a few hours, **All-Day Efficiency (Energy Efficiency)** is used:

$$\eta_{all-day} = \frac{\text{Output energy in kWh (24 hrs)}}{\text{Output energy} + \text{Iron loss (24 hrs)} + \text{Cu losses (load hours)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For distribution transformers, **iron loss is more important** than copper loss, so they are designed with **lower iron loss** (using CRGO steel, low-flux density).

---

## 2.5 Condition for Maximum Efficiency ⚡

To find the load at which efficiency is **maximum**, differentiate $\eta$ w.r.t. $x$ and set $\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$.

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0 \implies P_i = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}$$

**The condition for maximum efficiency is:**

$$\boxed{P_i = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad x = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,fl}}}}$$

### 🎯 Key Takeaways:
- **Iron loss = Copper loss** at maximum efficiency.
- The **load at max efficiency** is $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,fl}}$ (in per-unit).
- **Maximum efficiency occurs at less than full load** (since $P_i < P_{cu,fl}$ for power transformers).
- **For distribution transformers**: $P_i > P_{cu,fl}$, so $x_{max} > 1$ — i.e., max efficiency occurs at **some load greater than rated**.

### 🌟 The Two Special Cases:

| Condition | Transformer Type | Max. Efficiency At |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| $P_i = P_{cu,fl}$ | Hypothetical | $x = 1$ (Full Load) |
| $P_i < P_{cu,fl}$ | **Power Transformer** | $x < 1$ (Below full load) |
| $P_i > P_{cu,fl}$ | **Distribution Transformer** | $x > 1$ (Above full load) |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of $\vert$ for any vertical bars in tables. Below, magnitudes are shown using absolute value bars rendered outside the cell text.**

| **Sl. No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Remarks** |
|:-----------:|:-------------|:------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | No-load power factor | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_i}{V_0 \cdot I_0}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Working component of $I_0$ | $I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_i}{V_0}$ | Amperes |
| 3 | Magnetising component of $I_0$ | $\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | Amperes |
| 4 | Core-loss resistance | $R_0 = \dfrac{V_0^2}{P_i}$ | Ohms |
| 5 | Magnetising reactance | $X_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{\mu} = \dfrac{V_0}{I_0 \sin\phi_0}$ | Ohms |
| 6 | SC test power factor | $\cos\phi_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{cu,fl}}{V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Equivalent impedance (HV side) | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$ | Ohms |
| 8 | Equivalent resistance (HV side) | $R_{01} = \dfrac{P_{cu,fl}}{I_{sc}^2}$ | Ohms |
| 9 | Equivalent reactance (HV side) | $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$ | Ohms |
| 10 | Full-load copper loss | $P_{cu,fl} = I_{fl}^2 \cdot R_{01}$ | Watts |
| 11 | Output power at load fraction $x$ | $P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi$ | Watts |
| 12 | Copper loss at load fraction $x$ | $P_{cu} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}$ | Watts |
| 13 | Total losses | $P_{loss} = P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}$ | Watts |
| 14 | Efficiency (general) | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}}$ | Per unit or % |
| 15 | Condition for max efficiency | $P_i = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}$ | — |
| 16 | Load at max efficiency | $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,fl}}$ | Per unit |
| 17 | Max efficiency value | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{S \cdot \cos\phi}{S \cdot \cos\phi + 2 P_i}$ | Per unit or % |
| 18 | %Z (Percentage impedance) | $\%Z = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 19 | Voltage regulation (approx.) | $VR = \dfrac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi \pm X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$ | Percentage |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** This entire framework is used by **transformer manufacturers** (BHEL, ABB, Siemens, Schneider Electric) to design efficient transformers, by **power utilities** (KSEB, TNEB) for substation commissioning, and by **testing engineers** for **acceptance testing** under IS 2026 and IEC 60076.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications 🏭

1. **Transformer Manufacturing:** Every transformer manufactured is subjected to OC and SC tests as part of **routine tests** in the factory.
2. **Substation Commissioning:** When a new transformer is installed in a KSEB substation, OC and SC tests are performed to verify nameplate parameters and detect winding faults.
3. **Loss Optimisation:** The condition $P_i = x^2 P_{cu,fl}$ is used by design engineers to **tailor iron loss and copper loss** for specific load profiles (e.g., transformers for industrial loads vs. residential feeders).
4. **Economic Design:** The "knee point" of efficiency determines the most economical operating load.
5. **Fault Analysis:** The %Z obtained from SC test is used to calculate **short-circuit currents** for protection relay coordination.
6. **AI-Based Monitoring:** Modern digital substations use SC test data to train **transformer health index** ML models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

### 📜 Starting Point: The General Efficiency Equation

Consider a transformer delivering load at fraction $x$ of its full-load rating, with power factor $\cos\phi$.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Output Power} + \text{Total Losses}}$$

Let $S$ be the rated apparent power in VA. Then:

$$P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi \quad \text{(Output Power in Watts)}$$

$$P_{loss} = P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl} \quad \text{(Iron + Copper Losses)}$$

### ✍️ Substituting into the Efficiency Equation

$$\eta = \frac{x S \cos\phi}{x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}}$$

For maximum efficiency, the condition is $\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$.

### 🔢 Step 1: Apply Quotient Rule for Differentiation

Let $N = x S \cos\phi$ and $D = x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}$.

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = \frac{N' \cdot D - N \cdot D'}{D^2} = 0$$

For maximum efficiency, the numerator must be zero:

$$N' \cdot D - N \cdot D' = 0$$

### 🔢 Step 2: Compute the Derivatives

$$N' = \frac{d}{dx}(x S \cos\phi) = S \cos\phi$$

$$D' = \frac{d}{dx}(x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}) = S \cos\phi + 2x P_{cu,fl}$$

### 🔢 Step 3: Substitute Back into the Condition

$$(S \cos\phi)(x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}) - (x S \cos\phi)(S \cos\phi + 2x P_{cu,fl}) = 0$$

Expand term-by-term:

$$\begin{aligned}
& S \cos\phi \cdot x S \cos\phi + S \cos\phi \cdot P_i + S \cos\phi \cdot x^2 P_{cu,fl} \\
& - x S \cos\phi \cdot S \cos\phi - x S \cos\phi \cdot 2x P_{cu,fl} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

The first and fourth terms **cancel** ($S^2 \cos^2\phi \cdot x - S^2 \cos^2\phi \cdot x = 0$):

$$S \cos\phi \cdot P_i + S \cos\phi \cdot x^2 P_{cu,fl} - 2x^2 \cdot S \cos\phi \cdot P_{cu,fl} = 0$$

### 🔢 Step 4: Simplify the Remaining Equation

Factor out $S \cos\phi$ (which is non-zero):

$$S \cos\phi \left[P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl} - 2x^2 P_{cu,fl}\right] = 0$$

$$P_i - x^2 P_{cu,fl} = 0$$

### ✅ Final Result: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

$$\boxed{P_i = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl}}$$

### 🔢 Step 5: Derivation of Load at Maximum Efficiency

Solving for $x$:

$$x^2 = \frac{P_i}{P_{cu,fl}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,fl}}}}$$

### 🔢 Step 6: Derivation of Maximum Efficiency Value

Substitute $x^2 P_{cu,fl} = P_i$ into the efficiency equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max} &= \frac{x_{max} S \cos\phi}{x_{max} S \cos\phi + P_i + P_i} \\
&= \frac{x_{max} S \cos\phi}{x_{max} S \cos\phi + 2 P_i}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $x_{max}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max} &= \frac{x_{max}^2 S \cos\phi}{x_{max}^2 S \cos\phi + 2 x_{max} P_i} \\
&= \frac{(P_i / P_{cu,fl}) \cdot S \cos\phi}{(P_i / P_{cu,fl}) \cdot S \cos\phi + 2 \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,fl}} \cdot P_i}
\end{aligned}$$

The **most common textbook form** (and KTU-expected form) is:

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{S \cos\phi}{S \cos\phi + 2 P_i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This elegant result means that **at maximum efficiency**, the **iron loss ($P_i$) equals the copper loss ($x^2 P_{cu,fl}$)**. The transformer designer's job is to make $P_i$ and $P_{cu,fl}$ such that the operating load (usually 50%–80% of full load) is close to $x_{max}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between OC Test Quantities and Shunt Branch Parameters

### 📜 Given from OC Test:
- $V_0$ = rated voltage applied to one winding.
- $I_0$ = no-load current.
- $P_i$ = no-load input power (wattmeter reading) = iron loss.

### ✍️ Step 1: Power Triangle at No-Load

At no-load, the transformer draws a small current $I_0$ lagging the applied voltage $V_0$ by angle $\phi_0$.

Real power: $P_i = V_0 I_0 \cos\phi_0$

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_i}{V_0 I_0}$$

### ✍️ Step 2: Active (Working) and Reactive (Magnetising) Components

$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_i}{V_0}$$

$$\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = I_0 \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_0}$$

### ✍️ Step 3: Shunt Branch Resistance and Reactance

The active current $I_w$ flows through $R_0$, so:

$$R_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{V_0}{P_i / V_0} = \frac{V_0^2}{P_i}$$

The magnetising current $\mu$ flows through $X_0$:

$$X_0 = \frac{V_0}{\mu} = \frac{V_0}{I_0 \sin\phi_0}$$

### ✍️ Step 4: Expressing $X_0$ in Compact Form

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin\phi_0 &= \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_0} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{P_i}{V_0 I_0}\right)^2} \\
X_0 &= \frac{V_0}{I_0 \sqrt{1 - (P_i / V_0 I_0)^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The shunt branch is **NOT purely resistive or purely reactive** — it is a **parallel combination** of $R_0$ and $X_0$. A common student error is to treat $R_0$ and $X_0$ as if they are in **series**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Series Parameters from SC Test

### 📜 Given from SC Test:
- $V_{sc}$ = applied voltage (small, typically 5–12% of rated).
- $I_{sc}$ = short-circuit current = full-load current.
- $P_{cu,fl}$ = wattmeter reading = full-load copper loss.

### ✍️ Step 1: Apparent Power in SC Test

$$S_{sc} = V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}$$

### ✍️ Step 2: Power Factor at SC Condition

$$P_{cu,fl} = V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot \cos\phi_{sc}$$

$$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{P_{cu,fl}}{V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

### ✍️ Step 3: Impedance Triangle

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

$$R_{01} = Z_{01} \cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} \cdot \frac{P_{cu,fl}}{V_{sc} I_{sc}} = \frac{P_{cu,fl}}{I_{sc}^2}$$

$$X_{01} = Z_{01} \sin\phi_{sc} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$$

### ✍️ Step 4: Approximate Splitting (Optional in KTU Problems)

Sometimes KTU asks to split $R_{01}$ into $R_1$ and $R_2'$, assuming $R_1 = R_2' = R_{01}/2$ and $X_1 = X_2' = X_{01}/2$. This is a **design assumption** based on symmetrical winding design. **However, for most numerical problems, $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ are kept lumped.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Solving a Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem

> [!TIP]
> **A Standard KTU Problem Looks Like This:**
> "A 10 kVA, 2000/200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:
> OC test (LV side): 200 V, 1.2 A, 60 W
> SC test (HV side): 80 V, 5 A, 100 W
> Calculate: (a) Equivalent circuit parameters, (b) Efficiency at full load and 0.8 p.f. lagging, (c) Load at max efficiency and the value of max efficiency."

### 🎯 Solution Strategy Matrix (Step-by-Step)

| **Step** | **Task** | **Action** | **Key Formula** |
|:--------:|:---------|:-----------|:----------------|
| 1 | Identify rated values | $S = 10000$ VA, $V_1 = 2000$ V, $V_2 = 200$ V | $I_{fl,HV} = S/V_1$, $I_{fl,LV} = S/V_2$ |
| 2 | From OC test | Extract $P_i = 60$ W | $P_i = 60$ W (constant loss) |
| 3 | Compute $R_0, X_0$ | Use OC data on LV side | $R_0 = V_0^2 / P_i$, $X_0 = V_0 / (I_0 \sin\phi_0)$ |
| 4 | From SC test | Extract $P_{cu,fl} = 100$ W | $P_{cu,fl} = 100$ W |
| 5 | Compute $Z_{01}, R_{01}, X_{01}$ | Referred to HV side | $Z_{01} = V_{sc}/I_{sc}$, etc. |
| 6 | Referred to LV (if asked) | Divide by $k^2$ | $R_{02}' = R_{01}/k^2$, $X_{02}' = X_{01}/k^2$ |
| 7 | Efficiency at FL, 0.8 p.f. | $x = 1$, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ | $\eta = (S \cos\phi)/(S \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu,fl})$ |
| 8 | Load at max efficiency | Compute $x_{max}$ | $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,fl}}$ |
| 9 | Max efficiency | Substitute back | $\eta_{max} = (x_{max} S \cos\phi)/(\ldots)$ |
| 10 | Convert to % | Multiply by 100 | $\eta\% = \eta \times 100$ |

### 📊 Detailed Numerical Walkthrough (with assumed values):

Assume the problem: **$S = 10$ kVA, $V_1/V_2 = 2000/200$ V, OC: 200 V, 1.2 A, 60 W; SC: 80 V, 5 A, 100 W.**

**Step 1: Rated Currents**
$$I_{fl,HV} = \frac{10000}{2000} = 5 \text{ A}, \quad I_{fl,LV} = \frac{10000}{200} = 50 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: OC Test Analysis (LV side)**
$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{60}{200 \times 1.2} = 0.25 \implies \phi_0 = 75.52°$$
$$\sin\phi_0 = 0.968$$
$$I_w = 1.2 \times 0.25 = 0.3 \text{ A}, \quad \mu = 1.2 \times 0.968 = 1.162 \text{ A}$$
$$R_0 = \frac{200}{0.3} = 666.67 \text{ Ω (LV side)}$$
$$X_0 = \frac{200}{1.162} = 172.12 \text{ Ω (LV side)}$$

**Step 3: SC Test Analysis (HV side)**
$$Z_{01} = \frac{80}{5} = 16 \text{ Ω (HV side)}$$
$$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{100}{80 \times 5} = 0.25 \implies \sin\phi_{sc} = 0.968$$
$$R_{01} = 16 \times 0.25 = 4 \text{ Ω (HV side)}$$
$$X_{01} = 16 \times 0.968 = 15.49 \text{ Ω (HV side)}$$

**Step 4: Efficiency at Full Load, 0.8 p.f. Lagging**
$$\eta_{fl} = \frac{10000 \times 0.8}{10000 \times 0.8 + 60 + 100} = \frac{8000}{8160} = 0.9804 = 98.04\%$$

**Step 5: Load at Maximum Efficiency**
$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,fl}}} = \sqrt{\frac{60}{100}} = 0.7746 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 6: Maximum Efficiency (at 0.8 p.f.)**
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{0.7746 \times 10000 \times 0.8}{0.7746 \times 10000 \times 0.8 + 60 + 60} = \frac{6196.8}{6316.8} = 0.9809 = 98.09\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The maximum efficiency (98.09%) is **slightly higher** than the full-load efficiency (98.04%) and occurs at **77.46% of full load**. This is the **economic operating point**.

---

## 3.5 Common Confusions in Derivations (Resolved)

### ❓ Q1: Why is $P_i$ **constant** but $P_{cu}$ varies with $x^2$?
- **$P_i$ depends on $V$ and $f$** (both rated and constant in operation). Since $V_1$ is constant in normal operation, $P_i$ is **constant**.
- **$P_{cu} = I^2 R$** depends on **load current**. If load fraction is $x$, then $I = x \cdot I_{fl}$, so $P_{cu} = x^2 I_{fl}^2 R = x^2 P_{cu,fl}$.

### ❓ Q2: Why is $P_{cu,fl}$ directly obtained from SC test?
- In SC test, the **applied voltage is small (5–12%)**, so flux is small and **iron loss is negligible**.
- The current is **rated full-load current**, so the copper loss equals **full-load copper loss**.

### ❓ Q3: Why is $R_0$ much larger than $R_{01}$?
- $R_0$ represents the **iron-loss equivalent resistance**, which is a fictitious resistor carrying only the **small core-loss current** $I_w$. To dissipate $P_i$ at voltage $V_0$, $R_0$ must be **large**.
- $R_{01}$ represents the **actual winding resistance** (in copper, low resistivity), hence small.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Efficiency vs Load Characteristic Curve (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Light Load x small"] --> B["Pcu Negligible"]
    B --> C["P_i Dominates"]
    C --> D["Low Efficiency"]
    E["x increases"] --> F["Pcu grows as x squared"]
    F --> G["P_i = P_cu at x max"]
    G --> H["PEAK EFFICIENCY"]
    H --> I["Further increase in x"]
    I --> J["Pcu Dominates x squared"]
    J --> K["Efficiency falls"]
    
    style H fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
    style A fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style K fill:#D0021B,color:#fff
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Curve:**
> - At **light load**: $P_{cu}$ is small, $P_i$ dominates → low efficiency.
> - As **load increases**: $P_{cu}$ rises as $x^2$, $P_i$ remains constant.
> - At **$x = x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i/P_{cu,fl}}$**: $P_{cu} = P_i$ → **maximum efficiency**.
> - **Beyond** $x_{max}$: $P_{cu} > P_i$ → efficiency falls again (though the transformer can still operate up to 150% rated load for short durations).

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Block-Level Functional Architecture of OC + SC Test → Equivalent Circuit → Efficiency

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Transformer Under Test"] --> B{"Which Test?"}
    B -->|"OC Test (No-Load)"| C["Measure V0, I0, Pi"]
    B -->|"SC Test (Short Circuit)"| D["Measure Vsc, Isc, Pcu fl"]
    
    C --> E["Compute R0 and X0"]
    C --> F["Iron Loss Pi CONSTANT"]
    
    D --> G["Compute Z01, R01, X01"]
    D --> H["Full Load Copper Loss Pcu fl"]
    
    E --> I["COMPLETE EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT"]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    
    I --> J["Compute Voltage Regulation"]
    I --> K["Compute Efficiency at any load and p.f."]
    
    K --> L{"Find Maximum"}
    L --> M["x max = sqrt of Pi over Pcu fl"]
    L --> N["Eta max = S cos phi over S cos phi + 2 Pi"]
    
    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style B fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style I fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
    style M fill:#D0021B,color:#fff
    style N fill:#D0021B,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Power of These Two Tests:** Notice how just **two simple, low-power tests** completely characterise the transformer for all operating conditions — efficiency, regulation, heating, fault currents. This is why OC and SC tests are the **backbone of transformer engineering**.

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6: Phasor Diagram at No-Load (OC Condition)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["V0 Reference Phasor along +X axis"] --> B["I0 lagging by phi0"]
    B --> C["Iw in phase with V0 Active component"]
    B --> D["I_mu perpendicular to V0 Magnetising component"]
    
    C --> E["Power Pi = V0 times Iw"]
    D --> F["Reactive Power Q mu = V0 times I_mu"]
    
    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style B fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style C fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
    style D fill:#9013FE,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Phasor Interpretation:** $I_w$ is the in-phase (active) component causing core loss; $I_\mu$ is the quadrature (magnetising) component 90° behind $V_0$, responsible for creating the magnetic flux in the core.

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram 7: Power Flow in a Transformer (Efficiency Foundation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input Power Pin"] --> B["Iron Loss Pi CONSTANT"]
    A --> C["Output Power Pout"]
    
    B --> D["Heat in Core"]
    C --> E["Useful Output to Load"]
    
    C --> F["Copper Loss Pcu = I2R"]
    F --> G["Heat in Windings"]
    
    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style C fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
    style B fill:#D0021B,color:#fff
    style F fill:#D0021B,color:#fff
    style E fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
```

> [!TIP]
> **Efficiency = Useful Output / (Useful Output + All Losses)**. The two tests (OC and SC) precisely quantify **all** the losses without needing to load the transformer to its full rating.
