# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks equivalent)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are typically 3-mark short-answer questions. They test definitions, key formulas, or short derivations.

---

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**"Why is the Open Circuit Test conducted on the Low Voltage (LV) side of a transformer and the Short Circuit Test on the High Voltage (HV) side?"** (3 Marks)

#### ✅ Model Answer:
The Open Circuit Test is performed on the **LV side** because the **no-load current ($I_0$) is very small (2–10% of rated)**. Measuring such a small current on the LV side is convenient using standard low-range ammeters and wattmeters. The voltage required is also the rated LV voltage, which is safe and easily available.

The Short Circuit Test is performed on the **HV side** because the **rated full-load current on the HV side is smaller** (since $I = S/V$, higher voltage → smaller current). Standard ammeters can easily measure this smaller current. Also, the short-circuit voltage is only 5–12% of rated HV voltage, which is again safe to handle.

In summary, **OC test is on LV side for instrument convenience and safety**, while **SC test is on HV side for instrument range suitability and low applied voltage**.

---

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
**"Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a transformer."** (3 Marks)

#### ✅ Model Answer:
The efficiency of a transformer at load fraction $x$ and power factor $\cos\phi$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{x S \cos\phi}{x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}}$$

Differentiating $\eta$ with respect to $x$ and setting $\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$ for maximum efficiency:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = \frac{(S\cos\phi)(xS\cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}) - (xS\cos\phi)(S\cos\phi + 2x P_{cu,fl})}{(\ldots)^2} = 0$$

Simplifying (after cancellation of common terms):

$$P_i = x^2 P_{cu,fl}$$

**Therefore, the condition for maximum efficiency is: Iron Loss = Copper Loss at that load.**

The load at maximum efficiency is $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,fl}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions (14 marks) come with **internal choice**. The student must answer **one full question** (with sub-parts). Below are **TWO fully-worked alternative questions** for full preparation.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**A 50 kVA, 4400/220 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer gave the following test results:**

| **Test** | **Position of Instruments** | **Readings** |
|----------|------------------------------|--------------|
| OC Test  | LV side (energised), HV open | 220 V, 12 A, 480 W |
| SC Test  | HV side (energised), LV shorted | 180 V, 11.36 A, 1200 W |

**Calculate:**
**(a)** (7 marks) The parameters of the approximate equivalent circuit referred to:
   (i) HV side, and
   (ii) LV side.

**(b)** (7 marks) (i) The efficiency at 75% of full load at 0.85 p.f. lagging.
   (ii) The load at which maximum efficiency occurs, and the maximum efficiency at 0.85 p.f. lagging.

---

#### ✅ Detailed Model Solution:

**Step 1: Identify rated quantities.**
- $S = 50$ kVA = 50000 VA
- Transformation ratio $k = V_1 / V_2 = 4400 / 220 = 20$
- $I_{fl,HV} = S/V_1 = 50000/4400 = 11.36$ A ✓ (matches SC test)
- $I_{fl,LV} = S/V_2 = 50000/220 = 227.27$ A

**Step 2: From OC Test (LV side) — $V_0 = 220$ V, $I_0 = 12$ A, $P_i = 480$ W**

No-load power factor:
$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_i}{V_0 I_0} = \frac{480}{220 \times 12} = \frac{480}{2640} = 0.1818$$
$$\sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.1818^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.0331} = 0.9833$$

Working component of no-load current:
$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 12 \times 0.1818 = 2.182 \text{ A}$$

Magnetising component:
$$\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = 12 \times 0.9833 = 11.80 \text{ A}$$

Shunt branch parameters (referred to LV side):
$$R_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{220}{2.182} = 100.83 \text{ Ω (LV side)}$$
$$X_0 = \frac{V_0}{\mu} = \frac{220}{11.80} = 18.64 \text{ Ω (LV side)}$$

**Step 3: From SC Test (HV side) — $V_{sc} = 180$ V, $I_{sc} = 11.36$ A, $P_{cu,fl} = 1200$ W**

Equivalent impedance (HV side):
$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{180}{11.36} = 15.85 \text{ Ω}$$

Equivalent resistance (HV side):
$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{cu,fl}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{1200}{11.36^2} = \frac{1200}{129.05} = 9.30 \text{ Ω}$$

Equivalent reactance (HV side):
$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = \sqrt{15.85^2 - 9.30^2} = \sqrt{251.22 - 86.49} = \sqrt{164.73} = 12.84 \text{ Ω}$$

**(a-i) Equivalent circuit parameters referred to HV side:**
- $R_{01} = 9.30$ Ω, $X_{01} = 12.84$ Ω
- $R_0' = R_0 \times k^2 = 100.83 \times 400 = 40333$ Ω (HV side)
- $X_0' = X_0 \times k^2 = 18.64 \times 400 = 7456$ Ω (HV side)

**(a-ii) Equivalent circuit parameters referred to LV side:**
- $R_{02}' = R_{01} / k^2 = 9.30 / 400 = 0.02325$ Ω
- $X_{02}' = X_{01} / k^2 = 12.84 / 400 = 0.0321$ Ω
- $R_0 = 100.83$ Ω, $X_0 = 18.64$ Ω (already on LV side)

---

**Step 4: Efficiency at 75% full load, 0.85 p.f. lagging.**

Output power at $x = 0.75$:
$$P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi = 0.75 \times 50000 \times 0.85 = 31875 \text{ W}$$

Copper loss at $x = 0.75$:
$$P_{cu} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl} = 0.75^2 \times 1200 = 0.5625 \times 1200 = 675 \text{ W}$$

Iron loss (constant):
$$P_i = 480 \text{ W}$$

Total losses:
$$P_{loss} = P_i + P_{cu} = 480 + 675 = 1155 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = \frac{31875}{31875 + 1155} = \frac{31875}{33030} = 0.9650 = \mathbf{96.50\%}$$

---

**Step 5: Load at maximum efficiency.**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,fl}}} = \sqrt{\frac{480}{1200}} = \sqrt{0.4} = 0.6325 \text{ p.u.}$$

So maximum efficiency occurs at **63.25% of full load** (i.e., at 31.6 kVA load).

**Step 6: Maximum efficiency at 0.85 p.f. lagging.**

Output power at $x_{max}$:
$$P_{out,max} = 0.6325 \times 50000 \times 0.85 = 26881.25 \text{ W}$$

Total losses at $x_{max}$: Since $x_{max}^2 \cdot P_{cu,fl} = P_i$, total losses = $P_i + P_i = 2 P_i = 960$ W.

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{26881.25}{26881.25 + 960} = \frac{26881.25}{27841.25} = 0.9655 = \mathbf{96.55\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice:** The maximum efficiency (96.55%) is **slightly higher** than the 75% load efficiency (96.50%), and **lower** than what would be obtained at the exact $x_{max}$. This illustrates that the maximum efficiency point is **distinct** from arbitrary loading points.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**A 100 kVA, 3300/220 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has the following test data:**

| **Test** | **Instrument Side** | **Voltmeter** | **Ammeter** | **Wattmeter** |
|----------|---------------------|---------------|-------------|---------------|
| OC Test  | LV side             | 220 V         | 40 A        | 800 W         |
| SC Test  | HV side             | 124 V         | 30.3 A      | 1800 W        |

**Calculate:**
**(a)** (7 marks) (i) The efficiency at full load at unity power factor and at 0.8 p.f. lagging.
   (ii) The percentage impedance of the transformer.

**(b)** (7 marks) (i) The all-day efficiency if the transformer is loaded as follows:
   - Full load for 6 hours
   - Half load for 8 hours
   - No load for 10 hours
   (Power factor = 0.9 lagging throughout, and the all-day efficiency is to be computed at this p.f.)

   (ii) The maximum efficiency of the transformer.

---

#### ✅ Detailed Model Solution:

**Step 1: Rated quantities.**
- $S = 100$ kVA = 100000 VA
- $k = 3300/220 = 15$
- $I_{fl,HV} = 100000/3300 = 30.30$ A ✓ (matches SC test)
- $I_{fl,LV} = 100000/220 = 454.5$ A

**Step 2: From OC Test:** $P_i = 800$ W (iron loss, constant).

**Step 3: From SC Test:** $P_{cu,fl} = 1800$ W (full-load copper loss).

---

**(a-i) Efficiency at Full Load:**

**At Unity Power Factor ($\cos\phi = 1.0$):**
$$P_{out} = 100000 \times 1.0 = 100000 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{loss} = P_i + P_{cu,fl} = 800 + 1800 = 2600 \text{ W}$$
$$\eta_{fl,1.0} = \frac{100000}{100000 + 2600} = \frac{100000}{102600} = 0.9747 = \mathbf{97.47\%}$$

**At 0.8 p.f. Lagging:**
$$P_{out} = 100000 \times 0.8 = 80000 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{loss} = 800 + 1800 = 2600 \text{ W} \text{ (same)}$$
$$\eta_{fl,0.8} = \frac{80000}{80000 + 2600} = \frac{80000}{82600} = 0.9685 = \mathbf{96.85\%}$$

**(a-ii) Percentage Impedance:**

$$\%Z = \frac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated,HV}} \times 100 = \frac{124}{3300} \times 100 = 3.76\%$$

Equivalently, $Z_{01} = 124/30.3 = 4.09$ Ω, and rated HV impedance $= V_1/I_1 = 3300/30.3 = 108.91$ Ω, so $\%Z = (4.09/108.91) \times 100 = 3.76\%$.

---

**(b-i) All-Day Efficiency:**

For all-day efficiency, we need **output energy** in 24 hours and **total loss energy** in 24 hours.

| **Period** | **Duration** | **Load** | **Output kW** | **Output Energy (kWh)** | **Cu Loss kW** | **Cu Loss Energy (kWh)** | **Iron Loss kW** | **Iron Loss Energy (kWh)** |
|:----------:|:------------:|:---------|:--------------|:------------------------|:---------------|:-------------------------|:-----------------|:---------------------------|
| 1          | 6 hours      | Full (x=1) | $100 \times 0.9 = 90$ | $90 \times 6 = 540$ | $1.8$ | $1.8 \times 6 = 10.8$ | $0.8$ | $0.8 \times 6 = 4.8$ |
| 2          | 8 hours      | Half (x=0.5) | $50 \times 0.9 = 45$ | $45 \times 8 = 360$ | $0.5^2 \times 1.8 = 0.45$ | $0.45 \times 8 = 3.6$ | $0.8$ | $0.8 \times 8 = 6.4$ |
| 3          | 10 hours     | No load (x=0) | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0.8$ | $0.8 \times 10 = 8.0$ |
| **Total**  | **24 hours** | —         | —             | **900 kWh**             | —              | **14.4 kWh**              | —                | **19.2 kWh**                |

Total output energy (24 hrs):
$$E_{out} = 540 + 360 + 0 = 900 \text{ kWh}$$

Total copper loss energy (24 hrs):
$$E_{cu} = 10.8 + 3.6 + 0 = 14.4 \text{ kWh}$$

Total iron loss energy (24 hrs) — note iron loss occurs **24 hours**:
$$E_i = (0.8 \text{ kW}) \times 24 \text{ hrs} = 19.2 \text{ kWh}$$

All-day efficiency:
$$\eta_{all-day} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{out} + E_{cu} + E_i} = \frac{900}{900 + 14.4 + 19.2} = \frac{900}{933.6} = 0.9639 = \mathbf{96.39\%}$$

---

**(b-ii) Maximum Efficiency:**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,fl}}} = \sqrt{\frac{800}{1800}} = \sqrt{0.4444} = 0.6667 \text{ p.u.}$$

So max efficiency occurs at **66.67% of full load** (i.e., 66.67 kVA).

At 0.9 p.f. lagging:
$$P_{out,max} = 0.6667 \times 100000 \times 0.9 = 60000 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{loss,max} = 2 P_i = 2 \times 800 = 1600 \text{ W}$$
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{60000}{60000 + 1600} = \frac{60000}{61600} = 0.9740 = \mathbf{97.40\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this type of question:**
> 
> 1. **Confusion between "load at max efficiency" and "max efficiency value"**: These are TWO different quantities. The load is $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i/P_{cu,fl}}$ (a per-unit value), and the efficiency value is computed at THAT load. Don't mix them up.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to include iron loss in all-day efficiency for 24 hours**: Iron loss occurs **continuously** for all 24 hours, NOT just when load is connected. Many students compute it only for the loading hours, which is WRONG. (e.g., if loading is 14 hours, iron loss energy = $P_i \times 24$, not $P_i \times 14$.)
> 
> 3. **Using $x$ instead of $x^2$ for copper loss**: Copper loss is proportional to $I^2$, hence to $x^2$, not $x$. This is the **most common** error in efficiency problems.
> 
> 4. **Not converting units properly**: SC test wattmeter reading $P_{cu,fl}$ is in Watts, and rated kVA must be in VA. If $S = 50$ kVA = 50000 VA, use it as 50000, NOT 50.
> 
> 5. **Confusing which side is referred**: If instruments are on HV side, $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ are referred to HV side. To get LV side values, divide by $k^2$ (or multiply by $(1/k)^2$).
> 
> 6. **Ignoring the power factor in efficiency calculation**: The output is $x S \cos\phi$, NOT $x S$. Always include the power factor in the numerator.
> 
> 7. **In all-day efficiency, energy units must be kWh (or Wh)**: Be consistent with units across the calculation. A common error is mixing kW and kWh.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!TIP]
> ### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist
> 
> ✅ **OC Test (No-Load Test):**
>    - LV side energised, HV side open.
>    - Measures $V_0$, $I_0$, $P_i$.
>    - Determines **iron loss** ($P_i$ = constant) and **shunt branch parameters** $R_0$, $X_0$.
>    - At no-load, copper loss is **negligible** (since $I_0$ is small).
> 
> ✅ **SC Test (Short-Circuit Test):**
>    - HV side energised, LV side shorted (with ammeter).
>    - Measures $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{cu,fl}$.
>    - Determines **full-load copper loss** ($P_{cu,fl}$) and **equivalent series impedance** $R_{01}$, $X_{01}$.
>    - At SC, iron loss is **negligible** (since applied voltage is small, 5–12%).
> 
> ✅ **Equivalent Circuit Parameters:**
>    - **Series branch (referred to HV)**: $R_{01} = P_{cu,fl}/I_{sc}^2$, $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$.
>    - **Shunt branch (referred to LV)**: $R_0 = V_0^2/P_i$, $X_0 = V_0/(I_0 \sin\phi_0)$.
>    - To refer HV-side parameters to LV side: divide by $k^2$.
>    - To refer LV-side parameters to HV side: multiply by $k^2$.
> 
> ✅ **Efficiency Formula:**
>    $$\eta = \frac{x S \cos\phi}{x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}}$$
>    - Output depends on $x$ and $\cos\phi$.
>    - Iron loss $P_i$ is **constant**.
>    - Copper loss = $x^2 P_{cu,fl}$ (varies with square of load).
> 
> ✅ **Condition for Maximum Efficiency:**
>    $$P_i = x^2 P_{cu,fl} \quad \text{or} \quad x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,fl}}}$$
>    - **Power transformer** → $P_i < P_{cu,fl}$ → $x_{max} < 1$ (max eff. below full load).
>    - **Distribution transformer** → $P_i > P_{cu,fl}$ → $x_{max} > 1$ (max eff. above full load).
> 
> ✅ **Maximum Efficiency Value:**
>    $$\eta_{max} = \frac{S \cos\phi}{S \cos\phi + 2P_i}$$
>    (at the load $x_{max}$)
> 
> ✅ **Percentage Impedance:**
>    $$\%Z = \frac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$$
>    - Typical values: 4–12%.
>    - Used in **fault level calculations** and **parallel operation** of transformers.
> 
> ✅ **All-Day Efficiency:**
>    - Used for **distribution transformers** (energised 24 hrs, loaded for fewer hrs).
>    - $\eta_{all-day} = \dfrac{\text{kWh output (24 hrs)}}{\text{kWh output} + \text{kWh iron loss (24 hrs)} + \text{kWh Cu loss (loaded hrs)}}$
> 
> ✅ **KTU-Favourite Numerical Patterns:**
>    - "Calculate equivalent circuit parameters, efficiency, and max efficiency" — appears in almost every KTU exam.
>    - "Find the load at which max efficiency occurs" — a 7-mark or 14-mark favourite.
>    - "Compute all-day efficiency with given load schedule" — a typical 14-mark question.
>    - "Why are OC and SC tests done on different sides?" — a 3-mark conceptual favourite.
> 
> ✅ **Sanity Checks Before Finalising an Answer:**
>    - For a 50 kVA transformer, full-load current at 4400 V side ≈ 11.36 A — **verify with given SC test current**.
>    - SC test voltage should be **5–12% of rated HV voltage** (e.g., 180 V for 4400 V → 4.1% — borderline acceptable).
>    - Iron loss $P_i$ is in **Watts** (W), typically 0.5–2% of rated kVA in Watts.
>    - Efficiency should be **between 95% and 99%** for any well-designed transformer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: All Day Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** All Day Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All-Day Efficiency (η_all-day)** of a transformer is defined as the ratio of the **total energy (kWh) output** delivered by the transformer over a **24-hour period** to the **total energy (kWh) input** supplied to the transformer during the same 24-hour period.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \frac{\text{Output Energy in kWh (over 24 hrs)}}{\text{Input Energy in kWh (over 24 hrs)}} \times 100\%$$

It is also called **Energy Efficiency** or **Commercial Efficiency** of a transformer, as it reflects the actual commercial/operational performance over an entire day, rather than at a single loading instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "All-Day"?** Distribution transformers remain connected to the line 24 hours a day, but they carry their rated load only for a few hours. Hence, the iron (core) losses occur continuously, while the copper losses occur only during loaded hours. All-day efficiency accounts for this time-varying load cycle.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a delivery truck (your **distribution transformer**) parked at a depot for 24 hours. The truck is **always idling** (this idling represents the constant **iron/core losses**). However, the truck only carries actual parcels for, say, 6 hours a day (this represents the **variable copper losses** based on load).

- **Conventional Efficiency** = How much fuel the truck uses *while it is actively delivering parcels* (instantaneous performance).
- **All-Day Efficiency** = How much fuel the truck uses *over the entire 24 hours*, including the long idle periods when no parcels are being carried.

A truck that burns too much fuel while idling (high iron losses) is inefficient overall — even if its mileage during active delivery is excellent. Similarly, a transformer with low copper losses but high iron losses will have a poor all-day efficiency because it wastes energy 24/7.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a transformer to have **high all-day efficiency**, its **iron (core) losses** must be kept as low as possible, because these losses occur continuously regardless of whether the transformer is loaded or not.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Daily Period:** **24 hours (86,400 seconds)**
- **Iron Loss (P_i):** Occurs continuously, present **24 hours/day**
- **Copper Loss (P_cu):** Variable, depends on the **load cycle**
- **Units of Energy:** **kWh (kilowatt-hours)** — NOT kW
- **Typical Load Cycle Notation:** Fraction of rated load × duration in hours (e.g., full load for 6 hrs, half load for 8 hrs, no load for 10 hrs)

---

### 1.4 Why All-Day Efficiency Matters in Engineering

| Application Context | Importance |
|---|---|
| **Distribution Transformers** | These are ON 24/7 but partially loaded → All-day efficiency is the real metric |
| **Power Utilities** | Determines revenue vs. loss over the billing cycle |
| **Energy Auditing** | Used in DISCOM (Distribution Company) performance benchmarks |
| **Transformer Sizing** | Helps select transformers for cyclic industrial loads |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why Standard Efficiency Fails Here

Standard (instantaneous) efficiency of a transformer at any moment is:

$$\eta_{\text{std}} = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Output Power} + P_i + P_{cu}}$$

where $P_i$ = iron loss and $P_{cu}$ = copper loss at that load.

**The Problem:** A distribution transformer is rarely at full load 24 hours. It might be:
- Fully loaded for 6 hours (morning peak)
- Half loaded for 8 hours (afternoon)
- Unloaded for 10 hours (night, no load)

Standard efficiency computed at "full load" is **misleading** for such a transformer.

**The Solution:** Integrate the losses over 24 hours and compare total energy output to total energy input.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Identify the Load Cycle:** Divide the 24-hour day into $n$ discrete time intervals.
2. **Determine Load Fraction $x$ in each interval:** where $x = \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{Rated Load}}$.
3. **Compute Output Energy:** $\sum (x \times S_{\text{rated}}) \times t_{\text{hours}}$ in kWh.
4. **Compute Iron Loss Energy:** $P_i \times 24$ kWh (constant throughout).
5. **Compute Copper Loss Energy:** $\sum (x^2 \times P_{cu,\text{full}}) \times t_{\text{hours}}$ kWh.
6. **Apply the Formula:** $\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{out}} + W_{\text{iron}} + W_{\text{cu}}}$

---

### 2.3 The Core "Why" — Iron vs. Copper Loss Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** For a transformer operating on a varying load cycle, the condition for **maximum all-day efficiency** is:
> 
> $$\boxed{P_i = P_{cu(\text{weighted avg over 24 hrs})}}$$
> 
> i.e., the **iron loss should equal the average copper loss** over the day.

This is fundamentally different from conventional efficiency, where maximum efficiency occurs when:

$$P_i = P_{cu,\text{full load}}$$

**Why the difference?** In conventional efficiency, we look at a *fixed load* and find the point where the two losses match. In all-day efficiency, the copper loss is *averaged* over a variable load cycle, so the equality point shifts.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{out (24h)}}}{W_{\text{out (24h)}} + W_{i} + W_{cu}}$ kWh | Main all-day efficiency formula | % |
| 2 | $W_{\text{out (24h)}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot t_k$ | Total output energy over 24 hrs | kWh |
| 3 | $W_i = P_i \times 24$ | Iron loss energy (continuous) | kWh |
| 4 | $W_{cu} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{fl}} \cdot t_k$ | Copper loss energy (load-dependent) | kWh |
| 5 | $P_{cu} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{fl}}$ | Copper loss at fraction $x$ of full load | kW |
| 6 | $P_{i} = \text{constant} = P_{\text{no-load loss}}$ | Iron loss ≈ No-load loss (constant) | kW |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{all-day, max}}$ when $P_i = P_{cu,\text{avg (24h)}}$ | Condition for maximum all-day efficiency | — |
| 8 | $x_{\text{max all-day}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{cu,\text{fl}}}} \cdot \text{(cycle factor)}$ | Load fraction for max all-day efficiency | p.u. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** In all-day efficiency, $P_i$ is multiplied by **24 hours** (always ON), while $P_{cu}$ is multiplied by only the **loaded hours** at appropriate load fractions.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **DISCOM Tariff Calculations:** Utilities use all-day efficiency to assess transformer health.
- **Smart Grid Design:** Helps decide between multiple small transformers vs. one large transformer.
- **Renewable Integration:** Solar/peak-load distribution networks depend heavily on this metric.
- **Industrial Substations:** For plants with shift-based loads (e.g., 3-shift operation), all-day efficiency guides transformer selection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 General Derivation of the All-Day Efficiency Formula

Let us consider a transformer operating on a variable load over 24 hours.

**Step 1 — Let the load cycle be described:**

Suppose the transformer delivers a load fraction $x_1$ of its rated kVA for $t_1$ hours, $x_2$ for $t_2$ hours, ..., $x_n$ for $t_n$ hours, such that:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} t_k = 24 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Express the output energy (in kWh) over 24 hours:**

If the rated apparent power is $S_{\text{rated}}$ in kVA and the power factor is $\cos\phi$ (assumed constant for simplicity, or specified per interval):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{out (24h)}} &= \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big(x_k \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi_k \cdot t_k\big) \quad \text{[kWh]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> If the power factor is constant at $\cos\phi$ throughout the day:
> $$W_{\text{out (24h)}} = S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k \cdot t_k$$

**Step 3 — Iron loss energy (continuous for 24 hours):**

The iron loss $P_i$ is constant because the primary voltage and frequency are essentially constant:

$$W_i = P_i \times 24 \quad \text{[kWh]}$$

**Step 4 — Copper loss energy (load-dependent):**

At a load fraction $x_k$, the copper loss is $x_k^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{fl}}$ (where $P_{cu,\text{fl}}$ is the full-load copper loss). Therefore:

$$W_{cu} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big(x_k^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{fl}} \cdot t_k\big) \quad \text{[kWh]}$$

**Step 5 — Total energy input over 24 hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{in (24h)}} &= W_{\text{out (24h)}} + W_i + W_{cu} \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi_k \cdot t_k + 24 P_i + P_{cu,\text{fl}} \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k^2 \cdot t_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — All-day efficiency formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{all-day}} &= \frac{W_{\text{out (24h)}}}{W_{\text{in (24h)}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{\sum x_k S_{\text{rated}} \cos\phi_k t_k}{\sum x_k S_{\text{rated}} \cos\phi_k t_k + 24 P_i + P_{cu,\text{fl}} \sum x_k^2 t_k} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master formula** that the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce and apply.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> **Worked Example (KTU-Style Problem):**
> A **15 kVA**, **2300/230 V** distribution transformer has the following load cycle:
> - Full load at unity power factor for **6 hours**
> - Half load at 0.8 p.f. for **8 hours**
> - No load (negligible) for the remaining **10 hours**
> 
> Given: Iron loss $P_i = 80$ W, Full-load copper loss $P_{cu,\text{fl}} = 120$ W.
> 
> Calculate the **all-day efficiency**.

**Step 1 — Output Energy in kWh:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{out}} &= (15 \times 1.0 \times 1 \times 6) + (15 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 \times 8) + (0) \\
&= 90 + 48 + 0 \\
&= 138 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* First term = full load × unity p.f. × 6 hrs. Second term = 0.5 load × 0.8 p.f. × 8 hrs. Third term = 0 (no load).

**Step 2 — Iron Loss Energy in kWh (24 hours):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_i &= P_i \times 24 \\
&= 80 \times 24 \\
&= 1920 \text{ Wh} = 1.92 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Iron loss is present for all 24 hours irrespective of load. We convert W·h to kWh by dividing by 1000.

**Step 3 — Copper Loss Energy in kWh:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cu} &= (1^2 \times 120 \times 6) + (0.5^2 \times 120 \times 8) + (0) \\
&= (1 \times 720) + (0.25 \times 960) \\
&= 720 + 240 \\
&= 960 \text{ Wh} = 0.96 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* At full load, $P_{cu} = P_{cu,\text{fl}}$. At half load, $P_{cu} = (0.5)^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{fl}} = 0.25 P_{cu,\text{fl}}$. Multiply by respective hours.

**Step 4 — Apply the Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{all-day}} &= \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{out}} + W_i + W_{cu}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{138}{138 + 1.92 + 0.96} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{138}{140.88} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 97.96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: All-day efficiency ≈ 97.96 %**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Conventional vs. All-Day Efficiency

| Parameter | Conventional Efficiency (at full load) | All-Day Efficiency (over 24 hrs) |
|---|---|---|
| **Time basis** | Instantaneous (snapshot) | 24 hours (integrated) |
| **Output metric** | Power (kW) | Energy (kWh) |
| **Iron loss weighting** | × 1 hour | × 24 hours |
| **Copper loss weighting** | × 1 hour at given load | × actual hours at varying loads |
| **Best suited for** | Power transformers (industrial, constant load) | Distribution transformers (cyclic load) |
| **Condition for max** | $P_i = P_{cu,\text{fl}}$ | $P_i = P_{cu,\text{weighted avg}}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always express the answer in **percentage** with **2 decimal places**. Mismatch in units (kW vs kWh) is the most common mistake.

---

### 3.4 Edge Case Analysis

**Case 1: Transformer ON 24 hrs but NO load at all**
- $W_{\text{out}} = 0$
- $\eta_{\text{all-day}} = 0\%$ (no useful energy delivered)
- Iron losses still consume energy → represents pure loss.

**Case 2: Transformer at constant full load 24 hrs**
- Conventional efficiency = All-day efficiency
- Both iron and copper losses are weighted equally for 24 hrs.

**Case 3: Heavy cyclic load (e.g., industrial 3-shift)**
- Copper losses dominate → $W_{cu} \gg W_i$
- All-day efficiency < conventional full-load efficiency (since iron is paid for 24 hrs).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — All-Day Efficiency Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Load Cycle Input"] --> B["Parse Load Profile over 24 Hours"]
    B --> C["Identify N Load Intervals"]
    C --> D["For Each Interval k: Extract xk and tk and pfk"]
    D --> E["Compute Output Energy Sum xk x Sr x pfk x tk"]
    D --> F["Compute Copper Loss Energy Sum xk Squared x Pcu fl x tk"]
    C --> G["Compute Iron Loss Energy Pi x 24"]
    E --> H["Aggregate Wout + Wi + Wcu"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Calculate All Day Efficiency Wout over Total x 100"]
    I --> J["Check Condition Pi equals Pcu avg for Max"]
    J --> K["Output Result and Recommendation"]
    K --> L["End"]
```

**Explanation of the Flow:**
- **A → B:** The user (or system) inputs the load cycle (kVA values + durations).
- **B → C:** The 24-hour window is broken into $N$ discrete load intervals.
- **C → D:** For each interval, we extract the load fraction $x_k$, duration $t_k$, and power factor.
- **D → E / F:** Two parallel computations — output energy and copper loss energy.
- **C → G:** Iron loss is computed independently (constant × 24).
- **H → I → J:** All three energies are aggregated, efficiency is computed, and the condition for maximum efficiency is checked.
- **K → L:** Final result is reported with optimization advice.

---

### 4.2 Visual Concept Map — Loss Weighting Over 24 Hours

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Day["24-Hour Day"]
        H1["Full Load: 6 hrs"] --- H2["Half Load: 8 hrs"] --- H3["No Load: 10 hrs"]
    end
    H1 -->|Iron Loss Constant| IL["Pi x 24 hrs Total Iron Loss"]
    H2 -->|Iron Loss Constant| IL
    H3 -->|Iron Loss Constant| IL
    H1 -->|Copper Loss x Squared| CL["Pcu fl x 6 hrs Full Load Cu Loss"]
    H2 -->|Copper Loss 0.25x| CL2["0.25 Pcu fl x 8 hrs Half Load Cu Loss"]
    H3 -->|Zero Cu Loss| CL3["Zero Copper Loss"]
    CL --> ADE["All Day Efficiency Computation"]
    CL2 --> ADE
    CL3 --> ADE
    IL --> ADE
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **iron loss arrow** flows to ALL three time blocks (24-hour coverage).
- The **copper loss arrows** are weighted by the square of the load fraction and only flow to loaded hours.
- The aggregation block represents the final efficiency formula.

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Use Which Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Is transformer loaded 24/7 at near-constant load?"] -->|Yes| A["Use Conventional Efficiency"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2["Does load vary significantly over 24 hours?"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| B["Use All Day Efficiency"]
    Q2 -->|No| C["Either method is acceptable"]
    B --> D["Compute Energy Output kWh over 24 hrs"]
    D --> E["Add Iron Loss Energy Pi x 24"]
    E --> F["Add Weighted Copper Loss Energy"]
    F --> G["Apply Formula and Report"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Rule:** If the load varies OR the transformer is ON for extended idle periods → **All-Day Efficiency is mandatory**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define all-day efficiency of a transformer. Why is it important for distribution transformers?**

**Model Answer:**

All-day efficiency is defined as the ratio of **output energy in kWh** delivered by the transformer over a 24-hour period to the **input energy in kWh** consumed during the same 24-hour period.

$$\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \frac{\text{Output kWh (24 hrs)}}{\text{Input kWh (24 hrs)}} \times 100\%$$

**Importance:** Distribution transformers remain energized 24 hours a day but carry rated load only for a few hours. Since iron (core) losses occur continuously for 24 hours, the conventional (instantaneous) efficiency does not reflect the true operational performance. All-day efficiency accounts for this cyclic loading pattern, making it the **true commercial efficiency metric** for distribution transformers.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2. State the condition for maximum all-day efficiency of a transformer.**

**Model Answer:**

The condition for maximum all-day efficiency is that the **iron loss must equal the average copper loss** over the 24-hour load cycle:

$$\boxed{P_i = P_{cu,\text{avg (24 hrs)}}}$$

This is different from conventional efficiency, where the condition is $P_i = P_{cu,\text{full load}}$. The reason is that copper loss in all-day efficiency is **weighted by the load cycle**, so the equality point is shifted.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**
> **Question A:**

**A transformer has the following load cycle:**
- Full load at 0.9 p.f. for **10 hours**
- 60% of full load at 0.8 p.f. for **6 hours**
- 30% of full load at unity p.f. for **4 hours**
- No load for the remaining **4 hours**

**Given:** Rated kVA = 20 kVA, Iron loss $P_i = 90$ W, Full-load copper loss $P_{cu,\text{fl}} = 150$ W.

**(a)** Calculate the **all-day efficiency** of the transformer. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Comment on the design implication: should the designer increase or decrease the iron loss to improve all-day efficiency? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Output Energy (kWh):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{out}} &= (20 \times 1.0 \times 0.9 \times 10) + (20 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 \times 6) + (20 \times 0.3 \times 1.0 \times 4) + 0 \\
&= 180 + 57.6 + 24 + 0 \\
&= 261.6 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Iron Loss Energy (kWh):**

$$W_i = 90 \times 24 = 2160 \text{ Wh} = 2.16 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Copper Loss Energy (kWh):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cu} &= (1.0^2 \times 150 \times 10) + (0.6^2 \times 150 \times 6) + (0.3^2 \times 150 \times 4) + 0 \\
&= 1500 + 324 + 54 + 0 \\
&= 1878 \text{ Wh} = 1.878 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — All-Day Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{all-day}} &= \frac{261.6}{261.6 + 2.16 + 1.878} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{261.6}{265.638} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 98.48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer: $\eta_{\text{all-day}} \approx 98.48\%$** ✅

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Analysis of Loss Distribution:**

- Iron loss energy = **2.16 kWh** (over 24 hrs)
- Copper loss energy = **1.878 kWh** (over loaded hrs)
- Iron loss slightly exceeds copper loss in this cycle.

**Design Recommendation:**

The designer should aim to **decrease the iron loss** further. The rationale is:

1. **Iron loss is weighted by 24 hours** — every watt of iron loss costs 24 watt-hours per day.
2. **Copper loss is weighted only by loaded hours** — typically 10–18 hours in distribution cycles.
3. For this specific cycle, $P_i = 90$ W vs. effective average $P_{cu} \approx 78$ W over loaded hours. They are already close to the **maximum all-day efficiency condition** ($P_i = P_{cu,\text{avg}}$).
4. If the transformer is to operate at lower loads for longer durations, **reducing iron loss** (using better core steel like CRGO silicon steel) will yield greater all-day efficiency gains than reducing copper loss.

> **Conclusion:** Use **high-grade CRGO silicon steel** to minimize iron loss, since iron loss is the dominant 24/7 energy drainer.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**
> **Question B (Alternative Choice):**

**A 500 kVA distribution transformer has the following data:**
- Iron loss = 1.2 kW
- Full-load copper loss = 3.6 kW
- **Load cycle:**
  - 80% load at 0.9 p.f. for **12 hours**
  - 50% load at 0.7 p.f. for **6 hours**
  - No load for **6 hours**

**(a)** Calculate the **all-day efficiency** and **conventional full-load efficiency** (at 0.9 p.f.). Compare both. **(10 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the load fraction at which **maximum all-day efficiency** occurs for this load cycle. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [10 Marks]

**Step 1 — Output Energy (kWh):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{out}} &= (500 \times 0.8 \times 0.9 \times 12) + (500 \times 0.5 \times 0.7 \times 6) + 0 \\
&= 4320 + 1050 + 0 \\
&= 5370 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Iron Loss Energy (kWh):**

$$W_i = 1.2 \times 24 = 28.8 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Copper Loss Energy (kWh):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cu} &= (0.8^2 \times 3.6 \times 12) + (0.5^2 \times 3.6 \times 6) + 0 \\
&= 27.648 + 5.4 + 0 \\
&= 33.048 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — All-Day Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{all-day}} &= \frac{5370}{5370 + 28.8 + 33.048} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{5370}{5431.848} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 98.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conventional Full-Load Efficiency (at 0.9 p.f.):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{conv, FL}} &= \frac{500 \times 0.9}{500 \times 0.9 + 1.2 + 3.6} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{450}{454.8} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 98.94\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| All-Day Efficiency | **98.86 %** |
| Conventional FL Efficiency (0.9 p.f.) | **98.94 %** |

> **Observation:** The all-day efficiency is **slightly lower** than the conventional full-load efficiency because the transformer operates below full load for most of the day, yet still pays the full iron loss for 24 hours.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [4 Marks]

**Average Copper Loss over Loaded Hours:**

Loaded hours = 12 + 6 = 18 hours.

$$
P_{cu,\text{avg (loaded hrs)}} = \frac{W_{cu}}{18} = \frac{33.048}{18} \approx 1.836 \text{ kW}
$$

**For maximum all-day efficiency:** $P_i = P_{cu,\text{avg}}$

Since $P_i = 1.2$ kW, the current design has $P_i < P_{cu,\text{avg}}$. The maximum all-day efficiency is achieved when the equivalent average copper loss over 18 loaded hours equals 1.2 kW.

**Equivalently, the load fraction $x$ that yields this when operating full time for 18 hours:**

$$x^2 \times 3.6 \times 18 = 1.2 \times 18 \implies x^2 = \frac{1.2}{3.6} \implies x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \approx 0.577$$

> **Answer:** The load fraction for **maximum all-day efficiency is approximately 0.577 (≈ 57.7%)** of full load. ✅

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Confusion (kW vs. kWh):** Students often add iron loss (in kW) directly with output (in kW) without converting to kWh. **Always convert losses to energy (kWh)** by multiplying by hours before summation.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Power Factor:** Output energy = kVA × p.f. × hours. Students sometimes omit the power factor, leading to **overestimated efficiency**.
> 
> 3. **Iron Loss Time Multiplier:** Iron loss is multiplied by **24 hours** (not just loaded hours). Many students wrongly multiply by loaded hours only.
> 
> 4. **Copper Loss Squared Relation:** $P_{cu} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{fl}}$. Students often use $P_{cu} = x \cdot P_{cu,\text{fl}}$ (linear), which is **wrong**.
> 
> 5. **Confusing All-Day Max Condition with Conventional:** $P_i = P_{cu,\text{fl}}$ is for conventional; $P_i = P_{cu,\text{weighted avg}}$ is for all-day.
> 
> 6. **No-Load Copper Loss:** During the "no load" interval, copper loss is **zero**, not equal to $P_{cu,\text{fl}}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Definition:** All-day efficiency = (Output kWh over 24 hrs) ÷ (Input kWh over 24 hrs) × 100%.
- [x] **Applicable to:** Distribution transformers with cyclic loading.
- [x] **Iron loss** is multiplied by **24 hrs** (continuous).
- [x] **Copper loss** is multiplied by **loaded hours only**, at **squared load fraction**.
- [x] **Formula:** $\eta_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{\sum x_k S_{\text{rated}} \cos\phi_k t_k}{\sum x_k S_{\text{rated}} \cos\phi_k t_k + 24 P_i + P_{cu,\text{fl}} \sum x_k^2 t_k} \times 100\%$
- [x] **Maximum all-day efficiency condition:** $P_i = P_{cu,\text{weighted avg over 24 hrs}}$.
- [x] **All-day efficiency is generally lower** than conventional full-load efficiency for the same transformer.
- [x] **Design goal:** Minimize **iron loss** (use CRGO silicon steel) for better all-day efficiency.
- [x] **Units discipline:** Energy in **kWh**, power in **kW**. Convert W to kW or multiply W·h by 10⁻³ to get kWh.
- [x] **No-load intervals** still contribute **iron loss** to total losses, dragging down efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Simple Numerical Problems" in DC Machines?

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PEICT414 Electrical Machines (Module 2)**, "Simple Numerical Problems" refers to the direct application of fundamental equations governing **DC Generators** and **DC Motors** to compute standard electrical and mechanical quantities. These problems test the student's ability to translate circuit parameters into the core performance metrics of a DC machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The module primarily covers **DC Machines** — construction, classification, EMF and torque equations, armature reaction, commutation, and characteristics. "Simple Numerical Problems" typically require students to calculate quantities like **Generated EMF (E_g)**, **Back EMF (E_b)**, **Armature Torque (T_a)**, **Shaft Torque (T_sh)**, **Speed (N)**, **Efficiency (η)**, and **Power losses**.

The four governing equations that form the backbone of all numerical problems in this module are:

| # | Governing Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$ | Generated EMF in a DC machine |
| 2 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back EMF (Motor mode) |
| 3 | $T_a = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega} = k_t \Phi I_a$ | Armature Torque developed |
| 4 | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_f$ (friction & windage) | Shaft Torque delivered to load |

Where:
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in Webers, Wb)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of rotation (in RPM)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths ($A = P$ for lap winding, $A = 2$ for wave winding)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (in Amperes)
- $R_a$ = Armature resistance (in Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $V$ = Terminal voltage (in Volts, V)
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity in rad/s, where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pump" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition for EMF Equation:**
> Imagine a **DC machine** as a **water circulation system**. The **magnetic flux ($\Phi$)** is like the *water pressure*, the **number of conductors ($Z$)** is the *number of pipes*, the **speed ($N$)** is the *pump rotation speed*, and the **number of poles ($P$)** is the *number of pump chambers*. More pressure + more pipes + faster rotation + more chambers = more "flow" (EMF) generated. The **parallel paths ($A$)** act like the *output valves* — fewer valves mean more pressure (wave winding gives higher voltage but less current capacity).

This is exactly why a **wave-wound** DC machine (with $A = 2$) produces a **higher EMF** for the same number of conductors compared to a **lap-wound** machine ($A = P$), making it suitable for **high-voltage, low-current** applications.

## 1.3 The Two Operating Modes — A Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generator Mode vs. Motor Mode — The "Source vs. Load" Distinction:**
> - In a **DC Generator**, the machine *converts mechanical power into electrical power*. The generated EMF $E_g$ is the **cause**, and the terminal voltage $V$ is the **effect** → $V = E_g - I_a R_a$
> - In a **DC Motor**, the machine *converts electrical power into mechanical power*. The supply voltage $V$ is the **cause**, and the back EMF $E_b$ is the **opposing effect** (Lenz's Law) → $E_b = V - I_a R_a$

Students must master this duality — it's the single most common point of confusion in KTU exam problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for Solving Numerical Problems

Every "Simple Numerical Problem" in KTU Module 2 can be systematically solved using the following **5-step analytical framework**:

**Step 1 → Identify the Machine Type and Winding Configuration**
- Is it a **Generator** or **Motor**? (Determines the EMF equation sign convention)
- Is it **Lap wound** ($A = P$) or **Wave wound** ($A = 2$)?

**Step 2 → List All Given Parameters**
- Extract $V, I_L, I_a, R_a, N, \Phi, Z, P$ from the problem statement.
- Be alert: problems may give $I_L$ (line current) instead of $I_a$ (armature current). For a **shunt machine**, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$, where $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$.

**Step 3 → Select the Appropriate Governing Equation**
- Need EMF? Use $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$
- Need Torque? Use $T_a = \dfrac{P_{conv}}{\omega} = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{2\pi N / 60}$
- Need Efficiency? Use $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} \times 100\%$

**Step 4 → Perform Unit-Consistent Calculations**
- $\Phi$ must be in **Wb**, $N$ in **RPM**, and the result $E_g$ will be in **Volts**.

**Step 5 → Verify with Physical Sanity Checks**
- Does the EMF make sense? (Typically 100 V – 500 V for practical DC machines)
- Is the speed reasonable? (Typically 750 – 3000 RPM)
- Is the efficiency between 70% – 95%?

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they cover ~90% of all Module 2 numerical problems in KTU exams.**

| Equation # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|:----------:|---------|-------------|----------------|
| **F1** | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$ | EMF Equation (universal) | Volts (V) |
| **F2** | $A = P$ (Lap), $A = 2$ (Wave) | Parallel paths | Dimensionless |
| **F3** | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | Generator terminal voltage | Volts (V) |
| **F4** | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Motor back EMF | Volts (V) |
| **F5** | $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P Z \Phi}{A} \cdot I_a$ | Armature Torque | N·m |
| **F6** | $T_a = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega} = \dfrac{60 \, E_b I_a}{2\pi N}$ | Torque from power | N·m |
| **F7** | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| **F8** | $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Power developed (mechanical) | Watts (W) |
| **F9** | $P_{sh} = E_b \cdot I_a - \text{mechanical losses}$ | Shaft power output | Watts (W) |
| **F10** | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | Shunt field current | Amperes (A) |
| **F11** | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ (Shunt Motor) | Armature current from line | Amperes (A) |
| **F12** | $I_a = I_L$ (Series Motor) | Armature = Line current | Amperes (A) |
| **F13** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | Percentage (%) |
| **F14** | $N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}$ | Speed proportionality (motor) | RPM |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These numerical calculations are not just academic — they are the **daily bread** of electrical machine design engineers. The EMF equation $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$ is used to:
- **Design** the armature winding of every DC motor manufactured globally (from toy motors to industrial drives)
- **Predict** the no-load speed of a DC motor given its design parameters
- **Troubleshoot** field failures — if a DC motor runs at abnormally high speed, it usually means the shunt field has opened, reducing $\Phi$ to near zero (since $N \propto 1/\Phi$)
- **Size** the prime mover for a DC generator in power plants and standby systems

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The EMF Equation $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$

This is the **most important derivation** in Module 2. Let's derive it from first principles.

**Step 1: Average EMF Induced in One Conductor**

According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the average EMF induced in a single conductor cutting through a magnetic field is:

$$e_{avg} = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

The time taken for one conductor to traverse one pole pitch is $t = \dfrac{60}{N \cdot P}$ seconds (because the armature makes $N/60$ revolutions per second, and each revolution passes $P$ poles).

Therefore:

$$e_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi}{60 / (N \cdot P)} = \dfrac{\Phi N P}{60}$$

**Step 2: Total EMF in One Parallel Path**

Each parallel path contains $Z / A$ conductors connected in **series**. Therefore, the total EMF per path is:

$$E_{path} = e_{avg} \times \dfrac{Z}{A} = \dfrac{\Phi N P}{60} \times \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 3: Total Generated EMF**

Since all parallel paths are connected in **parallel** between the brushes, the terminal EMF between the brushes is the EMF of a single path:

$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The derivation hinges on the fact that conductors in a single parallel path add their EMFs (series), but parallel paths do not (parallel connection). This is a beautiful example of how machine design topology directly determines electrical output.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Torque Equation $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P Z \Phi}{A} \cdot I_a$

**Step 1: Force on a Single Conductor**

A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force given by the Lorentz force law:

$$F = B \, I \, l$$

where $B$ is the flux density at the conductor, $I$ is the current, and $l$ is the active length of the conductor.

**Step 2: Relate Flux Density to Total Flux**

The average flux density over one pole is $B_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_{pole}}$, where $A_{pole}$ is the cross-sectional area per pole. Using the flux per pole $\Phi$ and the pole pitch, the force on a single conductor is:

$$f = \Phi \cdot I_{cond} \cdot \dfrac{P}{\pi D} \quad \text{(simplified form)}$$

**Step 3: Torque Contribution from All Conductors**

The torque is force × radius. After summing the contributions of all $Z$ conductors and dividing by the appropriate geometric constants, the net armature torque is:

$$T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P Z \Phi I_a}{A}$$

**Step 4: Verification via Power Equation**

We can independently verify this using the power equation. The mechanical power developed is:

$$P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$$

Solving for $T_a$:

$$T_a = \dfrac{60 \cdot E_b \cdot I_a}{2\pi N} = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega}$$

Substituting $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$T_a = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}\right) \cdot I_a}{\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}} = \dfrac{\Phi Z P I_a}{2\pi A} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P Z \Phi I_a}{A} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 The Analytical Master Matrix — Mapping Problem Types to Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Examiner's Master Map:** Use this table to instantly identify which equation to deploy for any given numerical problem.

| If the Problem Asks... | Given Quantities | Apply Equation(s) | Watch Out For! |
|------------------------|------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Find Generated EMF** | $\Phi, Z, N, P, A$ | F1 directly | Check $A$ — lap vs wave |
| **Find Speed of Motor** | $V, I_a, R_a, \Phi, Z, P$ | F1 + F4 → solve for N | Units: $\Phi$ must be in Wb |
| **Find Armature Torque** | $E_b$ (or $\Phi, I_a$), $N$ | F6 or F5 | Use $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ |
| **Find Shaft Torque** | $T_a$, friction/windage losses | $T_{sh} = P_{out} / \omega$ | Subtract mechanical losses |
| **Find Efficiency** | $P_{out}$, $P_{in}$ | F13 | Watch for shunt field copper loss |
| **Find New Speed after Flux Change** | Old $N_1, \Phi_1, \Phi_2$ | F14 | Assumes constant $V$ and $I_a$ |
| **Find $I_a$ in Shunt Motor** | $I_L, V, R_{sh}$ | F10, F11 | Don't forget $I_{sh}$! |
| **Find $I_a$ in Series Motor** | $I_L$ | F12 | $I_a = I_L$ (no shunt branch) |

## 3.4 A Fully-Solved Illustrative Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt generator has 500 armature conductors, a flux per pole of 0.05 Wb, and runs at 1000 RPM. The armature resistance is 0.1 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 50 Ω. If the terminal voltage is 220 V, find:
**(a)** The generated EMF
**(b)** The armature current
**(c)** The copper losses and efficiency

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$ poles, Lap wound → $A = P = 4$
- $Z = 500$ conductors
- $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb
- $N = 1000$ RPM
- $R_a = 0.1 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 50 \, \Omega$
- $V = 220$ V

**Part (a): Generated EMF**

$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 500 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{0.05 \times 500 \times 1000}{60} = \dfrac{25000}{60}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = 416.67 \text{ V}}$$

**Part (b): Armature Current**

The shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{220}{50} = 4.4 \text{ A}$$

The generated EMF equation for a generator:
$$E_g = V + I_a R_a$$

Therefore:
$$I_a = \dfrac{E_g - V}{R_a} = \dfrac{416.67 - 220}{0.1} = \dfrac{196.67}{0.1}$$

$$\boxed{I_a = 1966.7 \text{ A}}$$

**Part (c): Copper Losses and Efficiency**

Armature copper loss:
$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (1966.7)^2 \times 0.1 = 386,790 \text{ W}$$

Shunt field copper loss:
$$P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (4.4)^2 \times 50 = 968 \text{ W}$$

Total copper losses:
$$P_{cu,total} = 386,790 + 968 = 387,758 \text{ W}$$

Output power:
$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = V \cdot (I_a + I_{sh}) = 220 \times (1966.7 + 4.4) = 220 \times 1971.1$$

$$P_{out} = 433,642 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{cu,total}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{433,642}{433,642 + 387,758} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{433,642}{821,400} \times 100\% = 52.8\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The unusually low efficiency (52.8%) is due to the unrealistically high armature current caused by the very low armature resistance. In a realistic KTU exam problem, $R_a$ would be slightly higher, yielding efficiencies in the 80-90% range. The methodology, however, is identical.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — Numerical Problem Solving Pipeline

This Mermaid diagram maps the **logical decision tree** a student should follow when solving any Module 2 numerical problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> B{Generator or Motor?}
    B -->|Generator| C[Apply V = Eg - IaRa]
    B -->|Motor| D[Apply Eb = V - IaRa]
    C --> E{Identify Winding Type}
    D --> E
    E -->|Lap Wound| F[Set A = P]
    E -->|Wave Wound| G[Set A = 2]
    F --> H[Extract All Given Parameters]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Shunt Field Current Ish = V/Rsh]
    I --> J[Compute Armature Current Ia]
    J --> K[Apply EMF Equation Eg = PhiZN P / 60A]
    K --> L{What is Asked?}
    L -->|EMF| M[Report Eg in Volts]
    L -->|Speed| N[Solve for N = 60AEg / PhiZP]
    L -->|Torque| O[Apply Ta = 60EbIa / 2piN]
    L -->|Efficiency| P[Compute Pout and Pin then Divide]
    M --> Q[Verify Units and Sanity Check]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[End: Final Answer]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — DC Machine Power Flow

This diagram illustrates the **power flow** through a DC machine, which is essential for solving efficiency-related numerical problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Side]
        M1[Mechanical Power Input Pin] --> A1[Mechanical Losses Friction Windage]
    end
    A1 --> B1[Electrical Power Developed Pdev = EbIa]
    B1 --> C1[Armature Copper Loss IaSqRa]
    C1 --> D1[Shunt Field Copper Loss IshSqRsh]
    D1 --> E1[Electrical Power Output Pout = VIL]
    E1 --> F1[Output Terminal to Load]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The Governing Equation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CORE[Core Governing Equations of DC Machine]
        F1[EMF Equation Eg = PhiZN P / 60A] --> G1[Links Flux Conductors Speed Poles Paths]
        F2[Torque Equation Ta = PZPhiIa / 2piA] --> G2[Links Flux Conductors Current Poles Paths]
        F3[Back EMF Relation Eb = V - IaRa] --> G3[Motor Mode Only]
        F4[Terminal Voltage V = Eg - IaRa] --> G4[Generator Mode Only]
    end
    G1 --> H[Power Developed Pdev = EgIa or EbIa]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Angular Speed omega = 2piN / 60]
    I --> J[Final Output: Torque Speed Power Efficiency]
```
