# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**
**State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Define each term with its unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EMF equation of a DC generator is:

$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$$

Where:
- $E_g$ = Generated EMF in **Volts (V)**
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in **Webers (Wb)**
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors (dimensionless)
- $N$ = Speed of the armature in **RPM**
- $P$ = Number of poles (dimensionless)
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths ($A = P$ for lap winding, $A = 2$ for wave winding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Equation (1 mark), Four terms with units (2 marks).

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**
**Distinguish between lap winding and wave winding in a DC machine based on the number of parallel paths.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Parallel paths ($A$) | $A = P$ | $A = 2$ |
| Current capacity | Higher | Lower |
| Voltage rating | Lower | Higher |
| Applications | Heavy current DC motors/generators | High-voltage machines |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions offer an internal choice between two questions. Both must be solved in exam scenarios only if they are the printed options. Here we present both as alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — DC Shunt Generator

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** A 4-pole, lap-connected DC shunt generator has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a shunt field resistance of 200 Ω. The armature has 200 conductors and generates an EMF of 250 V when running at 1000 RPM. Calculate the flux per pole and the line current when the terminal voltage is 240 V. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the flux per pole remaining constant, find the speed at which the machine must be driven to deliver a terminal voltage of 250 V to a load drawing 40 A. Also calculate the efficiency under this condition, neglecting mechanical losses. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given:** $P = 4$, Lap → $A = 4$, $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 200 \, \Omega$, $Z = 200$, $E_g = 250$ V at $N_1 = 1000$ RPM, $V = 240$ V.

**Step 1: Find Flux Per Pole**

Using EMF equation:
$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$$

$$250 = \dfrac{\Phi \times 200 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

$$250 = \dfrac{\Phi \times 200000}{60}$$

$$\Phi = \dfrac{250 \times 60}{200000} = \dfrac{15000}{200000} = 0.075 \text{ Wb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi = 0.075 \text{ Wb} \quad (3 \text{ marks})}$$

**Step 2: Find Armature Current and Line Current**

Shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{240}{200} = 1.2 \text{ A}$$

From generator equation $E_g = V + I_a R_a$:
$$I_a = \dfrac{E_g - V}{R_a} = \dfrac{250 - 240}{0.5} = \dfrac{10}{0.5} = 20 \text{ A}$$

Line current:
$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 20 + 1.2 = 21.2 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_L = 21.2 \text{ A} \quad (4 \text{ marks})}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** New terminal voltage $V' = 250$ V, Load current $I_L' = 40$ A, $\Phi$ = constant = 0.075 Wb.

**Step 1: Find New Armature Current**

$$I_{sh}' = \dfrac{V'}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{200} = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

$$I_a' = I_L' - I_{sh}' = 40 - 1.25 = 38.75 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Find New Generated EMF**

$$E_g' = V' + I_a' R_a = 250 + 38.75 \times 0.5 = 250 + 19.375 = 269.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Find New Speed**

Since $\Phi$ is constant, $E_g \propto N$:

$$\dfrac{N'}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_g'}{E_g}$$

$$N' = N_1 \times \dfrac{E_g'}{E_g} = 1000 \times \dfrac{269.375}{250} = 1000 \times 1.0775$$

$$\boxed{N' = 1077.5 \text{ RPM} \quad (3 \text{ marks})}$$

**Step 4: Find Efficiency**

Output power:
$$P_{out} = V' \times I_L' = 250 \times 40 = 10,000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}$$

Total copper losses:
$$P_{cu,a} = (I_a')^2 R_a = (38.75)^2 \times 0.5 = 750.78 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu,sh} = (I_{sh}')^2 R_{sh} = (1.25)^2 \times 200 = 312.5 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu,total} = 750.78 + 312.5 = 1063.28 \text{ W}$$

Since mechanical losses are neglected:
$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{cu,total} = 10,000 + 1063.28 = 11,063.28 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{10,000}{11,063.28} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 90.39\% \quad (4 \text{ marks})}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — DC Shunt Motor

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.3 Ω and a field resistance of 110 Ω. It draws a line current of 40 A at no-load and runs at 1000 RPM. Calculate the no-load armature current, the back EMF at no-load, and the developed torque. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At full load, the line current is 100 A. Calculate the full-load back EMF, the full-load speed (assuming flux is constant), and the full-load torque developed by the armature. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$, $I_L = 40$ A, $N_1 = 1000$ RPM.

**Step 1: Shunt Field Current**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: No-Load Armature Current**

$$I_{a1} = I_L - I_{sh} = 40 - 2 = 38 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{a1} = 38 \text{ A} \quad (2 \text{ marks})}$$

**Step 3: No-Load Back EMF**

For a motor: $E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a$

$$E_{b1} = 220 - 38 \times 0.3 = 220 - 11.4 = 208.6 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{b1} = 208.6 \text{ V} \quad (2 \text{ marks})}$$

**Step 4: No-Load Developed Torque**

$$\omega_1 = \dfrac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{a1} = \dfrac{E_{b1} \times I_{a1}}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{208.6 \times 38}{104.72}$$

$$\boxed{T_{a1} = 75.69 \text{ N·m} \quad (3 \text{ marks})}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $I_L' = 100$ A, $\Phi$ = constant, $V = 220$ V.

**Step 1: Full-Load Armature Current**

$$I_{a2} = I_L' - I_{sh} = 100 - 2 = 98 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Full-Load Back EMF**

$$E_{b2} = V - I_{a2} R_a = 220 - 98 \times 0.3 = 220 - 29.4 = 190.6 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{b2} = 190.6 \text{ V} \quad (2 \text{ marks})}$$

**Step 3: Full-Load Speed**

Since $\Phi$ is constant, $E_b \propto N$:

$$N_2 = N_1 \times \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} = 1000 \times \dfrac{190.6}{208.6} = 1000 \times 0.9137$$

$$\boxed{N_2 = 913.7 \text{ RPM} \quad (3 \text{ marks})}$$

**Step 4: Full-Load Armature Torque**

$$\omega_2 = \dfrac{2\pi N_2}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 913.7}{60} = 95.68 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{a2} = \dfrac{E_{b2} \times I_{a2}}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{190.6 \times 98}{95.68} = \dfrac{18,678.8}{95.68}$$

$$\boxed{T_{a2} = 195.24 \text{ N·m} \quad (2 \text{ marks})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As load increases (from no-load to full-load), the armature current increases, the back EMF decreases, and the speed drops slightly. This is the classic **"drooping speed-load characteristic"** of a DC shunt motor — a key KTU theory question.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Module 2 Numerical Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_a$** — In a **shunt machine**, $I_a \neq I_L$. Always compute $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ first, then find $I_a$. Failing to do this loses **2-3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Wrong sign convention for $E_g$ vs. $E_b$** — In a generator, $E_g > V$; in a motor, $E_b < V$. Writing the wrong equation ($V = E_g + I_a R_a$ for a generator) is a **common 1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s** — Torque equations use $\omega$ in rad/s. Using $N$ directly in the denominator instead of $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ leads to a wrong answer. **Always convert.**
> 4. **Mixing up Lap and Wave winding** — $A = P$ for lap, $A = 2$ for wave. This is a **silent killer** — your EMF value will be off by a factor of $P/2$ without any obvious error in calculation.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing $\Phi$ in mWb and forgetting to convert to Wb. **1 mWb = 0.001 Wb.** This single error can make your EMF answer 1000x off.
> 6. **Neglecting $I_{sh}$ in efficiency calculations** — Shunt field copper loss ($I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$) is a real loss and must be included. Omitting it costs **1-2 marks** in Part (b) problems.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 Numerical Problems:**

- ✅ **The EMF equation** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$ is the **single most important formula** — memorize it completely with all 5 variables and units.
- ✅ **Lap winding** → $A = P$ (used for high-current applications); **Wave winding** → $A = 2$ (used for high-voltage applications).
- ✅ **Generator equation:** $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (EMF exceeds terminal voltage)
- ✅ **Motor equation:** $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ (Back EMF opposes applied voltage)
- ✅ **Shunt field current:** $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ (always computed first in shunt machine problems)
- ✅ **Armature current in shunt motor:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
- ✅ **Armature current in series motor:** $I_a = I_L$ (no shunt branch)
- ✅ **Torque formula (power-based):** $T_a = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega}$, where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
- ✅ **Torque formula (design-based):** $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{PZ\Phi I_a}{A}$
- ✅ **Speed proportionality (constant flux):** $N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}}$
- ✅ **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **Total losses** = Armature Cu loss + Shunt field Cu loss + Mechanical losses (friction, windage) + Core losses
- ✅ **Always perform unit conversion** before plugging values into any equation (mWb → Wb, etc.)
- ✅ **Verify your answer** with a physical sanity check: EMF should be 50-500 V, speed 500-3000 RPM, efficiency 70-95%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Auto Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **Auto Transformer** is a special type of electrical transformer that consists of a **single continuous winding** wound on a laminated magnetic core, in which a portion of the same winding is common to both the **primary (input)** and the **secondary (output)** circuits. Unlike a conventional two-winding transformer that uses two electrically isolated coils, the auto transformer relies on **electrical (conductive) coupling** in addition to **magnetic (inductive) coupling** between its primary and secondary sides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim):** *"An auto transformer is a transformer in which there is a single winding common to both primary and secondary circuits. The secondary voltage is obtained from a suitable tap on the primary winding."*

Mathematically, the transformation ratio $K_a$ of an auto transformer is given by:

$$K_a = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{I_1}{I_2}$$

where the symbols carry their standard meanings (defined in the formula sheet of Section 2).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Adjustable Water Tap**
>
> Imagine a long vertical water pipe (the *single winding*). The water enters at the **top** (primary). Instead of having a second, completely separate pipe for the output, we simply drill a **hole partway down the same pipe** and attach a delivery tap there. Water flowing to the delivery tap comes from two paths simultaneously:
> 1. **Through the magnetic action of the surrounding core** (just like a normal transformer), AND
> 2. **By direct conduction through the metal pipe itself** (a path the two-winding transformer simply does NOT have).
>
> This dual-path is precisely why an auto transformer is **smaller, cheaper, and more efficient** than a two-winding transformer of the same rating — but it sacrifices the **galvanic isolation** between input and output that a two-winding transformer provides.

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Note for Kerala Lab Practicals:** Because the primary and secondary are NOT electrically isolated, a fault on the high-voltage side appears directly on the low-voltage side. Auto transformers are therefore **forbidden as isolation transformers** in most industrial and laboratory safety codes.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Frequency:** $f = \mathbf{50\ Hz}$ (Indian/Kerala standard, per IS specifications).
- **Core Material:** Typically **Cold-Rolled Grain-Oriented (CRGO) silicon steel** laminations of thickness **0.35 mm** to limit eddy-current losses.
- **Transformation Ratio ($K_a$):** Always a **positive real number**; in most KTU problems, $0.5 \leq K_a \leq 1$ for step-down or $1 \leq K_a \leq 2$ for step-up.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Auto Transformers (As per KTU Module 2)

| **Type** | **Description** | **Typical KTU Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Step-Down Auto Transformer** | $V_2 < V_1$, output is taken from a tap | Induction motor starters, voltage regulators |
| **Step-Up Auto Transformer** | $V_2 > V_1$, output is across full winding | Lab testing of equipment at higher voltages |
| **Variable (Adjustable) Auto Transformer (Variac)** | The tap is a sliding carbon brush | Laboratory and educational setups — most common KTU lab apparatus |
| **Three-Phase Auto Transformer** | Three single-phase auto transformers in star or delta | EHV transmission systems, large motor starters |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** The **Variac** is the single most-frequently drawn auto transformer in KTU university exams. Be ready to draw its schematic, label the brush position, and explain its working.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction — Step-by-Step

An auto transformer has the following constructional features:

- **Magnetic Core:** A shell-type or core-type laminated silicon-steel core provides a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux $\Phi$.
- **Single Winding:** A single, uniform, insulated copper wire is wound on the core. The total number of turns is $N_1$.
- **Tap Point:** A fixed (or movable, in Variacs) tap is brought out from a turn corresponding to $N_2$ from one end of the winding.
- **Terminals:** Three terminals are brought out — **A** (start of $N_1$), **B** (tap at $N_2$), and **C** (end of winding).
  - Terminal pair **A–C** is the full primary (or full secondary, depending on application) — has $N_1$ turns.
  - Terminal pair **B–C** is the common (tapped) portion — has $N_2$ turns and is shared between input and output.

> [!NOTE]
> The winding between the tap and the *common terminal* is called the **Common Winding** (carries the current difference $I_1 - I_2$). The remaining turns form the **Series Winding** (carries the full primary current $I_1$).

---

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-Down Case

Assume the auto transformer is used as a **step-down** unit: primary is the full winding (A–C) with $N_1$ turns, secondary is the tapped portion (B–C) with $N_2$ turns.

1. **Apply $V_1$ across A–C.** An alternating flux $\Phi$ is established in the core, with peak value $\Phi_m = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44\,f\,N_1}$.
2. **Induced EMF in $N_2$ turns:** By Faraday's law, $V_2 = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} V_1 = K_a V_1$.
3. **Load connected across B–C:** Load current $I_2$ flows. By MMF balance, $N_1 I_1 \approx N_2 I_2$ (ideally), so $I_1 = K_a I_2$.
4. **Current in the common winding (B–C):** By KCL at the tap, $I_{\text{common}} = I_2 - I_1$. This is the *small* difference current, which is why the common winding can be made physically smaller.

---

### 2.3 Why Auto Transformers Save Copper — The Heart of the Topic

In a **two-winding transformer** of identical rating, the total conductor length is proportional to $N_1 + N_2$.

In an **auto transformer**, the secondary *reuses* the $N_2$ turns of the primary via the tap. Only the **additional** $(N_1 - N_2)$ turns of series winding need to be wound physically in addition to $N_1$ total turns. The total conductor used is therefore proportional to:

$$\text{Length}_{\text{auto}} \;\propto\; N_1 + (N_1 - N_2) = 2N_1 - N_2$$

The **saving in copper** (and therefore cost, weight, and size) is:

$$\text{Saving} = \frac{(N_1 + N_2) - (2N_1 - N_2)}{N_1 + N_2} = \frac{2N_2 - N_1}{N_1 + N_2}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $N_1$ and using $K_a = N_2/N_1$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{Saving (\%)} = \left(\frac{2K_a - 1}{1 + K_a}\right) \times 100\;}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula you must memorise** for the KTU Module-2 Auto Transformer problems. Master the boundary conditions in the last column — they are favourite viva questions.

| # | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Boundary / Special Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transformation Ratio (Step-Down) | $K_a = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}$ | $0 < K_a < 1$ for step-down |
| 2 | Transformation Ratio (Step-Up) | $K_a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | $K_a > 1$ for step-up |
| 3 | Common-winding current | $I_{\text{common}} = \vert I_2 - I_1 \vert$ | Equals $I_2(1 - K_a)$ |
| 4 | Series-winding current | $I_{\text{series}} = I_1$ | Full primary current |
| 5 | Power transferred **inductively** | $P_{\text{ind}} = V_1(I_2 - I_1) = V_2\,I_1\,(1 - K_a)$ | $= 0$ when $K_a = 1$ |
| 6 | Power transferred **conductively** | $P_{\text{cond}} = V_1 I_2 = V_2 I_1$ | Maximum when $K_a \to 1$ |
| 7 | Total power handled | $P_{\text{total}} = V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ | Equals load power (ideal) |
| 8 | **Saving of copper / material** | $\text{Saving} = \left(\dfrac{2K_a - 1}{K_a + 1}\right)\times 100\,\%$ | $= 50\%$ when $K_a = 1$; $= 0\%$ when $K_a = 0.5$ |
| 9 | Equivalent primary impedance (referred) | $Z_{1,\text{eq}} = K_a^{2} Z_L + Z_p$ | $Z_p$ = primary winding impedance |
| 10 | Voltage Transformation | $V_2 = K_a V_1$ | — |
| 11 | Turns ratio inverse relation | $\dfrac{1}{K_a} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Used in step-up derivations |

> [!TIP]
> Notice that when $K_a$ is close to **1** (e.g., 400 V to 440 V), the saving approaches **50%** — this is exactly why auto transformers are used in **transmission voltage regulation** where the ratio is small.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

Auto transformers dominate three major KTU-mapped application domains:

1. **Induction Motor Starters (Star-Delta & Auto-Transformer Starters):** Reduced-voltage starting cuts the inrush current. Tap positions at **50%, 60%, 75%, 80%** of line voltage are standard.
2. **Transmission & Distribution Voltage Regulation:** Stepping 11 kV up to 11.5 kV (or 400 V up to 440 V) is cheaper with an auto transformer.
3. **Laboratory Variacs:** Continuously variable AC voltage supply from 0 V to 270 V for testing single-phase equipment.

---

### 2.6 Advantages and Disadvantages — KTU Standard

**Advantages:**
- Lower cost of material (copper saving up to ~50%).
- Better voltage regulation (lower leakage reactance).
- Higher efficiency (reduced $I^{2}R$ and core losses).
- Smaller size and lower weight for the same kVA rating.

**Disadvantages:**
- **No electrical isolation** between primary and secondary.
- Short-circuit current is much higher (lower series impedance) — requires special protection.
- Not suitable for step-ratios greater than **2:1** (safety/economy threshold).
- A break in the common winding means the full primary voltage appears across the load — **dangerous failure mode**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMF Equation and Voltage Relation

**Statement:** Show that $V_2 = K_a V_1$ for an ideal auto transformer.

**Starting Point:** The full primary winding has $N_1$ turns and is energised by an AC voltage $v_1(t) = V_{1m} \sin(\omega t)$.

By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the RMS voltage induced across $N_1$ turns is:

$$E_1 = 4.44\, f\, N_1\, \Phi_m$$

In an ideal transformer (neglecting winding resistance and leakage flux), $V_1 = E_1$. Therefore:

$$V_1 = 4.44\, f\, N_1\, \Phi_m$$

The same flux $\Phi_m$ links the $N_2$ turns of the common winding. The voltage induced in this $N_2$-turn portion is:

$$V_2 = 4.44\, f\, N_2\, \Phi_m$$

**Step 1 — Divide** the second equation by the first to eliminate the common terms $4.44\, f\, \Phi_m$:

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{4.44\, f\, N_2\, \Phi_m}{4.44\, f\, N_1\, \Phi_m}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel** $4.44\, f\, \Phi_m$ from numerator and denominator:

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange** to obtain the desired form:

$$\boxed{\;V_2 = \frac{N_2}{N_1}\, V_1 = K_a V_1\;}$$

This is the fundamental voltage relation. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current Relation in an Ideal Auto Transformer

**Statement:** Show that the current in the common winding is $I_2 - I_1$ and that $I_1 = K_a I_2$.

**Starting Point:** Apply Ampere's Law (MMF balance) to the magnetic core. The net magnetising MMF in an ideal transformer is zero (since the core is lossless and the magnetising current is neglected for ideal analysis):

$$N_1 I_1 \;=\; N_2\, I_{\text{common}}$$

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the tap point B.** Current entering B from the primary winding is $I_1$. Current leaving B to the load is $I_2$. Therefore, the current flowing in the common winding (B to C) is:

$$I_{\text{common}} = I_2 - I_1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $I_{\text{common}}$ back into the MMF balance:

$$N_1 I_1 = N_2 (I_2 - I_1)$$

**Step 3 — Expand** the right-hand side:

$$N_1 I_1 = N_2 I_2 - N_2 I_1$$

**Step 4 — Collect** the $I_1$ terms on the left side:

$$N_1 I_1 + N_2 I_1 = N_2 I_2$$

$$I_1 (N_1 + N_2) = N_2 I_2$$

**Step 5 — Solve** for $I_1$:

$$\boxed{\;I_1 = \frac{N_2}{N_1 + N_2}\, I_2 = \frac{K_a}{1 + K_a}\, I_2\;}$$

Or equivalently, solving for $I_2$:

$$\boxed{\;I_2 = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{N_2}\, I_1 = \frac{1 + K_a}{K_a}\, I_1\;}$$

Note that for $K_a = 1$: $I_1 = I_2/2$ and $I_{\text{common}} = I_2/2$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Saving of Copper Material (Exhaustive)

**Statement:** Derive the formula $\text{Saving (\%)} = \dfrac{2K_a - 1}{K_a + 1} \times 100$.

**Step 1 — Compute** the total conductor length in the **equivalent two-winding transformer** (which is the reference for the comparison):

The total number of turns is $N_1 + N_2$. Since the length of wire per turn is the same in both designs (same core window), the total length is:

$$L_{\text{2W}} = L_t (N_1 + N_2)$$

where $L_t$ is the mean length of turn.

**Step 2 — Compute** the total conductor length in the **auto transformer**.

- The full primary winding ($N_1$ turns) exists exactly as in the two-winding design.
- The secondary is the **tap** — no separate $N_2$-turn coil is needed.
- The *additional* series winding that supplies the difference between $V_1$ and $V_2$ has $(N_1 - N_2)$ turns.

Therefore:

$$L_{\text{auto}} = L_t (N_1) + L_t (N_1 - N_2) = L_t (2N_1 - N_2)$$

**Step 3 — Compute** the absolute saving in length:

$$\Delta L = L_{\text{2W}} - L_{\text{auto}} = L_t (N_1 + N_2) - L_t (2N_1 - N_2) = L_t (2N_2 - N_1)$$

**Step 4 — Compute** the *fractional* saving:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L_{\text{2W}}} = \frac{L_t(2N_2 - N_1)}{L_t(N_1 + N_2)} = \frac{2N_2 - N_1}{N_1 + N_2}$$

**Step 5 — Express** in terms of $K_a = N_2/N_1$. Divide numerator and denominator by $N_1$:

$$\frac{2(N_2/N_1) - 1}{1 + (N_2/N_1)} = \frac{2K_a - 1}{1 + K_a}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to percentage**:

$$\boxed{\;\text{Saving} = \left(\frac{2K_a - 1}{K_a + 1}\right) \times 100\,\%\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Cases (very important for viva):**
> - $K_a \to 1$: $\text{Saving} = \dfrac{2(1) - 1}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 50\%$.
> - $K_a = 0.5$: $\text{Saving} = \dfrac{1 - 1}{1.5} = 0\%$ (auto-transformer is identical to a two-winding one at this ratio).
> - $K_a \to 0$: $\text{Saving} \to -100\%$ (auto-transformer is impractically large).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Inductive vs Conductive Power

**Step 1 — Total load power** is:

$$P_{\text{load}} = V_2 I_2$$

**Step 2 — Power handled by the series winding** (the $(N_1 - N_2)$-turn portion). It carries the full primary current $I_1$ and sustains a voltage $(V_1 - V_2)$ across itself:

$$P_{\text{series}} = (V_1 - V_2) I_1$$

This is the power that crosses the *magnetic* path — i.e., transferred **inductively**:

$$P_{\text{ind}} = (V_1 - V_2) I_1$$

**Step 3 — Power handled conductively** is the remainder:

$$P_{\text{cond}} = P_{\text{load}} - P_{\text{ind}} = V_2 I_2 - (V_1 - V_2) I_1$$

Use $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ (ideal) to get:

$$P_{\text{cond}} = V_1 I_1 - V_1 I_1 + V_2 I_1 = V_2 I_1$$

So the conductive power is:

$$\boxed{\;P_{\text{cond}} = V_2 I_1\;}$$

**Step 4 — Verify** by adding them back:

$$P_{\text{ind}} + P_{\text{cond}} = (V_1 - V_2) I_1 + V_2 I_1 = V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2 = P_{\text{load}}\;$$

The bookkeeping is consistent. **Q.E.D.**

> [!TIP]
> **Board Trick:** When $K_a \to 1$, $V_1 \approx V_2$, so $P_{\text{ind}} \to 0$ and almost **all** power flows conductively. That is why close-ratio auto transformers are extraordinarily efficient.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — Standard KTU Problem

**Problem:** A 5 kVA, 200 V / 100 V single-phase auto transformer has its 100 V side connected to a 100 V, 50 Hz source. The load on the 200 V side draws the rated current at 0.8 p.f. lagging. Compute: (a) the currents in the primary, secondary, and common winding, (b) the saving of copper compared to a two-winding transformer.

**Given:** $S = 5$ kVA, $V_1 = 100$ V (primary side, since it is connected to source), $V_2 = 200$ V (load side), $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging.

**Step 1 — Compute** the transformation ratio. Since the load is on the 200 V side, the auto transformer operates in **step-up** mode. The full winding has $V_2 = 200$ V across it, the tap portion has $V_1 = 100$ V. Thus:

$$K_a = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5$$

Equivalently, $\frac{1}{K_a} = 2$ — this is the step-up ratio.

**Step 2 — Compute** the rated currents.

Load (secondary) current:

$$I_2 = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{5000}{200} = 25 \text{ A}$$

Primary current (from source):

$$I_1 = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{5000}{100} = 50 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute** the common-winding current:

$$I_{\text{common}} = I_2 - I_1 = 25 - 50 = -25 \text{ A}$$

The negative sign indicates the current direction is reversed compared to the assumed direction — the **magnitude is 25 A**.

**Step 4 — Compute** the saving of copper using $K_a = 0.5$:

$$\text{Saving} = \left(\frac{2(0.5) - 1}{0.5 + 1}\right) \times 100 = \frac{0}{1.5} \times 100 = 0\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Trap:** When $K_a = 0.5$ (i.e., a 2:1 ratio), there is **zero saving** of copper! Auto transformers are only economical when the ratio is **closer to unity** (e.g., < 1.5:1 or > 1:1.5). Many students lose marks by blindly writing "50% saving" without calculating $K_a$.

**Step 5 — Verification by the alternative formula.** Saving in terms of series winding turns ratio: the series winding has $(N_2 - N_1) = $ an additional 100 V of turns, equal to $N_1$. So the auto-transformer has $N_2$ turns of conductor on the common portion plus $(N_2 - N_1)$ turns of series winding. Total turns $\propto 2N_2 - N_1 = 2V_2 - V_1 = 300$ (in V-equivalent units). A two-winding transformer would use $N_1 + N_2 = 300$ (V-equivalent). Hence saving is 0%. ✓

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When to Use an Auto Transformer?"

| **Condition** | **Use Auto Transformer** | **Use Two-Winding Transformer** |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage ratio close to 1:1 (e.g., 440/400 V) | ✓ Strongly preferred | ✗ Wasteful |
| Voltage ratio > 2:1 (e.g., 11000/415 V) | ✗ Marginal / uneconomical | ✓ Standard choice |
| Galvanic isolation required | ✗ Cannot provide | ✓ Provides full isolation |
| Distribution transformer at consumer end | ✗ Safety regulations forbid | ✓ Mandatory |
| Reduced-voltage motor starter | ✓ Standard practice | ✗ Used only in special cases |
| EHV grid voltage regulator (small ratio) | ✓ Industry standard | ✗ Rare |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Constructional Schematic of a Step-Down Auto Transformer

The diagram below shows the physical construction: a single continuous winding wound on a rectangular core, with a tap brought out from a turn corresponding to $N_2$. Currents and voltages at every terminal are labelled.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Terminal A: Primary Start, Voltage V1"] -->|N1 - N2 turns, Series Winding| B["Terminal B: Tap Point, Common with Load"]
    B -->|N2 turns, Common Winding| C["Terminal C: Common End"]
    C --> D["Return to Primary Source"]
    A -->|V1 across full winding| D
    B -.->|V2 across load| E["Load ZL, Current I2"]
    E --> C
    F["Primary current I1 enters A"] --> A
    A -->|I_series = I1| B
    B -->|I_common = I2 - I1| C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top branch** (A → B) is the *series winding* of $(N_1 - N_2)$ turns carrying current $I_1$.
- The **bottom branch** (B → C) is the *common winding* of $N_2$ turns carrying the difference current $(I_2 - I_1)$.
- The **load** is connected across B–C, drawing $I_2$.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Flow in Auto Transformer

This block diagram shows the *dual path* of power flow (the very concept that distinguishes an auto transformer from a two-winding one). Use this to answer 14-mark questions that ask "explain the working with a block diagram".

```mermaid
graph TD
    P["Primary Voltage Source V1"] -->|Inductive Coupling via Core Flux| S["Series Winding N1 minus N2 turns"]
    S -->|V1 minus V2 induced across series winding| T["Tap Point B"]
    T -->|Conductive Path: Direct Wire| CW["Common Winding N2 turns"]
    CW -->|V2 delivered to load| L["Load ZL, draws I2 at p.f. cos phi"]
    L -->|Return current I2| T
    P -->|Primary current I1 enters| S
    T -->|Common winding current equals I2 minus I1| CW
    IND["Power Transferred Inductively: Pind equals V1 minus V2 times I1"] -.-> S
    COND["Power Transferred Conductively: Pcond equals V2 times I1"] -.-> CW
```

**Key insight from the diagram:** The inductive path supplies only the *difference* power $(V_1 - V_2)\,I_1$. The bulk of the power — when $K_a \to 1$ — flows conductively through the common winding.

---

### 4.3 Variac — Variable Auto Transformer (Lab Apparatus)

The Variac is the lab workhorse. Its diagram illustrates the use of a sliding carbon brush as the tap.

```mermaid
graph LR
    MAINS["AC Mains Supply 230 V, 50 Hz"] --> X1["Input Terminal X1"]
    X1 --> W["Single Layer Wound Winding, Tapped at Every Turn"]
    W --> X2["Common Terminal X2, Return to Mains Neutral"]
    BRUSH["Sliding Carbon Brush"] -->|Selects N2 turns for desired V2| W
    BRUSH --> OUT["Output Terminal, Variable Voltage from 0 to 270 V"]
    OUT --> LOAD["Lab Load or Equipment Under Test"]
    LOAD --> X2
    SCALE["Calibrated Dial in Volts"] -.-> BRUSH
```

**Working principle:** Rotating the dial moves the brush along the winding, varying the number of turns $N_2$ between the brush and the common terminal, and therefore varying $V_2 = (N_2/N_1)\,V_1$ continuously from **0 V up to $\sim 270$ V** (a small over-voltage tapping is provided beyond 230 V for testing tolerance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Variac the most-tested auto transformer in KTU labs?**
> Because it visually and physically demonstrates the tap-based voltage selection and the shared winding concept. In the KTU university lab exam, you will almost certainly be asked to (a) draw its circuit, (b) measure the output at various dial positions, and (c) compute the saving of copper versus a theoretical two-winding design.
