## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
**Define an auto transformer and state TWO advantages it has over a two-winding transformer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An auto transformer is a transformer having a **single continuous winding** on a laminated magnetic core, a part of which is common to both the primary and secondary circuits. The secondary voltage is obtained from a suitable **tap** on the primary winding.
>
> **Advantages (any 2, 1 mark each):**
> 1. **Saving of copper/material** — up to 50% less copper is needed compared to a two-winding transformer of the same rating, because the same winding is shared.
> 2. **Higher efficiency** — reduced copper ($I^{2}R$) losses due to lower conductor length, and lower leakage reactance, giving better voltage regulation.

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
**What is the significance of the transformation ratio $K_a$ in an auto transformer? Show how the saving of copper depends on $K_a$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Significance (1 mark):** The transformation ratio $K_a = N_2/N_1 = V_2/V_1$ determines the **step-up or step-down** behaviour of the auto transformer. It is the key design parameter that links the number of turns to the voltage ratio.
>
> **Dependence (2 marks):** The saving of copper is given by:
> $$\text{Saving (\%)} = \left(\frac{2K_a - 1}{K_a + 1}\right) \times 100$$
> - When $K_a = 1$ (voltages are equal), saving is **50%** — maximum economy.
> - When $K_a = 0.5$ (ratio of 2:1), saving is **0%** — auto transformer offers no material benefit.
> - Thus, the closer $K_a$ is to unity, the greater the saving. Auto transformers are therefore used only when the voltage ratio is small (typically < 2:1).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Auto Transformer Construction, Working, and Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]**

**(a)** *With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a single-phase step-down auto transformer. Clearly label the common winding, the series winding, and indicate the directions of all currents. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 10 kVA, 1000 V / 800 V single-phase auto transformer is used to step down 1000 V to 800 V for a resistive load. Compute: (i) the full-load primary and secondary currents, (ii) the current in the common winding, and (iii) the percentage saving of copper compared to a two-winding transformer of the same rating. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)

**Schematic description (2 marks):**

A single-phase step-down auto transformer consists of a laminated silicon-steel core on which a **single continuous winding** of $N_1$ turns is wound. A **tap** is brought out from the $N_2$-th turn (counted from the common terminal). The terminals are:

- **A — C:** full primary (across $N_1$ turns), connected to the AC source $V_1$.
- **B — C:** secondary (across $N_2$ turns), connected to the load.
- The portion **A — B** has $(N_1 - N_2)$ turns and is the **series winding**.
- The portion **B — C** has $N_2$ turns and is the **common winding**.

**Working explanation (3 marks):**

When the source $V_1$ is applied across A–C, an alternating flux $\Phi_m$ is set up in the core. By Faraday's law, the voltage induced per turn is $V_1/N_1 = 4.44\,f\,\Phi_m$. Across the $N_2$ turns of the tap (B–C), the induced voltage is $V_2 = (N_2/N_1) V_1 = K_a V_1$.

When a load is connected across B–C, current $I_2$ flows through it. The MMF balance $N_1 I_1 \approx N_2 I_{\text{common}}$ gives $I_1 = K_a I_2$. The current in the common winding is $I_2 - I_1$ (by KCL at the tap). This is **less** than $I_2$ (in step-down mode), so the common winding can be designed to carry this smaller current — reducing copper.

**Current directions and concluding remark (2 marks):**
The series winding carries the **full primary current** $I_1$ entering at A and leaving at B. At node B, this current adds vectorially with the load current $I_2$ (drawn from B to the load). The difference $I_2 - I_1$ flows from B to C in the common winding back to the source neutral. The transformer is electrically and magnetically coupled — both couplings contribute to the power transfer.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $S = 10$ kVA, $V_1 = 1000$ V (primary), $V_2 = 800$ V (secondary), load is resistive.

**Step 1 — Transformation ratio (1 mark):**

$$K_a = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{800}{1000} = 0.8$$

**Step 2 — Full-load currents (2 marks):**

$$I_1 = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{10\,000}{1000} = 10 \text{ A}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{10\,000}{800} = 12.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Current in the common winding (2 marks):**

$$I_{\text{common}} = I_2 - I_1 = 12.5 - 10 = 2.5 \text{ A}$$

> **Verification by KCL:** $I_1 + I_{\text{common}} = 10 + 2.5 = 12.5 = I_2$ ✓

**Step 4 — Saving of copper (2 marks):**

$$\text{Saving (\%)} = \left(\frac{2K_a - 1}{K_a + 1}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{2(0.8) - 1}{0.8 + 1}\right) \times 100$$

$$= \left(\frac{1.6 - 1}{1.8}\right) \times 100 = \frac{0.6}{1.8} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

**Conclusion:** Compared to a two-winding transformer, the auto transformer uses **33.33% less copper** for the same rating. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Copper Saving, Power Components, and Variac*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the percentage saving of copper in an auto transformer as compared to a two-winding transformer. Discuss the limiting cases $K_a = 1$ and $K_a = 0.5$. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A single-phase auto transformer is used to step down 2200 V to 200 V for supplying a load of 100 A at 0.85 p.f. lagging. Calculate: (i) the transformation ratio, (ii) the primary current, (iii) the power transferred inductively and conductively, and (iv) the saving of copper. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total conductor turns in a two-winding transformer (1 mark):**

The primary has $N_1$ turns and the secondary has $N_2$ turns. The total length of conductor (assuming the same mean length of turn $L_t$) is:

$$L_{\text{2W}} = L_t (N_1 + N_2)$$

**Step 2 — Total conductor turns in an auto transformer (2 marks):**

The full primary winding has $N_1$ turns. The secondary is obtained from a tap at $N_2$ turns, so no separate secondary coil exists. The **additional series winding** required to supply the voltage difference has $(N_1 - N_2)$ turns. Hence:

$$L_{\text{auto}} = L_t N_1 + L_t (N_1 - N_2) = L_t (2N_1 - N_2)$$

**Step 3 — Absolute saving (1 mark):**

$$\Delta L = L_{\text{2W}} - L_{\text{auto}} = L_t (N_1 + N_2) - L_t (2N_1 - N_2) = L_t (2N_2 - N_1)$$

**Step 4 — Fractional saving (1 mark):**

Dividing by $L_{\text{2W}}$ and using $K_a = N_2/N_1$:

$$\text{Saving} = \frac{2N_2 - N_1}{N_1 + N_2} = \frac{2K_a - 1}{1 + K_a}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting cases (2 marks):**

- **Case 1 — $K_a = 1$ (voltages equal):**
$$\text{Saving} = \frac{2(1) - 1}{1 + 1} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$
At unity ratio, the auto transformer saves **50%** of copper — its maximum economic benefit.

- **Case 2 — $K_a = 0.5$ (voltage ratio 2:1):**
$$\text{Saving} = \frac{2(0.5) - 1}{0.5 + 1} = \frac{0}{1.5} = 0\%$$
At this ratio, the auto transformer is **no better** than a two-winding transformer in terms of material. Hence auto transformers are not used for ratios exceeding 2:1.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_1 = 2200$ V (primary, high-voltage side), $V_2 = 200$ V (load side), $I_2 = 100$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ lagging.

**Step 1 — Transformation ratio (1 mark):**

$$K_a = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{200}{2200} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

**Step 2 — Primary current (1 mark):**

$$I_1 = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{V_2 I_2}{V_1} = \frac{200 \times 100}{2200} = \frac{20\,000}{2200} \approx 9.09 \text{ A}$$

> *Equivalently, $I_1 = K_a I_2 = 0.0909 \times 100 = 9.09$ A.*

**Step 3 — Power transferred inductively and conductively (3 marks):**

Total power:

$$P_{\text{load}} = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi = 200 \times 100 \times 0.85 = 17\,000 \text{ W} = 17 \text{ kW}$$

Power transferred inductively (handled by the series winding of voltage $V_1 - V_2$):

$$P_{\text{ind}} = (V_1 - V_2) I_1 = (2200 - 200) \times 9.09 = 2000 \times 9.09 = 18\,180 \text{ W}$$

> *Wait — this exceeds total power! Let me re-check using the form $P_{\text{ind}} = V_2 I_1 \cdot$ appropriate factor.*

Recompute correctly using the relation:

$$P_{\text{ind}} = V_2 (I_2 - I_1) = 200 \times (100 - 9.09) = 200 \times 90.91 = 18\,182 \text{ W (apparent)}$$

But on the *load* side, the real power is what we care about. The *apparent* power transferred inductively is $V_2 \cdot I_{\text{common}} = 200 \times 90.91 = 18.18$ kVA. The real component (after p.f. lag) is $P_{\text{ind,real}} = 18.18 \times 0.85 = 15.45$ kW.

Power transferred conductively:

$$P_{\text{cond}} = V_2 I_1 = 200 \times 9.09 = 1818 \text{ VA} \Rightarrow P_{\text{cond,real}} = 1818 \times 0.85 = 1.55 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:** $P_{\text{ind,real}} + P_{\text{cond,real}} = 15.45 + 1.55 = 17.0$ kW $= P_{\text{load}}$ ✓

**Step 4 — Saving of copper (2 marks):**

$$\text{Saving} = \left(\frac{2K_a - 1}{K_a + 1}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{2(0.0909) - 1}{0.0909 + 1}\right) \times 100$$

$$= \left(\frac{0.1818 - 1}{1.0909}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{-0.8182}{1.0909}\right) \times 100 \approx -75\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** A **negative saving** means the auto transformer is *worse* than a two-winding transformer in terms of copper economy. This is exactly why auto transformers are **not used** for large voltage ratios like 2200/200 V (which is 11:1). The KTU board regularly tests this realisation — students who blindly compute a positive percentage without checking the sign of the result will lose **2 full marks** here.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks on Auto Transformer Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the direction of the common-winding current.** Always apply KCL at the tap point carefully. For *step-down*, $I_{\text{common}} = I_2 - I_1$ (positive, meaning current flows from tap to common terminal). For *step-up*, the sign may reverse.
>
> 2. **Blindly writing "50% saving"** without substituting $K_a$. The 50% saving applies *only* when $K_a = 1$ (equal voltages). Always substitute and compute — auto transformers for large ratios can give *negative* saving.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the unit of $K_a$.** $K_a$ is a pure dimensionless ratio. Students sometimes write "volts" or "turns" next to it — full mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of ideality.** All derived formulas assume an *ideal* transformer (no losses, no magnetising current, perfect coupling). Always state this assumption before applying formulas.
>
> 5. **Drawing the Variac without the brush.** A Variac is *defined* by its sliding carbon brush. Drawing the tap as a fixed point loses 1–2 marks in a 14-mark question.
>
> 6. **Mixing up primary and secondary.** Read the problem statement carefully: which side is connected to the source (primary) and which is connected to the load (secondary)? This determines whether $K_a > 1$ or $K_a < 1$.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to verify by adding inductive + conductive power.** This is a quick sanity check — if they don't add up to the load power, an error has been made somewhere.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Auto Transformers (Module 2, PEICT414)**
>
> - **Definition:** A single-winding transformer with a tap providing both primary and secondary voltages. (1 mark: "single winding" + "tap")
> - **Construction:** One continuous winding on a laminated core; tap brought out from the $N_2$-th turn. Two sub-sections: **series winding** (carries $I_1$) and **common winding** (carries $I_2 - I_1$).
> - **Voltage relation:** $V_2 = K_a V_1$ where $K_a = N_2/N_1$.
> - **Current relations:** $I_1 = K_a I_2$ (ideal); $I_{\text{common}} = I_2 - I_1$.
> - **Transformation ratio:** $K_a = V_2/V_1 = I_1/I_2$ (step-down form).
> - **Saving of copper:** $\text{Saving} = \left(\dfrac{2K_a - 1}{K_a + 1}\right) \times 100\%$. Maximum is 50% (at $K_a = 1$); zero at $K_a = 0.5$; *negative* for $K_a < 0.5$ (i.e., ratio > 2:1).
> - **Inductive power:** $P_{\text{ind}} = (V_1 - V_2) I_1 = V_2 (I_2 - I_1)$ — handled by the series winding.
> - **Conductive power:** $P_{\text{cond}} = V_2 I_1$ — flows directly through the common winding.
> - **Variac:** Variable auto transformer with sliding carbon brush; output 0 to 270 V from a 230 V mains.
> - **Applications:** Motor starters, transmission voltage regulators, lab Variacs.
> - **Limitation:** No electrical isolation between primary and secondary — cannot be used as an isolation transformer.
> - **Economic limit:** Only used when voltage ratio is < 2:1 (i.e., $K_a$ is between 0.5 and 1, or 1 and 2).
> - **Sanity check:** $P_{\text{ind}} + P_{\text{cond}}$ must always equal $P_{\text{load}}$.
> - **Safety:** A break in the common winding applies the full primary voltage across the load — this is a key board viva question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase Induction Motors, Slip Ring and Squirrel Cage Types, Principles of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Three Phase Induction Motors, Slip Ring and Squirrel Cage Types, Principles of Operation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Three-Phase Induction Motor (TPIM)** is a self-starting, singly-excited AC electrical machine that converts three-phase AC electrical energy into mechanical energy through the principle of **electromagnetic induction**. It operates on the interaction between a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** produced by the stator and the **induced currents in the short-circuited rotor**, without any direct electrical connection to the rotor circuit for excitation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** A 3-phase induction motor is essentially a *rotating transformer* whose secondary (rotor) is short-circuited and free to rotate. The induced EMF and current in the rotor interact with the stator's RMF to produce torque — this is the foundational principle for ALL induction machine analysis. Mathematically, the synchronous reference speed of the rotating magnetic field is given by: $$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} \;\; (\text{rpm})$$ where **f** is the supply frequency in **Hz** and **P** is the total number of **poles**. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnetic Carousel" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — "The Carousel Analogy"** > Imagine a giant invisible carousel (merry-go-round) of magnetic force created by the three-phase supply on the stator. The rotor conductors are like **swing seats** that never quite catch up to the carousel. The rotor *chases* the rotating field but can **never reach** the same speed — this permanent speed difference (called *slip*) is what keeps the induction process alive. If the rotor ever matched the field speed, no relative motion would exist, no flux would be cut, no EMF would be induced, and torque would collapse to zero. A second intuitive analogy is the **water-wheel principle**: water (flux) striking curved blades (rotor bars) creates a turning force. Here, the "water" is the rotating magnetic field and the "blades" are the rotor conductors carrying induced current. --- ### 1.3 Classification of Three-Phase Induction Motors | Type | Rotor Construction | External Rotor Resistance | Starting Method | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Squirrel Cage IM** | Cast aluminum/copper bars shorted via end rings | Fixed (very low) | Direct-on-Line (DOL) / Star-Delta | Constant speed drives, fans, pumps | | **Slip Ring (Wound Rotor) IM** | Wound 3-phase winding connected to slip rings | Variable (via rheostat) | Rotor resistance starter | High-inertia loads, cranes, elevators | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** The stator construction is **identical** for both squirrel cage and slip ring motors. The only difference lies in the **rotor design**. The stator has a balanced 3-phase distributed winding placed in laminated slots. --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard Industrial Supply Frequencies:** **50 Hz (India/EU)** and **60 Hz (US)** - **Synchronous Speed Values (50 Hz, P = 2):** **3000 rpm** ; (P = 4): **1500 rpm** ; (P = 6): **1000 rpm** - **Typical Full-Load Slip:** **2 % to 5 %** for squirrel cage motors; up to **10 %** for slip ring motors under load - **Permeability of Stator Iron Core (assumed):** $\mu_r \to \infty$ (ideal magnetic circuit assumption in equivalent circuit) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Principle of Operation — Step-by-Step Logic The complete operating principle of a 3-phase induction motor is built on **four sequential electromagnetic phenomena**. Mastering these is essential for KTU Part A and Part B questions. **Step 1 — Production of Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF):** - A balanced 3-phase supply ($V_a, V_b, V_c$) displaced by **120°** is fed to a spatially displaced 3-phase stator winding (also shifted by 120° electrical). - The combined effect of time-displaced currents in space-displaced windings produces a **magnetic field of constant magnitude** rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$. - This is the famous **Ferraris Principle (1888)**. **Step 2 — Flux Cutting / Rate of Change of Flux Linkage:** - The RMF sweeps past the stationary rotor conductors. - According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced in each rotor conductor. $$\varepsilon_{rotor} = -\frac{d\psi}{dt} = B \cdot l \cdot v_{rel}$$ where $v_{rel}$ is the **relative velocity** between the RMF and the rotor conductor. **Step 3 — Rotor Current Circulation:** - In a **squirrel cage motor**, rotor bars are pre-short-circuited by end rings → induced EMF drives large currents through the low-resistance loop. - In a **slip ring motor**, the rotor winding is connected through slip rings and can be either shorted or connected to external resistors. **Step 4 — Force Production (Lorentz Force / Lenz's Law):** - The induced current-carrying rotor conductor now sits in the stator's magnetic field. - According to **Biot-Savart's Force Law**, a mechanical force acts on the conductor: $$\vec{F} = I \cdot (\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$$ - This force, acting tangentially on the rotor periphery, produces a **torque** that drags the rotor in the **same direction** as the rotating field. > [!NOTE] > **Why the rotor can NEVER reach synchronous speed:** > If $N_r = N_s$, relative velocity is zero → no flux cutting → no induced EMF → no rotor current → no torque. Thus, a finite slip must always exist for sustained operation. This is a **2-mark favorite** in KTU exams. --- ### 2.2 Concept of Slip — The Heart of Induction Motor Analysis **Slip (s)** is defined as the **per-unit (or percentage) difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed**, expressed as a fraction. $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \;\; \text{(per unit)} \quad ; \quad s\% = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \times 100$$ **Boundary Conditions of Slip (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):** | Rotor Condition | Rotor Speed $N_r$ | Slip **s** | Rotor Frequency $f_r$ | Operating Mode | |---|---|---|---|---| | Standstill (starting) | **0** | **1.0** (100 %) | $f$ | Maximum induced EMF, no mechanical output | | Ideal no-load (hypothetical) | $\approx N_s$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | No torque (theoretical) | | Full-load (typical) | $\approx 0.95 \, N_s$ to $0.98 \, N_s$ | **0.02 to 0.05** | $sf$ (small) | Normal motoring | | Negative slip (generating) | $> N_s$ | **< 0** | $< 0$ | **Induction Generator** mode | | Slip > 1 (plugging) | Negative | **> 1** | $> f$ | **Braking** mode | **Rotor Frequency Relation:** $$f_r = s \cdot f$$ This is crucial because the rotor EMF and rotor reactance are functions of $f_r$, not $f$. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Terms | Units / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm | | 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Per-unit slip | dimensionless, $0 < s < 1$ for motoring | | 3 | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Rotor frequency | Hz | | 4 | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | Actual rotor speed | rpm | | 5 | $E_2 = 4.44 \, \phi \, f_r \, T_2 \, k_{w2}$ | Rotor induced EMF per phase (rotor side) | Volts | | 6 | $E_2 = s \cdot E_{2,\text{standstill}} = s \cdot E_{20}$ | Rotor EMF in terms of standstill value | Volts | | 7 | $X_2 = s \cdot X_{20}$ | Rotor reactance at slip **s** | Ohms | | 8 | $\phi = \dfrac{V}{4.44 \, f \, T_1 \, k_{w1}}$ | Stator flux per pole | Weber (Wb) | | 9 | $T \propto \dfrac{s \cdot E_{20}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_{20})^2}$ | Torque-slip relation (qualitative) | N·m | | 10 | $s_{\text{max torque}} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_{20}}$ | Slip at maximum torque | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Formula 9 is the backbone of ALL torque-slip characteristic questions. Memorize it. KTU typically tests the relationship, not the full derivation, in Part A (3 marks). Part B (14 marks) may require derivation from the equivalent circuit. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Three-phase induction motors consume approximately **65–70 %** of all industrial electrical energy globally, making them the most commercially significant electrical machine. They drive **pumps, compressors, conveyors, machine tools, fans, blowers, mills, and electric vehicles**. Their **ruggedness, low cost, absence of brushes, and self-starting capability** make them irreplaceable in Industry 4.0 automation. Understanding their slip behavior is critical in **V/f control** (variable frequency drives), **regenerative braking (elevators/EVs)**, and **soft starters**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed ($N_s$) **Starting Premise:** A 2-pole stator produces one north-south pair per cycle of supply. The mechanical rotation equals two poles per electrical cycle. **Step 1:** Define the relationship between electrical and mechanical degrees. For **P** poles, one mechanical revolution corresponds to **P/2** electrical cycles. $$\text{Electrical cycles per mechanical revolution} = \frac{P}{2}$$ **Step 2:** In one second, the supply completes **f** electrical cycles. Therefore, the number of mechanical revolutions per second is: $$\text{Revolutions per second} = \frac{f}{P/2} = \frac{2f}{P}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to revolutions per minute by multiplying by 60: $$N_s = \frac{2f}{P} \times 60$$ **Step 4:** Final simplified form: $$\boxed{\,N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}\,} \;\; \text{rpm}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slip and Rotor Frequency Relationship **Step 1:** By definition: $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ **Step 2:** Relative speed between rotating field and rotor (in rpm) is: $$N_{rel} = N_s - N_r = s \cdot N_s$$ **Step 3:** The frequency of induced EMF in the rotor is proportional to relative speed. Since the rotor has **P** poles (same as stator for uniform field): $$f_r = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{N_{rel}}{60}$$ **Step 4:** Substituting $N_{rel} = s \cdot N_s = s \cdot \dfrac{120f}{P}$: $$f_r = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{s \cdot 120 \, f}{60 \, P}$$ **Step 5:** Simplify $\dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{120}{60 \, P} = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2}{P} = 1$: $$\boxed{\,f_r = s \cdot f\,}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rotor EMF at Slip **s** (Standstill Reference) **Step 1:** General EMF equation for an AC winding: $$E = 4.44 \, \phi \, f \, T \, k_w$$ **Step 2:** At standstill, rotor frequency = supply frequency = $f$. So the standstill rotor EMF is: $$E_{20} = 4.44 \, \phi \, f \, T_2 \, k_{w2}$$ **Step 3:** When the rotor rotates at slip **s**, rotor frequency becomes $f_r = s f$. Since flux $\phi$ is (approximately) constant and equal to the stator-produced flux: $$E_2 = 4.44 \, \phi \, (s f) \, T_2 \, k_{w2}$$ **Step 4:** Compare with standstill value: $$E_2 = s \cdot (4.44 \, \phi \, f \, T_2 \, k_{w2}) = s \cdot E_{20}$$ $$\boxed{\,E_2 = s \cdot E_{20}\,}$$ **Step 5:** Similarly, rotor reactance at slip **s**: $$X_2 = 2 \pi \, f_r \, L_2 = 2 \pi \, (s f) \, L_2 = s \cdot X_{20}$$ $$\boxed{\,X_2 = s \cdot X_{20}\,}$$ --- ### 3.4 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU Part B Style) **Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor runs at 1440 rpm at full load. Calculate: (a) Synchronous speed (b) Per-unit slip (c) Rotor frequency (d) Rotor speed at half-load if slip is reduced to 0.02 **Solution:** **(a) Synchronous speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \frac{6000}{4} = 1500 \;\; \text{rpm}$$ **(b) Per-unit slip:** $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \;\; \text{(i.e., 4 %)}$$ **(c) Rotor frequency:** $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \;\; \text{Hz}$$ **(d) Rotor speed at slip = 0.02:** $$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.02) = 1500 \times 0.98 = 1470 \;\; \text{rpm}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Validation:** Notice that **as load decreases, slip decreases and rotor approaches synchronous speed** but never equals it. This is consistent with the principle that induction motors are essentially **constant-speed machines** with a small speed drop from no-load to full-load. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Operating Principle Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[3-Phase AC Supply
50 Hz, 120 deg displaced] node2[Stator Winding
3-Phase Distributed
Spatially 120 deg apart] node3[Rotating Magnetic Field
RMF at Ns 120f per P] node4[Rotor Conductors
Cage Bars or Wound Coil] node5{Finite Slip Exists?
Ns minus Nr greater than 0} node6[Fluz Cuts Rotor
Faradays Law] node7[Induced EMF in Rotor
E2 equals s times E20] node8[Rotor Current Flows
I2 equals E2 per Z2] node9[Lorentz Force on Conductors
F equals I times L times B] node10[Mechanical Torque Produced
T proportional to phi times I2 times cos phi2] node11[Rotor Accelerates
In direction of RMF] node12[Steady State Operation
When Load Torque equals Motor Torque] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 -- YES --> node6 node5 -- NO zero torque --> node12 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node9 --> node10 node10 --> node11 node11 --> node12 node12 --> node5 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Squirrel Cage Rotor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Stator Core
Laminated Silicon Steel] nodeB[Stator Slots
Hold 3-Phase Winding] nodeC[Air Gap
0.5 to 3 mm uniform] nodeD[Rotor Core
Laminated Silicon Steel] nodeE[Rotor Slots
Semi-Closed Type] nodeF[Conductor Bars
Cast Aluminum or Copper] nodeG[End Ring 1
Short Circuit Front] nodeH[End Ring 2
Short Circuit Rear] nodeI[Shaft
Mechanical Output] nodeJ[Fan Cowl
Cooling] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Slip Ring Rotor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeK[Stator
Identical to Squirrel Cage Type] nodeL[Air Gap] nodeM[Rotor Core with Slots] nodeN[3-Phase Rotor Winding
Wave or Lap Type] nodeO[Star Point
Internal Connection] nodeP[Slip Ring 1 R Phase] nodeQ[Slip Ring 2 Y Phase] nodeR[Slip Ring 3 B Phase] nodeS[Carbon Brush Set] nodeT[External Rheostat
Variable Rotor Resistance] nodeU[Short Circuit Switch
After Starting] nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeN nodeN --> nodeO nodeN --> nodeP nodeN --> nodeQ nodeN --> nodeR nodeP --> nodeS nodeQ --> nodeS nodeR --> nodeS nodeS --> nodeT nodeT --> nodeU ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Squirrel Cage vs Slip Ring ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeV[Three Phase Induction Motor Selection] nodeW{Starting Torque
Requirement} nodeX{Speed Control
Needed Under Load} nodeY[Squirrel Cage Type] nodeZ[Slip Ring Type] nodeAA[Rugged Cheap Low Maintenance] nodeBB[High Starting Torque
Smooth Acceleration] nodeCC[DOL or Star Delta Starter] nodeDD[Rotor Resistance Starter] nodeEE[Fans Pumps Compressors] nodeFF[Cranes Hoists Elevators
High Inertia Loads] nodeV --> nodeW nodeW -- Low Moderate --> nodeY nodeW -- High --> nodeZ nodeX -- Not Required --> nodeY nodeX -- Required --> nodeZ nodeY --> nodeAA nodeY --> nodeCC nodeY --> nodeEE nodeZ --> nodeBB nodeZ --> nodeDD nodeZ --> nodeFF ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Why can a 3-phase induction motor never run at synchronous speed under any load condition? Justify with the help of the slip concept. **Model Answer:** A 3-phase induction motor requires a **non-zero relative speed** between the stator's rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors to induce an EMF in the rotor (Faraday's law). The rotor EMF is given by $E_2 = s \cdot E_{20}$, and rotor current is $I_2 = \dfrac{E_2}{Z_2}$. The mechanical torque developed is $T \propto \phi \, I_2 \cos\phi_2$. If the rotor were to reach synchronous speed ($N_r = N_s$), then slip $s = 0$, induced EMF $E_2 = 0$, rotor current $I_2 = 0$, and hence torque $T = 0$. With zero torque, the rotor cannot sustain synchronous speed against any load torque and will decelerate back. Therefore, **a finite slip (typically 2–5 % at full load) must always exist**, and the rotor always runs at a speed **slightly less than $N_s$**. **[3 Marks Breakdown:** Faraday's link 1 M, equation $E_2 = s E_{20}$ 1 M, conclusion with slip range 1 M**]** --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between squirrel cage and slip ring induction motors based on construction, starting method, and applications. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Squirrel Cage IM | Slip Ring (Wound Rotor) IM | |---|---|---| | **Rotor Construction** | Cast aluminum/copper bars embedded in slots, permanently short-circuited by two end rings | 3-phase insulated winding on rotor, terminals brought out via 3 slip rings and brushes | | **Rotor Resistance** | Fixed and very low (inherently low) | Variable; external resistance can be added during starting | | **Starting Method** | Direct-on-Line (DOL), Star-Delta, Autotransformer starter | Rotor resistance starter using external rheostat | | **Starting Torque** | Moderate (1.5 to 2 times full load) | High (up to 2.5 to 3 times full load) | | **Speed Control** | Limited (pole changing, V/f control only) | Good (varying rotor resistance gives speed control) | | **Maintenance** | Low (no brushes, no slip rings) | Higher (brush wear, slip ring maintenance) | | **Cost** | Low | High | | **Applications** | Fans, pumps, compressors, machine tools | Cranes, hoists, elevators, conveyors with high inertia | **[3 Marks Breakdown:** Construction 1 M, Starting 1 M, Applications + comparison 1 M**]** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]`** ### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 **(a)** Derive the expression for the synchronous speed of a 3-phase induction motor. A 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4 %. Find: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Actual rotor speed, (iii) Rotor frequency, and (iv) Speed of the rotating magnetic field relative to the rotor. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the constructional features of a squirrel cage induction motor with a neat sketch. State its advantages, disadvantages, and two important applications. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation + Numerical [7 Marks] **Derivation of Synchronous Speed:** For a stator winding having **P** total poles, one mechanical revolution of the magnetic field corresponds to **P/2** electrical cycles. **Step 1:** Electrical cycles per mechanical revolution = P/2 **Step 2:** If supply frequency is **f** Hz, the number of electrical cycles per second = f **Step 3:** Mechanical revolutions per second: $$\frac{f}{P/2} = \frac{2f}{P}$$ **Step 4:** Converting to revolutions per minute (multiplying by 60): $$N_s = \frac{2f}{P} \times 60 = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \;\; \text{rpm}$$ **Numerical Computation:** Given: $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $s = 0.04$ (i.e., 4 %) **(i) Synchronous speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \frac{6000}{4} = 1500 \;\; \text{rpm}$$ **(ii) Actual rotor speed:** $$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \;\; \text{rpm}$$ **(iii) Rotor frequency:** $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \;\; \text{Hz}$$ **(iv) Relative speed between RMF and rotor:** $$N_{rel} = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1440 = 60 \;\; \text{rpm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in (a):** > 1. **Using $P$ as pole pairs** instead of total poles. A "4-pole" motor has $P = 4$, NOT 2. This is the single most common error in $N_s$ calculations. > 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion** — using $f$ directly without multiplying by 60. The formula must be $\dfrac{120f}{P}$ for rpm. > 3. **Confusing rotor frequency with supply frequency** — rotor frequency is $f_r = s f$, NOT $f$. > 4. Students often quote relative speed in Hz instead of rpm (or vice versa) — be explicit about units. #### Part (b) — Construction of Squirrel Cage IM [7 Marks] **Constructional Features:** 1. **Stator:** A cylindrical frame of cast iron or steel. Inside, a **laminated silicon steel core** with semi-closed slots houses a **3-phase distributed winding**. The windings are spatially displaced by 120° electrical. 2. **Rotor:** Also built of **laminated silicon steel** to reduce eddy current losses. The slots are typically **semi-closed** and skewed (twisted by one slot pitch) to: - Reduce cogging torque - Minimize harmonic effects - Provide smoother starting 3. **Rotor Conductors:** **Cast aluminum** (most common) or **copper bars** placed in rotor slots. In small motors, the bars, end rings, and even fan blades may be cast in a single die-casting operation. 4. **End Rings:** Two heavy **short-circuiting end rings** permanently join all rotor bars at both ends. No external electrical connection exists to the rotor. 5. **Shaft:** Mechanical output is taken through a robust mild steel shaft. 6. **Bearings & Cooling:** Ball/roller bearings with **fan cowl ventilation** for self-cooling. **Advantages:** - Extremely rugged and simple construction - Low cost (mass-produced by die-casting) - Almost maintenance-free (no brushes, no slip rings) - High reliability and long service life - Good efficiency and high overload capacity - Can operate in dirty, explosive, or corrosive environments **Disadvantages:** - Low starting torque (typically 1.5–2 × full load) - High starting current (5 to 7 times full load current) - Poor speed control (essentially constant speed machine) - Cannot add external rotor resistance **Applications:** - **Pumps and compressors** (water pumps, air compressors) - **Fans and blowers** (HVAC systems, industrial ventilation) - **Machine tool drives** (lathes, milling machines, grinders) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in (b):** > 1. **Forgetting the "skewing" of rotor slots** — this is a frequent 1-mark question. Always mention skewing with reason. > 2. **Confusing stator and rotor** — stator has a 3-phase winding; rotor of squirrel cage has NO winding, only bars. > 3. **Missing "lamination"** — both stator and rotor cores MUST be laminated to reduce eddy current losses. Examiners specifically test this. > 4. Listing applications without justification — connect each application to a specific feature (e.g., "pumps because constant torque load"). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a 3-phase induction motor. Show that the rotating magnetic field is produced by the combined effect of three-phase currents in spatially displaced coils. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the construction and working of a slip ring (wound rotor) induction motor. Discuss its starting characteristics and state its industrial applications. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — RMF and Working Principle [7 Marks] **Working Principle of 3-Phase IM:** When a balanced 3-phase supply ($I_a, I_b, I_c$ displaced by 120° in time) is fed to a 3-phase stator winding spatially displaced by 120° in space, a **magnetic field of constant magnitude** rotating at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ is produced. **Mathematical Proof of RMF:** Consider three stator coils A-A', B-B', C-C' displaced by 120° in space and carrying currents: $$i_a = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$i_b = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$i_c = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ The individual MMF waves produced by each phase are: $$F_a = F_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$$ $$F_b = F_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) \cos(\theta - 120°)$$ $$F_c = F_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) \cos(\theta - 240°)$$ **Resultant MMF** (using trigonometric identity): $$F_{total} = \frac{3}{2} F_m \sin(\omega t - \theta)$$ This represents a **sinusoidally distributed MMF wave of constant amplitude $\frac{3}{2} F_m$** rotating in space at angular velocity $\omega$. Hence the rotating magnetic field is established. **Working Steps:** 1. RMF rotates at $N_s$ in the air gap. 2. It cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing EMF (Faraday's law). 3. Induced EMF drives current through short-circuited rotor. 4. Current-carrying conductors in magnetic field experience force (Lorentz law). 5. Cumulative force produces torque, rotating the rotor in the direction of RMF. 6. Rotor settles at a speed $N_r < N_s$ where motor torque equals load torque. #### Part (b) — Slip Ring IM Construction & Working [7 Marks] **Constructional Details:** The stator is identical to that of the squirrel cage motor (laminated core, 3-phase distributed winding). The **rotor** is the distinguishing element: 1. **Rotor Core:** Laminated silicon steel with **slotted periphery**. 2. **Rotor Winding:** A **3-phase insulated double-layer winding** (lap or wave type) wound in rotor slots. The three phases are internally connected in **star** at one end. 3. **Slip Rings:** The **three open ends** of the rotor winding are brought out and connected to **three copper slip rings** mounted on the rotor shaft but insulated from it. 4. **Brushes:** **Carbon brushes** ride on the slip rings and connect the rotor circuit to **external rheostats** through flexible cables. 5. **External Circuit:** A **3-phase variable resistor (rheostat)** bank is connected across the brushes. After starting, when the motor picks up speed, the resistances are gradually cut out, and finally the rotor is short-circuited directly at the brushes via a shorting switch. **Starting Characteristics:** - At starting ($N_r = 0$, $s = 1$), the rotor EMF is at its **maximum** value $E_{20}$, and rotor frequency equals supply frequency. Adding **external rotor resistance** $R_{ext}$ increases total rotor circuit impedance. - Although rotor current reduces, the **power factor** $\cos\phi_2 = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{\sqrt{(R_2 + R_{ext})^2 + (sX_{20})^2}}$ improves dramatically. - The **starting torque** $T_{start} \propto \dfrac{s E_{20}^2 R_2^{total}}{(R_2^{total})^2 + (sX_{20})^2}$ increases significantly. - **High starting torque** (2.5 to 3 × full load torque) is obtained with **reduced starting current** — this is the primary advantage of slip ring motors. **Industrial Applications:** - **Cranes and hoists** (require high starting torque) - **Elevators and lifts** (smooth acceleration under load) - **Rolling mills** (high inertia loads with shock loading) - **Compressors with flywheel loading** - **Conveyor belts** in mining and heavy industry > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Question B:** > 1. **Forgetting to prove the RMF mathematically** — a 7-mark question on principle expects at least the resultant MMF equation $F = \frac{3}{2} F_m \sin(\omega t - \theta)$. > 2. **Stating "brushes are on stator"** — brushes ride on **rotor-mounted slip rings**, not on the stator. > 3. **Missing the role of slip rings during steady-state** — in normal running, slip rings are **short-circuited** (via the shorting switch) and operate just like a squirrel cage; brushes may be lifted to reduce wear. > 4. **Confusing the direction of rotation reversal** — for reversing rotation of a 3-phase IM, swap any **two** of the three stator supply lines (NOT rotor terminals). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (TPIM Principles)** > > - **TPIM = Rotating Transformer** → stator = primary, rotor = short-circuited secondary. > - **RMF is produced** by 3-phase supply in 3-phase spatially-shifted winding; constant magnitude $1.5 F_m$, rotates at $N_s = 120f/P$. > - **Synchronous speed depends only on f and P**, never on load. > - **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; **must be positive and finite** for motoring; can be 0 to 1 in motoring, < 0 in generating, > 1 in braking. > - **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s f$; **Rotor EMF** $E_2 = s E_{20}$; **Rotor Reactance** $X_2 = s X_{20}$. > - **Squirrel Cage:** cast bars + end rings, no slip rings, no brushes, rugged, low cost, low starting torque, fixed rotor resistance. > - **Slip Ring:** wound rotor + slip rings + brushes + external rheostat, high starting torque, variable speed control, higher maintenance. > - **Stator construction is identical** for both types — only rotor differs. > - **Rotor slot skewing** is done to reduce cogging torque and harmonics. > - **Direction of rotation** is reversed by interchanging any two stator supply phases. > - **EMF equation** general form: $E = 4.44 \, \phi \, f \, T \, k_w$. > - **Torque-slip curve** shape: parabolic rising to max torque at $s_{max} = R_2/X_{20}$, then falling to zero at $s = 1$. > - **Maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance** (only its location on slip axis changes) — a classic KTU 14-mark question. > - **Practical full-load slip range:** 2–5 % squirrel cage, 5–10 % slip ring. > - **Real-world dominant motor:** ~70 % of all industrial electric motors are 3-phase IMs. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When solving TPIM problems, **always list the given data first** ($f, P, N_r, s$ if known), then **compute $N_s$** using the formula, then **use slip relations** to find the unknowns. This systematic 3-step approach fetches full marks in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Rotating Magnetic Field, Torque Slip Characteristics, No Load and Blocked Rotor Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rotating Magnetic Field, Torque Slip Characteristics, No Load and Blocked Rotor Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** is a magnetic field whose magnitude remains **constant** but whose axis continuously rotates in space at a uniform angular velocity. It is produced when a balanced **three-phase supply** is fed to a **three-phase winding** whose coils are spatially displaced by **120° electrical** from each other.

**Mathematical Statement of RMF:**
The resultant flux phasor is the vector sum of three pulsating fluxes produced by each phase:

$$\vec{\phi}_r(t) = \vec{\phi}_a(t) + \vec{\phi}_b(t) + \vec{\phi}_c(t)$$

The magnitude $\vert \vec{\phi}_r \vert$ is a **constant (1.5 × Φ_m)** and its angular position advances by **120° electrical** every **1/3 of a cycle**.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three Watchmen Walking in a Circle" Analogy:**
> Imagine three security guards standing at the vertices of an equilateral triangle. Each holds a flashlight pointing in a fixed direction. Now, suppose their flashlights are not constant — they **pulse in brightness** one after another, with Guard A peaking first, then B, then C, in a cyclic pattern synchronized with a 3-phase supply.
> 
> Although each individual flashlight does not rotate, the **combined bright spot (the brightest region)** smoothly walks around the center. This walking bright spot is exactly what happens inside a 3-phase induction motor stator. The rotor "chases" this rotating spot, and that chase is what produces **mechanical torque**.

---

## 1.2 Torque–Slip Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Torque–Slip (T–s) characteristic** of a three-phase induction motor is a curve showing the relationship between the electromagnetic torque developed by the rotor ($T_e$) and the **slip** ($s$) of the motor, at a constant supply voltage and frequency.

**Slip is defined as:**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

where $N_s$ is the synchronous speed (in rpm) and $N_r$ is the actual rotor speed. **Slip is a dimensionless quantity**, often expressed as a per-unit value or percentage.

**Intuition:** Slip represents "how far behind the rotor is running from the synchronous rotating magnetic field." If the rotor is at standstill, $N_r = 0$, so $s = 1$ (100%). If the rotor runs at synchronous speed, $s = 0$. In real operation, $s$ lies between 0 and 1 (motoring mode).

---

## 1.3 No-Load Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **No-Load Test** (also called the **Running Light Test**) is performed on a three-phase induction motor by running it on a balanced three-phase supply at **rated voltage and frequency** with **no mechanical load** on the shaft. The input power, current, and voltage are measured.

**Purpose:** To determine the **no-load branch parameters** of the equivalent circuit:
- Core (iron) loss resistance $R_c$
- Magnetizing reactance $X_m$
- Rotational losses (friction + windage)

---

## 1.4 Blocked Rotor Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Blocked Rotor Test** (also called the **Locked Rotor Test** or **Short-Circuit Test**) is performed by mechanically locking the rotor so that it **cannot rotate**, and then applying a **reduced balanced three-phase voltage** (typically 5–10% of rated value) to the stator such that the rated current flows.

**Purpose:** To determine the **series branch parameters** of the equivalent circuit referred to the stator:
- Equivalent resistance $R_{01}$ (or $R_{eq}$)
- Equivalent leakage reactance $X_{01}$ (or $X_{eq}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Together, the No-Load and Blocked Rotor Tests allow complete determination of the **per-phase equivalent circuit** of a three-phase induction motor **without loading it** — a huge practical advantage since loading large motors (e.g., 1000 kW) in a lab is infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Production of Rotating Magnetic Field — Theory

A three-phase, two-pole stator has three identical coils **A–A′, B–B′, C–C′** placed 120° apart in space. They are fed by balanced three-phase voltages:

$$v_a(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$v_b(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$
$$v_c(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$

The corresponding fluxes (assuming linear magnetic circuit) pulsate along the coil axes:

$$\phi_a(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$\phi_b(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$
$$\phi_c(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$

**Resolving along the X-axis (along coil A):**

$$\phi_X = \phi_a + \phi_b \cos(120°) + \phi_c \cos(240°)$$

**Resolving along the Y-axis (90° ahead of coil A):**

$$\phi_Y = 0 + \phi_b \sin(120°) + \phi_c \sin(240°)$$

After trigonometric simplification, the **resultant flux** is:

$$\phi_r(t) = \sqrt{\phi_X^2 + \phi_Y^2} = 1.5\,\Phi_m \quad \text{(constant magnitude)}$$

$$\theta(t) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\phi_Y}{\phi_X}\right) = \omega t \quad \text{(linearly advancing angle)}$$

This proves: **(i)** the magnitude is **constant at 1.5 Φ_m**, and **(ii)** the flux rotates at **angular velocity ω rad/s** (synchronous speed).

**Synchronous Speed Formula:**

$$N_s = \frac{120\,f}{P} \;\;[\text{rpm}]$$

where $f$ = supply frequency in Hz, $P$ = number of poles.

---

## 2.2 Theory of Torque in an Induction Motor

The electromagnetic torque developed in an induction motor is given by the **power transfer** concept:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s}$$

where $P_{gap}$ is the air-gap power (power transferred across the air gap from stator to rotor) and $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s/60$ is the synchronous angular speed in rad/s.

The air-gap power equals the **rotor copper loss divided by slip**:

$$P_{gap} = \frac{P_{cu2}}{s} = \frac{3\,I_2^{'2}\,R_2^{'}}{s}$$

The rotor current referred to stator:

$$I_2^{'} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2^{'}}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}}$$

Hence the **general torque equation** is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2^{'}/s)}{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2^{'}}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}$$

### 2.2.1 Starting Torque ($s = 1$)

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 R_2^{'}}{(R_1 + R_2^{'})^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}$$

### 2.2.2 Maximum Torque Condition

Maximum torque occurs when:

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2^{'}}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}}$$

The **maximum (breakdown) torque** is:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3\,V_1^2}{2\,\omega_s\,\left[R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_2'$. Changing $R_2'$ shifts the slip at which max torque occurs, but the **magnitude** of $T_{max}$ remains the same. This is the principle behind **adding external rotor resistance** for high-starting-torque designs (e.g., slip-ring induction motors).

### 2.2.3 Ratio of Starting Torque to Maximum Torque

$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{2\,R_2^{'}\,\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}}{(R_1 + R_2^{'})^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}$$

For a typical motor where $R_1 \ll X_1 + X_2'$, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} \approx \frac{2\,R_2^{'}}{X_1 + X_2^{'}}$$

---

## 2.3 Thevenin's Equivalent & Simplified Torque Equation

For deeper analysis, the stator side is reduced to a **Thevenin equivalent**:

$$V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \frac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$$

$$Z_{th} = \frac{jX_m (R_1 + jX_1)}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$$

For typical motors, $X_m \gg R_1$, so:

$$V_{th} \approx V_1 \cdot \frac{X_m}{X_1 + X_m} \quad ; \quad Z_{th} \approx R_1 \cdot \frac{X_m}{X_1 + X_m} + j\,X_1 \cdot \frac{X_m}{X_1 + X_m}$$

The torque expression becomes:

$$T_e = \frac{3\,V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2^{'}/s)}{\omega_s \left[\left(R_{th} + \frac{R_2^{'}}{s}\right)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2^{'})^2\right]}$$

---

## 2.4 No-Load Test — Analytical Theory

During no-load, slip $s \approx 0$, so $R_2^{'}/s \to \infty$. Almost all the input current flows through the **magnetizing branch** $R_c \parallel jX_m$.

**Measurements:**
- Line voltage $V_L$ (rated)
- No-load line current $I_0$
- Total no-load input power $P_0$ (per phase: $P_{0,ph} = P_0/3$)

**Per-phase calculations (assuming wye connection):**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad I_{ph} = I_0, \quad P_{ph} = \frac{P_0}{3}$$

**No-load power factor angle:**

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_0}$$

**Magnetizing branch admittance:**

$$Y_0 = \frac{I_0}{V_{ph}} = G_c - jB_m = \frac{1}{R_c} - \frac{j}{X_m}$$

Therefore:

$$R_c = \frac{V_{ph}^2}{P_{core\,(per\,phase)}}, \quad X_m = \frac{V_{ph}^2}{Q_0\,(per\,phase)}$$

where $Q_0$ is the no-load reactive power per phase.

**Loss segregation** (using the constant-loss / variable-loss split):
- $P_0 = P_{core} + P_{mech}$ (friction + windage)
- $P_{mech}$ is found by plotting $P_0$ vs. $V^2$ and extrapolating to $V=0$ (Y-intercept gives mechanical loss, slope gives $P_{core}/V^2$).

---

## 2.5 Blocked Rotor Test — Analytical Theory

During blocked rotor, $s = 1$, so $R_2^{'}/s = R_2^{'}$. The magnetizing branch $X_m$ is **negligibly small** compared to the leakage reactances and is **shorted out** in the equivalent circuit.

**Reduced equivalent circuit:** $R_1 + R_2^{'}$ in series with $j(X_1 + X_2^{'})$.

**Measurements (at reduced voltage):**
- Blocked rotor line voltage $V_{sc}$ (5–10% of rated)
- Blocked rotor line current $I_{sc}$ (≈ rated)
- Blocked rotor input power $P_{sc}$

**Per-phase:**

$$V_{ph,sc} = \frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad Z_{01} = \frac{V_{ph,sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{3\,I_{sc}^2} = R_1 + R_2^{'}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = X_1 + X_2^{'}$$

**Assumption for splitting** $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ between stator and rotor:
- $R_1 = R_2^{'} = R_{01}/2$
- $X_1 = X_2^{'} = X_{01}/2$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for this module. Memorize it thoroughly.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Conditions** |
|------|------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | rpm; $f$ in Hz |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per unit; dimensionless |
| 3 | Synchronous Angular Speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 4 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz |
| 5 | Resultant RMF Magnitude | $\phi_r = 1.5\,\Phi_m$ | Weber (constant) |
| 6 | Electromagnetic Torque (general) | $T_e = \dfrac{3\,V_1^2\,(R_2^{'}/s)}{\omega_s\left[\left(R_1 + R_2^{'}/s\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2\right]}$ | N·m |
| 7 | Starting Torque ($s=1$) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3\,V_1^2\,R_2^{'}}{\omega_s\left[(R_1 + R_2^{'})^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2\right]}$ | N·m |
| 8 | Slip at Max Torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2^{'}}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Maximum Torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3\,V_1^2}{2\,\omega_s\left[R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}\right]}$ | N·m (independent of $R_2'$) |
| 10 | Air-Gap Power | $P_{gap} = \dfrac{3\,I_2^{'2}\,R_2^{'}}{s}$ | Watts |
| 11 | Rotor Copper Loss | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ | Watts |
| 12 | Mechanical Power Developed | $P_{m} = (1-s)\,P_{gap}$ | Watts |
| 13 | No-Load P.F. (per phase) | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_0}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_0}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | No-Load Resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{V_{ph}^2}{P_{core,ph}}$ | Ohms |
| 15 | No-Load Reactance | $X_m = \dfrac{V_{ph}^2}{Q_{0,ph}}$ | Ohms |
| 16 | Blocked Rotor Impedance | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc,ph}}{I_{sc}}$ | Ohms |
| 17 | Blocked Rotor Resistance | $R_{01} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{3\,I_{sc}^2}$ | Ohms |
| 18 | Blocked Rotor Reactance | $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$ | Ohms |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Slip-ring IM with rotor resistance control:** $s_{max} \propto R_2'$, so adding external rotor resistance pulls the maximum torque point toward $s=1$ — giving **high starting torque** for cranes, elevators, and hoist drives.
> - **Soft starters & VFDs:** $T_{max} \propto V_1^2$, so reducing voltage drastically reduces torque. Variable Frequency Drives vary $V/f$ to maintain flux and torque.
> - **No-Load & Blocked Rotor Tests** are mandatory **acceptance tests** in factories (per **IS 325 / IEC 60034**) for every induction motor above 1 kW before dispatch — no load bank required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resultant Rotating Magnetic Field

We have the three pulsating fluxes along the three coil axes (displaced 120° in space):

$$\phi_a(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$\phi_b(t) = \Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$$
$$\phi_c(t) = \Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Set up a 2-axis coordinate system. Let the X-axis be along coil A. The Y-axis is 90° ahead (rotated by $+90°$).

**Step 2:** Resolve $\phi_b$ and $\phi_c$ onto X and Y axes. Coil B is 120° from coil A, coil C is 240° from coil A.

**X-component of resultant:**

$$\phi_X = \phi_a + \phi_b \cos(120°) + \phi_c \cos(240°)$$

**Y-component of resultant:**

$$\phi_Y = 0 + \phi_b \sin(120°) + \phi_c \sin(240°)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the time-varying expressions.

$$\phi_X = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) + \Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)\cos(120°) + \Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)\cos(240°)$$

**Step 4:** Use the identity $\sin(\alpha)\cos(\beta) = \tfrac{1}{2}[\sin(\alpha + \beta) + \sin(\alpha - \beta)]$ and $\cos(120°) = -0.5$, $\cos(240°) = -0.5$.

$$\phi_X = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) - 0.5\,\Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) - 0.5\,\Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$$

Using the sum-to-product identity $\sin(A-B) + \sin(A+B) = 2\sin A \cos B$:

$$-0.5\left[\sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) + \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)\right] = -0.5 \cdot 2 \sin(\omega t) \cos(120°) = 0.5\sin(\omega t)$$

Therefore:

$$\phi_X = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) + 0.5 \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) = 1.5\,\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5:** Repeat for Y-component. Using $\sin(120°) = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\sin(240°) = -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$$\phi_Y = 0.866\,\Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) - 0.866\,\Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$$

Using $\sin(A-B) - \sin(A+B) = -2\cos A \sin B$:

$$\phi_Y = -1.732 \cdot \Phi_m \cos(\omega t) \sin\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) \cdot 0.5 \quad \text{(after careful algebra)}$$

Carrying out the algebra cleanly:

$$\phi_Y = \Phi_m \cdot 0.866 \left[\sin\!\left(\omega t - 120°\right) - \sin\!\left(\omega t + 120°\right)\right] = \Phi_m \cdot 0.866 \cdot (-2 \cos(\omega t) \sin(120°))$$

Wait — let me re-apply the identity carefully. Using $\sin P - \sin Q = 2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{P+Q}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\tfrac{P-Q}{2}\right)$:

$$P = \omega t - 120°, \quad Q = \omega t + 120°$$
$$P + Q = 2\omega t, \quad P - Q = -240°$$
$$\phi_Y = 0.866\,\Phi_m \cdot 2 \cos(\omega t) \cdot \sin(-120°) = 0.866\,\Phi_m \cdot 2 \cos(\omega t) \cdot (-0.866)$$
$$\phi_Y = -1.5\,\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 6:** Combine X and Y components into magnitude and phase.

$$\phi_r^2 = \phi_X^2 + \phi_Y^2 = (1.5\,\Phi_m)^2 \sin^2(\omega t) + (1.5\,\Phi_m)^2 \cos^2(\omega t)$$

$$\boxed{\phi_r = 1.5\,\Phi_m \quad \text{(constant magnitude)}}$$

**Step 7:** The angular position is:

$$\theta(t) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\phi_Y}{\phi_X}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-1.5\,\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)}{1.5\,\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(-\cot(\omega t)\right) = \omega t$$

**Conclusion:** The resultant flux has constant magnitude **1.5 Φ_m** and rotates at **angular velocity ω**, i.e., at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation: General Torque Equation

**Starting point:** Air-gap power equals $P_{cu2}/s$, so:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} = \frac{P_{cu2}}{s\,\omega_s} = \frac{3\,I_2^{'2}\,R_2^{'}}{s\,\omega_s}$$

**Step 1:** Express $I_2'$ from the approximate equivalent circuit (stator impedance neglected, $V_1$ applied directly to rotor branch):

$$I_2^{'} = \frac{V_1}{Z_2^{'}} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2^{'}}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the torque equation:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2\,(R_2^{'}/s)}{\left(\dfrac{R_2^{'}}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s^2$:

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3\,V_1^2\,(R_2^{'}/s)}{\omega_s\left[\left(\dfrac{R_2^{'}}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2\right]}}$$

Including stator resistance $R_1$ (exact form):

$$T_e = \frac{3\,V_1^2\,(R_2^{'}/s)}{\omega_s\left[\left(R_1 + \dfrac{R_2^{'}}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2\right]}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Slip at Maximum Torque

Starting from the simplified torque (ignoring $R_1$):

$$T_e = \frac{3\,V_1^2\,(R_2^{'}/s)}{\omega_s\left[(R_2^{'}/s)^2 + X_{total}^2\right]}$$

where $X_{total} = X_1 + X_2'$.

**Step 1:** Set $dT_e/ds = 0$ for maxima/minima. Let $u = R_2'/s$ (so $du/ds = -R_2'/s^2 = -u/s$).

$$T_e = \frac{3\,V_1^2\,u}{\omega_s\,(u^2 + X_{total}^2)}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $u$:

$$\frac{dT_e}{du} = \frac{3\,V_1^2}{\omega_s}\cdot\frac{(u^2 + X_{total}^2) - u(2u)}{(u^2 + X_{total}^2)^2} = 0$$

The numerator must be zero:

$$u^2 + X_{total}^2 - 2u^2 = 0 \implies X_{total}^2 = u^2 \implies u = X_{total}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back $u = R_2'/s = X_{total}$:

$$\boxed{s_{max} = \frac{R_2^{'}}{X_1 + X_2^{'}}}$$

When $R_1$ is included (full exact form), the same procedure gives:

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2^{'}}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}}$$

**Step 3:** Maximum torque magnitude. Substitute $u = X_{total}$ into the simplified $T_e$ equation:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3\,V_1^2\,X_{total}}{\omega_s\,(X_{total}^2 + X_{total}^2)} = \frac{3\,V_1^2}{2\,\omega_s\,X_{total}} = \frac{3\,V_1^2}{2\,\omega_s\,(X_1 + X_2^{'})}$$

Including $R_1$:

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3\,V_1^2}{2\,\omega_s\left[R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}\right]}}$$

Note: $T_{max}$ has **no $R_2'$** in the numerator — it is independent of rotor resistance. ∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation: No-Load Test Calculations

Given measured $V_L$, $I_0$, $P_0$ on a wye-connected motor:

**Step 1 — Per-phase quantities:**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad I_{ph} = I_0, \quad P_{ph} = \frac{P_0}{3}$$

**Step 2 — No-load impedance per phase:**

$$Z_0 = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_0}$$

**Step 3 — No-load power factor angle and components:**

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_0}, \quad \sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_0}$$

**Step 4 — Active (resistive) and reactive components of no-load current:**

$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 \quad \text{(working component)}$$
$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 \quad \text{(magnetizing component)}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent parameters:**

$$\boxed{R_c = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_w} = \frac{V_{ph}^2}{P_{ph}}}$$
$$\boxed{X_m = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_\mu}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Loss segregation (optional but KTU-favorite):** Plot $P_0$ vs $V_L^2$. The straight-line fit gives $P_0 = mV_L^2 + c$, where $c$ (intercept at $V=0$) is **rotational (mechanical) loss** and $mV_L^2$ is the **core loss** at operating voltage.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Blocked Rotor Test Calculations

Given measured $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{sc}$ on a wye-connected motor:

**Step 1 — Per-phase quantities:**

$$V_{ph,sc} = \frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad I_{ph,sc} = I_{sc}, \quad P_{ph,sc} = \frac{P_{sc}}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent impedance per phase:**

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{ph,sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}\,I_{sc}}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent resistance per phase:**

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{3\,I_{sc}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent leakage reactance per phase:**

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{ph,sc}}{I_{sc}}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{P_{sc}}{3\,I_{sc}^2}\right)^2}$$

$$\boxed{X_{01} = X_1 + X_2^{'}}$$

**Step 5 — Stator/rotor split (assumption, valid for design B motors):**

$$R_1 = R_2^{'} = \frac{R_{01}}{2}, \quad X_1 = X_2^{'} = \frac{X_{01}}{2}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole, star-connected induction motor gave the following test results:
- **No-load test:** $V_L = 400$ V, $I_0 = 9$ A, $P_0 = 1310$ W
- **Blocked rotor test:** $V_{sc} = 90$ V, $I_{sc} = 45$ A, $P_{sc} = 2730$ W

Find: (a) Synchronous speed and no-load slip, (b) No-load branch parameters $R_c$ and $X_m$, (c) Equivalent series parameters $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$.

### Solution:

**(a) Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**No-load slip** (assuming no-load speed $N_r \approx 1490$ rpm):

$$s_0 = \frac{1500 - 1490}{1500} = 0.00667 \text{ p.u.}$$

**(b) No-load parameters:**

Per-phase voltage: $V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V.

No-load power factor:

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{1310}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 9} = \frac{1310}{6235.4} = 0.2101$$

$\sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.2101^2} = 0.9777$

Working component of current:

$$I_w = 9 \times 0.2101 = 1.891 \text{ A}$$

Magnetizing component:

$$I_\mu = 9 \times 0.9777 = 8.799 \text{ A}$$

Therefore:

$$R_c = \frac{230.94}{1.891} = 122.13 \text{ Ω}$$
$$X_m = \frac{230.94}{8.799} = 26.24 \text{ Ω}$$

**(c) Blocked rotor parameters:**

Per-phase short-circuit voltage: $V_{ph,sc} = 90/\sqrt{3} = 51.96$ V.

$$Z_{01} = \frac{51.96}{45} = 1.155 \text{ Ω}$$

$$R_{01} = \frac{2730}{3 \times 45^2} = \frac{2730}{6075} = 0.4494 \text{ Ω}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{1.155^2 - 0.4494^2} = \sqrt{1.334 - 0.202} = \sqrt{1.132} = 1.064 \text{ Ω}$$

**Final answers:**

$$\boxed{N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm}; \quad R_c = 122.13 \text{ Ω}; \quad X_m = 26.24 \text{ Ω}; \quad R_{01} = 0.4494 \text{ Ω}; \quad X_{01} = 1.064 \text{ Ω}}$$

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Production of Rotating Magnetic Field

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["3-Phase<br>Balanced<br>AC Supply"] --> B1["Stator<br>Winding<br>Phase A"]
    A1 --> B2["Stator<br>Winding<br>Phase B"]
    A1 --> B3["Stator<br>Winding<br>Phase C"]
    B1 --> C1["Pulsating<br>Flux Phi-a"]
    B2 --> C2["Pulsating<br>Flux Phi-b"]
    B3 --> C3["Pulsating<br>Flux Phi-c"]
    C1 --> D1["Vector<br>Summation<br>in Space"]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["Resultant<br>Rotating<br>Magnetic Field<br>1.5 Phi-m"]
    E1 --> F1["Rotor<br>Chases<br>Field"]
    F1 --> G1["Mechanical<br>Torque<br>on Shaft"]
    E1 --> H1["Synchronous<br>Speed Ns<br>120f over P"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Torque-Slip Curve Generation

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Slip s<br>varies from 0 to 1"] --> Q1["Rotor Reactance<br>X2 prime s"]
    S1 --> Q2["Rotor Resistance<br>R2 prime over s"]
    Q1 --> R1["Impedance<br>Magnitude Z"]
    Q2 --> R1
    R1 --> T1["Rotor Current<br>I2 prime"]
    T1 --> U1["Air Gap Power<br>Pgap"]
    U1 --> V1["Electromagnetic<br>Torque Te<br>Pgap over omega-s"]
    V1 --> W1["Plot T vs s<br>Torque Slip Curve"]
    W1 --> X1["Region 1<br>Motoring 0 less than s less than 1"]
    W1 --> Y1["Region 2<br>Plugging 1 less than s less than 2"]
    W1 --> Z1["Region 3<br>Generating s less than 0"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: No-Load and Blocked Rotor Test Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Supply
        SUPPLY["3-Phase<br>Variable<br>AC Source"]
    end
    SUPPLY --> AMM1["Ammeter<br>I0 or Isc"]
    AMM1 --> MOTOR["Induction<br>Motor<br>Stator"]
    SUPPLY --> VOLT["Voltmeter<br>V0 or Vsc"]
    VOLT --> MOTOR
    SUPPLY --> WATTMETER["3-Phase<br>Wattmeter<br>P0 or Psc"]
    WATTMETER --> MOTOR
    MOTOR --> NL["No Load<br>Test<br>Rotor Free<br>s near 0"]
    MOTOR --> BR["Blocked Rotor<br>Test<br>Rotor Locked<br>s equals 1"]
    NL --> O1["Yields<br>Rc and Xm"]
    BR --> O2["Yields<br>R01 and X01"]
    O1 --> FIN["Complete<br>Equivalent<br>Circuit"]
    O2 --> FIN
```

## 4.4 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit Block Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1["V1<br>Stator Voltage"] --> R1["R1<br>Stator Resistance"]
    R1 --> X1["jX1<br>Stator Leakage Reactance"]
    X1 --> NODE1["Air Gap Node"]
    NODE1 --> RC["Rc<br>Core Loss Branch"]
    NODE1 --> XM["jXm<br>Magnetizing Reactance"]
    NODE1 --> R2S["R2 prime over s<br>Rotor Resistance<br>Referred to Stator"]
    R2S --> X2["jX2 prime<br>Rotor Leakage Reactance"]
    X2 --> GND["Reference Node"]
    RC --> GND
    XM --> GND
```

## 4.5 Torque-Slip Curve — Qualitative Block Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Slip s = 0<br>Te = 0<br>Synchronous Speed"] --> B["Slip Small<br>0.02 to 0.05<br>Normal Operation<br>Te rising"]
    B --> C["Slip s-max<br>R2 prime over sqrt R1 squared plus X1 plus X2 prime squared<br>T-max reached"]
    C --> D["Slip = 1<br>Starting Torque T-st<br>Less than T-max for cage IM"]
    D --> E["Slip greater than 1<br>Plugging Region<br>Torque reverses"]
    E --> F["Slip less than 0<br>Generator Mode<br>Negative Torque"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Torque-Slip Curve:**
> - **Point A** ($s=0$): Synchronous speed, no torque. The rotor cannot reach this in practice.
> - **Point B** (low slip): Normal motoring region — stable, high efficiency.
> - **Point C** ($s = s_{max}$): Maximum (breakdown) torque — the absolute upper limit before stalling.
> - **Point D** ($s=1$): Starting torque — the torque available at standstill.
> - **Points E, F**: Plugging and generating regions (outside normal motoring).

## 4.6 Influence of Rotor Resistance on Torque-Slip Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Original Rotor<br>Resistance R2-a"] --> B["Max Torque Point A<br>s-max A equals R2-a over X"]
    C["Increased Rotor<br>Resistance R2-b equals 2 R2-a"] --> D["Max Torque Point B<br>s-max B equals 2 s-max A"]
    E["Rotor Resistance R2-c large"] --> F["Max Torque at s equals 1<br>Starting Torque equals Max Torque"]
    B --> G["T-max Magnitude<br>UNCHANGED"]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    H["Conclusion"] --> I["Increasing R2 prime<br>Shifts Curve Right<br>T-max Magnitude Fixed"]
```
