# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **KTU Pattern (Module 3, 3 marks each):** These are direct conceptual questions testing definitions, formulas, or one-line derivations.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Rotating Magnetic Field. State the expression for synchronous speed.**

**Model Answer:**
> A Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) is a magnetic field of constant magnitude ($1.5\,\Phi_m$) that continuously rotates in space at synchronous angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s. It is produced by feeding a balanced 3-phase AC supply to a 3-phase winding whose coils are displaced by 120° in space.
>
> $$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120\,f}{P} \text{ rpm}}$$

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is slip in an induction motor? Why cannot a 3-phase induction motor run at synchronous speed?**

**Model Answer:**
> Slip $s$ is the relative difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed, expressed as a fraction of synchronous speed:
> $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$
> 
> A 3-phase induction motor **cannot run at synchronous speed** because, if $N_r = N_s$, then $s = 0$, rotor conductors experience **no relative motion** with respect to the rotating magnetic field, **no EMF is induced**, **no rotor current flows**, and therefore **no torque is produced**. The rotor would decelerate due to friction until slip becomes non-zero.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice (i.e., two alternative full questions of 14 marks each). Each question typically has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the resultant rotating magnetic field produced by a balanced 3-phase supply. Show that its magnitude is constant ($1.5\,\Phi_m$). **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has a slip of 4% at full load. Find: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Actual rotor speed, (iii) Frequency of rotor EMF, (iv) Mechanical power developed if the air-gap power is 12 kW. **\[7 marks\]**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

We start with three pulsating fluxes displaced 120° in space and 120° in time:

$$\phi_a = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t), \quad \phi_b = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°), \quad \phi_c = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$

Resolving along X and Y axes (see Section 3.1 for full derivation):

$$\phi_X = 1.5\,\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$\phi_Y = -1.5\,\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

Magnitude:

$$\phi_r = \sqrt{\phi_X^2 + \phi_Y^2} = 1.5\,\Phi_m \sqrt{\sin^2\omega t + \cos^2\omega t} = \boxed{1.5\,\Phi_m}$$

Angular position:

$$\theta(t) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\phi_Y}{\phi_X}\right) = \omega t$$

The field rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s, completing one revolution in $T = 1/f$ seconds. With $P$ poles, mechanical speed is $N_s = 120f/P$ rpm. ∎

#### **Model Solution (b):**

Given: $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$, $s = 0.04$, $P_{gap} = 12$ kW.

**(i) Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Actual rotor speed:**

$$N_r = N_s(1 - s) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.04) = \boxed{960 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(iii) Frequency of rotor EMF:**

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = \boxed{2 \text{ Hz}}$$

**(iv) Mechanical power developed:**

$$P_m = (1 - s) P_{gap} = (1 - 0.04) \times 12 = \boxed{11.52 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable equivalent circuit, derive the **general torque equation** of a 3-phase induction motor. Hence obtain the condition for **maximum torque** and the expression for **maximum torque**. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the **No-Load Test** and **Blocked Rotor Test** on a 3-phase induction motor. Show how the equivalent circuit parameters ($R_c$, $X_m$, $R_{01}$, $X_{01}$) are determined from the test readings. **\[7 marks\]**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

Refer to Section 3.2 and 3.3 of these notes for the **complete derivation**. Key final expressions:

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3\,V_1^2\,(R_2^{'}/s)}{\omega_s\left[\left(R_1 + R_2^{'}/s\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2\right]}}$$

**Condition for maximum torque** (from $dT_e/ds = 0$):

$$\boxed{s_{max} = \frac{R_2^{'}}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}}}$$

**Maximum torque:**

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3\,V_1^2}{2\,\omega_s\left[R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2^{'})^2}\right]}}$$

**Key inference:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_2'$. Adding external rotor resistance shifts the slip $s_{max}$ but does not change the maximum torque magnitude — this is the basis of rotor-resistance starting methods.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**No-Load Test:**
- Motor runs on **rated voltage and frequency** with **no mechanical load**.
- Measure: line voltage $V_L$, line current $I_0$, total input power $P_0$.
- Since slip $s \approx 0$ at no-load, the rotor branch impedance is very high; almost all current flows through the magnetizing branch.
- Per-phase quantities: $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$, $I_{ph} = I_0$, $P_{ph} = P_0/3$.
- Power factor: $\cos\phi_0 = P_0/(\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_0)$.
- Working and magnetizing components: $I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0$, $I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0$.
- Therefore:
$$R_c = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_w} = \frac{V_{ph}^2}{P_{ph}}, \quad X_m = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_\mu}$$

**Blocked Rotor Test:**
- Rotor is **mechanically locked**. Reduced voltage (5–10% of rated) is applied.
- Measure: $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{sc}$.
- At $s=1$, the magnetizing branch is negligible and is shorted out; the rotor branch appears as $R_2' + jX_2'$.
- Per-phase: $V_{ph,sc} = V_{sc}/\sqrt{3}$.
- Equivalent impedance: $Z_{01} = V_{ph,sc}/I_{sc}$.
- Resistance: $R_{01} = P_{sc}/(3 I_{sc}^2)$.
- Reactance: $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** when converting total 3-phase power to per-phase power. Always write $P_{ph} = P_{total}/3$ explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_L$ and $V_{ph}$** — for star-connected motors, $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$.
> 3. **In Blocked Rotor Test, students forget** that the magnetizing branch is neglected — examiner deducts 1 mark if the assumption is not stated.
> 4. **Sign of slip in plugging/generating regions** — KTU loves to ask "if $s = -0.05$ or $s = 1.5$, what is the operating mode?" Memorize: $0 < s < 1$ motoring, $s > 1$ plugging, $s < 0$ generating.
> 5. **The expression for $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2'$** — this is a KTU favorite. If asked "does increasing rotor resistance increase $T_{max}$?", the answer is **NO** — it only shifts the slip at which $T_{max}$ occurs.
> 6. **In derivation questions**, students often skip the intermediate trig identities. Always show the $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$ simplification explicitly for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Questions (Self-Assessment)

### **Q3. [KTU University Exam - 2022]**
A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has rotor resistance and reactance (per phase referred to stator) of $0.2\,\Omega$ and $1.0\,\Omega$ respectively. Stator impedance is negligible. Calculate:
(a) Slip at maximum torque
(b) Maximum torque if the supply voltage is 415 V (line-to-line, star-connected)
(c) Starting torque

**Solution Hints:**
- $V_{ph} = 415/\sqrt{3} \approx 239.6$ V
- $\omega_s = 2\pi(1500)/60 = 50\pi$ rad/s
- $s_{max} = R_2'/\sqrt{R_1^2 + X_{total}^2} = 0.2/\sqrt{0.04 + 1.0} = 0.196$
- $T_{max} = 3V_{ph}^2/(2\omega_s X_{total}) = 3(239.6)^2/(2 \cdot 50\pi \cdot 1.0) \approx 548$ N·m
- $T_{st} = 3V_{ph}^2 R_2'/\omega_s[(R_1 + R_2')^2 + X_{total}^2] \approx 161$ N·m

### **Q4. [KTU University Exam - 2021]**
Explain how the **rotor resistance** affects the **torque-slip characteristics** of a 3-phase induction motor. Sketch the curves for two different rotor resistances.

**Solution Outline:** Three points to cover:
1. $s_{max} \propto R_2'$ — slip at maximum torque increases with rotor resistance.
2. $T_{max}$ is **independent** of $R_2'$ — the curve peaks at the **same height** for all $R_2'$.
3. Starting torque $T_{st}$ **increases** with $R_2'$ (for cage motors) up to $T_{max}$.
4. Drawing: Show two curves on the same T-s plane, both peaking at the same $T_{max}$ but at different slips.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **RMF magnitude** = $1.5\,\Phi_m$ (constant) and rotates at $N_s = 120f/P$ rpm.
- [ ] **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. At standstill $s=1$, at synchronous speed $s=0$.
- [ ] **General torque equation** is the single most important formula — memorize the form with both $R_1$ and $R_2'/s$ in the denominator.
- [ ] **Maximum torque** $T_{max}$ depends on $V_1^2$ and $X_1+X_2'$, but **NOT on $R_2'$**.
- [ ] **Slip at max torque** $s_{max} = R_2'/\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2} \approx R_2'/(X_1+X_2')$ when $R_1$ is small.
- [ ] **Increasing rotor resistance** shifts the torque-slip curve to the **right** (higher $s_{max}$) and **increases starting torque** without changing $T_{max}$ — this is the principle of slip-ring IM starters.
- [ ] **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s \cdot f$.
- [ ] **Air-gap power** = $P_{cu2}/s$; **Rotor Cu loss** = $s P_{gap}$; **Mechanical power** = $(1-s) P_{gap}$.
- [ ] **No-Load Test** gives $R_c$ and $X_m$ (magnetizing branch).
- [ ] **Blocked Rotor Test** gives $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ (series/short-circuit branch).
- [ ] **Per-phase convention** for star-connected motors: $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$, $P_{ph} = P_{total}/3$.
- [ ] **No-load slip** is typically $0.001$ to $0.01$ — almost zero, so $R_2'/s$ is huge.
- [ ] **Three operating regions** of induction machine: Motoring ($0 < s < 1$), Generating ($s < 0$), Plugging ($s > 1$).
- [ ] **Thevenin's theorem** applied to stator side yields a simplified circuit for cleaner torque analysis.
- [ ] **Loss segregation**: No-load losses = Core loss + Friction & windage; segregation done by plotting $P_0$ vs $V^2$.
- [ ] **$T \propto V^2$** at any slip — critical for understanding soft-starter and VFD-based speed control.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Diagram for Quick Recall:**
>
> **Equivalent Circuit (per phase, referred to stator):**
> 
> $V_1 \rightarrow [R_1] \rightarrow [jX_1] \rightarrow \text{NODE} \rightarrow \text{(parallel branch)} \begin{cases} R_c \\ jX_m \end{cases} \rightarrow \text{(series branch)} [R_2'/s] \rightarrow [jX_2'] \rightarrow 0$
>
> **Power flow:** $P_{in} \rightarrow P_{stator\,Cu} \rightarrow P_{core} \rightarrow P_{gap} \rightarrow P_{rotor\,Cu} \rightarrow P_{mech} \rightarrow P_{shaft}$
>
> **Numerical identities:** $P_{gap} = P_{rotor\,Cu}/s = P_{mech}/(1-s)$

**End of Module 3 Notes — Good luck on your KTU exam! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Starting of Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Starting of Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Starting of an Induction Motor** refers to the method employed to bring the stationary rotor of a three-phase (or single-phase) squirrel-cage or wound-rotor (slip-ring) induction motor from rest (slip $s = 1$) up to a safe, stable operating speed close to synchronous speed without causing excessive current draw, supply disturbance, or mechanical stress on the windings, shaft, and coupled load.

At the instant of starting:
- Rotor speed $N_r = 0$, hence slip $s = 1$
- The frequency of rotor currents $f_r = s \cdot f_s = f_s$ (supply frequency)
- The rotor reactance is at its **maximum** (locked rotor condition)
- The rotor resistance appears small compared to its reactance

This results in a large inrush current (typically **5 to 7 times** the full-load current) and a poor starting torque/power factor — the two fundamental problems that *every* starting method tries to mitigate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The student is expected to derive expressions for starting torque, explain the necessity of starters, classify starting methods for both Squirrel-Cage and Slip-Ring (Wound Rotor) Induction Motors, and compare their performance using torque-slip characteristics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a heavy, oil-drum-laden cart (the rotor + load) from rest on a level road. The instant you push, the cart is stationary — your legs must work the hardest right at $t=0$ to overcome **static friction**. Once the cart begins rolling, kinetic friction takes over and your effort drops dramatically. Now imagine the cart is so heavy that *you cannot budge it directly* — what do you do?

- **Method 1 (DOL):** Get 6 friends to push simultaneously — guaranteed motion, but you may damage the road, and your neighbours will complain about the noise.
- **Method 2 (Star-Delta):** Get only 2 friends to push first to get it rolling, then bring in the remaining 4 once it is moving — gentle on everyone.
- **Method 3 (Auto-Transformer):** Use a mechanical lever to amplify your push — less effort from you, same final force.
- **Method 4 (Rotor Resistance):** Instead of reducing the push, reduce the load on the cart by removing some of the oil drums temporarily.

The induction motor faces the exact same dilemma: high locked-rotor current and low starting torque. Every starter is a clever engineering "trick" to balance these twin evils.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - $V_L$ — Line Voltage (**415 V** standard for 3-phase, India)
> - $V_{ph}$ — Phase Voltage ($V_L/\sqrt{3}$ = **239.6 V** in star)
> - $f_s$ — Supply Frequency (**50 Hz** in India)
> - $N_s = 120 f_s / P$ — Synchronous Speed (rpm)
> - Starting current multiplier for DOL: **5 to 7** times FLC
> - Standard auto-transformer taps: **50 %, 60 %, 70 %, 80 %** of supply voltage

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Twin Problems of Induction Motor Starting

To deeply understand *why* starters are necessary, we must inspect the equivalent circuit at $s = 1$.

At starting, the rotor is stationary. The rotor EMF ($E_2$) is at its **maximum** value because it is induced by the full rotating air-gap flux at line frequency. This maximum EMF drives a huge rotor current, which — through the magnetising reaction — causes the stator to draw a correspondingly large current from the supply. Simultaneously, the rotor frequency $f_r$ equals the stator frequency $f_s$, making rotor leakage reactance $X_2$ very large. Because rotor resistance $R_2$ is small compared to $X_2$, the rotor current lags the rotor EMF by a large angle, producing poor power factor and a weak starting torque.

**The two engineering problems are therefore:**

1. **Excessive Starting Current ($I_{st}$):** 5–7 times the full-load current. This causes:
   - Large $I^2R$ losses → overheating of windings
   - Severe voltage drop in the supply cables → disturbance to other connected loads
   - Protective devices (MCBs, fuses) may trip unnecessarily
2. **Low Starting Torque ($T_{st}$):** Because of the high $X_2/R_2$ ratio at $s=1$, the developed torque is poor (often only 1.5 to 2.5 times full-load torque), which is insufficient for heavy loads requiring high breakaway torque.

The *ideal* starter should reduce starting current **without** sacrificing starting torque — but in practice, almost every method trades one for the other. Let us now examine each method.

### 2.2 Classification of Starting Methods

```
STARTING METHODS
├── A. Squirrel-Cage Induction Motors
│   ├── 1. Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter
│   ├── 2. Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Starter
│   ├── 3. Auto-Transformer Starter
│   ├── 4. Soft Starter (Thyristor-based)
│   └── 5. Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)
└── B. Slip-Ring (Wound Rotor) Induction Motors
    └── 1. Rotor Resistance Starter
```

### 2.3 Brief Operating Principle of Each Method

**A1. Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter**
The motor stator windings are connected directly to the full supply voltage through a contactor and an overload relay. Used for small motors (up to ~**5–7.5 kW**) where high inrush is acceptable.

**A2. Star-Delta Starter**
The motor is first connected in **star (Y)** so each phase receives only $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ volts. After the motor accelerates to ~75–80 % of rated speed, it is reconnected in **delta (Δ)** for normal full-voltage running. The changeover is made via a triple-pole double-throw (TPDT) contactor arrangement.

**A3. Auto-Transformer Starter**
A step-down auto-transformer with tappings (typically 50 %, 65 %, 80 %) is inserted between the supply and the motor stator during starting. The motor is fed with a reduced voltage $xV$ where $x$ is the tapping fraction. Once the motor accelerates, the auto-transformer is bypassed.

**A4. Soft Starter**
Thyristors (SCRs) in each phase control the firing angle $\alpha$ to apply a gradually increasing voltage ramp to the motor, eliminating the inrush transient. Used in modern industrial plants.

**A5. Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)**
The supply frequency is electronically varied along with voltage to maintain a constant $V/f$ ratio, allowing smooth speed control from zero to rated speed. Although primarily a speed controller, the soft starting is a natural by-product.

**B1. Rotor Resistance Starter (Slip-Ring Motors)**
External star-connected rheostats are connected to the slip rings of the wound rotor via brushes. At starting, maximum resistance is inserted in each rotor phase. This:
- Increases starting torque (since $T \propto R_2$ at low $s$)
- Reduces starting current
- Improves starting power factor

The resistance is progressively cut out as the motor speeds up. Finally, the slip rings are short-circuited and brushes lifted (in some designs) for normal operation.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Condition / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f_s}{P}$ | $N_s$ in **rpm**, $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Dimensionless, $s=1$ at start |
| 3 | Rotor EMF at slip $s$ | $E_{2s} = s \, E_2$ | Volts |
| 4 | Rotor Reactance at slip $s$ | $X_{2s} = s \, X_2$ | Ohms |
| 5 | Rotor Current at slip $s$ | $I_2 = \dfrac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$ | Amperes |
| 6 | Starting Current (DOL) | $I_{st(DOL)} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{R_{eq}^2 + X_{eq}^2}}$ | Approx **5–7 × FLC** |
| 7 | Starting Torque (General) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 \, R_2/s}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$ | At $s=1$: $T_{st} = \dfrac{3 V^2 R_2}{2\pi N_s(R_2^2 + X_2^2)}$ |
| 8 | Star-Delta: Line Current | $I_{L(st, Y)} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \, I_{L(st, \Delta)}$ | $V$ reduces by factor $\sqrt{3}$ |
| 9 | Star-Delta: Starting Torque | $T_{st(Y)} = \dfrac{1}{3} \, T_{st(\Delta)}$ | Torque reduces to **33.3 %** of DOL |
| 10 | Auto-Transformer: Line Current | $I_{L(st, AT)} = x \cdot I'_{st}$ | where $x$ is tap fraction, $I'_{st}$ is motor current at reduced voltage |
| 11 | Auto-Transformer: Starting Torque | $T_{st(AT)} = x^2 \cdot T_{st(DOL)}$ | Torque reduces by $x^2$ |
| 12 | Rotor Resistance: Condition for max starting torque at $s=1$ | $R_2 + R_{ext} = X_2$ | The total rotor loop resistance equals rotor standstill reactance |
| 13 | Pull-out / Max Torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2 X_2}$ | Occurs at $s = R_2 / X_2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal tip for KTU problems:** When asked "why is the starting torque reduced when voltage is reduced?", recall that **torque is proportional to the square of the applied voltage**, whereas current is proportional to voltage. Hence, halving the voltage reduces current to half but torque to one-quarter.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

Starting methods are critical in:
- **Cement and Mining Industries:** Crushers and ball mills require very high breakaway torque, favouring slip-ring motors with rotor resistance starters.
- **HVAC Systems:** Fans and pumps use Star-Delta or Soft Starters to prevent water-hammer and pipe stress.
- **Electric Vehicles & Elevators:** Use VFDs for smooth, jerk-free start/stop transitions.
- **Pumps in Water Treatment Plants:** Soft Starters are preferred to avoid hydraulic shocks.
- **Textile and Paper Mills:** Use VFDs for precise speed control along with soft starting.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Starting Torque of a Three-Phase Induction Motor

We begin with the approximate equivalent circuit (referred to stator) at any slip $s$.

The rotor branch impedance is:

$$Z_{2,eq} = \frac{R_2}{s} + j X_2$$

The magnitude is:

$$Z_{2,eq} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + X_2^2}$$

The rotor current magnitude:

$$I_2 = \frac{E_2}{\sqrt{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}}$$

The mechanical power developed (air-gap power transferred to rotor) is:

$$P_{ag} = 3 I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

Substituting $I_2$:

$$P_{ag} = 3 \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

The rotor copper loss is:

$$P_{rcu} = 3 I_2^2 R_2 = s \cdot P_{ag}$$

Therefore, the mechanical power developed is:

$$P_m = P_{ag} - P_{rcu} = (1 - s) P_{ag}$$

$$P_m = \frac{3 E_2^2 (1 - s) (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$$

The torque developed is mechanical power divided by angular velocity:

$$T = \frac{P_m}{2 \pi N_r / 60} = \frac{P_m}{(1 - s) \cdot 2 \pi N_s / 60}$$

Substituting $P_m$ and simplifying:

$$T = \frac{3}{2 \pi N_s / 60} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 (R_2 / s)}{(R_2 / s)^2 + X_2^2}$$

In synchronous watts form (per radian/sec):

$$T = \frac{3 E_2^2 (R_2 / s)}{\omega_s \left[(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2\right]}$$

**At starting ($s = 1$):**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3 E_2^2 \, R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)}$$

If we neglect the small stator impedance drop and use $E_2 \approx V_{ph}$, we get:

$$\boxed{T_{st} = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2 \, R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Condition for Maximum Torque and Slip at Maximum Torque

Starting from the general torque equation:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 (R_2 / s)}{(R_2 / s)^2 + X_2^2}$$

To find the slip at which torque is maximum, differentiate $T$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = 0$$

A more elegant approach: let $a = R_2 / s$. Then $T = \dfrac{3 V^2 a}{\omega_s (a^2 + X_2^2)}$.

For maximum, treat $a$ as variable (since $a$ depends on $s$):

$$\frac{dT}{da} = \frac{3V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(a^2 + X_2^2) - a(2a)}{(a^2 + X_2^2)^2} = 0$$

Numerator must vanish:

$$a^2 + X_2^2 - 2a^2 = 0 \Rightarrow X_2^2 = a^2 \Rightarrow a = X_2$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{R_2}{s_{max}} = X_2 \Rightarrow \boxed{s_{max} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

Substituting back to get maximum torque (now independent of $R_2$):

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{X_2}{2 X_2^2} = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The maximum torque is **independent of rotor resistance**! However, the slip at which it occurs *does* depend on $R_2$. This is the theoretical basis for using **rotor resistance starting in slip-ring motors** — we can shift the peak of the torque-slip curve towards $s = 1$ to obtain high starting torque.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Star-Delta Starting Analysis

**Step 1:** In delta connection (running condition), each phase receives full line voltage:

$$V_{ph, \Delta} = V_L$$

The starting line current (with full voltage) is:

$$I_{L(st, \Delta)} = \sqrt{3} \, I_{ph(st)} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

**Step 2:** In star connection (starting), each phase receives:

$$V_{ph, Y} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The starting line current equals the phase current (in star):

$$I_{L(st, Y)} = I_{ph(st, Y)} = \frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

**Step 3:** Taking the ratio of line currents:

$$\frac{I_{L(st, Y)}}{I_{L(st, \Delta)}} = \frac{(1/\sqrt{3}) V_L / Z_{ph}}{\sqrt{3} V_L / Z_{ph}} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L(st, Y)} = \frac{1}{3} I_{L(st, \Delta)}}$$

**Step 4:** Starting torque ratio. Since $T \propto V^2$:

$$\frac{T_{st(Y)}}{T_{st(\Delta)}} = \left(\frac{V_{ph, Y}}{V_{ph, \Delta}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{V_L}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{T_{st(Y)} = \frac{1}{3} T_{st(\Delta)}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Auto-Transformer Starting Analysis

Let the auto-transformer tap fraction be $x$ (e.g., $x = 0.5$ for a 50 % tap). The voltage applied to the motor during starting is:

$$V_{motor} = x \cdot V_L$$

The motor current (referred to the motor side) at this reduced voltage:

$$I'_{motor} = x \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$$

**Reflection to the line side:** The current drawn from the supply is reduced by the same factor $x$ (transformer action, since power is conserved approximately):

$$I_{L(st, AT)} = x \cdot I'_{motor} = x^2 \cdot I_{st(DOL)}$$

**Starting torque ratio:** Since $T \propto V^2$:

$$\frac{T_{st(AT)}}{T_{st(DOL)}} = \left(\frac{x V_L}{V_L}\right)^2 = x^2$$

$$\boxed{T_{st(AT)} = x^2 \cdot T_{st(DOL)}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Rotor Resistance Starting: Optimum External Resistance

To make the motor develop maximum torque **at starting** ($s = 1$), we require $s_{max} = 1$:

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{X_2} = 1$$

$$\boxed{R_{ext} = X_2 - R_2}$$

By adding this external resistance, the motor develops its **maximum torque at standstill**, which is the ideal starting condition.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **DOL** | **Star-Delta** | **Auto-Trans. (50 %)** | **Rotor Resistance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Starting Voltage | $V_L$ (100 %) | $V_L / \sqrt{3}$ (57.7 %) | $0.5 V_L$ (50 %) | $V_L$ (100 %) |
| Starting Current | $I_{st}$ | $I_{st} / 3$ | $0.25 \, I_{st}$ | Reduced (depends on $R_{ext}$) |
| Starting Torque | $T_{st}$ | $T_{st} / 3$ | $0.25 \, T_{st}$ | Can be made equal to $T_{max}$ |
| Starting Power Factor | Low | Improved | Improved | Significantly improved |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | High | High (slip-ring motor needed) |
| Smoothness | Poor (jerk) | Two-step | Two-step | Smooth (multi-step) |
| Speed Control | No | No | No (limited) | Yes (limited) |
| Typical Application | < 7.5 kW | Pumps, fans | Large motors | Cranes, lifts, mills |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Rotor Resistance Wins:** Only the slip-ring induction motor with rotor resistance starting gives us the rare ability to **reduce starting current AND simultaneously increase starting torque** — by exactly adding the right amount of external resistance. This is the fundamental reason slip-ring motors are used in heavy-load applications.
