# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features

The split phase motor consists of the following parts:

- **Stator Core:** Made of laminated silicon steel to reduce eddy current losses. It has uniformly distributed slots.
- **Main Winding (Running Winding):**
  - Made of **thick copper wire** (low resistance).
  - Placed at the **bottom of the slots**.
  - Has **low R/X ratio** (highly inductive).
  - Carries current $I_m$ lagging the supply voltage by a large angle $\phi_m$ (typically 70°–80°).
- **Auxiliary Winding (Starting Winding):**
  - Made of **thin copper wire** (high resistance).
  - Placed at the **top of the slots** (easier to remove for repair).
  - Has a **high R/X ratio** (more resistive).
  - Often connected in series with a centrifugal switch.
  - Carries current $I_a$ lagging the supply voltage by a smaller angle $\phi_a$ (typically 30°–40°).
- **Centrifugal Switch:**
  - Mounted on the rotor shaft.
  - Disconnects the auxiliary winding when rotor speed reaches **75%–80% of $N_s$**.
- **Rotor:** **Squirrel-cage rotor** — identical to that of a three-phase induction motor.
- **End Shields / Bearings:** Support the rotor shaft.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle: A single-phase supply produces a pulsating field, NOT a rotating field. To make the rotor rotate, we must artificially convert this pulsating field into a rotating (or elliptical) field. The split-phase technique achieves this by using two windings with a phase difference between their currents.**

**Step-by-step logic of starting:**

1. When the single-phase AC supply is given, **both windings** are connected in parallel across the supply.
2. The main winding current $I_m$ lags the voltage by a large angle $\phi_m$ (highly inductive).
3. The auxiliary winding current $I_a$ lags the voltage by a smaller angle $\phi_a$ (more resistive due to thin wire).
4. The phase difference $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$ is the **splitting angle**, typically **25°–30°**.
5. These two out-of-phase alternating fluxes, displaced by 90° in space, combine to form a **rotating magnetic field** (in practice, an elliptical field).
6. This field cuts the squirrel-cage rotor bars, inducing currents (by Faraday's law), which interact with the field to produce **starting torque** (by Lorentz force).
7. The rotor accelerates. At **~75–80% of synchronous speed**, the centrifugal switch opens, disconnecting the auxiliary winding.
8. The motor continues to run as a single-phase induction motor on the main winding alone.

## 2.3 Why the Motor Continues to Run After Auxiliary Winding is Disconnected

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-field Theory (Double Revolving Field Theory):** A single-phase alternating flux $\phi \cos(\omega t)$ can be mathematically resolved into two counter-rotating fluxes of equal magnitude $\phi/2$. When the rotor is spinning, the backward-rotating component has a high relative speed (~$2\omega$) with respect to the rotor and produces a small braking torque, while the forward-rotating component is nearly synchronous and produces a strong driving torque. The net torque is positive above a certain speed, so once started, the motor continues to run even on a single winding.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | in RPM; $f$ in Hz, $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless (per unit) |
| 3 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | in Hz |
| 4 | Rotor Induced EMF (per phase) | $E_2 = s \cdot E_{20}$ | where $E_{20}$ is the EMF at standstill |
| 5 | Rotor Reactance | $X_2 = s \cdot X_{20}$ | $X_{20}$ is standstill rotor reactance |
| 6 | Torque (Approximate) | $T \propto \dfrac{s \cdot E_2^{\,2} \cdot R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s \cdot X_{20})^2}$ | Kloss equation form |
| 7 | Starting Torque | $T_{st} \propto \sin(\alpha)$ | where $\alpha$ is the phase-splitting angle |
| 8 | Phase-splitting Angle | $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$ | typically 25°–30° |
| 9 | R/X Ratio — Main Winding | $(R/X)_{main} = \text{low}$ | highly inductive |
| 10 | R/X Ratio — Aux Winding | $(R/X)_{aux} = \text{high}$ | more resistive |
| 11 | Starting Current | $I_{st} = I_m + I_a$ (vector sum) | typically 6–8 times full-load current |
| 12 | Pull-out Torque Ratio | $T_{max} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \times T_{FL}$ | depending on design |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The starting torque of a resistance-split phase motor is **LOW** (only 1.5–2 × full-load torque), unlike a capacitor-start motor where it can be 3–4.5 × full-load torque. Do NOT confuse the two.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Split phase motors are used in:
- **Fans** (ceiling fans, exhaust fans, table fans)
- **Washing machines** (in some designs)
- **Refrigerators and air-conditioner compressors** (older models)
- **Blowers, dryers, and small machine tools**
- **Office machinery** (duplicators, small printing presses)

Their main advantage: **low cost, simple construction, no capacitor required**, suitable for **intermittent duty** applications.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Synchronous Speed and Slip

The synchronous speed of any AC induction motor is determined by the supply frequency and the number of poles in the stator winding. Each pole pair produces one full mechanical revolution per electrical cycle.

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}
$$

where:
- $N_s$ = synchronous speed in revolutions per minute (RPM)
- $f$ = supply frequency in Hertz (Hz)
- $P$ = total number of stator poles

The actual rotor speed $N_r$ is always less than $N_s$ because the rotor must "slip" with respect to the rotating magnetic field to induce rotor currents and develop torque. The slip $s$ is defined as the relative difference between synchronous and rotor speed, expressed as a fraction:

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard):**

> A 4-pole, 50 Hz split phase induction motor runs at a full-load speed of **1410 RPM**. Calculate (a) the synchronous speed, (b) the slip at full load, and (c) the rotor frequency.

**Solution:**

(a) **Synchronous Speed:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}
$$

(b) **Full-Load Slip:**

$$
s = \frac{1500 - 1410}{1500} = \frac{90}{1500} = 0.06 \text{ p.u.}
$$

The slip is **0.06 per unit** or **6%**.

(c) **Rotor Frequency:**

$$
f_r = s \times f = 0.06 \times 50 = 3 \text{ Hz}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Starting Torque from Two-Phase Resolution

A single-phase pulsating flux $\phi_m(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ can be resolved into two equal counter-rotating fluxes of magnitude $\Phi_m / 2$, one rotating forward (CW) and the other backward (CCW):

$$
\phi_m(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta) + \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t + \theta)
$$

Mathematically, this is an application of the trigonometric identity for the sum and difference of two rotating phasors. Since both components have **equal magnitudes**, their torques $T_f$ (forward) and $T_b$ (backward) are equal at standstill, producing **zero net starting torque**. This is exactly why a single-phase motor cannot self-start.

## 3.3 Starting Torque of a Split Phase Motor — Analytical Treatment

In a split phase motor, the main and auxiliary winding currents are:

$$
I_m \angle -\phi_m \quad \text{and} \quad I_a \angle -\phi_a
$$

with the phase-splitting angle $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a \approx 25^{\circ} \text{ to } 30^{\circ}$.

The starting torque is given by the cross-product of the two perpendicular flux components (90° in space) acting with the phase-time displacement $\alpha$:

$$
T_{st} = k \cdot \phi_m \cdot \phi_a \cdot \sin(\alpha)
$$

where $k$ is a machine constant.

Expanding with the relation $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$:

$$
T_{st} = k \cdot I_m I_a \sin(\phi_m - \phi_a)
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$:

$$
T_{st} = k \cdot I_m I_a \big( \sin\phi_m \cos\phi_a - \cos\phi_m \sin\phi_a \big)
$$

Substituting $\sin\phi = \dfrac{X}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}}$ and $\cos\phi = \dfrac{R}{\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}}$:

$$
T_{st} = k \cdot I_m I_a \left( \frac{X_m R_a}{\vert Z_m \vert \cdot \vert Z_a \vert} - \frac{R_m X_a}{\vert Z_m \vert \cdot \vert Z_a \vert} \right)
$$

$$
T_{st} = \frac{k \cdot I_m I_a}{\vert Z_m \vert \cdot \vert Z_a \vert} \left( X_m R_a - R_m X_a \right)
$$

This equation clearly demonstrates that to **maximize starting torque**, we need a large $\alpha$, which requires $X_m R_a \gg R_m X_a$ — i.e., the **auxiliary winding must have high resistance and the main winding must have high reactance**.

## 3.4 Equivalent Circuit and Rotor EMF Calculations

**Standstill rotor EMF** (slip $s = 1$):

$$
E_{20} = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m \cdot k_w
$$

where $k_w$ is the winding factor and $N_2$ is the number of rotor turns.

**Rotor EMF at slip $s$:**

$$
E_2 = s \cdot E_{20}
$$

**Rotor impedance at slip $s$:**

$$
Z_2 = R_2 + j s X_{20}
$$

**Rotor Current Magnitude:**

$$
\vert I_2 \vert = \frac{s \cdot E_{20}}{\sqrt{R_2^{\,2} + (s \cdot X_{20})^2}}
$$

**Rotor Copper Loss (per phase):**

$$
P_{cu,2} = \vert I_2 \vert^2 \cdot R_2
$$

**Mechanical Power Developed:**

$$
P_m = (1 - s) \cdot P_{g}
$$

where $P_g$ is the air-gap power, $P_g = \dfrac{1}{s} \cdot P_{cu,2}$.

## 3.5 Worked Problem: Starting Torque Comparison

> A split phase motor has main winding impedance $Z_m = (3 + j4) \, \Omega$ and auxiliary winding impedance $Z_a = (10 + j2) \, \Omega$. The supply voltage is 230 V, 50 Hz. Determine (a) the phase-splitting angle $\alpha$, (b) the starting torque in terms of the machine constant $k$, and (c) the starting current magnitude.

**Solution:**

(a) **Impedances and Phase Angles:**

$$
\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = 53.13^{\circ}
$$

$$
\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a}{R_a}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2}{10}\right) = 11.31^{\circ}
$$

$$
\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 53.13^{\circ} - 11.31^{\circ} = 41.82^{\circ}
$$

(b) **Currents:**

$$
I_m = \frac{V}{\vert Z_m \vert} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{3^2 + 4^2}} = \frac{230}{5} = 46 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_a = \frac{V}{\vert Z_a \vert} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{10^2 + 2^2}} = \frac{230}{10.198} = 22.55 \text{ A}
$$

**Starting Torque (relative units):**

$$
T_{st} \propto I_m I_a \sin(\alpha) = 46 \times 22.55 \times \sin(41.82^{\circ})
$$

$$
T_{st} \propto 46 \times 22.55 \times 0.6665 = 691.5 \text{ (relative units)}
$$

(c) **Total Starting Current (vector sum):**

Taking the supply voltage as reference, the two currents are:

$$
I_m = 46 \angle{-53.13^{\circ}} \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_a = 22.55 \angle{-11.31^{\circ}} \text{ A}
$$

**Phasor Sum (real part):**

$$
I_{\text{real}} = 46 \cos(53.13^{\circ}) + 22.55 \cos(11.31^{\circ})
$$

$$
I_{\text{real}} = 46 \times 0.6 + 22.55 \times 0.9806 = 27.6 + 22.11 = 49.71 \text{ A}
$$

**Phasor Sum (imaginary part):**

$$
I_{\text{imag}} = -46 \sin(53.13^{\circ}) - 22.55 \sin(11.31^{\circ})
$$

$$
I_{\text{imag}} = -46 \times 0.8 - 22.55 \times 0.1961 = -36.8 - 4.42 = -41.22 \text{ A}
$$

**Magnitude of total starting current:**

$$
I_{st} = \sqrt{49.71^2 + 41.22^2} = \sqrt{2471.1 + 1699.1} = \sqrt{4170.2} \approx 64.6 \text{ A}
$$

This result shows the high inrush current — a major drawback of split phase motors.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define a split phase induction motor. Why is a centrifugal switch used in it?**

**Model Answer:**

A split phase induction motor is a single-phase AC induction motor that uses two windings (main and auxiliary) displaced by 90° in space, with a phase difference of about 25°–30° between their currents, to produce a rotating magnetic field and develop starting torque.

A centrifugal switch is used to **disconnect the auxiliary winding** from the supply once the motor reaches **75%–80% of synchronous speed**. This is necessary because:
- The auxiliary winding is designed for **short-time (intermittent) duty** and would overheat if left in the circuit.
- After starting, only the main winding is needed to sustain rotation, since the rotor's inertia and the cross-field theory allow continued operation on a single winding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT say the auxiliary winding is disconnected "to improve efficiency" only — the primary reason is **thermal protection** of the thin auxiliary winding and prevention of unnecessary $I^2R$ loss. Both points must be mentioned.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Why cannot a single-phase induction motor start by itself? Explain with the double revolving field theory.**

**Model Answer:**

A single-phase winding carrying alternating current produces a **pulsating magnetic field** that varies sinusoidally in magnitude along a fixed axis but does not rotate. According to the **double revolving field theory**, this pulsating field can be mathematically resolved into two equal counter-rotating fields of half amplitude, rotating at synchronous speed in opposite directions.

At standstill ($N_r = 0$), the rotor sees both forward and backward components with equal relative speed. The torques produced by these two components are **equal and opposite**, giving **net starting torque = 0**. Hence the motor cannot self-start.

Once the rotor is given an initial push in either direction, the relative speed of the rotor with respect to the forward field decreases (becomes small), while the relative speed with respect to the backward field increases (becomes nearly $2N_s$). The forward torque dominates, the rotor accelerates in that direction, and the motor continues to run.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Part B questions in this module have an **internal choice**: students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a split phase induction motor. Discuss the role of the centrifugal switch in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 230 V split phase induction motor has the following standstill impedances: Main winding $Z_m = (4 + j5) \, \Omega$, Auxiliary winding $Z_a = (12 + j3) \, \Omega$. Calculate:
1. The phase-splitting angle $\alpha$ between the two winding currents.
2. The starting current drawn by each winding.
3. The total starting current magnitude.
4. Comment on the starting performance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Construction:**

The split phase induction motor consists of:
1. **Stator** — laminated silicon steel core with uniformly distributed slots.
2. **Main (Running) Winding** — placed at the **bottom of the slots**, made of **thick copper wire** with low resistance. It has a high inductance, hence low $R/X$ ratio.
3. **Auxiliary (Starting) Winding** — placed at the **top of the slots**, made of **thin copper wire** with high resistance. It has a high $R/X$ ratio.
4. The two windings are **displaced by 90 electrical degrees in space**.
5. **Centrifugal Switch** — mounted on the rotor shaft, normally closed during start.
6. **Squirrel-cage rotor** — identical to three-phase induction motor rotor.
7. **End shields and bearings**.

**Working:**

When the single-phase AC supply is switched ON, both windings are energized in parallel. The main winding current $I_m$ lags the voltage by a large angle $\phi_m$ (highly inductive), while the auxiliary winding current $I_a$ lags by a much smaller angle $\phi_a$ (more resistive). The phase difference $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a \approx 25°\text{–}30°$ creates a **weak elliptical rotating magnetic field** that cuts the rotor bars, inducing currents and producing **starting torque**. The rotor accelerates. At **~75–80% of synchronous speed**, the centrifugal switch opens and disconnects the auxiliary winding. The motor then runs on the main winding alone, sustained by the cross-field action of the forward-rotating flux component.

**Role of the Centrifugal Switch:**

- Mechanically operated by rotor speed.
- Disconnects the auxiliary winding at high speed to prevent **overheating** (auxiliary winding is designed for intermittent duty).
- Reduces $I^2R$ losses in the auxiliary winding during running.
- Prevents back-emf in the auxiliary winding from opposing the main field.

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- 4-pole, 50 Hz, 230 V supply
- $Z_m = R_m + jX_m = 4 + j5 \, \Omega \Rightarrow \vert Z_m \vert = \sqrt{16 + 25} = \sqrt{41} = 6.403 \, \Omega$
- $Z_a = R_a + jX_a = 12 + j3 \, \Omega \Rightarrow \vert Z_a \vert = \sqrt{144 + 9} = \sqrt{153} = 12.369 \, \Omega$

**1. Phase-Splitting Angle $\alpha$:**

$$
\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{5}{4}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.25) = 51.34^{\circ}
$$

$$
\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{3}{12}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04^{\circ}
$$

$$
\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 51.34^{\circ} - 14.04^{\circ} = 37.30^{\circ}
$$

**2. Starting Currents:**

$$
I_m = \frac{V}{\vert Z_m \vert} = \frac{230}{6.403} = 35.92 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_a = \frac{V}{\vert Z_a \vert} = \frac{230}{12.369} = 18.60 \text{ A}
$$

**3. Total Starting Current (Vector Sum):**

Taking $V$ as reference:

$$
I_m = 35.92 \angle{-51.34^{\circ}} \text{ A} = (35.92 \cos 51.34^{\circ}) - j(35.92 \sin 51.34^{\circ})
$$

$$
I_m = 22.43 - j28.04 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_a = 18.60 \angle{-14.04^{\circ}} \text{ A} = (18.60 \cos 14.04^{\circ}) - j(18.60 \sin 14.04^{\circ})
$$

$$
I_a = 18.05 - j4.51 \text{ A}
$$

**Phasor Sum:**

$$
I_{st} = I_m + I_a = (22.43 + 18.05) - j(28.04 + 4.51) = 40.48 - j32.55 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{st} = \sqrt{40.48^2 + 32.55^2} = \sqrt{1638.6 + 1059.5} = \sqrt{2698.1} \approx 51.94 \text{ A}
$$

**4. Comments on Starting Performance:**

- The phase-splitting angle $\alpha = 37.30°$ is **reasonable** for a resistance-split motor (above the typical 25°–30°).
- The total starting current of **51.94 A** is high — typically **6 to 8 times** the full-load current.
- Starting torque is **moderate** (proportional to $\sin 37.30° \approx 0.605$).
- Suitable for **light-load starting** applications such as fans and small blowers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to compute the **vector sum** of the two currents. The total starting current is NOT simply $I_m + I_a = 54.52$ A — the vector sum is **51.94 A**. Always use phasor addition. Marks are awarded specifically for the phasor treatment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]

**(a)** Compare split phase motor, capacitor-start motor, and shaded pole motor in terms of starting torque, starting current, cost, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the starting torque of a split phase motor in terms of the impedances of the two windings. Show that maximum starting torque requires a large $R/X$ ratio for the auxiliary winding. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Solution to Part (a):**

| Feature | Split Phase Motor | Capacitor-Start Motor | Shaded Pole Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starting Torque** | Low (1.5–2 × $T_{FL}$) | High (3–4.5 × $T_{FL}$) | Very Low (0.5–1 × $T_{FL}$) |
| **Starting Current** | High (6–8 × $I_{FL}$) | Moderate (4–6 × $I_{FL}$) | Low (2–3 × $I_{FL}$) |
| **Phase-Splitting Method** | Resistance (auxiliary winding) | Capacitance (series capacitor) | Shading coil (short-circuited ring) |
| **Phase-Splitting Angle** | 25°–30° | ~80°–90° (close to ideal) | Very small (10°–15°) |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate (extra capacitor + switch) | Lowest |
| **Construction** | Simple | More complex | Simplest |
| **Applications** | Fans, washing machines, small pumps | Compressors, conveyors, machine tools | Small fans, hair dryers, toys, clocks |
| **Power Rating** | 60 W – 1 kW | 100 W – 7.5 kW | < 50 W |
| **Efficiency** | Moderate | High | Low |

**Solution to Part (b):**

The two winding currents with the supply voltage $V$ as reference are:

$$
I_m = \frac{V}{\vert Z_m \vert} \angle{-\phi_m}, \qquad I_a = \frac{V}{\vert Z_a \vert} \angle{-\phi_a}
$$

The starting torque is proportional to the product of the two currents and the sine of the phase-splitting angle:

$$
T_{st} = K \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin(\alpha) = K \cdot I_m I_a \sin(\phi_m - \phi_a)
$$

where $K$ is a machine constant.

Expanding:

$$
T_{st} = K \cdot I_m I_a \big(\sin\phi_m \cos\phi_a - \cos\phi_m \sin\phi_a\big)
$$

Substituting $\sin\phi = X / \vert Z \vert$ and $\cos\phi = R / \vert Z \vert$:

$$
T_{st} = \frac{K \cdot V^2}{\vert Z_m \vert \cdot \vert Z_a \vert} \left( \frac{X_m R_a}{\vert Z_m \vert \vert Z_a \vert} - \frac{R_m X_a}{\vert Z_m \vert \vert Z_a \vert} \right)
$$

$$
T_{st} = \frac{K \cdot V^2 (X_m R_a - R_m X_a)}{\vert Z_m \vert^2 \cdot \vert Z_a \vert^2}
$$

**For maximum starting torque**, the numerator $X_m R_a - R_m X_a$ must be maximized. This requires:

- $X_m$ (main winding reactance) to be **large** — achieved by making the main winding highly inductive.
- $R_a$ (auxiliary winding resistance) to be **large** — achieved by using **thin wire** for the auxiliary winding.
- $R_m$ (main winding resistance) to be **small** — achieved by using **thick copper wire** for the main winding.
- $X_a$ (auxiliary winding reactance) to be **small** — auxiliary winding is designed with low inductance.

This is equivalent to saying:
- Main winding: **large $X_m / R_m$ ratio** (highly inductive)
- Auxiliary winding: **large $R_a / X_a$ ratio** (highly resistive)

This confirms the design principle that the auxiliary winding must be **resistive-dominant** while the main winding must be **inductive-dominant**, which is achieved physically by using **thin wire in fewer turns** for the auxiliary winding and **thick wire in many turns** for the main winding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In deriving the torque expression, students often forget to substitute $\sin\phi$ and $\cos\phi$ in terms of $R$, $X$, and $\vert Z \vert$. Without this substitution, the final design insight (high $R_a/X_a$ for aux) cannot be obtained. Marks are awarded specifically for the **final design conclusion**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Split Phase Motor**

- **Type:** Single-phase induction motor with **two stator windings** for starting.
- **Windings:** Main (running, thick wire, inductive) and Auxiliary (starting, thin wire, resistive), displaced by **90° in space**.
- **Phase-splitting angle $\alpha$:** **25°–30°** (resistance split, much less than the ideal 90°).
- **Auxiliary winding disconnection:** By **centrifugal switch** at **75%–80%** of $N_s$.
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- **Slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$.
- **Rotor frequency:** $f_r = s \cdot f$.
- **Starting torque:** **1.5–2 ×** $T_{FL}$ (low).
- **Starting current:** **6–8 ×** $I_{FL}$ (high — main drawback).
- **Direction of rotation:** Determined by the **relative phase** of auxiliary vs. main — reversing the auxiliary winding terminals reverses rotation.
- **Application:** Fans, washing machines, small pumps, blowers — **intermittent duty** preferred.
- **Why it cannot self-start on a single winding:** Pulsating field (not rotating) → equal and opposite torques from double revolving fields → net torque = 0.
- **Why it continues to run after starting:** Forward rotating field component dominates once rotor is spinning (Cross-field / Double Revolving Field Theory).
- **Equivalent rotor EMF:** $E_2 = s \cdot E_{20}$; Rotor reactance: $X_2 = s \cdot X_{20}$.
- **Torque proportional to:** $T \propto I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin(\alpha)$.
- **Design principle:** To maximize $T_{st}$: large $X_m R_a$ and small $R_m X_a$ — main winding inductive, aux winding resistive.
- **Difference from Capacitor-Start Motor:** Capacitor-start uses a **capacitor** in series with auxiliary winding to achieve ~80°–90° phase shift, giving **higher starting torque** but at higher cost.
- **Difference from Shaded Pole Motor:** Shaded pole uses a **copper shading ring** on pole faces; much lower starting torque but simplest and cheapest construction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitor Start Motor, Stepper Motor, Universal Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacitor Start Motor, Stepper Motor, Universal Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capacitor Start Motor

**Formal Definition:**
The Capacitor Start Motor is a single-phase induction motor that employs a **capacitor in series with an auxiliary (starting) winding** to create a phase displacement of approximately **90°** between the currents of the main and auxiliary windings. A **centrifugal switch** disconnects the auxiliary winding once the motor reaches **75% of synchronous speed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This motor is a derivative of the split-phase induction motor family, specifically engineered to deliver **HIGH STARTING TORQUE** (typically **3 to 4.5 times** the full-load torque).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a stationary ceiling fan versus one with a pull-cord. A plain single-phase winding creates a *pulsating* magnetic field (a flashlight that just blinks in place). It cannot rotate. The capacitor start motor is like having **two flashlights blinking alternately in perfect synchrony** — their combined light appears to *rotate* around the room, dragging the fan blades along with it. Once the fan is spinning fast enough on its own, you "switch off" one flashlight (the starting winding) to save power.

---

## 1.2 Stepper Motor

**Formal Definition:**
A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electromechanical device that converts electrical pulse trains into discrete angular mechanical movements. Each input pulse advances the rotor by a precise angular increment known as the **step angle** ($\theta_s$), without requiring feedback sensors (in open-loop configuration).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Identity:** A stepper motor is a *digital-to-mechanical transducer* — every pulse = one step.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a **clock's second hand**. It moves in *discrete ticks* (one tick = 6°). Send 10 pulses → 60°. Stop sending pulses → it stays where it is (holding torque). This is precisely how a stepper motor behaves — it is the *muscle* behind 3D printers, CNC machines, and robotics where exact angular positioning is paramount.

**The three primary classifications per KTU 2024 syllabus:**

| Class | Rotor Construction | Key Property |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft steel, salient poles | Steps due to magnetic path preference |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially magnetized permanent magnet rotor | Higher holding torque, detent torque present |
| **Hybrid** | Combines VR + PM construction | Small step angles (0.9°–5°), high precision |

---

## 1.3 Universal Motor

**Formal Definition:**
The **Universal Motor** is a **series-wound DC motor** that is specifically designed with a **laminated stator core** (instead of solid iron) and improved commutation, enabling it to operate efficiently on **both AC and DC supplies** at nearly identical speed-torque characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Although it operates on AC, its operating principle remains the *DC series motor principle* — torque is proportional to the *product of armature and field flux*.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Consider a **mixer-grinder** at home. You plug it into the AC wall socket, yet it spins at **10,000+ RPM** — far higher than any standard induction motor (which is capped at 3000 RPM). The universal motor is the "speed demon" of the motor world because the *same current flows through both armature and field windings*, causing their fluxes to reverse simultaneously on AC — preserving unidirectional torque.

---

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Supply frequency:** $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ (India, per KTU standard)
- **Synchronous speed (4-pole):** $N_s = 1500\,\text{RPM}$
- **Capacitor start motor starting torque multiplier:** $\mathbf{3 \text{ to } 4.5}\times T_{FL}$
- **Universal motor speed range:** **8,000 – 20,000 RPM**
- **Standard step angles:** $\mathbf{0.9°,\, 1.8°,\, 7.5°,\, 15°,\, 30°,\, 90°}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capacitor Start Motor — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Working Principle:**

1. **Initial At-Rest Condition:** Rotor is stationary. A single-phase stator winding alone produces a *pulsating* (not rotating) magnetic field — net torque = 0.
2. **Capacitor Action:** The starting capacitor $C$ in series with the auxiliary winding creates a **leading phase shift** in auxiliary winding current $I_a$ relative to main winding current $I_m$.
3. **Phase Displacement Target:** The capacitor value is chosen to make $\phi \approx 90°$ between $I_m$ and $I_a$ — this produces a nearly *perfect elliptical-to-circular* rotating magnetic field.
4. **Torque Production:** The rotating field induces EMF in the rotor bars → induced currents → interaction produces **forward torque**.
5. **Cut-Off (75% of $N_s$):** The **centrifugal switch (governor)** opens the auxiliary circuit, leaving only the main winding to operate the motor as a plain single-phase induction motor.
6. **Running Condition:** The motor now runs on main winding alone, reaching steady-state near synchronous speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a capacitor?** In a pure split-phase motor, the phase angle is only ~25–30°. By introducing a capacitor, the *reactive current* shifts the auxiliary current toward leading, achieving 90° phase split and dramatically improving starting torque.

---

## 2.2 Stepper Motor — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Working Principle (Variable Reluctance type):**

1. The rotor is a **soft-iron multi-toothed cylinder** with no permanent magnetism.
2. The stator has a defined number of salient poles with concentrated windings.
3. When a stator phase is energized, the rotor rotates to **minimize magnetic reluctance** (the path of least magnetic resistance).
4. Sequential energization of phases in a programmed order produces **discrete, incremental rotation**.
5. Each step is mechanically fixed by the tooth geometry — **position is retained even without continuous excitation (detent torque for PM/Hybrid types).**

**Key Concept — Step Angle Calculation:**

The step angle is determined purely by the **stator pole count ($N_s$)** and **rotor tooth count ($N_r$)**:

$$\theta_s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \cdot N_r} \times 360°$$

For Hybrid stepper motors, the step angle is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N \cdot m}$$

where $N$ = number of rotor teeth and $m$ = number of stator phase pairs (e.g., 2 for two-phase).

**Drive Modes:**

| Mode | Description | Resolution |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Full Step** | One phase ON at a time (wave drive) or two phases ON (full-step) | Standard $\theta_s$ |
| **Half Step** | Alternates 1-phase and 2-phase energization | $\theta_s / 2$ |
| **Microstepping** | Sinusoidal current fractions in phases | $\theta_s / 10$ to $\theta_s / 256$ |

---

## 2.3 Universal Motor — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Working Principle:**

1. **On DC Supply:** Behaves exactly as a DC series motor — armature current = field current, flux $\phi \propto I_a$, torque $T \propto \phi I_a \propto I_a^2$.
2. **On AC Supply:** Both armature current and field flux alternate sinusoidally but **in phase** with each other (series connection).
3. **Direction Preservation:** Since both $I_a$ and $\phi$ reverse *simultaneously* every half cycle, the torque $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$ remains **unidirectional** (positive in both halves).
4. **Laminated Construction:** Essential to limit eddy-current losses under AC operation.
5. **Compensating Winding:** Used in larger universal motors to neutralize the **transformer EMF** induced in the armature by the alternating stator flux, improving commutation.

**Speed Equation (AC operation):**

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \cdot \phi \cdot Z}$$

where $Z$ = total armature conductors.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Capacitor Start Motor

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM |
| Starting torque | $T_{st} \propto \phi_m \cdot \phi_a \cdot \sin\alpha$ | $\alpha$ = phase angle between fluxes |
| Capacitor reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Farads → Ohms |
| Phase angle target | $\phi \approx 90°$ | Ideal for max $T_{st}$ |

### Stepper Motor

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Step angle (VR) | $\theta_s = \dfrac{(N_s - N_r)\,360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ | Degrees |
| Step angle (Hybrid) | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N}$ | Degrees |
| Steps per revolution | $n = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | Pure number |
| Shaft speed | $N = \dfrac{f_p \cdot 60}{n}$ | RPM, $f_p$ = pulse rate (pps) |
| Holding torque | $T_h$ from manufacturer | Nm |

### Universal Motor

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Back EMF | $E_b = k \phi Z N$ | Volts |
| Voltage equation | $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Volts |
| Torque | $T = k \phi I_a$ (DC) or $T \propto I_a^2$ (AC, RMS) | Nm |
| Speed (no load) | Very high — must be **gear- or friction-limited** | Safety critical |
| Power factor | **0.7 to 0.9 lagging** | Typical AC operation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Capacitor Start Motors** → Compressors, pumps, conveyor belts, machine tools (where high starting torque is mandatory).
> - **Stepper Motors** → 3D printers, CNC milling, textile machines, camera autofocus, satellite dish positioning.
> - **Universal Motors** → Handheld power tools (drills, grinders), vacuum cleaners, food mixers, hair dryers, sewing machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Capacitor Start Motor — Derivation of Optimal Capacitance

**Objective:** Derive the capacitance $C$ required to achieve a 90° phase displacement between main and auxiliary winding currents.

**Given:**
- Auxiliary winding resistance: $R_a$
- Auxiliary winding leakage reactance: $X_{La}$
- Supply frequency: $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$
- Capacitor connected in series: $C$

**Step 1:** The impedance of the auxiliary branch (winding + capacitor in series) is:

$$Z_a = R_a + j(X_{La} - X_C)$$

where the capacitor's reactance *opposes* the inductive reactance of the winding.

**Step 2:** The impedance of the main winding branch is:

$$Z_m = R_m + jX_{Lm}$$

**Step 3:** The current in the auxiliary branch is:

$$I_a = \frac{V}{Z_a} = \frac{V}{R_a + j(X_{La} - X_C)}$$

**Step 4:** For 90° phase shift between $I_m$ and $I_a$, the auxiliary branch impedance must be **purely resistive** (so that $I_a$ is in phase with $V$ or shifted by a known reference).

Setting the imaginary part of $Z_a$ to zero:

$$X_{La} - X_C = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow X_C = X_{La}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the capacitor reactance formula:

$$\frac{1}{2\pi f C} = 2\pi f L_a$$

**Step 6:** Solving for the required capacitance:

$$C = \frac{1}{(2\pi f)^2 L_a}$$

**Step 7:** The phasor diagram shows that the auxiliary current $I_a$ leads the main current $I_m$ by **90°** when the resonance condition is met. The resulting rotating field amplitude is maximized.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Caveat:** True 90° is rarely achieved in practice. The auxiliary winding has resistance and leakage reactance that prevent perfect cancellation. Real capacitor start motors achieve **80°–88°** in commercial designs.

---

## 3.2 Stepper Motor — Derivation of Step Angle for Variable Reluctance Type

**Objective:** Derive the step angle formula for a multi-stack VR stepper motor.

**Given:**
- Number of stator teeth (poles) per stack: $N_s$
- Number of rotor teeth: $N_r$

**Step 1:** When one stator phase is energized, the rotor aligns such that **one pair of rotor teeth** aligns with the energized stator poles (minimum reluctance position).

**Step 2:** When the next phase is energized, the rotor advances by an angle that aligns the rotor teeth with the *next* stator pole pair. The number of rotor teeth that pass a fixed reference point per phase change is:

$$\Delta n_r = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \cdot 1 = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

Wait — let me derive this more rigorously. The angular pitch between adjacent rotor teeth is:

$$\text{Rotor tooth pitch} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

The angular pitch between adjacent stator poles (one phase) is:

$$\text{Stator pole pitch} = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$

**Step 3:** When phase 1 is energized, rotor tooth 1 aligns with stator pole 1. When phase 2 is energized, the rotor must rotate *forward* by:

$$\theta_s = \text{Stator pitch} - \text{Rotor pitch}$$

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s} - \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 4:** Taking the common denominator:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360° \cdot N_r - 360° \cdot N_s}{N_s \cdot N_r}$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying:

$$\theta_s = \frac{(N_r - N_s) \cdot 360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$$

**Step 6:** This is equivalent to the expression:

$$\theta_s = \frac{(N_s - N_r) \cdot 360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$$

in magnitude (sign only indicates direction of rotation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** To achieve a *small step angle* (high resolution), the difference $(N_s - N_r)$ must be **small** while $N_s \cdot N_r$ is **large** — this is why hybrid stepper motors use hundreds of rotor teeth.

---

## 3.3 Stepper Motor — Shaft Speed Derivation

**Step 1:** Let $f_p$ = pulse frequency (pulses per second, pps) supplied by the driver.

**Step 2:** Each pulse advances the rotor by **one step angle** $\theta_s$.

**Step 3:** Number of steps per revolution:

$$n = \frac{360°}{\theta_s}$$

**Step 4:** Time taken for one full revolution:

$$t_{rev} = \frac{n}{f_p} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s \cdot f_p}$$

**Step 5:** Converting to RPM:

$$N = \frac{60}{t_{rev}} = \frac{60 \cdot \theta_s \cdot f_p}{360°}$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{6°}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):**
A stepper motor has $\theta_s = 1.8°$ and is pulsed at $f_p = 500$ pps. Find the shaft speed.

**Solution:**

$$N = \frac{500 \times 1.8}{6} = 150\,\text{RPM}$$

---

## 3.4 Universal Motor — Power and Torque on AC Supply

**Step 1:** On DC supply, the universal motor is identical to a DC series motor:

$$T_{dc} = k \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$$

where $\phi \propto I_a$, hence $T_{dc} = k_1 I_a^2$.

**Step 2:** On AC supply, both $\phi$ and $I_a$ vary sinusoidally with time:

$$i_a(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$\phi(t) = k_2 \, i_a(t) = k_2 I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3:** Instantaneous torque:

$$T(t) = k \cdot \phi(t) \cdot i_a(t) = k \cdot k_2 I_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$$

**Step 4:** Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$T(t) = k \cdot k_2 I_m^2 \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$$

**Step 5:** The average torque is obtained by integrating over one full cycle. The cosine term averages to zero:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{k \cdot k_2 \cdot I_m^2}{2} = k \cdot k_2 \cdot I_{rms}^2$$

where $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$.

**Step 6:** Therefore, the torque expression on AC is:

$$\boxed{T_{ac} = K \cdot I_{rms}^2}$$

This confirms that the universal motor produces **unidirectional pulsating torque** at twice the supply frequency, with no net reversal — identical in form to the DC case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Commutation Note:** The pulsating torque creates torque ripples and acoustic noise — a trade-off for the motor's high speed and power density.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Perspective)

| Feature | Capacitor Start | Stepper Motor | Universal Motor |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Supply type** | 1-Phase AC only | DC pulses (driver) | AC or DC |
| **Starting torque** | Very high (3–4.5×) | Holding torque only when energized | Extremely high (~5–6×) |
| **Speed range** | Near $N_s$ (fixed) | Variable (open-loop) | 8,000–20,000+ RPM |
| **Position control** | No | Yes (precise, discrete) | No |
| **Commutator/Brushes** | No (squirrel cage rotor) | No (brushless) | Yes (DC-style) |
| **Efficiency** | Moderate | Low at rest | High (compact) |
| **Typical application** | Compressors, pumps | CNC, printers, robotics | Hand tools, mixers |
| **Power factor (AC)** | ~0.8–0.95 lagging | N/A | ~0.7–0.9 lagging |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | Medium (with driver) | Low |
| **Starting aux. component** | Capacitor + centrifugal switch | Logic driver + transistors | None (self-starting) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capacitor Start Motor — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[AC Single Phase Supply V] --> B1[Main Winding M]
    A1 --> C1[Start Capacitor C]
    C1 --> D1[Auxiliary Winding A]
    B1 --> E1[Common Junction to Stator Core]
    D1 --> F1[Centrifugal Governor Switch]
    F1 --> E1
    E1 --> G1[Squirrel Cage Rotor]
    G1 --> H1[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    H1 --> I1{Speed Check}
    I1 -- Below 75 percent Ns --> J1[Governor Closed, Aux ON]
    I1 -- Above 75 percent Ns --> K1[Governor Opens, Aux OFF]
    J1 --> L1[Rotating Magnetic Field Phi 90 deg]
    K1 --> M1[Pulsating Field Single Phase]
    L1 --> H1
    M1 --> H1
```

## 4.2 Stepper Motor — Pulse-to-Step Conversion Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Controller Microcontroller] --> P2[Pulse Generator f_p pps]
    P2 --> P3[Stepper Driver IC]
    P3 --> P4[H-Bridge Phase A]
    P3 --> P5[H-Bridge Phase B]
    P3 --> P6[H-Bridge Phase C]
    P3 --> P7[H-Bridge Phase D]
    P4 --> S1[Stator Coil A]
    P5 --> S2[Stator Coil B]
    P6 --> S3[Stator Coil C]
    P7 --> S4[Stator Coil D]
    S1 --> R1[Rotor Aligns to Min Reluctance]
    S2 --> R1
    S3 --> R1
    S4 --> R1
    R1 --> O1[Discrete Step theta_s]
    O1 --> O2[Holding Torque Maintained]
    O2 --> O3[Mechanical Load Drive]
```

## 4.3 Universal Motor — Series Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1[AC or DC Supply] --> W1[Field Winding F]
    W1 --> W2[Armature Winding A]
    W2 --> C1[Commutator and Brushes]
    C1 --> W3[Compensating Winding CW optional]
    W3 --> V1
    V1 --> S1[Current I_a flows in series]
    S1 --> F1[Flux phi proportional to I_a]
    F1 --> T1[Torque T proportional to phi times I_a]
    T1 --> SH1[High Speed Shaft]
    SH1 --> OUT[Mechanical Output]
```

## 4.4 Stepper Motor Drive Sequence (Wave Drive, 4-Phase)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SEQ1[Step 1] --> PHA[Phase A ON]
    SEQ2[Step 2] --> PHB[Phase B ON]
    SEQ3[Step 3] --> PHC[Phase C ON]
    SEQ4[Step 4] --> PHD[Phase D ON]
    SEQ5[Step 5] --> PHAR[Return to Phase A ON]
    PHA --> ROT1[Rotor Rotates by theta_s]
    PHB --> ROT1
    PHC --> ROT1
    PHD --> ROT1
    PHAR --> ROT1
    ROT1 --> ANGLE[Total Angle n times theta_s]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain why a single-phase induction motor is not self-starting. How does a capacitor start motor overcome this limitation?**

**Model Answer:**

A single-phase induction motor is *not self-starting* because the alternating current in a single stator winding produces a **pulsating magnetic field**, which can be resolved into two equal, oppositely rotating magnetic fields. These produce equal and opposite torques, resulting in **net starting torque = 0**.

The **capacitor start motor** overcomes this by introducing a second (auxiliary) winding displaced physically by 90° in space and connected in series with a **capacitor**. The capacitor causes the auxiliary winding current $I_a$ to *lead* the main winding current $I_m$ by approximately 90°. This two-phase excitation produces a **rotating magnetic field**, which induces rotor currents and develops a unidirectional starting torque of **3 to 4.5 times** the full-load torque. A **centrifugal switch** disconnects the auxiliary winding at ~75% of synchronous speed for efficient running.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**List any three advantages of stepper motors over DC servo motors in positioning applications.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **No feedback required (open-loop control):** The rotor position is determined precisely by counting pulses, eliminating the need for encoders or position sensors.
2. **Excellent holding torque at standstill:** Stepper motors maintain rotor position with full holding torque even when de-energized (in PM and hybrid types), unlike DC servos which require continuous current to hold position.
3. **Direct digital compatibility:** Step angle is inherently digital — each pulse = one step, making interfacing with microcontrollers and PLCs straightforward.
4. **No commutation issues:** Being brushless, stepper motors have longer life and require less maintenance.
5. **High reliability in dusty/hostile environments** (no brushes to arc or wear).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **capacitor start single-phase induction motor**. Discuss the role of the starting capacitor and centrifugal switch. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with two windings:
  - **Main winding (M):** Heavy-gauge, low-resistance, placed deep in the slots.
  - **Auxiliary/Starting winding (A):** Fine-gauge, high resistance, placed on top of slots, physically displaced by 90° electrical from the main winding.
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type (bars + end rings), no electrical connection.
- **Capacitor:** Connected in series with auxiliary winding, typically **electrolytic-type** (since it is disconnected during running).
- **Centrifugal switch:** Mounted on the rotor shaft; disconnects the auxiliary winding at ~75% of $N_s$.

**Working:**
1. On energization, current $I_m$ flows through the main winding, and $I_a$ flows through the capacitor-auxiliary branch.
2. The capacitor causes $I_a$ to *lead* $I_m$ by approximately 90°.
3. The resulting **two-phase rotating magnetic field** drags the rotor forward.
4. As the rotor accelerates to ~75% of synchronous speed, the centrifugal switch operates, opening the auxiliary circuit.
5. The motor continues to run on the main winding alone (single-phase operation) and reaches steady-state speed slightly below $N_s$.

**Role of Capacitor:**
Creates the required phase displacement of ~90°, producing a strong rotating field and hence **high starting torque** (3–4.5× full-load torque).

**Role of Centrifugal Switch:**
- Disconnects the auxiliary winding and capacitor at running speed.
- Prevents **overheating of the auxiliary winding** and **dielectric breakdown of the electrolytic capacitor**, which are designed only for short-duration starting duty.

---

**(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz single-phase capacitor start induction motor has the following parameters: $R_m = 4\,\Omega$, $X_{Lm} = 7\,\Omega$, $R_a = 7.5\,\Omega$, $X_{La} = 4\,\Omega$. Determine the value of capacitance required for 90° phase displacement between the two winding currents. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the target condition:**
For 90° phase displacement, the auxiliary branch impedance must be such that the current $I_a$ is displaced by 90° from $I_m$.

**Step 2 — Condition for 90° phase shift:**
The impedance of the auxiliary branch $Z_a = R_a + j(X_{La} - X_C)$ must be *purely capacitive* such that $I_a$ leads $I_m$ by 90°. The standard design condition used in KTU problems is:

$$X_C = X_{La} + \frac{R_a^2}{X_{Lm}} \cdot \frac{R_m}{R_m}$$

**Simpler KTU-Standard Approximation (most common in exams):**
The capacitor value required to bring the auxiliary current to lead the main current by 90° is determined by making the auxiliary branch impedance purely resistive at the operating frequency:

For exact 90° shift:
$$X_C - X_{La} = \frac{R_a \cdot X_{Lm} - R_m \cdot X_{La}}{R_m + R_a}$$

(Wait — using the rigorous KTU derivation:)

**Step 3 — Rigorous Derivation:**

The condition for $I_a$ leading $I_m$ by 90° is:

$$\frac{X_C - X_{La}}{R_a} = \frac{R_m}{X_{Lm}}$$

(Since the main branch has an impedance angle $\theta_m = \tan^{-1}(X_{Lm}/R_m)$, for $I_a$ to lead $I_m$ by 90°, the auxiliary branch must be at angle $\theta_a = -90° + \theta_m$ relative to the supply, but the standard KTU formulation is:)

**KTU Standard Formula Used:**

$$X_C = X_{La} + R_a \cdot \tan\theta_m$$

where $\tan\theta_m = \dfrac{X_{Lm}}{R_m}$.

**Step 4 — Calculate $\tan\theta_m$:**

$$\tan\theta_m = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75$$

$$\theta_m = \tan^{-1}(1.75) = 60.26°$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$X_C = 4 + 7.5 \times 1.75$$

$$X_C = 4 + 13.125 = 17.125\,\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Compute $C$:**

$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi f X_C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 17.125}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{5380.96}$$

$$\boxed{C = 185.84\,\mu F}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]` — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **variable reluctance (VR) stepper motor** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for step angle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Multi-toothed salient poles with concentrated windings. Typically 8 or more poles arranged in pairs.
- **Rotor:** Soft-iron, multi-toothed cylindrical core with **no permanent magnet** and **no windings**.
- **Bearings and shaft** provide mechanical output.
- **Driver circuit:** External electronic switching circuit energizes stator phases in a programmed sequence.

**Working:**
1. The rotor tends to align with the energized stator pole because this position has **minimum magnetic reluctance** (maximum inductance).
2. When the next phase is energized (and the previous one is de-energized), the rotor rotates by a small angle to align with the new energized pole.
3. Sequential energization of phases produces **stepwise rotation** of the rotor.
4. The direction of rotation is controlled by the *sequence* of phase energization (clockwise or anti-clockwise).

**Step Angle Derivation:**

Let $N_s$ = number of stator poles (teeth) and $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth.

Stator pole pitch (angular spacing between adjacent stator poles):

$$\text{Stator pitch} = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$

Rotor tooth pitch (angular spacing between adjacent rotor teeth):

$$\text{Rotor pitch} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

When one phase is energized, the rotor rotates to align one rotor tooth with the energized stator pole. When the next phase is energized, the rotor moves to align with the *adjacent* stator pole. The angular displacement is the *difference* between stator and rotor pitches:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s} - \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°(N_r - N_s)}{N_s \cdot N_r}$$

In magnitude:

$$\boxed{\theta_s = \frac{(N_s - N_r) \cdot 360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}}$$

---

**(b)** A hybrid stepper motor has 200 rotor teeth and is a 2-phase motor. It is driven by a pulse train of 1000 pulses per second in half-step mode. Calculate: **(i)** Step angle, **(ii) Steps per revolution, **(iii) Shaft speed in RPM. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**
- Rotor teeth: $N = 200$
- Phase pairs: $m = 2$ (two-phase hybrid)
- Pulse rate: $f_p = 1000$ pps
- Mode: Half-step

**Step 2 — Step Angle (Full Step):**

$$\theta_{s,\text{full}} = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N} = \frac{360°}{2 \times 200} = \frac{360°}{400} = 0.9°$$

**Step 3 — Step Angle (Half-Step):**

In half-step mode, the resolution doubles:

$$\theta_{s,\text{half}} = \frac{0.9°}{2} = 0.45°$$

**Step 4 — Steps per Revolution:**

$$n = \frac{360°}{\theta_{s,\text{half}}} = \frac{360°}{0.45°} = 800\,\text{steps/rev}$$

**Step 5 — Shaft Speed:**

$$N = \frac{f_p \cdot \theta_{s,\text{half}}}{6°} = \frac{1000 \times 0.45}{6} = \frac{450}{6}$$

$$\boxed{N = 75\,\text{RPM}}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Step angle formula confusion (2–3 marks lost):** Students often write $\theta_s = 360°/N_r$ (forgetting the stator role) or swap $N_s$ and $N_r$. **Always use** $\theta_s = \dfrac{(N_s - N_r) \times 360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ for VR motors.
>
> 2. **Universal motor DC vs AC analysis (1–2 marks):** On DC, $T \propto I_a$ (with $\phi$ constant saturation aside); on AC, $T \propto I_{rms}^2$. Mixing the two formulations is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Capacitor start motor — missing the 90° target (1 mark):** Many answers describe split-phase motors but fail to mention that the *capacitor* specifically targets 90° phase shift, which is the key engineering innovation.
>
> 4. **Centrifugal switch — what happens if it fails (often asked):** If the centrifugal switch fails *open*, the motor will *not start* (auxiliary winding is dead). If it fails *closed*, the auxiliary winding will *overheat* and burn out. This is a favorite KTU follow-up question worth 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Stepper motor holding vs detent torque (1 mark):** Confusing *holding torque* (with phase energized) with *detent torque* (residual torque in PM/hybrid types when de-energized) is a common error.
>
> 6. **Unit errors in stepper speed formula (1 mark):** Forgetting to convert pulses-per-second to RPM via the $\times 60$ factor.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🔑 High-Density Revision Checklist — Capacitor Start, Stepper & Universal Motor**

- **Capacitor Start Motor:**
  - Single-phase induction motor family member.
  - **Main + Auxiliary winding** displaced 90° in space; **capacitor** in series with aux creates ~90° *time* phase shift.
  - **High starting torque:** 3–4.5 × full-load torque.
  - **Centrifugal switch** opens at ~75% of $N_s$ → auxiliary disconnected.
  - Capacitor is **electrolytic** (AC-rated, short duty) — *not* paper or oil-type.
  - Running operation is *single-phase* (only main winding active).

- **Stepper Motor:**
  - **Brushless**, pulse-driven, open-loop positioning device.
  - **Three types:** Variable Reluctance (VR), Permanent Magnet (PM), Hybrid.
  - Step angle formula:
    - VR: $\theta_s = \dfrac{(N_s - N_r) \cdot 360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$
    - Hybrid/PM: $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N}$
  - Steps per revolution: $n = 360° / \theta_s$.
  - Shaft speed: $N = \dfrac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{6°}$ RPM.
  - **Holding torque** when energized; **detent torque** in PM/hybrid when de-energized.
  - Drive modes: Wave (1-phase), Full-step (2-phase), Half-step, Microstepping.
  - **Resonance** and **torque drop at high speeds** are critical limitations.

- **Universal Motor:**
  - **Series-wound DC motor** with laminated core → runs on **AC or DC**.
  - On DC: $T = k \phi I_a \propto I_a^2$ (series characteristic).
  - On AC: $T_{avg} = K \cdot I_{rms}^2$ — *pulsating* torque at $2f$.
  - **Very high no-load speed** (8,000–20,000+ RPM) — *mechanical governors or load is mandatory for safety*.
  - **Laminated stator** reduces eddy-current losses on AC.
  - **Compensating winding** in larger motors improves commutation under AC.
  - Power factor typically 0.7–0.9 lagging.
  - Applications: hand tools, mixers, vacuum cleaners, hair dryers.

- **Cross-Cutting Mnemonics:**
  - **"Cap-90-Start"** → Capacitor + 90° phase + High Starting torque.
  - **"VR-PM-Hybrid"** → Three types of stepper motors in increasing precision.
  - **"Series + Lamination = Universal"** → Series DC motor + laminated core = AC/DC capability.

- **Key Numerical Conversions to Memorize:**
  - $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$
  - $1$ pps × $1°$ step = $\dfrac{1}{6}$ RPM
  - $I_{rms} = \dfrac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$
  - $\sin^2(\omega t) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Principle of Operation # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition — The Three-Phase Induction Motor > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A **Three-Phase Induction Motor (3-φ IM)** is a *self-starting*, *brushless*, *singly-excited* AC machine that operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** between the stationary stator and the rotating rotor. The stator is energized by a balanced 3-phase AC supply, which produces a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** of constant magnitude in the air gap. This RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (per **Faraday's Law**); the resulting induced current interacts with the RMF to produce a torque (per **Lorentz Force Law / Lenz's Law**), causing the rotor to rotate in the same direction as the RMF. The rotor **never** catches up with the rotating field — the *relative speed* between them is what sustains induction. This relative speed, normalized, is called **Slip (s)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Invisible Tug of War" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for First-Time Learners** > Imagine a giant, invisible **rotating bar magnet** (the RMF) placed in the center of a circular swimming pool. Inside the pool floats a sealed, lightweight **metal ring** (the squirrel-cage rotor) with no power connection of its own. > > 1. The bar magnet starts spinning clockwise. The **changing magnetic flux** passing through the metal ring **induces eddy currents** in the ring (Faraday's Law). > 2. By **Lenz's Law**, these induced currents create their *own* magnetic field that *opposes* the relative motion — meaning it tries to "chase" the rotating magnet so that the relative motion disappears. > 3. Result: The ring is **dragged along** in the same direction as the magnet, but always at a slightly *slower* speed (because if it matched speed exactly, there would be no relative motion, no flux change, no induced current, and no torque). > > This chasing-but-never-catching behaviour is the soul of every induction motor ever built. ### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Constants | Term | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Supply frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** (India), 60 Hz (USA) | | Number of stator poles | $P$ | 2, 4, 6, 8, … (always even) | | Synchronous speed | $N_s$ | **rpm** (revolutions per minute) | | Rotor actual speed | $N_r$ | **rpm** | | Slip | $s$ | per-unit / fraction (0 to 1) | | Air-gap flux | $\Phi$ | **Weber (Wb)** | | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s$ | **rad/s** | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Induction" and not "Synchronous"?** > The rotor is **not** fed by any external electrical supply. The rotor current is purely *induced* from the stator field — hence the name **Induction Motor**. Contrast this with a *Synchronous Motor* where the rotor is DC-excited and rotates at *exactly* the synchronous speed. # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Features (Recap Required for KTU) A 3-phase induction motor has **two main parts**: **A. Stator (Stationary Part)** - Laminated silicon-steel core with **uniformly distributed slots**. - Three-phase balanced winding placed in the slots, with axes displaced by **120° electrical** in space. - Each phase winding is identical (same number of turns, same resistance, same pitch). - Connected to a balanced 3-phase AC supply (either Star or Delta). **B. Rotor (Rotating Part) — Two Variants** 1. **Squirrel-Cage Rotor:** Bars of copper/aluminium shorted at both ends by end-rings. No external connection, no slip rings, no brushes. **Rugged, cheap, maintenance-free.** 2. **Phase-Wound (Slip-Ring / Wound) Rotor:** Has a 3-phase winding similar to the stator, brought out via **slip rings and brushes** to external resistors (used for starting speed control). ## 2.2 Production of the Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) — The Heart of the Topic > [!IMPORTANT] > **The RMF is the single most important concept of this module.** Almost every KTU question on this module tests your understanding of *why and how* the RMF is produced. ### Step-by-Step Logic of RMF Formation - **Step 1 — Spatial Displacement:** The three stator phase windings (let's call them $R$, $Y$, $B$) are physically placed at **120° electrical apart** around the stator bore. - **Step 2 — Temporal Displacement:** They are energized by a balanced 3-phase supply, whose currents are **120° apart in time**: - $i_R = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ - $i_Y = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$ - $i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$ - **Step 3 — Pulsating MMFs:** Each phase, by itself, produces a **stationary (pulsating) sinusoidally distributed MMF** along its own axis. The amplitude of each pulsating MMF varies sinusoidally with time at supply frequency $f$. - **Step 4 — Vector Summation at Any Instant:** The space-vector sum of the three pulsating MMFs is computed at several time instants. We observe: - The **magnitude** of the resultant is **constant** (1.5 × peak MMF of one phase). - The **direction** of the resultant rotates in space at a uniform angular velocity. - The rotation direction is from the **leading-phase axis to the lagging-phase axis** (R → Y → B for a standard RYB sequence). - **Step 5 — Synchronous Speed:** The mechanical speed at which this magnetic field rotates is called the **synchronous speed** $N_s$. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility of RMF Concept** > The RMF principle is the foundation of every AC rotating machine: 3-phase induction motors, synchronous motors, AC servomotors, and even modern BLDC drives. Understanding it unlocks the operating principle of roughly **80% of industrial motors** in the world. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of RMF | $N_s$ in rpm, $f$ in Hz, $P$ = total poles | | 2 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous angular speed | rad/s | | 3 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip (per-unit / fraction) | $0 \le s \le 1$ for motor; $s=1$ at start, $s \approx 0.02$–$0.05$ full load | | 4 | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Frequency of rotor-induced current | Hz | | 5 | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | Actual rotor mechanical speed | rpm | | 6 | $E_2 = 4.44 \, f_r \, N_2 \, \Phi \, k_{w2}$ | Rotor induced EMF (per phase) | Volts | | 7 | $E_2 = s \cdot E_{20}$ | Rotor EMF at slip $s$ ($E_{20}$ is standstill EMF) | Volts | | 8 | $X_2 = s \cdot X_{20}$ | Rotor reactance at slip $s$ | Ohms | | 9 | $I_2 = \dfrac{s E_{20}}{\sqrt{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_{20})^{2}}}$ | Rotor current per phase | Amperes | | 10 | $\cos \phi_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_{20})^{2}}}$ | Rotor power factor | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **Critical LaTeX Note for Table Cells:** All absolute-value and conditional bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $0 \le s \le 1$ instead of $|s|$) to **protect the markdown table syntax**. Never use the bare pipe symbol `|` inside a table cell. ## 2.4 Why the Rotor Cannot Run at Synchronous Speed This is a **favourite KTU conceptual question (3-marks favourite).** - If $N_r = N_s$ → relative speed = 0 → no flux cutting → no induced rotor EMF → no rotor current → no torque. - Therefore, the rotor **must** always run at a speed *less than* $N_s$ (for motoring action, $0 < s < 1$). - At **no-load**, slip is very small ($s \approx 0.001$ to $0.005$). The rotor almost "chases" the RMF. - At **standstill / starting**, $N_r = 0$ → $s = 1$. - If driven by an external prime-mover *faster* than $N_s$ ($N_r > N_s$, $s < 0$) → machine acts as an **induction generator**. - If driven *opposite* to RMF direction ($N_r$ negative, $s > 1$) → machine acts as an **induction brake** (plugging). # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation — Production of Rotating Magnetic Field This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 4. Marks are almost guaranteed for a clean proof. ### Setup Consider a 2-pole, 3-phase stator winding with phase axes $R$, $Y$, $B$ displaced by **120° electrical** in space. The instantaneous MMF produced by each phase along its own axis (taking the positive maximum of the $R$-axis as reference) is: $$ F_R(x,\theta) = F_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta) $$ $$ F_Y(x,\theta) = F_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) \cos(\theta - 120°) $$ $$ F_B(x,\theta) = F_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) \cos(\theta - 240°) $$ where: - $F_m$ = peak MMF of one phase, - $\omega = 2\pi f$ = supply angular frequency, - $\theta$ = space-angle in electrical radians. ### Step 1 — Total MMF at any Space-Angle The total space-distributed MMF is the algebraic sum: $$ F_{total}(\theta,t) = F_R + F_Y + F_B $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Product-to-Sum Identity Using $\sin A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)\right]$ on each term: $$ F_R = \tfrac{1}{2} F_m \left[\sin(\omega t + \theta) + \sin(\omega t - \theta)\right] $$ $$ F_Y = \tfrac{1}{2} F_m \left[\sin(\omega t - 120° + \theta - 120°) + \sin(\omega t - 120° - \theta + 120°)\right] $$ $$ F_Y = \tfrac{1}{2} F_m \left[\sin(\omega t + \theta - 240°) + \sin(\omega t - \theta)\right] $$ $$ F_B = \tfrac{1}{2} F_m \left[\sin(\omega t - 240° + \theta - 240°) + \sin(\omega t - 240° - \theta + 240°)\right] $$ $$ F_B = \tfrac{1}{2} F_m \left[\sin(\omega t + \theta - 480°) + \sin(\omega t - \theta)\right] $$ ### Step 3 — Collect the "Forward" and "Backward" Travelling Waves The first term in each expression is of the form $\sin(\omega t + \theta - \phi)$; the second term is **identically** $\sin(\omega t - \theta)$ in all three. **Backward wave sum** (second terms add directly): $$ F_{back} = 3 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} F_m \sin(\omega t - \theta) = 1.5 F_m \sin(\omega t - \theta) $$ **Forward wave sum** (using $\sin\alpha + \sin(\alpha - 240°) + \sin(\alpha - 480°) = 0$ where $\alpha = \omega t + \theta$): $$ F_{fwd} = 0 $$ ### Step 4 — Final Result The total MMF is therefore: $$ \boxed{\,F_{total}(\theta,t) = 1.5 \, F_m \, \sin(\omega t - \theta)\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation of the Result** > The equation $F_{total} = 1.5 F_m \sin(\omega t - \theta)$ is the equation of a **sinusoidally distributed travelling wave** moving in the **+θ direction** with angular velocity $\omega$. This is the **Rotating Magnetic Field**. > - **Magnitude:** $1.5 F_m$ (constant, independent of $\theta$ and $t$). > - **Direction of rotation:** +θ (i.e., $R \to Y \to B$ for standard RYB phase sequence). > - **Angular speed:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ electrical rad/s, i.e., mechanical speed $= 2f/P$ rev/s $= 120f/P$ rpm. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Synchronous Speed $N_s$ A magnetic field of $P$ poles completes one electrical cycle per pair of poles. Hence, per mechanical revolution, the field undergoes $P/2$ electrical cycles. Time for one electrical cycle $= 1/f$ seconds. Time for one mechanical revolution $= (P/2) \cdot (1/f)$ seconds. $$ N_s = \frac{1}{(P/2f)} = \frac{2f}{P} \ \text{rev/s} $$ $$ \boxed{\,N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \ \text{rpm}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Quick Sanity Check:** For $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz → $N_s = (120 \times 50)/4 = 1500$ rpm. ✓ Matches textbook value. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Rotor Frequency $f_r$ The rotor physically rotates at $N_r$ rpm; the RMF rotates at $N_s$ rpm. The *relative* speed between RMF and rotor conductors is $(N_s - N_r)$ rpm. The relative speed in electrical revolutions per second $= (P/2) \cdot (N_s - N_r)/60$. Therefore, the frequency of EMF/current induced in the rotor is: $$ f_r = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{N_s - N_r}{60} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{s \, N_s}{60} = s \cdot \frac{P N_s}{120} = s \cdot f $$ $$ \boxed{\,f_r = s \, f\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This relationship ($f_r = s f$) is the *fundamental reason* why induction-motor rotor design and transformer design are *analogous*. The rotor behaves like a transformer secondary whose frequency depends on the slip. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Rotor EMF and Rotor Reactance as Functions of Slip Let $E_{20}$ = rotor EMF per phase at standstill ($s = 1$, $f_r = f$). Let $X_{20} = 2\pi f L_2$ = standstill rotor leakage reactance. Since the induced EMF is proportional to the *rate of change of flux* (Faraday's Law), and the rate of flux cutting is proportional to the relative speed (and hence to $f_r = s f$): $$ \frac{E_2}{E_{20}} = \frac{f_r}{f} = s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{E_2 = s \, E_{20}} $$ Similarly, reactance is proportional to frequency: $$ \boxed{X_2 = s \, X_{20}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Consequence for the Equivalent Circuit:** Because both $E_2$ and $X_2$ scale with $s$, dividing the entire rotor branch by $s$ gives a *frequency-independent* equivalent circuit — the standard textbook model used for circle-diagram analysis in Module 5. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Operating Conditions of an Induction Machine | Operating Mode | Range of $s$ | Range of $N_r$ | Direction of Power Flow | |---|---|---|---| | **Plugging / Braking** | $s > 1$ | Negative (opposite to RMF) | Both stator & rotor absorb power | | **Motoring** | $0 < s < 1$ | $0 < N_r < N_s$ | Electrical → Mechanical | | **Ideal No-Load (theoretical)** | $s = 0$ | $N_r = N_s$ | No torque, no power transfer | | **Generating** | $s < 0$ | $N_r > N_s$ | Mechanical → Electrical | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Induction Motor Operating Chain > [!NOTE] > The schematic below traces the complete cause-and-effect chain from a 3-phase mains supply all the way to mechanical shaft output. **Memorize this chain** — it is a high-yield diagram for KTU 14-mark theory questions. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Balanced 3-Phase AC Supply
f = 50 Hz] --> B[Stator 3-Phase Winding
Axes displaced 120 deg in space] B --> C[Time-Displaced Currents
120 deg phase shift in time] C --> D[Three Pulsating MMFs
One per phase axis] D --> E[Vector Sum of MMFs
F total equals 1.5 Fm sin wt minus theta] E --> F[Rotating Magnetic Field RMF
Speed Ns equals 120 f over P] F --> G[Air-Gap Flux Cuts Rotor Bars
Relative speed equals Ns minus Nr] G --> H[Faradays Law: EMF Induced in Rotor
E2 equals s times E20] H --> I[Rotor Circuit Closed
Current I2 flows in bars] I --> J[Lorentz Force on Current Carrying Conductors
F equals BIL] J --> K[Mechanical Torque Developed
T proportional to Phi times I2 times cos phi 2] K --> L[Rotor Accelerates
Nr increases until T equals T load] L --> M[Steady State Operation
Slip s settles to load value] M --> N[Shaft Mechanical Output
Useful work done] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style H fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style J fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Decision Logic Diagram — Why Slip Cannot Be Zero ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is rotor speed equal to Ns?} Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Relative speed equals 0] R1 --> R2[No flux cutting rotor conductors] R2 --> R3[Induced EMF E2 equals 0] R3 --> R4[Rotor current I2 equals 0] R4 --> R5[Torque T equals 0] R5 --> R6[Rotor decelerates immediately] Q1 -- No --> S1[Relative speed exists] S1 --> S2[Flux cuts rotor, EMF induced] S2 --> S3[Current flows, torque produced] S3 --> S4[Rotor accelerates toward Ns] S4 --> S5[Steady state: torque equals load torque
Slip s stabilises at small positive value] style R6 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style S5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Three Modes of Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MOTOR_MODE[Motoring 0 less than s less than 1] M1[Electrical Input] --> M2[Stator RMF] --> M3[Induced Rotor Current] --> M4[Mechanical Output at Shaft] end subgraph GEN_MODE[Generating s less than 0] G1[External Prime Mover] --> G2[Drives Rotor faster than Ns] --> G3[Slip negative] --> G4[Electrical Power Fed Back to Supply] end subgraph BRAKE_MODE[Braking s greater than 1] B1[Reversed Phase Supply] --> B2[Rotor pulled opposite to RMF] --> B3[Slip greater than 1] --> B4[Kinetic Energy Dissipated as Heat] end style M4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style G4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style B4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for KTU Viva** > Think **MGB** → **M**otor (slip small +ve), **G**enerator (slip −ve), **B**rake (slip > 1). # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]** ### Q1. Define a Rotating Magnetic Field. Why is it essential for the operation of a 3-phase induction motor? `[3 marks]` **Model Answer:** A **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** is a magnetic flux distribution of **constant magnitude** that **rotates continuously in space** at a uniform angular velocity, produced by a balanced 3-phase winding energized from a balanced 3-phase AC supply. For a 2-pole, 50 Hz machine, the RMF rotates at $N_s = (120 \times 50)/2 = 1500$ rpm. **Why it is essential:** The RMF is essential because it is the *only* source of flux cutting the stationary rotor conductors. Without relative motion between the rotor and the RMF, **Faraday's Law** cannot induce rotor EMF, no rotor current can flow, and therefore **no torque can be developed** by Lorentz force. The RMF thus acts as the *prime mover of flux* that drives the entire energy-conversion process. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. Define Slip. Why cannot an induction motor run at synchronous speed? `[3 marks]` **Model Answer:** **Slip (s)** is defined as the **per-unit relative speed** between the synchronous speed of the RMF and the actual mechanical speed of the rotor: $$ s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} $$ It is usually expressed as a fraction or as a percentage (typical full-load values: 2%–5%). **Why it cannot run at synchronous speed:** If the rotor were to rotate at the synchronous speed ($N_r = N_s$), the relative speed between the rotor conductors and the RMF would be **zero**. As a result, no flux lines would cut the rotor bars, no EMF would be induced, no current would flow, and consequently **no torque** could be produced. The rotor would immediately slow down. Therefore, an induction motor **must always run at a speed less than the synchronous speed**; this speed difference, normalised, is the slip. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### 📘 Question A — Choice 1 `[14 marks]` > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Pattern A]** **Q. (a)** Explain, with a neat sketch and necessary equations, the **production of a rotating magnetic field** by a balanced 3-phase winding. `[7 marks]` **(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor runs at 1440 rpm at full load. Determine: (i) Synchronous speed (ii) Slip at full load (iii) Frequency of rotor-induced EMF (iv) Speed of the rotor when slip is doubled (at constant supply frequency) `[7 marks]` --- ### Model Solution — Q. (a) `[7 marks]` **1. Construction:** A 3-phase, 2-pole stator has three identical coils $R$, $Y$, $B$ placed in slots such that their magnetic axes are displaced by **120° electrical in space**. The coils are connected to a balanced 3-phase supply producing currents: $$ i_R = I_m \sin(\omega t), \quad i_Y = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120°), \quad i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) $$ **2. Instantaneous analysis** (Tabulate the sign of each MMF at six instants, one per 60° of $\omega t$): | $\omega t$ | MMF along $R$ | MMF along $Y$ | MMF along $B$ | Resultant | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0° | $+F_m$ | $-0.5 F_m$ | $-0.5 F_m$ | $1.5 F_m$ along $R$ | | 60° | $+0.866 F_m$ | $+0.866 F_m$ | $-0.866 F_m$ | $1.5 F_m$ rotated 60° (R→Y) | | 120° | $+0.5 F_m$ | $+1.0 F_m$ | $-0.5 F_m$? | $1.5 F_m$ along $Y$ axis | | 180° | $0$ | $+0.866 F_m$ | $-0.866 F_m$? | … | *For brevity the standard 0°/60°/120°/180°/240°/300° table from the textbook is to be drawn in the answer script. The conclusion is:* **3. Conclusion:** - The **magnitude** of the resultant MMF is **constant** = $1.5 F_m$. - The **direction** rotates through one full revolution (360° electrical) in one time-period of the supply ($T = 1/f = 20$ ms for 50 Hz). - The rotation is in the direction **R → Y → B** for a standard RYB phase sequence. - Speed of rotation = $N_s = 120 f / P$ rpm. **(7 marks — full credit for: 2 marks sketch, 3 marks current/MMF table or math derivation, 2 marks conclusion)** --- ### Model Solution — Q. (b) `[7 marks]` **Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$, $N_r = 1440$ rpm. **(i) Synchronous speed:** $$ N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \mathbf{1500 \ rpm} $$ **(ii) Slip at full load:** $$ s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = \mathbf{0.04 \ p.u. \ (or\ 4\%)} $$ **(iii) Frequency of rotor-induced EMF:** $$ f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = \mathbf{2 \ Hz} $$ **(iv) New rotor speed when slip is doubled (constant $f$):** New slip $s' = 2 \times 0.04 = 0.08$ $$ N_r^{\,'} = N_s (1 - s') = 1500 (1 - 0.08) = 1500 \times 0.92 = \mathbf{1380 \ rpm} $$ --- ### 📘 Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) `[14 marks]` > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]** **Q. (a)** With the help of the **equivalent-circuit analogy with a transformer**, derive expressions for the **rotor induced EMF $E_2$** and **rotor leakage reactance $X_2$** as functions of slip. State the assumptions made. `[7 marks]` **(b)** Describe the **construction of a squirrel-cage induction motor** with a labelled diagram. Explain how the starting torque is produced in such a motor without any external supply to the rotor. `[7 marks]` --- ### Model Solution — Q. (a) `[7 marks]` **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Rotor MMF and stator MMF sinusoidally distributed in space. 2. Magnetic circuit linear (no saturation) ⇒ inductances constant. 3. Mechanical losses ignored for EMF calculation. 4. Rotor winding symmetry: identical 3-phase rotor winding (only for wound rotor) or equivalent symmetric cage. **Analogy with transformer (3 marks):** The stator acts as the **primary** and the rotor (when short-circuited) acts as the **secondary** of a *generalised transformer*. The only difference from a static transformer is that the secondary frequency is **not** fixed at supply frequency but is the rotor frequency $f_r = s f$. **Derivation of $E_2$ and $X_2$ (3 marks):** At standstill ($s = 1$, $f_r = f$): let rotor EMF be $E_{20}$ and rotor leakage reactance be $X_{20} = 2\pi f L_2$. Since the **rate of cutting of flux** is proportional to relative speed, the rotor EMF is proportional to the relative speed, i.e., to slip: $$ \frac{E_2}{E_{20}} = \frac{\text{relative speed at slip } s}{\text{relative speed at standstill}} = s $$ $$ \boxed{\,E_2 = s \, E_{20}\,} $$ Similarly, the rotor leakage reactance, which is proportional to frequency, becomes: $$ \boxed{\,X_2 = 2 \pi f_r L_2 = 2 \pi (s f) L_2 = s \, X_{20}\,} $$ --- ### Model Solution — Q.
