(b) `[7 marks]` **Construction of Squirrel-Cage Rotor (3 marks):** - Rotor core: laminated silicon-steel cylindrical core pressed on a cast-iron or fabricated steel shaft. - Slots: Semi-closed or closed slots punched on the outer periphery of the rotor laminations. **No insulation** is required between bars and slot walls (the bars are in direct contact with the iron, which acts as part of the magnetic circuit). - Rotor bars: **Copper or aluminium bars** inserted one per slot, running the entire axial length of the rotor. - End rings: The two ends of all the bars are short-circuited by two heavy **end-rings** (continuous short-circuiting rings), giving the assembly the appearance of a "squirrel cage" — hence the name. - Cooling fins: Often cast integrally with the end-rings on small motors for self-cooling. **Production of Starting Torque (4 marks):** 1. At standstill, $N_r = 0$, so slip $s = 1$. The relative speed between the stator RMF and the rotor is at its **maximum** (equal to $N_s$). 2. The rotating flux cuts the stationary rotor bars at the **synchronous speed** $N_s$, inducing a large EMF $E_{20}$ in each bar. 3. The bars being short-circuited by the end-rings, a **large short-circuit current $I_2 = E_{20}/Z_{20}$** flows through each bar. 4. This current-carrying bar, placed in the RMF, experiences a **Lorentz force** $F = B I_2 L$, which produces a torque about the shaft axis. 5. Since every bar in the *forward half* of the rotor contributes torque in the same rotational sense, the cumulative effect is a strong **starting torque** — and the rotor begins to accelerate in the direction of the RMF. 6. As the rotor picks up speed, slip decreases, induced EMF drops, current decreases, and the torque settles at the value required to balance the load torque (steady state). --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to mention the "constant magnitude" property of the RMF** — A common 1-mark loss. Always state that the resultant MMF magnitude is **1.5 Fm** (constant), *not* varying with time. > 2. **Confusing RMF direction with phase-sequence direction** — For the RYB sequence, RMF rotates *from R to Y to B*. For a reversed sequence (RBY), it rotates *from R to B to Y*. Both facts are commonly asked. > 3. **Using the wrong formula for synchronous speed** — A classic mistake is writing $N_s = 60f/P$ (which is for *one cycle per revolution*). The correct formula is $N_s = 120 f / P$ (because each mechanical revolution corresponds to $P/2$ electrical cycles). > 4. **Forgetting the units in slip calculation** — Slip is *dimensionless*; the answer must be in per-unit or percentage, not rpm. > 5. **Assuming rotor is at standstill for running condition** — Many students erroneously use $s=1$ even when the motor is running. Always compute $s$ from the *given* $N_r$ first. > 6. **Mixing up rotor EMF at standstill vs running** — $E_{20}$ is the **standstill** EMF. The actual running EMF is $E_2 = s E_{20}$. Writing $E_2 = E_{20}$ in a running condition costs a mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨ - 🔑 **RMF** = Resultant of three space-and-time displaced pulsating MMFs. Magnitude = $1.5 F_m$ (constant). Direction rotates at $\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s. - 🔑 **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$ rpm. For 50 Hz: 2-pole → 3000, 4-pole → 1500, 6-pole → 1000 rpm. - 🔑 **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. Typical full-load slip: 2–5%. **s = 0** → ideal no-load; **s = 1** → standstill; **s < 0** → generating; **s > 1** → braking. - 🔑 **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s f$ — the fundamental *transformer-analogy* result. - 🔑 **Rotor EMF** scales linearly with slip: $E_2 = s E_{20}$. **Rotor reactance** also: $X_2 = s X_{20}$. - 🔑 **Direction reversal:** Swap any *two* of the three supply leads to reverse the direction of rotation. Swap *all three* → no change. Swap *none* → no change. - 🔑 **Three pillars of the principle:** (1) Faraday's Law → EMF, (2) Closed rotor circuit → Current, (3) Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor in B-field → Torque. - 🔑 **Squirrel-cage** rotor = robust, cheap, maintenance-free, but low starting torque per ampere. - 🔑 **Phase-wound (slip-ring)** rotor = external resistance possible → improved starting torque and speed control, but costlier. - 🔑 **Induction motor is self-starting** because the RMF is present from the *instant* the supply is switched on (no need for rotor pre-excitation). This is the principal advantage over a synchronous motor. - 🔑 **Energy conversion flow:** Electrical Input → Stator Cu loss + Core loss → Air-gap power $P_g$ → Rotor Cu loss ($s P_g$) + Mechanical power developed $(1-s) P_g$ → Friction & Windage + Shaft output $P_{out}$.

topics.title: Multitask Variable Reluctance Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multitask Variable Reluctance Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multi-Stack Variable Reluctance Motor (VRM)**, often classified as a type of variable reluctance **stepper motor**, is a salient-pole synchronous machine in which the rotation is achieved purely by the principle of *minimum reluctance* (or maximum permeance) of the magnetic flux path. The multi-stack designation indicates that the machine is constructed from **three or more independent magnetically-isolated rotor-stator sections (stacks)** mounted rigidly on a common shaft, with each stack acting as a discrete phase and displaced from the next by a specific mechanical angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A multi-stack variable reluctance motor is a self-starting, brushless, synchronous motor in which the rotor aligns to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** for each excited stator phase, producing discrete angular steps rather than continuous rotation. The "multi-stack" architecture provides finer step resolution and smoother torque than a single-stack equivalent.

Mathematically, the electromagnetic torque in any VRM arises from the tendency of the rotor to move to a position where the **co-energy** of the magnetic system is maximized:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

where $L(\theta)$ is the position-dependent phase inductance and $\theta$ is the rotor electrical angle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnetic Seesaw"

Imagine a child seated on one end of a seesaw. The seesaw will always tilt toward the heavier side (lower potential energy). Now, place a child on the other end—the seesaw rocks toward the new heavier side. The child represents **stator-excited magnetic flux**, and the pivot is the **rotor**. The seesaw never "drives" itself continuously; instead, it jumps from one preferred position to another—exactly how a multi-stack VRM steps its rotor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Reluctance"?** Reluctance is the magnetic equivalent of electrical resistance. The flux always prefers the path of *least reluctance* (just as water prefers the path of least friction). The rotor is essentially a "shape that wants to align with the stator field to minimize reluctance."

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| **Number of stator phases** | **3, 4, or 5** (commonly 3 for multi-stack) |
| **Step angle (standard)** | **1.8°, 2.5°, 7.5°, 15°** |
| **Supply voltage** | **5 V – 24 V DC** (with driver electronics) |
| **Air-gap flux density** | **0.5 T – 1.2 T** |
| **Pull-out torque** | **0.1 Nm – 50 Nm** (application dependent) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike DC or induction motors, a VRM has **no continuous rotation under natural excitation**. It produces discrete steps in synchronism with pulses—hence it is the foundational device behind every digital positioning system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture of a Multi-Stack VRM

A multi-stack variable reluctance motor is built by stacking **multiple identical rotor-stator laminations** along the axial length of a common shaft. Each individual stack is:

1. **Magnetically isolated** from neighboring stacks (using non-magnetic spacers of brass or aluminum).
2. Mechanically **rotor-displaced** from the previous stack by a fixed angular offset equal to the desired **step angle**.
3. Excited by its **own dedicated phase winding**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multi-stack?** A single-stack VR motor has a coarse step angle. By dividing the angular displacement across N stacks, the effective step resolution is multiplied by N, enabling finer motion for precision applications like CNC and robotics.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialization:** All phase windings are de-energized; the rotor rests in an undefined position (free state).
2. **Phase-A excitation:** Stator phase A is energized. The stator pole faces attract the rotor's salient poles toward minimum reluctance alignment.
3. **Phase-A de-energized → Phase-B excited:** The rotor moves to the next aligned position defined by stack B's geometry.
4. **Sequential pulsing:** Repeating the A → B → C → A sequence produces **unidirectional stepping**.
5. **Reversal:** Reversing the phase sequence (A → C → B → A) reverses the rotation direction.

### 2.2.1 Why does the rotor move?
The torque produced in any phase is:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

- When $\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} > 0$ (rotor approaching aligned position), torque is **positive** (accelerating).
- When $\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} < 0$ (rotor moving past aligned position), torque is **negative** (decelerating).
- The rotor therefore "locks" into the aligned position of the excited phase.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formulas — they appear in 90% of KTU numerical questions on VRM.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ | Step angle for single-stack VRM | degrees |
| 2 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot q}$ | Step angle for multi-stack (m phases) where $q$ = teeth/pole | degrees |
| 3 | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Electromagnetic torque (reluctance principle) | Nm |
| 4 | $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$ | Position-dependent phase inductance | H |
| 5 | $W_{fld} = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$ | Co-energy stored in magnetic field | Joules |
| 6 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{\omega_s}{\theta_s \cdot \pi/180}$ | Step pulse frequency for synchronous speed | Hz |
| 7 | $n_s = \dfrac{f \cdot 60}{N_r}$ | Synchronous speed (general SRM form) | RPM |
| 8 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Step angle for N-stack motor (equal displacement) | degrees |

> **Notation Key:** $N_s$ = number of stator poles, $N_r$ = number of rotor poles, $m$ = number of stacks, $q$ = number of rotor teeth per stack, $N$ = number of stacks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC machines and 3D printers:** Sub-millimeter positional accuracy.
- **Robotic actuators:** Open-loop position control without encoders.
- **Quartz watch movements:** Ultra-fine micro-stepping.
- **Hard disk drives:** Head positioning actuator.
- **Automotive gauges:** Speedometers, fuel indicators.
- **Valve actuators in process control:** Industrial flow regulation.

> [!TIP]
> **Why no feedback encoder is needed:** Because the rotor position is "digitally defined" by the excited phase, open-loop control is sufficient for many applications—this is a major cost advantage over servo motors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle of a Multi-Stack VRM

### Problem Setup
A multi-stack variable reluctance motor has **3 stacks (m = 3)** and each rotor has **q = 4 salient teeth** uniformly distributed around the rotor circumference. Determine the step angle per pulse.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the angular pitch between consecutive rotor teeth.**

$$
\theta_{tooth} = \frac{360°}{q} = \frac{360°}{4} = 90°
$$

This is the mechanical angle between two adjacent rotor teeth on a single stack.

**Step 2: Determine the mechanical displacement per stack.**

In a multi-stack motor, the rotor of the $(k+1)^{th}$ stack is angularly displaced from the rotor of the $k^{th}$ stack by:

$$
\delta = \frac{\theta_{tooth}}{m} = \frac{90°}{3} = 30°
$$

This is the *fundamental mechanical offset* introduced during mechanical assembly.

**Step 3: Apply the general multi-stack step-angle formula.**

The step angle is the angle by which the rotor advances when excitation transfers from one phase to the next. By the KTU formula:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot q}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{3 \times 4} = \frac{360°}{12} = 30°
$$

**Step 5: Verify the result using the displacement identity.**

$$
\theta_s = \delta = \frac{\theta_{tooth}}{m} = \frac{90°}{3} = 30° \;\; \checkmark
$$

Both approaches converge on the same answer, confirming consistency.

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\theta_s = 30°}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When a problem gives you the number of stacks and teeth per rotor, ALWAYS use the KTU formula $\theta_s = 360° / (m \cdot q)$. This is a 3-mark direct question in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Expression from Co-Energy

### Problem Setup
Derive the electromagnetic torque expression for a variable reluctance motor starting from the co-energy principle.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the expression for magnetic co-energy.**

For a singly-excited magnetic system with current $i$ and flux linkage $\lambda = L(\theta) \cdot i$:

$$
W'_{fld} = \int_0^{i} \lambda(i, \theta) \, di = \int_0^{i} L(\theta) \cdot i \, di = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2
$$

The co-energy is the magnetic energy available to do mechanical work.

**Step 2: Apply the principle of virtual work.**

The mechanical work done by the system in a virtual displacement $d\theta$ equals the change in co-energy at constant current (since current source is assumed):

$$
T_e \cdot d\theta = dW'_{fld} \big\vert_{i = \text{const}}
$$

**Step 3: Take the partial derivative with respect to $\theta$.**

Differentiating $W'_{fld} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2$ with respect to $\theta$ at constant $i$:

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{fld}}{\partial \theta}\bigg\vert_{i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2\right)
$$

**Step 4: Recognize that $i$ is independent of $\theta$ under constant-current excitation.**

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Fourier series form of $L(\theta)$.**

For a periodic inductance profile with $N_r$ rotor teeth:

$$
L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)
$$

Differentiating with respect to $\theta$:

$$
\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} = -N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)
$$

**Step 6: Final torque expression.**

$$
T_e = -\frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{T_e = -\frac{1}{2} i^2 N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that the torque acts to *reduce* the displacement from alignment (drives the rotor toward the minimum-reluctance position). Maximum torque occurs at $N_r \theta = 90°$ (midway between aligned and unaligned positions).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Single-Stack vs Multi-Stack VRM

| Parameter | Single-Stack VRM | Multi-Stack VRM |
|---|---|---|
| **Step resolution** | Coarse (15°–30°) | Fine (1.8°–7.5°) |
| **Number of phases** | 3 or 4 | 3 × N stacks |
| **Construction** | Simple | Complex (multi-section shaft) |
| **Torque ripple** | Higher | Lower (multi-stack averaging) |
| **Driver electronics** | 3 or 4 H-bridges | 3N half-bridge drivers |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Typical application** | Clocks, simple indicators | CNC, robotics, printers |
| **Rotor saliency** | Single set of teeth | Multiple sets (one per stack) |
| **Magnetic isolation** | Not required | Required (brass spacers) |
| **Reliability** | High | High (modular design) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the exam:** *"Multi-stack = Multi-step = Multi-precision."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Multi-Stack VRM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Supply 5V to 24V] --> B[Microcontroller / Pulse Generator]
    B --> C1[Phase A Driver H-Bridge]
    B --> C2[Phase B Driver H-Bridge]
    B --> C3[Phase C Driver H-Bridge]
    C1 --> D1[Stack 1 Stator Winding]
    C2 --> D2[Stack 2 Stator Winding]
    C3 --> D3[Stack 3 Stator Winding]
    D1 --> E1[Rotor Section 1 Salient Poles]
    D2 --> E2[Rotor Section 2 Displaced by theta_s]
    D3 --> E3[Rotor Section 3 Displaced by 2 theta_s]
    E1 --> F[Common Shaft]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Mechanical Output Load]
    H[Sequential Phase Logic A then B then C] --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram shows the **power flow** and **control signal flow** for a 3-stack, 3-phase VRM. The three stator phases are energized in a cyclic sequence, and the rotor (with three mechanically-displaced salient sections) follows the field.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Stepping Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> PhaseA: Pulse 1 Received
    PhaseA --> PhaseB: Pulse 2 Received
    PhaseB --> PhaseC: Pulse 3 Received
    PhaseC --> PhaseA: Pulse 4 Received
    PhaseA --> PhaseC: Reverse Direction Command
    PhaseC --> PhaseB: Reverse Direction Command
    PhaseB --> PhaseA: Reverse Direction Command
    PhaseA --> Standby: All Pulses Stop
```

> [!NOTE]
> This state machine models the **rotor behavior** under sequential excitation. For KTU theory, the student must understand that the rotor "visits" each aligned position in sequence, and reversing the sequence reverses the rotation.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Magnetic Flux Path in One Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stator Pole A North] --> B1[Air Gap to Rotor Tooth 1]
    B1 --> C1[Rotor Iron Core]
    C1 --> D1[Air Gap to Rotor Tooth 2]
    D1 --> E1[Stator Pole A South]
    E1 --> F1[Stator Yoke]
    F1 --> A1
```

> [!TIP]
> The rotor aligns so that the flux path encounters **minimum air-gap length**—this is the "minimum reluctance" condition that drives the rotation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**

> **Q1.** Define a multi-stack variable reluctance motor. Why is it called a "reluctance" motor?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A multi-stack variable reluctance motor is a **stepper motor** that produces discrete angular steps based on the principle of *minimum magnetic reluctance*. It consists of **multiple rotor-stator stacks mounted on a common shaft**, with each stack magnetically isolated and excited by a separate phase winding.

It is called a "reluctance" motor because the rotor motion arises purely from the magnetic circuit's tendency to align itself to a position of **minimum reluctance** (analogous to minimum resistance in an electric circuit), without any reliance on permanent magnets or rotor-induced currents.

> **Marking Note (1.5 + 1.5):** 1.5 marks for the definition and 1.5 marks for the reluctance explanation.

---

### Question 2: Operating Principle
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

> **Q2.** Explain why a multi-stack variable reluctance motor is *self-starting*, unlike a conventional synchronous motor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A multi-stack VRM is self-starting because **its rotor is salient (has discrete teeth)** and the **stator phases are excited sequentially in a defined switching pattern**. When phase A is energized, the rotor aligns to the minimum-reluctance position of stack A; the excitation then transfers to phase B, and the rotor advances to the new aligned position.

In contrast, a conventional smooth-rotor synchronous motor is **not self-starting** because at standstill the rotating field generated by a balanced 3-phase supply rotates at synchronous speed, which the rotor cannot follow from rest due to inertia.

> **Marking Note:** 2 marks for the explanation of VRM self-starting, 1 mark for the contrast with synchronous motor.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Main Exam]**

> **Q(A)(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a multi-stack variable reluctance motor. State any three advantages and two disadvantages. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q(A)(b)** A 3-stack variable reluctance stepper motor has 4 rotor teeth per stack. Calculate: (i) the step angle, (ii) the number of pulses required for one complete revolution, and (iii) the shaft speed in RPM if the pulse rate is 1000 pulses per second. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(A)(a):

**Construction (3 marks):**

- A multi-stack VRM has **3 stacks** (or more) of stator-rotor laminations mounted on a **common shaft**.
- Each rotor section has **salient teeth** (typically 4 to 8) and is **mechanically displaced** from the adjacent section by a fixed angle.
- **Brass or aluminum spacers** provide magnetic isolation between stacks.
- Each stack has its own **single-phase winding** wound on diametrically opposite stator poles.
- A **microcontroller-based driver** sequences the phase excitations.

**Working (3 marks):**

- Phase A is energized → rotor aligns with stack A's minimum-reluctance position.
- Phase B is energized → rotor advances by step angle to align with stack B.
- Phase C is energized → rotor advances again.
- The cycle A → B → C → A produces **continuous unidirectional stepping**.
- Reversing the sequence A → C → B → A reverses the rotation.

**Advantages (0.5 + 0.5 = 1 mark, any 3):**
- High positional accuracy.
- Open-loop control (no encoder needed).
- Long life (brushless).
- Excellent start-stop and hold characteristics.

**Disadvantages (0.5 mark, any 2):**
- Resonance at low speeds.
- Lower efficiency than DC servo systems.
- Requires dedicated driver electronics.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(A)(b):

**Given:** $m = 3$ stacks, $q = 4$ teeth per rotor, pulse rate $f_p = 1000$ pulses/sec.

**(i) Step angle:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot q} = \frac{360°}{3 \times 4} = \frac{360°}{12} = 30°
$$

**(ii) Pulses per revolution:**

$$
N_{pulse} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{30°} = 12 \text{ pulses}
$$

**(iii) Shaft speed in RPM:**

$$
n = \frac{f_p \times 60}{N_{pulse}} = \frac{1000 \times 60}{12} = 5000 \text{ RPM}
$$

> **Marking Note:** Each sub-part carries ~2.3 marks. Final answer must be boxed. KTU evaluators award 0.5 marks for the formula and 1.5 marks for substitution and final value.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

> **Q(B)(a)** Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque in a variable reluctance motor from the co-energy principle. Briefly explain the significance of the inductance profile $L(\theta)$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q(B)(b)** Compare single-stack and multi-stack variable reluctance motors in terms of construction, step resolution, torque ripple, and applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(B)(a):

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from co-energy:

$$
W'_{fld} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2
$$

Torque by virtual work at constant current:

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{fld}}{\partial \theta}\bigg\vert_{i}
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

Substituting $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$:

$$
T_e = -\frac{1}{2} i^2 N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)
$$

**Significance of $L(\theta)$ (2 marks):**

- $L(\theta)$ defines the **position-dependent magnetic coupling** between stator and rotor.
- Its **derivative** $dL/d\theta$ is the **torque-producing term**; the steeper the inductance slope, the higher the torque.
- $L_1$ is the **fundamental component** of the inductance ripple; harmonics in $L(\theta)$ cause torque ripple and must be minimized.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(B)(b):

| Parameter | Single-Stack VRM | Multi-Stack VRM |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Single stator-rotor pair | Multiple stacked sections on common shaft |
| **Step resolution** | Coarse, typically 15°–30° | Fine, typically 1.8°–7.5° |
| **Torque ripple** | Higher (single air-gap pulse) | Lower (averaged over multiple stacks) |
| **Magnetic isolation** | Not needed | Required (brass spacers) |
| **Applications** | Clocks, simple gauges, low-cost toys | CNC machines, printers, robotics |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher due to complexity |

> **Marking Note:** Award 7 marks distributed as: 2 marks for construction, 2 marks for step resolution, 1.5 marks for torque ripple, 1.5 marks for applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing rotor poles with rotor teeth** — In stepper motors, "rotor poles" often means the *number of salient teeth*, NOT magnetic pole pairs. Misinterpretation leads to a step-angle calculation error (typically −2 to −3 marks).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $m$ (number of stacks)** — Students often use the single-stack formula $\theta_s = 360°/N_r$ instead of the multi-stack formula $\theta_s = 360°/(m \cdot q)$. This is the **#1 mistake** reported by KTU evaluators.
>
> 3. **Not writing units in the final answer** — KTU strictly penalizes missing units in numerical answers (typically 0.5 mark deduction).
>
> 4. **Confusing "holding torque" with "pull-out torque"** — Holding torque is the *static* torque at standstill; pull-out torque is the *maximum dynamic* torque before losing synchronism. Examiners test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Skipping the assumption of constant current** in the co-energy derivation — Without this, partial derivative with respect to $\theta$ becomes invalid.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Multi-Stack Variable Reluctance Motors:**

- ✅ Multi-stack VRM = **stepper motor** based on the **minimum reluctance principle**.
- ✅ **Construction:** Multiple magnetically-isolated rotor-stator sections on a **common shaft**.
- ✅ **Rotor:** Salient poles (teeth), no windings, no permanent magnets.
- ✅ **Operation:** Sequential phase excitation (A → B → C → A) advances the rotor by one step angle.
- ✅ **Step Angle Formula:** $\theta_s = 360° / (m \cdot q)$ — **most important formula**.
- ✅ **Pulses per Revolution:** $N_p = 360° / \theta_s$.
- ✅ **Shaft Speed (RPM):** $n = (f_p \times 60) / N_p$.
- ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot (dL/d\theta)$.
- ✅ **Inductance Profile:** $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Brushless, self-starting, open-loop position control, high reliability.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Resonance at low speeds, lower efficiency than servo systems, requires driver electronics.
- ✅ **Applications:** CNC, robotics, printers, disk drives, valve actuators.
- ✅ **Examiner's Favorite Topics:** Step-angle calculation, torque derivation, comparison with single-stack, role of magnetic isolation.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** *"Three stacks, four teeth, 12 steps per revolution."* — This single sentence covers the standard KTU numerical problem. Memorize it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Stack Variable Reluctance Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single Stack Variable Reluctance Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Stack Variable Reluctance Motor (VRM):** A salient-pole synchronous motor in which the **rotor has no field winding or permanent magnet** and rotates due to the **tendency of the magnetic circuit to align the rotor to a position of minimum reluctance** with respect to the energized stator phase. The term *single stack* refers to the use of a **single laminated rotor and stator stack** on a common shaft, as opposed to multi-stack machines which use multiple axially separated stacks.

The torque produced is purely **reluctance torque** (alignment torque), governed by the principle that the rotor always seeks the path of **minimum magnetic reluctance** (i.e., maximum inductance) in the energized stator field.

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta}
$$

where $L$ is the phase inductance and $\theta$ is the rotor angular position.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron Filing in a Shifting Field"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine a small iron pin floating on water, with two bar magnets that you can switch ON and OFF alternately. The iron pin *always* rotates so that it points along the magnetic field lines (the path of least resistance for the flux). When you suddenly turn OFF magnet A and turn ON magnet B, the iron pin *snaps* to align with magnet B. By sequentially switching A, B, A, B… the pin rotates in discrete *steps*. The **iron pin is your rotor** (no winding, just a ferromagnetic salient body), and the **alternately-energized electromagnets are your stator phases**.

This "alignment with minimum reluctance" behaviour is the heartbeat of the VRM — no rotor current, no permanent magnet, **pure magnetic attraction**.

## 1.3 Classification Context (KTU Module-4 Hierarchy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Position in the Stepper / SRM Family:**
> 1. **Stepper Motors** (covered in parallel modules)
>    - Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper — *this topic*
>    - Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper
>    - Hybrid Stepper
> 2. **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** — high-power industrial cousin, runs on the same principle but with closed-loop control and continuous rotation.

The Single-Stack VRM is the **simplest and oldest** member of this family, ideal for open-loop positioning applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy

| Component | Description | Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Stator** | Laminated silicon-steel core with **concentrated phase windings** on salient poles | M-19 / M-43 silicon steel laminations (0.35–0.5 mm) |
| **Stator Winding** | Concentrated coils, one (or two) coils per pole; phases energized sequentially | Copper wire, Class F/H insulation |
| **Rotor** | **Salient-pole, unwound, non-magnetized** soft-iron body | Same lamination material as stator |
| **Air Gap** | Non-uniform; minimum at the **aligned** rotor–stator pole overlap | — |
| **Shaft / Bearings** | Rigid shaft supported on ball bearings; no slip-rings or brushes | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salient Feature:** Because the rotor has **no magnets, no windings, and no slip-rings**, the VRM is **brushless, rotor-cooling-free, and highly robust** — making it a maintenance-free machine.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Phase-A energized** → A magnetic field is established across the air gap between two diametrically opposite Phase-A stator poles.
2. **Rotor aligns**: The salient rotor pole nearest the energized stator pole rotates to the **minimum-reluctance position** (full overlap of rotor and stator pole arcs).
3. **Phase-A switched OFF; Phase-B energized** → The magnetic field jumps to the Phase-B stator poles, which are *spatially shifted* by an electrical angle of $90°$ in a 2-phase machine.
4. **Rotor takes a discrete step** to re-align with the new flux axis.
5. **Sequential switching** of A → B → A' → B' → A … produces **continuous discrete rotation**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **direction of rotation** is set by the *sequence* in which the phases are energized (A-B-A'-B' for CW, A-B'-A'-B for CCW). This is the cornerstone of open-loop digital control in robotics and CNC.

## 2.3 Pole Combination Theory — The Heart of the Topic

For the rotor to take **discrete, repeatable steps**, the stator and rotor pole counts must obey strict numerical rules.

### 2.3.1 The Two Critical Conditions

**Condition 1 — Non-Equality:**
$$
N_s \neq N_r
$$
If $N_s = N_r$, *all* rotor poles would align simultaneously and the rotor would simply vibrate — no stepping.

**Condition 2 — The Difference Rule (for 2-phase, single-stack):**
$$
(N_s - N_r) \neq 0, \pm 2, \pm 4, \pm 6 \dots
$$
$$
\text{(i.e., } (N_s - N_r) \text{ must not be a multiple of 4 in 2-phase machines)}
$$

This prevents the situation where one phase energization aligns *all* rotor poles at once, leaving no "pull" for the next phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approved Pole Pairs for 2-Phase Single-Stack VRM:**
> Common textbook-approved combinations: (8, 6), (6, 4), (10, 8), (12, 8) — i.e., $N_s - N_r = 2$ or $4$.

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — every KTU question on this topic revolves around these equations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Step Angle** ($\alpha$) | $\alpha = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ | degrees (mech.) | $m$ = number of phases, $N_r$ = rotor poles |
| 2 | **Step Angle (2-phase)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{180°}{N_r}$ | degrees | When $m = 2$ |
| 3 | **Step Angle (3-phase)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{120°}{N_r}$ | degrees | When $m = 3$ |
| 4 | **Steps per Revolution** ($N_{rev}$) | $N_{rev} = m \cdot N_r = \dfrac{360°}{\alpha}$ | pulses/rev | — |
| 5 | **Synchronous Speed** ($N_s$) | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{p}$ | rpm | Only used if run as synchronous motor |
| 6 | **Electrical Step Angle** | $\alpha_e = m \cdot \alpha$ | degrees (elec.) | Equals $360°$ / $N_r$ in some cases |
| 7 | **Reluctance Torque** | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | N·m | Core operating equation |
| 8 | **Inductance Variation** | $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_2 \cos(2N_r \theta)$ | H | Fourier form; $L_0, L_2$ depend on geometry |
| 9 | **Stator Pole Pitch** | $\tau_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s}$ | degrees | — |
| 10 | **Rotor Pole Pitch** | $\tau_r = \dfrac{360°}{N_r}$ | degrees | $\tau_r \neq \tau_s$ always |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **stator pole pitch** with **rotor pole pitch**. The very *reason* a VRM works is that these two pitches are **deliberately different** ($\tau_s \neq \tau_r$), which forces the rotor to step rather than align universally.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Single-Stack VRMs are deployed:**
> - **Quartz-analog wrist watches** (60 steps/rev, ultra-low power, 2-phase micro-VRM)
> - **Computer printers & plotters** (paper feed, print-head positioning)
> - **Floppy disk drives & early hard-disk head positioners**
> - **Robotic joints and X-Y tables** (open-loop positioning)
> - **Textile looms, valve actuators, and small pumps**

The defining engineering merit: **no rotor copper → no rotor I²R loss → exceptional efficiency** at low speeds and high holding torque.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle of a 2-Phase Single-Stack VRM

> **Aim:** To derive $\alpha = 180°/N_r$ from first principles using the geometry of pole alignment.

**Step 1 — Define the electrical cycle.**

A *full electrical cycle* corresponds to the rotor advancing by **two rotor pole pitches** (because adjacent rotor poles have alternating magnetic polarity under the same stator field). Therefore:

$$
\text{Electrical cycle} = 2 \tau_r = 2 \cdot \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{720°}{N_r} \quad \text{(mechanical)}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that one full electrical cycle is equivalent to 360° electrical.**

The electrical angle $\theta_e$ and mechanical angle $\theta_m$ are related by the number of rotor pole pairs. For salient-pole rotor, the number of pole pairs equals $N_r$ (since every rotor tooth is a pole):

$$
\theta_e = N_r \cdot \theta_m
$$

So one full mechanical revolution of $360°$ corresponds to $360° \cdot N_r$ of electrical rotation. But each "step" of the rotor covers $1/N_r$ of a mechanical revolution, i.e.:

$$
\theta_m^{\text{step}} = \frac{360°}{N_r}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 2-phase switching rule.**

In a **2-phase** VRM, the stator winding is split into two phases A and B. When phase A is on, the rotor aligns to phase A. When phase B is energized (and A is off), the rotor must move to the nearest phase-B pole. The **angular distance between consecutive stator poles energized by alternating phases** is:

$$
\text{Switching pitch} = \frac{360°}{N_s}
$$

For an *approved* pole combination (e.g., $N_s = N_r + 2$), this switching pitch equals one rotor pole pitch **divided by 2**:

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{180°}{N_r}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to an $m$-phase machine.**

For an $m$-phase single-stack VRM, sequential switching of $m$ phases advances the rotor by one rotor pole pitch over $m$ steps:

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of steps per revolution.**

$$
N_{rev} = \frac{360°}{\alpha} = m \cdot N_r
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** For a watch movement with $N_r = 60$, $m = 2$:
> $\alpha = 180°/60 = 3°$, giving $N_{rev} = 120$ steps/rev.
> This is exactly the resolution needed for a quartz-analog second hand.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Reluctance-Torque Equation

> **Aim:** To derive $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ from co-energy principles.

**Step 1 — Energy stored in a magnetic circuit with varying reluctance.**

For a phase winding of inductance $L(\theta)$ carrying instantaneous current $i$, the **co-energy** is:

$$
W'_{fld} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta)\, i^2
$$

**Step 2 — Co-energy method for mechanical force/torque.**

The electromagnetic torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular position, at constant current (current-fed assumption):

$$
T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{fld}}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i = \text{const}}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate explicitly.**

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left[\frac{1}{2} L(\theta)\, i^2\right]_{i=\text{const}}
$$

Since $i$ is held constant, it can be taken outside the derivative:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2\, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Sign convention and physical meaning.**

$$
\boxed{\;T_e = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2\, \frac{dL}{d\theta}\;}
$$

- If $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$ (inductance rising with $\theta$), torque is **positive** → rotor accelerates in $+\theta$ direction.
- If $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} < 0$, torque is **negative** (still produces useful braking/holding torque because of the $i^2$ term — torque is unidirectional in sign of $dL/d\theta$, but always produces alignment).

**Step 5 — Express $L(\theta)$ as a Fourier series.**

For a doubly-salient machine with $N_r$ rotor poles, the air-gap permeance is periodic in $\theta$ with period $2\pi/N_r$:

$$
L(\theta) = L_0 + L_2 \cos\!\big(2 N_r\, \theta\big)
$$

(The $L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$ term vanishes due to symmetry of the salient-pole rotor.)

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -2 N_r L_2 \sin\!\big(2 N_r\, \theta\big)
$$

Substituting into the torque equation:

$$
T_e = -N_r\, i^2\, L_2\, \sin\!\big(2 N_r\, \theta\big)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Torque is **independent of rotor current direction** (it is a function of $i^2$), so a **unipolar (DC) drive** is sufficient — the motor does *not* require AC sinusoidal excitation. This is why VRMs are typically driven by a simple unipolar electronic switching circuit.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Pole-Combination Validity Rule

**Step 1 — Count of stable equilibrium positions.**

When phase A is energized, the rotor settles at positions where a rotor pole is fully aligned with a phase-A stator pole. There are exactly $N_r$ such stable positions (one for each rotor pole).

**Step 2 — Phase-B alignment offset.**

The phase-B stator poles are *physically displaced* from phase-A poles by a mechanical angle:

$$
\Delta = \frac{360°}{N_s} \quad \text{(one stator pole pitch)}
$$

**Step 3 — Condition for a non-zero step.**

For the rotor to *move* when phase A is replaced by phase B, $\Delta$ must *not* coincide with a multiple of the rotor pole pitch $\tau_r = 360°/N_r$:

$$
\Delta \neq k \cdot \tau_r, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{360°}{N_s} \neq k \cdot \frac{360°}{N_r}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{N_s} \neq \frac{k}{N_r}
$$

$$
N_r \neq k \cdot N_s
$$

**Step 4 — Combined with the 2-phase switching symmetry.**

For a 2-phase machine, the "worst-case" symmetry occurs when $k = 2$ (i.e., two stator pole pitches match exactly with one rotor pole pitch), which means the rotor would have to jump a full pole pitch — impossible in practice. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;N_s - N_r \neq 0, \pm 2, \pm 4, \pm 6, \dots\;}
$$

The first *valid* small difference is $N_s - N_r = \pm 2$, leading to:

$$
\alpha = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{180°}{N_r} \quad \text{(2-phase, 2-pole difference)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Mermaid Functional Mapping)

The physical structure of a single-stack VRM is a doubly-salient (both stator and rotor have projecting poles) electromagnetic device. Mermaid cannot perfectly render a physical cross-section, so we map the **functional architecture** below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Supply Bus] --> B1[Phase-A Drive Switch]
    A --> B2[Phase-B Drive Switch]
    A --> B3[Phase-C Drive Switch]
    B1 --> P1[Phase-A Stator Winding]
    B2 --> P2[Phase-B Stator Winding]
    B3 --> P3[Phase-C Stator Winding]
    P1 --> AG1[Non-Uniform Air Gap Field 1]
    P2 --> AG2[Non-Uniform Air Gap Field 2]
    P3 --> AG3[Non-Uniform Air Gap Field 3]
    AG1 --> R1[Rotor Salient Pole Pair 1]
    AG2 --> R2[Rotor Salient Pole Pair 2]
    AG3 --> R3[Rotor Salient Pole Pair 3]
    R1 --> SH[Common Shaft Output]
    R2 --> SH
    R3 --> SH
    SH --> LD[Mechanical Load]
    CTRL[Sequential Logic Controller] --> B1
    CTRL --> B2
    CTRL --> B3
```

## 4.2 Operating Sequence State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PhaseA_On
    PhaseA_On --> PhaseB_On: Switch OFF A, ON B
    PhaseB_On --> PhaseA_On: Switch OFF B, ON A
    PhaseA_On --> HoldA: Stop pulse train
    PhaseB_On --> HoldB: Stop pulse train
    HoldA --> PhaseB_On: Resume pulses
    HoldB --> PhaseA_On: Resume pulses
    PhaseA_On --> PhaseA_On: Direction reversal via sequence change
    PhaseB_On --> PhaseB_On: Direction reversal via sequence change
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each `PhaseX_On` state represents the rotor locked at a *minimum-reluctance position* for phase X. Transitions represent **discrete electrical steps**; the rotor physically moves by $\alpha$ mechanical degrees at each transition.

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a VRM Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CTRL[Control Electronics]
        direction TB
        PU[Pulse Generator / Microcontroller] --> DRV[Sequential Driver Circuit]
        DRV --> SW1[Switch SA - Phase A]
        DRV --> SW2[Switch SB - Phase B]
        DRV --> SW3[Switch SC - Phase C]
    end
    subgraph PWR[Power Stage]
        VDC[DC Bus Supply] --> SW1
        VDC --> SW2
        VDC --> SW3
    end
    subgraph MACH[Single Stack VRM]
        SW1 --> PA[Phase A Winding on Ns Poles]
        SW2 --> PB[Phase B Winding on Ns Poles]
        SW3 --> PC[Phase C Winding on Ns Poles]
        PA --> AG[Air Gap - Variable Reluctance]
        PB --> AG
        PC --> AG
        AG --> ROTOR[Salient Pole Rotor - No Windings]
    end
    ROTOR --> SHAFT[Output Shaft]
    SHAFT --> FB[Optional Encoder Feedback to PU]
    style MACH fill:#fff4e6
    style CTRL fill:#e6f3ff
    style PWR fill:#f0e6ff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the rotor in the diagram:** It is deliberately shown as the *only* electromechanical moving part with **no electrical connection** — emphasizing the brushless, rotor-windingless architecture of the VRM.

## 4.4 Inductance vs. Rotor Position Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Position theta = 0 Fully Unaligned] -->|Rotor moves toward alignment| P1[Position theta = 45 deg Partially Aligned]
    P1 -->|Rotor moves further| P2[Position theta = 90 deg Fully Aligned]
    P2 -->|Rotor continues| P3[Position theta = 135 deg Partially Misaligned]
    P3 -->|Rotor continues| P0
    P0 -. L is minimum Lmin .-> P1
    P1 -. L is rising .-> P2
    P2 -. L is maximum Lmax .-> P3
    P3 -. L is falling .-> P0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **L(θ) profile is a periodic trapezoidal/triangular waveform** with period equal to one rotor pole pitch $\tau_r = 360°/N_r$. The torque is **positive (motoring)** on the rising edge and **negative (regenerative)** on the falling edge of $L(\theta)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**"Define a single-stack variable reluctance motor. Why is it called 'variable reluctance'?"** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

A single-stack variable reluctance motor (VRM) is a **brushless, synchronous** electric machine in which the **rotor is a salient-pole ferromagnetic body without any winding or permanent magnet**, and rotation is produced by sequentially energizing the stator phase windings.

It is called *variable reluctance* because the **air-gap reluctance (and hence the phase inductance) varies cyclically with rotor position** $\theta$:

$$
L(\theta) = L_0 + L_2 \cos(2 N_r \theta)
$$

The rotor always seeks the **minimum-reluctance position** with respect to the energized stator pole — this is what produces torque, hence the name. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Repeated Pattern]**

**"State the two essential conditions for the pole combination $(N_s, N_r)$ of a 2-phase single-stack VRM. Give one valid example."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Condition 1:** $N_s \neq N_r$ — otherwise, all rotor poles align simultaneously and stepping is impossible. **(1 mark)**

**Condition 2:** $(N_s - N_r)$ must not be a multiple of 4 (for a 2-phase, single-stack machine). This prevents simultaneous multiple-pole alignment when one phase is switched off and the next is energized. **(1 mark)**

**Valid example:** $N_s = 8,\ N_r = 6$ (i.e., $N_s - N_r = 2$, which is *not* a multiple of 4). Step angle $\alpha = 180°/6 = 30°$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). The student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a single-stack variable reluctance motor with a neat functional diagram. State its two main merits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-phase single-stack VRM has **8 stator poles and 6 rotor poles**. Calculate:
  (i) Step angle
  (ii) Number of steps per revolution
  (iii) Shaft speed if the pulse rate is **200 pulses/second**
  (iv) Holding torque direction **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with $N_s$ salient poles; concentrated copper windings on these poles; wound in two phases A and B.
- **Rotor:** Salient-pole soft-iron lamination stack, **unwound, unmagnetized**, mounted on the shaft.
- **Air gap:** Non-uniform; *minimum* at aligned pole overlap, *maximum* when rotor poles lie between two stator poles.
- **Shaft & bearings:** Provides the mechanical output.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- When phase A is energized, the rotor pole nearest phase A rotates to align with the energized stator pole (minimum reluctance).
- When phase A is switched OFF and phase B is energized, the rotor takes a discrete step to align with the nearest phase B stator pole.
- By sequencing the energization (A → B → A → B …), the rotor rotates in discrete steps.
- Reversing the phase sequence reverses the direction of rotation.

**Merits (1 mark):**
1. No rotor winding → no rotor copper loss → high efficiency.
2. Brushless construction → maintenance-free, long life.
3. Excellent holding (detent) torque at standstill.
4. Open-loop position control without feedback.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $m = 2$ (two-phase), $N_s = 8$, $N_r = 6$, $f_p = 200$ pulses/sec.

**(i) Step angle:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{180°}{N_r} = \frac{180°}{6} = 30° \quad \text{(mechanical)}
$$

**(3 marks for correct substitution and answer)**

**(ii) Number of steps per revolution:**

$$
N_{rev} = m \cdot N_r = 2 \times 6 = 12 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**(iii) Shaft speed:**

$$
N_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{f_p}{N_{rev}} = \frac{200 \text{ pulses/s}}{12 \text{ pulses/rev}} = 16.67 \text{ rev/s} \approx 1000 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iv) Holding torque direction:** The rotor holds at the **minimum-reluctance position** — i.e., the rotor pole is **aligned with the energized stator pole** (overlap). The torque is **zero** at perfect alignment (since $dL/d\theta = 0$ at that point) but **rises sharply** with any disturbance, providing a strong restoring (holding) torque. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for step angle of a single-stack variable reluctance motor. State the conditions for successful operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase single-stack VRM has **12 stator poles and 8 rotor poles**.
  (i) Verify the pole-combination validity.
  (ii) Calculate step angle, steps per revolution, and shaft speed at 240 pulses/sec.
  (iii) Explain why VRM torque is independent of current direction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

We use the fact that one full electrical cycle of the rotor corresponds to two rotor pole pitches. For an $m$-phase machine with $N_r$ rotor poles:

- The angular span of *one rotor pole pitch* = $360°/N_r$ (mechanical).
- In an $m$-phase machine, this pole pitch is divided into $m$ equal sub-steps, each corresponding to one phase being energized.

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}\;}
$$

For $m = 2$: $\alpha = 180°/N_r$; for $m = 3$: $\alpha = 120°/N_r$.

**Conditions for Successful Operation (2 marks):**

1. **$N_s \neq N_r$** — prevents universal simultaneous alignment.
2. **$(N_s - N_r)$ is not a multiple of 4** for a 2-phase machine (and not a multiple of $2m$ in general for $m$-phase) — prevents symmetry that would immobilize the rotor.
3. **At least one rotor pole must be misaligned** when a stator phase is energized — this provides the starting torque.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $m = 3$ (three-phase), $N_s = 12$, $N_r = 8$, $f_p = 240$ pulses/s.

**(i) Validity check:**
- $N_s \neq N_r$ ✓ ($12 \neq 8$)
- $N_s - N_r = 4$; for 3-phase, the forbidden difference is a multiple of $2m = 6$. Since $4$ is **not** a multiple of $6$, the combination is **valid**. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Calculations:**

Step angle:

$$
\alpha = \frac{120°}{N_r} = \frac{120°}{8} = 15°
$$

**(1 mark)**

Steps per revolution:

$$
N_{rev} = m \cdot N_r = 3 \times 8 = 24 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**(1 mark)**

Shaft speed:

$$
N_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{f_p}{N_{rev}} = \frac{240}{24} = 10 \text{ rev/s} = 600 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**(iii) Why torque is independent of current direction (2 marks):**

From the derived torque equation:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}
$$

The current appears as $i^2$ — which is **always positive** regardless of the *sign* of $i$. Therefore, **reversing the current does not reverse the torque**. This is why a **unipolar (DC) drive circuit** with simple switching transistors is sufficient to operate a VRM — no AC excitation or H-bridge inverter is needed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing *rotor pole count* ($N_r$) with *pole pairs*.** Always count *poles*, not pairs, in VRM step-angle formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting to validate pole combinations** before plugging into formulas. If $N_s = N_r$, the step angle formula gives a finite number, but the motor *will not run* — KTU specifically tests this with "calculate the step angle, comment on its operability" type questions.
> 3. **Mixing up synchronous speed and stepping speed.** The VRM is a stepping device — its speed is controlled by the *pulse rate*, not by the supply frequency directly.
> 4. **Using $N_s$ instead of $N_r$ in the step-angle formula.** The *rotor* pole count defines the *spatial resolution* of the step, not the stator pole count.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (steps/s vs rpm, electrical vs mechanical degrees).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Core Identity:** Single-stack VRM = doubly-salient, brushless, rotor-windingless, rotor-magnetless synchronous stepping machine. **(1 mark question fav)**
- **Torque source:** Pure reluctance torque $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ — proportional to $i^2$ (current always positive in effect). **(Favourite 2-mark question)**
- **Rotor:** Soft-iron salient poles; **no copper, no magnet, no brushes.**
- **Stator:** Concentrated windings; $N_s$ poles distributed over $m$ phases.
- **Step angle formula (THE formula):** $\alpha = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ — memorize, derive, and apply. **(3–7 mark questions)**
- **Steps per revolution:** $N_{rev} = m \cdot N_r$.
- **Pulse-rate controlled speed:** $N_{\text{shaft}} = f_p / N_{rev}$ (in rev/s).
- **Pole-Combination Validity Rules:**
  - $N_s \neq N_r$
  - $(N_s - N_r) \not\equiv 0 \pmod{2m}$ (for $m$-phase machine, the difference must not be a multiple of $2m$)
  - At least one rotor pole misaligned for starting torque.
- **Direction control:** Reverse the phase *sequence* (A→B→C becomes A→C→B).
- **Three merits of VRM:** No rotor copper loss, brushless (maintenance-free), high detent/holding torque.
- **Three demerits:** No closed-loop position correction, resonance at low speeds, requires open-loop driver electronics.
- **Common 2-phase combinations:** (8, 6), (6, 4), (10, 8), (12, 8).
- **Watch application:** $N_r = 60$, $m = 2$ → $\alpha = 3°$ → $N_{rev} = 120$ steps/rev.
- **L(θ) profile:** Trapezoidal/sinusoidal, periodic in $\theta$ with period $360°/N_r$.
- **KTU golden mantra:** *"Step angle depends on rotor poles and number of phases, NOT on stator poles."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Stepper Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Stepper Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)** is a specialized electromechanical energy conversion device that synergizes the operational principles of both the **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** and the **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** to deliver high detent torque, excellent holding torque, and finer step resolutions (typically 0.9°, 1.8°, or even 0.36° per step).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A hybrid stepper motor is a multi-toothed salient-pole machine in which the rotor carries a permanent magnet axially magnetized, sandwiched between two toothed ferromagnetic cups. Torque is produced by the simultaneous action of permanent magnet flux and the variable reluctance principle, yielding the highest torque-to-volume ratio among all stepper motor families.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Magnetized Compass Gear":**
> Imagine a small bar magnet mounted on a horizontal shaft, but instead of a smooth magnet, the magnet is "capped" on both ends with two iron cups whose edges are cut like the teeth of a fine saw. When you try to rotate the shaft, you feel **two resistances simultaneously**:
> 1. The bar magnet "wants" to align with the stator's north-south poles (PM action).
> 2. The toothed iron cups "want" to snap into the nearest position where teeth align with stator poles (VR action).
> 
> This is exactly how a hybrid stepper behaves. The two effects reinforce each other, giving the rotor strong "preferred" resting positions (detent positions) AND strong holding when energized.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Step Angles:** $\beta = 0.9^\circ, 1.8^\circ, 3.75^\circ$ (most common: $1.8^\circ$ → 200 steps/rev)
- **Rotor tooth pitch:** Typically $7.5^\circ$ to $15^\circ$ mechanical between teeth
- **Number of rotor teeth ($N_r$):** Usually 50, 100, or 200
- **Stator poles ($P_s$):** Typically 8 (4 pairs)
- **Number of phases:** Usually **2** (bipolar drive) or 4 (unipolar drive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** In KTU 2024 numericals, a hybrid stepper is most often assumed to have a step angle of $1.8^\circ$ with $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth. Always verify from the given data.

### 1.4 Why "Hybrid"?

The name **Hybrid** signifies the marriage of two distinct torque-producing mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Source | Dominant Feature |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| PM Action | Axially magnetized rotor magnet | Provides detent torque and torque at zero current |
| VR Action | Toothed rotor/stator pole pieces | Multiplies torque via flux concentration at aligned teeth |

The combination yields step resolutions that **neither pure PM nor pure VR can achieve** independently.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Architecture

The hybrid stepper motor has a highly sophisticated mechanical structure:

**Stator (Stationary Part):**
- Made of laminated silicon steel to reduce eddy-current losses.
- Carries **two phase windings** (Phase A and Phase B), distributed across **8 salient poles** (or multiples thereof).
- Each pole has **multiple teeth** (often 5 teeth per pole) to enhance flux concentration.
- The two phases are physically displaced by **1/4 of a tooth pitch** ($90^\circ$ electrical).

**Rotor (Rotating Part):**
- A permanent magnet, axially magnetized (say, North on one end, South on the other).
- Two ferromagnetic end caps ("cups") pressed onto the ends of the magnet.
- Each end cap has **$N_r$ fine teeth** (typically 50).
- The teeth on the **two end caps are displaced by half a tooth pitch** relative to each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Half-Tooth-Pitch Offset — The Heart of the Hybrid:** This offset is what enables microstepping and high resolution. When one end cap's teeth align with stator teeth, the other end cap's teeth are perfectly misaligned.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Energize Phase A only:**
- Current flows in Phase A winding only.
- Stator poles A1, A2, A3, A4 become magnetized (say, N-S-N-S).
- Rotor's N-pole cup teeth align with stator A-poles; S-pole cup teeth align with stator A'-poles.
- Rotor locks at Position 1.

**Step 2 — Energize Phase A & B (A-off, B-on):**
- Phase A is de-energized; Phase B is energized.
- The stator flux axis shifts by 90° electrical (1/4 tooth pitch).
- Rotor rotates by **one step angle** $\beta$ to maintain minimum reluctance.

**Step 3 — Energize Phase $\bar{A}$ (reverse of A):**
- Current direction in Phase A is reversed.
- Rotor advances by another step.

**Step 4 — Energize Phase $\bar{B}$:**
- Rotor advances again, completing one **electrical cycle** of 4 steps.

After every **4 electrical steps**, the motor has advanced by **one full tooth pitch** on the rotor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are routinely asked in KTU ESE questions. Memorize the relationships — do not just the final forms.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_s}$ | Step angle in degrees (mechanical) | degrees |
| 2 | $N_s = N_r \times m$ | Total steps per revolution | steps/rev |
| 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_r \cdot m}$ | Step angle in terms of rotor teeth and phases per electrical cycle | degrees |
| 4 | $N_r = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\text{Tooth pitch}}$ | Number of rotor teeth | integer |
| 5 | $f_{step} = n \cdot N_s$ | Step pulse frequency for shaft speed $n$ | pulses/s (Hz) |
| 6 | $\omega = 2\pi n = \dfrac{2\pi f_{step}}{N_s}$ | Angular velocity of rotor | rad/s |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Developed electromagnetic torque (reluctance + PM) | N·m |
| 8 | $T_{total} = T_{PM} + T_{VR}$ | Torque is sum of PM and reluctance components | N·m |
| 9 | $T_{holding} \propto N_r \cdot \phi_m \cdot i$ | Holding torque scales with rotor teeth, PM flux, current | N·m |
| 10 | $P_o = T \cdot \omega$ | Output mechanical power | W |

**Variable definitions** (kept outside table to avoid pipe characters):
- $\beta$ = step angle in degrees
- $N_s$ = number of steps per revolution
- $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth on each end cap
- $m$ = number of phases per electrical cycle (typically $m=4$ for 2-phase motor)
- $n$ = shaft speed in revolutions per second
- $f_{step}$ = stepping frequency in pulses per second
- $T$ = developed torque
- $i$ = phase current
- $L$ = phase inductance as a function of rotor angle
- $\phi_m$ = permanent magnet flux

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Hybrid stepper motors are the **workhorse of precision motion control** in:
- **CNC machines and 3D printers** (X-Y-Z axis positioning)
- **Robotics** (joint actuators, grippers)
- **Textile machinery** (yarn feed, embroidery heads)
- **Medical equipment** (infusion pumps, dialysis machines, lab automation)
- **Semiconductor wafer handling** (wafer stage positioning)
- **Disk drives and printers** (head carriage positioning)
- **Camera autofocus and zoom mechanisms**

> [!TIP]
> **Why Industry Prefers Hybrid:** A typical 1.8° hybrid stepper delivers 3–5 times the torque of a comparable PM stepper of the same frame size, and 2–3 times that of a VR stepper — making it the default choice for high-torque, fine-resolution applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Step Angle Formula

We start from the fundamental geometric relationships of a hybrid stepper.

**Step 1: Define tooth pitch.**

The angular tooth pitch $\tau$ (in degrees, mechanical) on the rotor is given by the full circle divided by the number of rotor teeth:

$$
\tau = \frac{360^\circ}{N_r}
$$

Here $N_r$ is the total number of teeth on ONE end cap of the rotor. This is the angular distance between two adjacent rotor teeth.

**Step 2: Relate step angle to tooth pitch.**

In a two-phase hybrid stepper, one full electrical cycle consists of $m = 4$ distinct switching states (A, B, $\bar{A}$, $\bar{B}$). During these 4 states, the rotor advances by **exactly one tooth pitch** $\tau$. This is because, after 4 steps, the same tooth alignment pattern is restored, requiring the rotor to move forward by one full tooth to be in the next identical magnetic configuration.

Therefore, the step angle (advance per single switching event) is:

$$
\beta = \frac{\tau}{m} = \frac{\tau}{4}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $\tau$.**

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{360^\circ}{N_r}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{90^\circ}{N_r}
$$

**Step 4: Express in standard textbook form.**

For a motor with $m$ phases per cycle and $N_r$ rotor teeth, the generalized step angle is:

$$
\boxed{\beta = \frac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r}}
$$

**Step 5: Total steps per revolution.**

The total number of discrete steps required for a complete mechanical revolution:

$$
N_s = \frac{360^\circ}{\beta} = m \cdot N_r
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Electromagnetic Torque

The instantaneous electromagnetic torque in a hybrid stepper has two components, which we derive from energy principles.

**Step 1: Co-energy approach.**

For a single-phase winding with inductance $L(\theta)$ and current $i$, the magnetic co-energy is:

$$
W'_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2 + \phi_m \, i
$$

The first term represents the variable-reluctance energy storage; the second term represents the coupling with the permanent magnet.

**Step 2: Torque from co-energy gradient.**

The electromagnetic torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular position (at constant current and flux):

$$
T = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{mag}}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i = \text{const}}
$$

**Step 3: Differentiate each term.**

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} + \phi_m \frac{di}{d\theta}
$$

Since $\phi_m$ (PM flux) is independent of rotor angle, the second term vanishes:

$$
\frac{di}{d\theta} = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 4: Fourier expansion of $L(\theta)$.**

The phase inductance of a hybrid stepper is periodic with the tooth pitch and has a strong 2nd-harmonic content due to the half-tooth-offset on the two end caps:

$$
L(\theta) = L_0 + L_2 \cos(2 N_r \theta)
$$

**Step 5: Differentiate.**

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -2 N_r \, L_2 \sin(2 N_r \theta)
$$

**Step 6: Substitute back.**

$$
T = -i^2 \, N_r \, L_2 \sin(2 N_r \theta)
$$

The negative sign indicates a restoring torque that drives the rotor toward minimum reluctance (aligned tooth) position. The magnitude is:

$$
\boxed{\vert T \vert = i^2 \, N_r \, L_2 \, \vert \sin(2 N_r \theta) \vert}
$$

**Step 7: Multi-phase torque summation.**

For a two-phase motor with currents $i_A$ and $i_B$ displaced by $90^\circ$ electrical (i.e., $i_B$ leads $i_A$ by quarter tooth pitch), the total developed torque is the sum of both phase torques. With proper phase commutation, the torque contributions add constructively, yielding a smooth resultant torque.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The $\sin(2 N_r \theta)$ dependence reveals a critical engineering fact — torque ripple frequency is $2 N_r$ times the mechanical rotation frequency. For $N_r = 50$, a single mechanical revolution produces 100 torque ripple cycles. This is why hybrid steppers have **low detent torque and high resolution**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Stepping Frequency vs. Shaft Speed

**Step 1: Define relationship.**

If the rotor must complete $n$ revolutions per second, and each revolution needs $N_s$ steps, then the stepping pulse frequency is:

$$
f_{step} = n \cdot N_s
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $N_s = m \cdot N_r$.**

$$
f_{step} = n \cdot m \cdot N_r
$$

**Step 3: Express angular velocity.**

$$
\omega = 2 \pi n = \frac{2 \pi \, f_{step}}{N_s}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 2-phase hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth on each end cap. Calculate: (a) Step angle, (b) Total steps per revolution, (c) Stepping frequency required for 600 rpm, (d) Holding torque if $L_2 = 5$ mH and phase current is 2 A at the worst-case detent position.

**Solution:**

**(a) Step angle:**

$$
\beta = \frac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360^\circ}{4 \times 50} = \frac{360^\circ}{200} = 1.8^\circ
$$

**(b) Total steps per revolution:**

$$
N_s = m \cdot N_r = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**(c) Stepping frequency for 600 rpm:**

First convert rpm to revolutions per second:

$$
n = \frac{600}{60} = 10 \text{ rev/s}
$$

Then:

$$
f_{step} = n \cdot N_s = 10 \times 200 = 2000 \text{ pulses/s} = 2 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(d) Holding torque at worst-case position:**

Worst-case detent position: $\sin(2 N_r \theta) = 1$.

$$
T_{peak} = i^2 \, N_r \, L_2 = (2)^2 \times 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 4 \times 50 \times 0.005 = 1.0 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification check:** This 1.0 N·m value is realistic for a NEMA-23 (57 mm frame) hybrid stepper — the result is dimensionally and physically sensible.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

The following diagram captures the **functional architecture** of a hybrid stepper motor, including the rotor magnet, end caps, stator phases, and the directional flux paths that produce hybrid (PM + VR) torque.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Supply via Chopper Drive] --> B[Phase A Winding on Stator Pole 1]
    A --> C[Phase A Winding on Stator Pole 2]
    A --> D[Phase B Winding on Stator Pole 3]
    A --> E[Phase B Winding on Stator Pole 4]
    B --> F[Toothed Stator Pole 1 with N Teeth]
    C --> G[Toothed Stator Pole 2 with N Teeth]
    D --> H[Toothed Stator Pole 3 with N Teeth]
    E --> I[Toothed Stator Pole 4 with N Teeth]
    F --> J[Axially Magnetized Rotor Magnet with N on Top and S on Bottom]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Upper Toothed End Cap with NR Teeth Aligned to Pole 1]
    J --> L[Lower Toothed End Cap with NR Teeth Offset by Half Tooth Pitch]
    K --> M[Variable Reluctance Torque TVR Acts at Tooth Tips]
    L --> M
    J --> N[Permanent Magnet Torque TPM Acts via Axial Flux Linkage]
    M --> O[Resultant Hybrid Torque TH equals TPM plus TVR on Rotor Shaft]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Mechanical Output to Load with Step Angle Beta]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Concept (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The figure below is a **block-level representation** of the axial cross-section, showing how the two rotor end caps are offset by half a tooth pitch, with the central permanent magnet providing axial bias flux.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stator Phase A1 Tooth Aligned] --> B[Upper Rotor End Cap Tooth Aligned with A1]
    C[Stator Phase A2 Tooth Misaligned] --> D[Lower Rotor End Cap Tooth Offset by Half Pitch from A2]
    B --> E[Axial PM Magnet in Center Provides Bias Flux]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Net Radial Flux Concentrates at Aligned Teeth]
    F --> G[Tangential Force Produces Electromagnetic Torque]
    G --> H[Rotor Advances by Step Angle Beta]
```

### 4.3 Commutation Sequence (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Step1
    Step1: Step 1 - Phase A ON
    Step1 --> Step2: Pulse 1
    Step2: Step 2 - Phase B ON
    Step2 --> Step3: Pulse 2
    Step3: Step 3 - Phase A Reverse ON
    Step3 --> Step4: Pulse 3
    Step4: Step 4 - Phase B Reverse ON
    Step4 --> Step1: Pulse 4 - One Full Electrical Cycle
    Step1 --> [*]: After NS pulses - One Full Mechanical Revolution
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Note for Students:** Always start node IDs with letters (e.g., `Step1`, `nodeA`) to avoid parser conflicts. Never use reserved words like `end`, `start`, `subgraph` as standalone node IDs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is a hybrid stepper motor called 'hybrid'? What is the role of the permanent magnet in its rotor?"**

**Model Answer:**

A hybrid stepper motor is called "hybrid" because it combines the operating principles of **two distinct stepper motor types**: the **Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor** and the **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**. 

The rotor consists of an axially magnetized permanent magnet sandwiched between two toothed ferromagnetic end caps. The PM provides an axial bias flux that links the stator windings, producing a detent torque even when the stator is unexcited. Simultaneously, the toothed rotor and stator create variable-reluctance paths. The PM flux concentrates at the minimum-reluctance positions (aligned teeth), which dramatically amplifies the holding and stepping torques. The result is a high-resolution, high-torque motor — superior to either pure PM or pure VR designs.

#### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"A hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth and is wound for 2-phase operation. Calculate the step angle and the number of steps per revolution."**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth, $m = 4$ phases per electrical cycle (2-phase winding $\Rightarrow$ 4 switching states).

**Step angle:**

$$
\beta = \frac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360^\circ}{4 \times 50} = \frac{360^\circ}{200} = 1.8^\circ
$$

**Steps per revolution:**

$$
N_s = m \cdot N_r = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ steps/revolution}
$$

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat constructional sketch, explain the working of a **hybrid stepper motor**. Describe the role of the axially magnetized permanent magnet in producing the holding and detent torques. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-phase hybrid stepper motor has 80 rotor teeth. It is excited by a bipolar drive running at a stepping frequency of 3200 pulses per second. Calculate:  
(i) Step angle in degrees  
(ii) Total steps per revolution  
(iii) Shaft speed in rpm  
(iv) Holding torque if $L_2 = 4$ mH and rated phase current is 1.5 A. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The hybrid stepper motor has the following construction:

**Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel stack with 8 (or more) salient poles, each carrying concentrated coils. The 8 poles are grouped into 2 phases (A and B), with each phase occupying 4 poles arranged in a North-South alternating pattern. Each pole is slotted with $N_s$ fine teeth to enhance flux concentration.

**Rotor:** A central cylindrical permanent magnet, axially magnetized (e.g., N on top, S on bottom), is press-fitted onto the shaft. Two ferromagnetic end caps, each with $N_r$ fine teeth on their outer circumference, are mounted on the two ends of the magnet. The teeth on the two end caps are **mechanically offset by half a tooth pitch** (i.e., $180^\circ$ electrical).

**Working principle:** When Phase A is energized, flux enters the rotor from the North end-cap teeth and exits through the South end-cap teeth (or vice versa, depending on current direction). The PM provides a steady axial bias flux, while the stator winding current controls the radial flux direction. The rotor aligns such that the rotor teeth on the North end cap are aligned with the energized North stator poles, and the teeth on the South end cap align with the South stator poles.

**Detent torque:** Even with the stator unenergized, the PM alone pulls the rotor into a preferred position (minimum reluctance for axial flux return). This is the detent torque.

**Holding torque:** When the stator is energized, the radial flux adds to the axial PM flux at the aligned teeth and subtracts at the misaligned teeth. The asymmetric flux distribution produces a strong tangential force, generating a high holding torque at the energized position.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $N_r = 80$, $f_{step} = 3200$ pulses/s, $L_2 = 4$ mH, $i = 1.5$ A, $m = 4$.

**(i) Step angle:**

$$
\beta = \frac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360^\circ}{4 \times 80} = \frac{360^\circ}{320} = 1.125^\circ
$$

**(ii) Total steps per revolution:**

$$
N_s = m \cdot N_r = 4 \times 80 = 320 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**(iii) Shaft speed:**

$$
n = \frac{f_{step}}{N_s} = \frac{3200}{320} = 10 \text{ rev/s}
$$

Converting to rpm:

$$
N_{rpm} = 10 \times 60 = 600 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(iv) Holding torque:**

$$
T_{peak} = i^2 \cdot N_r \cdot L_2 = (1.5)^2 \times 80 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
T_{peak} = 2.25 \times 80 \times 0.004 = 2.25 \times 0.32 = 0.72 \text{ N·m}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **torque equation** of a hybrid stepper motor, clearly identifying the contributions from the PM flux and the variable-reluctance mechanism. Discuss how the half-tooth-pitch offset on the rotor end caps influences the developed torque. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **hybrid stepper motor** with PM and VR stepper motors on the basis of: (i) Holding torque, (ii) Step resolution, (iii) Detent torque, (iv) Power consumption at standstill, (v) Cost. List **four typical applications**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The developed electromagnetic torque in a hybrid stepper has two principal components:

**1. Reluctance (Variable-Reluctance) Component:**

From the co-energy analysis, with a phase current $i$ and a phase inductance $L(\theta)$ that varies with rotor angle:

$$
T_{VR} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

For a hybrid stepper, the variation of inductance is dominated by the second harmonic because of the half-tooth-pitch offset on the two rotor end caps. The Fourier series gives:

$$
L(\theta) = L_0 + L_2 \cos(2 N_r \theta)
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -2 N_r L_2 \sin(2 N_r \theta)
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{VR} = -i^2 N_r L_2 \sin(2 N_r \theta)
$$

The negative sign indicates a restoring torque toward the minimum-reluctance (aligned) position.

**2. Permanent-Magnet Component:**

The axially magnetized rotor magnet produces an interaction with the radial component of the stator MMF, giving:

$$
T_{PM} = k_t \cdot \phi_m \cdot i \cdot \sin(N_r \theta)
$$

where $k_t$ is a machine constant and $\phi_m$ is the PM flux.

**3. Total Torque:**

$$
T_{total} = T_{PM} + T_{VR}
$$

**Effect of half-tooth-pitch offset:** The offset doubles the effective number of cogging cycles per revolution (from $N_r$ to $2 N_r$), increasing the spatial frequency of torque pulsation. This yields a much finer "detent" granularity, enabling the small step angles typical of hybrid motors. The offset also amplifies the $L_2$ coefficient, directly increasing the peak reluctance torque.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Feature | PM Stepper | VR Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---------|------------|------------|----------------|
| (i) Holding Torque | Moderate | Low | **Very High** |
| (ii) Step Resolution | Coarse (typically 7.5°–15°) | Fine (down to 0.9°) | **Very Fine (down to 0.36°)** |
| (iii) Detent Torque | High | Zero (no PM) | **High (but less cogging than PM)** |
| (iv) Power at Standstill | Requires continuous current | Requires continuous current | **Lower — can hold with detent alone** |
| (v) Cost | Moderate | Low | **Higher (precision manufacturing)** |

**Four Typical Applications of Hybrid Stepper Motors:**

1. **CNC milling and engraving machines** — precise X-Y-Z axis motion.
2. **3D printers** — extruder head and bed positioning.
3. **Robotic joint actuators and grippers** — precise angular positioning.
4. **Medical infusion pumps and lab automation** — controlled fluid delivery.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $m$ (phases per cycle) with the number of physical phases.** For a 2-phase motor, $m = 4$, not 2. Many students write $m = 2$ and get the step angle doubled. **Always re-read the question and identify whether $m$ means "physical phases" or "switching states per electrical cycle."**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the half-tooth-pitch offset** when explaining high resolution. The HSM is "fine" precisely BECAUSE of this offset, not just because of the high $N_r$.
>
