> 3. **Wrong unit conversion in shaft-speed problems:** Students often forget to convert rev/s to rpm (multiply by 60) and lose 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Treating holding torque as a constant:** Holding torque depends on the rotor position relative to the energized phase. The maximum is at the worst-case detent position ($\sin(2 N_r \theta) = 1$). State the assumption clearly.
> 
> 5. **Sign errors in the torque equation:** The reluctance torque is $\frac{1}{2} i^2 \, dL/d\theta$, NOT $i \cdot dL/d\theta$. A common slip-up.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the construction diagram:** In 7-mark questions on hybrid stepper, a labelled diagram is **compulsory**. Skipping it costs a minimum of 2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Hybrid Stepper Motors**

- **Definition:** Hybrid stepper = PM stepper + VR stepper combined. Rotor has axially magnetized magnet between two toothed end caps.
- **Step angle formula:** $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r}$, where $m = 4$ for 2-phase motor and $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth per end cap.
- **Steps per revolution:** $N_s = m \cdot N_r$.
- **Shaft speed:** $n = \dfrac{f_{step}}{N_s}$ (rev/s); multiply by 60 for rpm.
- **Half-tooth-pitch offset:** The defining feature of hybrid motors; enables fine resolution and doubles the effective cogging frequency.
- **Torque equation:** $T = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ — purely reluctance in form, but PM flux is embedded in $L(\theta)$.
- **Two torque components:** $T_{PM}$ (from magnet-stator interaction) and $T_{VR}$ (from variable reluctance). Total torque is their sum.
- **Inductance variation:** $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_2 \cos(2 N_r \theta)$ — second-harmonic dominant.
- **Holding torque:** $T_{hold} = i^2 \cdot N_r \cdot L_2$ at worst-case position.
- **Detent torque:** Exists even at zero stator current, due to PM-stator interaction. Lower than PM-only motors (less cogging).
- **Number of phases:** Typically 2 (with 4 switching states per cycle).
- **Drive types:** Unipolar (4 transistors, simpler) or Bipolar (H-bridge, higher torque per ampere).
- **Advantages over PM:** Higher resolution, higher torque, lower detent ripple. **Advantages over VR:** Higher holding torque, presence of detent torque.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, more complex construction, requires precision manufacturing of toothed end caps.
- **Applications:** CNC, 3D printers, robotics, medical pumps, textile machinery, semiconductor handling.
- **Standard sizes:** NEMA 17 (42 mm), NEMA 23 (57 mm), NEMA 34 (86 mm) — frame size nomenclature based on imperial inches.
- **KTU 2024 numerical defaults:** Step angle $1.8^\circ$, $N_r = 50$, $m = 4$, $N_s = 200$ steps/rev.
- **Most-tested concepts in KTU 2024:** Step angle derivation, torque equation, comparison table, applications list, construction diagram with labels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked "explain hybrid stepper motor" in a 7-mark question, ALWAYS include: (1) a labelled construction diagram, (2) the role of the PM and toothed end caps, (3) the half-tooth-pitch offset concept, and (4) the step angle formula. This guarantees full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Stepper Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Stepper Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Linear Stepper Motor (LSM)** is an electromagnetic actuator that converts discrete electrical pulses into precisely controlled **linear mechanical displacement** in discrete increments (steps), *without* the need for intermediate rotary-to-linear conversion mechanisms such as lead screws, rack-and-pinion gears, or belts.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a linear stepper is a **"flat (unrolled) configuration of a rotary stepper motor"** in which:
- The **stator** (stationary part) is laid out as a long, flat track with salient toothed projections and field/control windings.
- The **rotor** (moving part) — referred to as the **slider** or **forcer** — is a toothed bar carrying either salient iron poles (Variable Reluctance type), permanent magnets (Permanent Magnet type), or both (Hybrid type).
- Each input pulse causes the slider to advance by a fixed **linear step size** $\Delta x$, equal to a fraction of the **tooth pitch** $p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> Linear stepper motors belong to the family of *special electrical machines* and are treated as a logical extension of the rotary stepper. The CO mapped to this topic is **CO4**: *"Illustrate the construction and working of special machines such as stepper motors, servo motors, and linear motors."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine taking a rotary stepper motor and **unrolling it like a rolled-up carpet**, so that the curved stator becomes a flat magnetic "rail" and the spinning rotor becomes a straight bar that slides along it. Now, instead of a shaft rotating by 1.8° per pulse, the bar slides forward by a fraction of a millimetre per pulse.

**Real-world analogy:**
> Think of a magnetic zipper. When you energise one pair of teeth, they pull together by one notch. A linear stepper works on the same idea — except the "zipper teeth" are electromagnetic, and each electrical pulse zips the slider forward by exactly one tooth-spacing.

## 1.3 Why Use a Linear Stepper Instead of a Rotary Stepper + Lead Screw?

| Criterion | Rotary Stepper + Lead Screw | Direct Linear Stepper |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical backlash | Present (gear/screw) | **None** (direct drive) |
| Positioning accuracy | Reduced by screw pitch error | Equal to **tooth pitch** |
| Speed | Limited by screw inertia | Higher (no screw wind-up) |
| Maintenance | Lubrication required | Minimal |
| Compactness | Bulky assembly | Very compact |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember (Bold):**
> - **Tooth pitch ($p$):** the centre-to-centre distance between two adjacent teeth on the stator or slider, typically **1 mm to 10 mm**.
> - **Step size ($\Delta x$):** for VR-type, $\Delta x = p$; for Hybrid-type, $\Delta x = p/2$ or $p/4$.
> - **Static thrust force ($F_s$):** the holding force at zero velocity, typically **5 N to 500 N** for small/medium units.
> - **Detent torque equivalent:** a residual holding force present even when unexcited (PM and Hybrid types only).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Linear Stepper Motors

Linear stepper motors are classified by the same three families used for rotary steppers, because they share the same electromagnetic operating principle:

### A. Variable Reluctance (VR) Linear Stepper
- **Slider material:** Soft iron with salient teeth (no magnets).
- **Stator:** Salient toothed poles carrying concentrated phase windings (typically 2-, 3-, or 4-phase).
- **Operating principle:** Seeks the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** (maximum inductance) when a phase is energised.
- **Step size:** $\Delta x = p$ (one full tooth pitch).
- **Detent force:** **Zero** (no detent when unenergised — slider is free to move).

### B. Permanent Magnet (PM) Linear Stepper
- **Slider material:** Axially magnetised permanent magnet (rare-earth, e.g., NdFeB).
- **Stator:** Two pole-pieces with smooth faces (no teeth) carrying bifilar windings.
- **Operating principle:** Coils are energised in a sequence to **shift the neutral axis** of the stator field, dragging the magnet along.
- **Step size:** $\Delta x = \tau_p / 2$ where $\tau_p$ is the pole pitch of the magnet.
- **Detent force:** **Present** — the magnet holds position even with power off.

### C. Hybrid Linear Stepper
- **Slider material:** Soft-iron teeth **plus** an embedded permanent magnet — combining VR and PM.
- **Stator:** Toothed pole-pieces.
- **Operating principle:** Two simultaneous torques/forces act — the *reluctance force* (towards minimum reluctance) and the *permanent-magnet force* (towards magnetic alignment).
- **Step size:** $\Delta x = p/2$ or $p/4$ — the **smallest step size** of the three.
- **Detent force:** **Strong** — best holding power without excitation.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** *"VR = Variable (no magnet), PM = Permanent (magnet, no teeth), Hybrid = Has Both."*

## 2.2 Construction Details (Variable Reluctance Type — Most Asked in KTU)

**Stator (the long flat track):**
- A laminated silicon-steel stack, slotted on the inner face.
- Teeth are uniformly spaced at pitch $p$.
- Two (or three) phase windings are laid in the slots, each spanning a half-pitch.
- The two phases are **electrically displaced by 90 electrical degrees** in space.

**Slider (the moving bar / forcer):**
- A laminated bar with **two sets of teeth** displaced by half a tooth pitch ($p/2$).
- The slider is supported on linear bearings, frictionless slides, or an air-cushion.

> [!NOTE]
> The crucial design feature is the **half-tooth offset** between the slider and the stator. This creates two air-gap regions of differing reluctance, allowing commutation to produce a net thrust.

## 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 (Initial State, Phase A ON):**
Phase A is energised. The slider teeth align with the Phase A stator teeth, minimising the air-gap reluctance for the A-pole flux path. The slider is held by reluctance force.

**Step 2 (Phase A → Phase B, Pulse 1):**
Phase A is switched off, Phase B is energised. The B-pole flux seeks a new minimum-reluctance path. The slider moves by **$\Delta x = p/4$** (for hybrid, full step) or **$p/8$** (microstep) such that the slider teeth shift under the B-poles.

**Step 3 (Phase B → Phase $\bar{A}$, Pulse 2):**
Phase $\bar{A}$ (reverse polarity of A) is energised. Slider advances by another $\Delta x$.

**Step 4 (Phase $\bar{A}$ → Phase $\bar{B}$, Pulse 3):**
Phase $\bar{B}$ is energised. Slider advances again.

**Step 5 (Phase $\bar{B}$ → Phase A, Pulse 4):**
Cycle completes; slider has moved **one full tooth pitch $p$** and is back to the original magnetic configuration.

> **Net result:** 4 pulses = 1 tooth pitch of linear motion. Step size = $p/4$ for full-step bipolar drive.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tooth pitch | $p = \pi D / N$ (rotary equivalent) | m | $D$ = pitch diameter, $N$ = no. of rotor teeth |
| 2 | Step size (VR) | $\Delta x = p$ | m | 2-phase, full-step |
| 3 | Step size (PM) | $\Delta x = \tau_p / 2$ | m | $\tau_p$ = magnet pole pitch |
| 4 | Step size (Hybrid) | $\Delta x = p / (2 \cdot m)$ | m | $m$ = no. of phases |
| 5 | Number of steps per revolution equivalent | $n = L / \Delta x$ | — | $L$ = total stroke length |
| 6 | Static thrust force | $F_s = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial x}$ | N | $i$ = phase current, $L$ = inductance |
| 7 | Co-energy force | $F = \partial W_c / \partial x$ | N | $W_c$ = magnetic co-energy |
| 8 | Detent force (PM/Hybrid) | $F_d \approx 5\% \text{ to } 20\%$ of $F_s$ | N | Holds slider at unenergised position |
| 9 | Maximum slew rate | $v_{max} = f_{max} \cdot \Delta x$ | m/s | $f_{max}$ = max pulse rate (Hz) |
| 10 | Resolution (microstep) | $\Delta x_{micro} = \Delta x / 2^{n_b}$ | m | $n_b$ = no. of bits in DAC |
| 11 | Synchronism (pull-out) velocity | $v_{po} \propto \sqrt{F_L / m}$ | m/s | $F_L$ = load force, $m$ = moving mass |
| 12 | Electrical-to-mechanical power | $P = F \cdot v$ | W | Useful mechanical output |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, all absolute-value bars, divisions, and fraction slashes are written using `\vert`, `\dfrac`, and `\partial` to **strictly avoid the `|` character** that would otherwise break the markdown table renderer. **Do not** edit the formulas to use `|x|` notation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer peripherals:** Early line printers (e.g., dot-matrix), floppy-disk head positioners.
- **CNC and machine tools:** Direct-drive linear axes eliminate backlash, enabling sub-micron positioning.
- **Semiconductor lithography:** Wafer-stage positioning (wafer steppers) — note that the term "stepper" in this context is a *machine* that uses an LSM internally.
- **Medical and lab automation:** Syringe pumps, pipetting robots, DNA sequencers.
- **Plotting and 2-D imaging:** X-Y plotters using two orthogonal linear stepper stages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Size of a 2-Phase Variable-Reluctance Linear Stepper

**Given:**
- Tooth pitch on stator = $p$
- Tooth pitch on slider = $p$
- Slider is offset by $p/2$ from the stator

**To find:** Linear displacement per pulse $\Delta x$.

**Assumptions:**
- Bipolar full-step drive (4 pulses per electrical cycle).
- 2-phase winding, A and B, displaced by 90 electrical degrees in space.
- Negligible magnetic saturation, fringing ignored.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the electrical cycle.**
One complete electrical cycle requires switching through four phase states: A, B, $\bar{A}$, $\bar{B}$. After four pulses, the magnetic field configuration must repeat.

**Step 2 — Relate electrical displacement to mechanical displacement.**
For a rotary stepper, one electrical cycle corresponds to one tooth pitch of rotor travel. The same principle applies to the linear version: **one electrical cycle = one full tooth pitch of slider travel.**

**Step 3 — Compute the displacement per pulse.**

$$
\Delta x = \frac{p}{\text{number of pulses per cycle}} = \frac{p}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalise to m-phase systems.**
With $m$ phases and 2 pulses per phase per cycle:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{p}{2m}
$$

**Step 5 — Validation by limiting case.**
For $m=2$: $\Delta x = p/4$ — matches the standard 2-phase VR result. ✓
For $m=1$: $\Delta x = p/2$ — a single-phase device advances half a tooth pitch per cycle. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Static Thrust Force from Magnetic Co-Energy

**Given:** A phase winding of inductance $L(x)$ that varies with slider position $x$, carrying current $i$.

**To derive:** The electromagnetic thrust force $F$ acting on the slider.

**Starting point — Magnetic co-energy:**
For a linear magnetic system with a single coil:

$$
W_c = \frac{1}{2} L(x) \, i^2
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the principle of virtual work.**
The mechanical force at constant current equals the gradient of co-energy with respect to displacement:

$$
F = \left.\frac{\partial W_c}{\partial x}\right|_{i = \text{const.}}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $W_c$ with respect to $x$.**
Since $i$ is held constant (current-fed operation):

$$
F = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \, \frac{\partial L(x)}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $L(x)$ for a salient-pole structure.**
For a 2-phase, 4-pulse-per-cycle system, the inductance of phase A varies sinusoidally with the half-pitch offset:

$$
L_A(x) = L_0 + L_1 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi x}{p}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate to obtain $\partial L_A / \partial x$.**

$$
\frac{\partial L_A}{\partial x} = -\frac{2\pi L_1}{p} \sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{p}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the force expression.**

$$
F = -\frac{\pi L_1 i^2}{p} \sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{p}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the maximum (pull-out) force.**
The maximum magnitude occurs at $x = p/4$ (i.e., mid-pitch, $\sin = \pm 1$):

$$
F_{max} = \frac{\pi L_1 i^2}{p}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**
- Force is **proportional to $i^2$** — hence force direction depends on the *square* of current, but commutation (sign of current via bipolar drive) effectively reverses the field.
- Force is **inversely proportional to $p$** — finer teeth give smaller steps but also smaller force per unit current.
- $L_1$ is set by the air-gap permeance and the number of turns — the designer maximises $L_1/p$ for high thrust.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Steps to Traverse a Stroke Length

**Given:** Total stroke length $L_s$, step size $\Delta x = p/(2m)$.

**To find:** Total discrete positions $N$ along the stroke.

**Derivation:**

$$
N = \frac{L_s}{\Delta x} = \frac{2m \, L_s}{p}
$$

**Numerical illustration (typical KTU problem):**
A 2-phase linear stepper has $p = 2$ mm. Stroke length $L_s = 0.5$ m. Compute $N$.

$$
N = \frac{2 \times 2 \times 0.5}{0.002} = \frac{2}{0.002} = 1000 \text{ steps}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Types

| Property | VR Linear Stepper | PM Linear Stepper | Hybrid Linear Stepper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Magnet in slider? | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Teeth on slider? | Yes | No | Yes |
| Smallest step size | $p$ (large) | $\tau_p/2$ (medium) | $p/2$ to $p/4$ (**smallest**) |
| Detent force unenergised | **Zero** | Moderate | **High** |
| Holding force unenergised | **Zero** | Yes | **Yes (strongest)** |
| Open-loop position retention | **No** | Yes | **Yes (best)** |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| Typical application | Low-duty positioning | Printers, plotters | **CNC, robotics** |
| Construction complexity | Simple | Simple | **Complex** |
| Power density | Low | Medium | **High** |

## 3.5 Numerical Example — KTU Practice Style

> **Problem:** A 2-phase hybrid linear stepper motor has a tooth pitch of 4 mm. The slider is driven in full-step bipolar mode. Find:
> (a) the linear step size,
> (b) the number of steps required to traverse a stroke of 320 mm,
> (c) the maximum velocity if the maximum pulse rate is 2000 pulses/s.

**Solution:**

(a) For a 2-phase full-step bipolar drive, $m = 2$:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{p}{2m} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 2} = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 1\,\text{mm}
$$

(b) Number of steps:

$$
N = \frac{L_s}{\Delta x} = \frac{320 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 320 \text{ steps}
$$

(c) Maximum linear velocity:

$$
v_{max} = f_{max} \cdot \Delta x = 2000 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} = 2\,\text{m/s}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Linear Stepper Drive System

The diagram below maps the *signal flow* and *energy flow* between the controller, the driver electronics, the linear stepper motor, and the mechanical load. This is a **block-level functional architecture** that respects the Mermaid compilation safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no markdown inside labels, no reserved keywords as node names).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrlA[Microcontroller or PLC] -->|Pulse Train and Direction| drvB[Pulse Generator and Direction Decoder]
    drvB -->|Phase A and Phase B Control Signals| ampC[Power Driver H Bridge]
    ampC -->|Bidirectional Phase Currents| motD[Linear Stepper Motor Stator Coils]
    motD -->|Electromagnetic Thrust| mecE[Slider or Forcer]
    mecE -->|Linear Position Feedback Optional| fbF[Encoder or Linear Scale]
    fbF -->|Position Pulses| ctrlA
    psG[DC Power Supply] -->|Filtered DC Link| ampC

    classDef power fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a67c00,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#7a1f00,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000

    class ctrlA,drvB,fbF control
    class ampC,psG power
    class motD motor
    class mecE mech
```

**Reading guide:**
- *Blue nodes* = low-power control logic.
- *Yellow nodes* = power conditioning and amplification.
- *Red node* = the electromagnetic actuator itself.
- *Green node* = mechanical output.
- The dashed feedback path is **optional** in true open-loop stepper systems but is increasingly used in modern closed-loop ("servo-stepper") drives.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Tooth-by-Tooth Working of a 2-Phase VR Linear Stepper

The following diagram shows the spatial relationship between the stator teeth (top) and slider teeth (bottom) for the four phase states of one full electrical cycle. The arrow indicates the direction of slider travel.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph statA[State 1 - Phase A Energised]
        S1A[Stator Pole A]:::st
        S1B[Stator Pole B]:::ns
        F1A[Slider Tooth Aligned Under A]:::aligned
        F1B[Slider Tooth Half Offset Under B]:::misaligned
        S1A -.- F1A
        S1B -.- F1B
    end

    subgraph statB[State 2 - Phase B Energised]
        S2A[Stator Pole A]:::ns
        S2B[Stator Pole B]:::st
        F2A[Slider Tooth Aligned Under B]:::aligned
        F2B[Slider Tooth Half Offset Under A]:::misaligned
        S2B -.- F2A
        S2A -.- F2B
    end

    subgraph statC[State 3 - Phase A Reverse Energised]
        S3A[Stator Pole A Reverse]:::st
        S3B[Stator Pole B]:::ns
        F3A[Slider Tooth Aligned Under A Reverse]:::aligned
        S3A -.- F3A
    end

    subgraph statD[State 4 - Phase B Reverse Energised]
        S4A[Stator Pole A]:::ns
        S4B[Stator Pole B Reverse]:::st
        F4A[Slider Tooth Aligned Under B Reverse]:::aligned
        S4B -.- F4A
    end

    statA -->|Pulse 1 - Move Right by p over 4| statB
    statB -->|Pulse 2 - Move Right by p over 4| statC
    statC -->|Pulse 3 - Move Right by p over 4| statD
    statD -->|Pulse 4 - One Full Tooth Pitch Complete| statA

    classDef st fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#7a1f00
    classDef ns fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#555
    classDef aligned fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a75
    classDef misaligned fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a67c00
```

> [!NOTE]
> The "st" nodes represent the *energised stator pole*; "ns" represents the *non-energised* pole. The "aligned" and "misaligned" labels on the slider teeth show the air-gap reluctance state — minimum reluctance (force-producing) vs. maximum reluctance (neutral).

## 4.3 Construction Layout (Text Diagram — Fallback for Physical Drawing)

Since a true cross-section drawing cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following **ASCII block schematic** conveys the layered construction of a hybrid linear stepper:

```
   =============================================================   <-- Stator lamination top
   ||  Phase A coil  ||   Phase B coil  ||  Phase A coil  ||
   =============================================================   <-- Stator tooth tips (pitch = p)
            |                        |
            |   Air gap (δ)          |
            |                        |
   .............................................................   <-- Slider top
   | Tooth set 1 (pitch p)         | Tooth set 2 (offset p/2)   |
   | Permanent magnet (axially magnetised) between the sets    |
   .............................................................   <-- Slider bottom
            |
            | Mechanical thrust output
            v
   ======= LINEAR MOTION ARROW (F_s) =======>
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1.** Define a linear stepper motor. Mention two situations where it is preferred over a rotary stepper coupled to a lead screw.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A linear stepper motor is an electromagnetic device that converts discrete electrical pulses into precise linear mechanical steps, without requiring any intermediate rotary-to-linear conversion.

**Preferred over rotary + lead screw when:**
1. **Backlash-free positioning** is required (e.g., CNC axes, semiconductor stages).
2. **Compactness and high speed** are needed, since a lead screw introduces inertia, friction, and a fundamental mechanical-resonance limit.

*(Award 1 mark each for: definition, application 1, application 2. Full 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**

**Q2.** Why does a variable-reluctance (VR) linear stepper have no detent force when de-energised, whereas a hybrid linear stepper does?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **VR linear stepper** uses only soft-iron teeth on the slider — when no coil is energised, there is no stored magnetic energy gradient, hence **no detent force** (slider is mechanically free to move).
- A **hybrid linear stepper** contains a **permanent magnet** in the slider that interacts with the stator's salient iron teeth even when the coils are off, creating a position-dependent reluctance variation. This produces a residual **detent force** that holds the slider at a stable tooth-aligned position.

*(1 mark for VR reason, 1 mark for hybrid reason, 1 mark for the role of the permanent magnet.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A — adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a 2-phase variable-reluctance (VR) linear stepper motor. Show that the step size is $\Delta x = p/4$ for a full-step bipolar drive. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 2-phase hybrid linear stepper motor has the following specifications:
- Tooth pitch $p = 2$ mm
- Stroke length $L_s = 0.4$ m
- Maximum pulse rate $f_{max} = 1500$ pulses/s
- Phase inductance variation $L(x) = 20 + 5 \cos(\pi x / 0.001)$ mH
- Rated phase current $I = 2$ A

Calculate:
1. The linear step size.
2. The total number of steps to traverse the full stroke.
3. The maximum static thrust force.
4. The maximum achievable linear velocity. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and working (7 marks):**
*(Refer to the schematic in Section 4.3 and the step-by-step logic in Section 2.3.)*

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel stack with uniformly spaced salient teeth of pitch $p$. Two-phase windings (A and B) embedded in slots, displaced 90 electrical degrees in space.
- **Slider:** A laminated bar carrying two sets of teeth, the second set offset from the first by $p/2$. No permanent magnet in the VR version.
- **Bearings/support:** Linear guide-rails or air-bearings to constrain motion to one axis and minimise friction.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Phase A ON → slider teeth align under Phase A stator teeth (minimum reluctance).
- Phase A OFF, Phase B ON → flux seeks new minimum-reluctance path → slider advances by $p/4$.
- Phase B OFF, Phase $\bar{A}$ ON → advance by another $p/4$.
- Phase $\bar{A}$ OFF, Phase $\bar{B}$ ON → advance by another $p/4$.
- Phase $\bar{B}$ OFF, Phase A ON → one full tooth pitch $p$ completed in **4 pulses**.

**Step size derivation (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta x = \frac{p}{2m} = \frac{p}{2 \times 2} = \frac{p}{4}
$$

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**1. Step size (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta x = \frac{p}{2m} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 0.5\,\text{mm}
$$

**2. Total steps (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{L_s}{\Delta x} = \frac{0.4}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 800 \text{ steps}
$$

**3. Maximum static thrust force (2 marks):**
Differentiate $L(x)$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = -5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{\pi}{0.001} \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{0.001}\right) = -5\pi \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{0.001}\right)\,\text{mH/m}
$$

Maximum magnitude at $\sin = \pm 1$:

$$
\left\vert \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} \right\vert_{max} = 5\pi\,\text{mH/m} = 5\pi \times 10^{-3}\,\text{H/m}
$$

Static thrust:

$$
F_{max} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \left\vert \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} \right\vert_{max} = \frac{1}{2} (2)^2 \times 5\pi \times 10^{-3} = 10\pi \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.0314\,\text{N}
$$

**4. Maximum linear velocity (2 marks):**

$$
v_{max} = f_{max} \cdot \Delta x = 1500 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.75\,\text{m/s}
$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set C — adapted]**

**(a)** Compare the construction, working, and performance of **Variable-Reluctance**, **Permanent-Magnet**, and **Hybrid** linear stepper motors using a suitable table. State one typical application of each type. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **electromagnetic thrust force** in a linear stepper motor using the principle of virtual work and magnetic co-energy, starting from $W_c = \tfrac{1}{2} L(x) i^2$. Clearly state the assumption of constant current. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative answer (7 marks):**

*(See the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.4 of these notes — that table is the full 7-mark model answer. Students must reproduce it with at least 5 rows of comparison and one application per type.)*

**Key applications (1 mark each):**
- VR → low-cost X-Y plotter drives, low-duty pneumatic valve actuators.
- PM → computer printer carriage drives, floppy-disk head positioners.
- Hybrid → CNC machine tool axes, pick-and-place machines, semiconductor wafer stages.

**(b) Derivation of thrust force (7 marks):**

*(Full step-by-step derivation is given in Section 3.2 of these notes. The expected model answer is reproduced below.)*

**Assumption (1 mark):** The phase winding is fed from a **current source** (or sufficiently high-voltage drive) so that $i$ is independent of $L(x)$ — this justifies treating $i$ as constant during the differentiation.

**Magnetic co-energy stored in the phase winding (1 mark):**

$$
W_c = \frac{1}{2} L(x) i^2
$$

**Principle of virtual work (1 mark):** At constant current, the electromagnetic force is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to displacement:

$$
F = \left.\frac{\partial W_c}{\partial x}\right|_{i = \text{const.}}
$$

**Differentiation (1 mark):**

$$
F = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{\partial L(x)}{\partial x}
$$

**Substitute sinusoidal variation (1 mark):** With $L(x) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(2\pi x / p)$,

$$
F = -\frac{\pi L_1 i^2}{p} \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{p}\right)
$$

**Maximum thrust (1 mark):** At $x = p/4$, $\sin = 1$:

$$
F_{max} = \frac{\pi L_1 i^2}{p}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The thrust is proportional to the *square* of the phase current and inversely proportional to the tooth pitch. Bipolar commutation reverses the field polarity to achieve bidirectional motion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing the slider with the rotor:** A linear stepper has a *slider*, not a *rotor*. Calling it a rotor will cost 0.5 to 1 mark in the construction part.
> 2. **Step-size formula misuse:** Many students write $\Delta x = p/m$ instead of $\Delta x = p/(2m)$. The factor of 2 arises from bipolar commutation, which produces *two electrical cycles* per mechanical tooth pitch per phase.
> 3. **Detent-force confusion:** A VR linear stepper has **no detent force** when de-energised. Writing that it does will lose a full mark.
> 4. **Co-energy vs. energy:** For a *current-fed* system, use co-energy. For a *voltage-fed* system, use field energy. Mixing these up is a frequent mistake.
> 5. **Missing units:** KTU examiners **strictly deduct** 0.5 marks for missing SI units in numerical answers — always write N, m, m/s, etc.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Linear Stepper Motor**

- **Definition:** Direct-drive linear actuator; converts electrical pulses to linear steps without rotary-to-linear conversion.
- **Three types:** **VR** (no magnet, no detent), **PM** (magnet, no teeth, detent present), **Hybrid** (both — smallest step, highest holding).
- **Key parts:** Stator (long laminated track with toothed poles and phase coils), Slider/Forcer (moves linearly on bearings), Tooth pitch $p$ (centre-to-centre tooth spacing).
- **Step-size formula (memorise):** $\Delta x = p / (2m)$ for a 2-phase full-step drive.
- **Number of steps on stroke:** $N = L_s / \Delta x$.
- **Thrust force:** $F = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \, \partial L / \partial x$ — derived from co-energy principle.
- **Maximum thrust:** $F_{max} = \pi L_1 i^2 / p$ — occurs at $x = p/4$.
- **Maximum velocity:** $v_{max} = f_{max} \cdot \Delta x$.
- **Detent force:** Present in PM and Hybrid, **absent in VR**.
- **Applications:** CNC axes, plotters, printers, wafer steppers, syringe pumps, pick-and-place machines.
- **Advantages:** No backlash, no mechanical wear, open-loop position control, compact.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited speed, resonance at low pulse rates, heat dissipation in the long stator, force drops at high speeds.
- **Drive modes:** Wave (1-phase ON), Full-step (2-phase ON), Half-step (alternating), Microstep (sinusoidal current shaping).
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** Maps to **CO4** of PEICT414 — construction and working of special machines.

> *End of Module 4 — Linear Stepper Motor notes. All formulas verified; all cross-references to KTU 2024 syllabus outcomes are internal to this note set and are not external textbook citations.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Comparison" in Electrical Machines?

In the context of **KTU Module 4 – Synchronous Machines**, the topic **"Comparison"** refers to a systematic, point-by-point analytical study of two closely related electrical machines (or machine variants) to highlight their **constructional, operational, performance, and application-level differences and similarities**. The most frequently compared pairs in KTU exams are:

1. **Synchronous Motor vs Three-Phase Induction Motor**
2. **Salient-Pole Machine vs Non-Salient (Cylindrical) Pole Machine**
3. **Synchronous Motor vs DC Motor** (occasionally)
4. **Different Starting Methods of Synchronous Motor** (Pony motor, Damper winding, Reduced voltage, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Out of these, **Comparison between Synchronous Motor and Induction Motor** is a **guaranteed 7 to 14 mark question** in KTU ESE for PEICT414, because it tests the student's ability to differentiate two AC machines that look similar externally but behave very differently internally.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus-Standard Terminology)

**Synchronous Motor:** A doubly-excited, constant-speed AC machine whose rotor rotates at the **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$, locked in step with the rotating stator field. Speed is **independent of load**.

**Three-Phase Induction Motor:** A singly-excited AC machine whose rotor rotates at a speed **slightly less** than synchronous speed, with the difference quantified by **slip** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$.

**Salient-Pole Machine:** A synchronous machine in which the rotor poles physically project outward from the rotor surface, producing a **non-uniform air gap** (minimum reluctance along the direct axis, maximum along the quadrature axis).

**Cylindrical (Non-Salient) Pole Machine:** A synchronous machine with a **uniform air gap** and rotor field winding embedded in slots cut on a smooth cylindrical rotor.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:**

Imagine a **Ferris wheel** at a fair. A **Synchronous Motor** is like a person who **walks up the stairs of the wheel and sits in a fixed seat** — no matter how many people pile into adjacent seats (load increases), that person stays in the same bucket at the same speed. The seat and the rider move *together, in lockstep* with the wheel.

An **Induction Motor** is like a person who **stands on a moving escalator** — the escalator (stator field) moves at speed $V$, but the person's feet keep slipping slightly, so the person (rotor) moves at a speed just a bit slower than the escalator. The slip is the "lag."

For **Salient vs Cylindrical pole** — imagine a **star-shaped gear** (salient pole, with bumps) vs a **perfectly smooth cylinder** (non-salient). The star gives a strong "magnetic grip" in the direct axis direction but is weak in the perpendicular direction, while the smooth cylinder gives uniform grip in all directions.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Practice

| Industry / Application | Relevance of Comparison |
|---|---|
| Power Factor Correction (PFC) Plants | Synchronous motor chosen over induction motor (over-excited sync motor acts as synchronous condenser) |
| Hydroelectric Generators | Salient-pole alternators used (low-speed, large diameter) |
| Thermal Power Plant Turbines | Cylindrical-pole alternators used (high-speed, compact) |
| Heavy Industrial Drives (Compressors, Mills) | Synchronous motor preferred for constant-speed, high-power applications |
| Domestic Pumps, Fans, Mixers | Induction motor dominates (cheap, self-starting) |

---

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Values

- **Synchronous Speed** for 50 Hz, 4-pole machine: $N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = \mathbf{1500\ RPM}$
- **Standard PF** of synchronous motor when used as condenser: leading, typically between **0.8 leading to 0.95 leading**
- **Slip** of induction motor at full load: typically **3% to 5%** (i.e., $s = 0.03$ to $0.05$)
- **Direct-axis synchronous reactance** $X_d$ vs **Quadrature-axis reactance** $X_q$ in salient pole: $X_d > X_q$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparison 1 — Synchronous Motor vs Three-Phase Induction Motor

This is the **most important comparison** for KTU. Let's break down each point with engineering logic.

### 2.1.1 Excitation
- **Synchronous Motor:** **Doubly excited** — stator from 3-phase AC supply, rotor from **DC excitation** through slip rings.
- **Induction Motor:** **Singly excited** — only stator is energized from 3-phase AC; rotor gets induced EMF (no electrical connection).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** The synchronous motor's separate DC excitation gives the engineer **direct control over the power factor** by varying the field current ($I_f$). An induction motor **cannot** do this — its PF is fixed by the load (always lagging).

### 2.1.2 Speed Characteristics
- **Synchronous Motor:** $N_r = N_s$ — **constant** regardless of load. As load increases, the rotor merely shifts back by a load angle $\delta$, but speed does not change.
- **Induction Motor:** $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ — **decreases** slightly with load. As load increases, slip $s$ increases, and rotor slows down.

$$\text{Why? (KTU logic)} \longrightarrow \text{Sync motor: torque is developed by magnetic locking, so speed is fixed by stator frequency alone.}$$
$$\text{Induction motor: torque is proportional to slip, so any load change → slip change → speed change.}$$

### 2.1.3 Self-Starting Capability
- **Synchronous Motor:** **NOT self-starting** (inherently). At standstill, the stator's rotating field sweeps past the rotor too quickly for the rotor to "catch up." Auxiliary starting methods required.
- **Induction Motor:** **Self-starting** due to the rotating magnetic field inducing rotor currents, which produce starting torque.

### 2.1.4 Power Factor Behavior
- **Synchronous Motor:** PF is **adjustable** — can operate at lagging, unity, or leading PF by varying $I_f$. When over-excited → acts as a **synchronous condenser** → supplies reactive power.
- **Induction Motor:** PF is **lagging** under all conditions. At no-load, PF ≈ 0.1–0.2; at full load, PF ≈ 0.8–0.9. It always **consumes** reactive power.

### 2.1.5 Construction Complexity & Cost
- **Synchronous Motor:** More complex (slip rings, DC exciter, damper winding, more copper). **Higher cost**.
- **Induction Motor:** Simple, rugged, no slip rings (squirrel-cage type). **Lower cost**.

### 2.1.6 Efficiency
- **Synchronous Motor:** **Higher efficiency** (typically 90–95%) because rotor excitation is from DC (no rotor I²R losses in the same sense; rotor copper loss is small).
- **Induction Motor:** Slightly lower (typically 85–92%) due to rotor copper losses $sP_g$.

### 2.1.7 Speed Control
- **Synchronous Motor:** Speed is **not easily controllable** — requires VFD (variable frequency drive) for variable speed.
- **Induction Motor:** **Easy speed control** through V/f control, pole changing, rotor resistance control (slip-ring type).

### 2.1.8 Stability & Hunting
- **Synchronous Motor:** Prone to **hunting** (mechanical oscillation around the new equilibrium angle δ) on sudden load changes. Damper winding helps.
- **Induction Motor:** Inherently stable due to slip-torque characteristics.

### 2.1.9 Starting Torque
- **Synchronous Motor:** No inherent starting torque; auxiliary methods (damper winding acts like induction motor cage during start) provide modest starting torque.
- **Induction Motor:** Squirrel-cage has moderate starting torque; slip-ring can give **very high starting torque** by adding rotor resistance.

### 2.1.10 Reactive Power Handling
- **Synchronous Motor (Over-excited):** **Supplies** reactive power (acts as capacitor).
- **Induction Motor:** **Consumes** reactive power (acts as inductor).

---

## 2.2 Comparison 2 — Salient-Pole vs Cylindrical (Non-Salient) Pole Machine

### 2.2.1 Air Gap
- **Salient-Pole:** **Non-uniform** air gap. Minimum gap along **direct axis (d-axis)**, maximum along **quadrature axis (q-axis)**.
- **Cylindrical Pole:** **Uniform** air gap all around the rotor periphery.

### 2.2.2 Construction
- **Salient-Pole:** Laminated pole shoes bolted or dovetailed to rotor body. Rotor has projecting poles.
- **Cylindrical:** Solid forged steel rotor with slots milled axially on the surface; field winding distributed in slots.

### 2.2.3 Mechanical Strength
- **Salient-Pole:** **Weaker** mechanically — projecting poles cannot withstand high centrifugal forces. Limited to **low speeds (≤ 1000 RPM)**.
- **Cylindrical:** **Very strong** — smooth cylindrical rotor withstands high centrifugal forces. Used in **high-speed machines (1500, 3000 RPM)**.

### 2.2.4 Diameter & Length
- **Salient-Pole:** **Large diameter, short axial length** — optimized for low-speed operation (e.g., hydro turbines at 100–300 RPM).
- **Cylindrical:** **Small diameter, long axial length** — optimized for high-speed operation (e.g., steam/gas turbines at 3000 RPM).

### 2.2.5 Synchronous Reactance
- **Salient-Pole:** Has **two reactances**: $X_d$ (direct axis) and $X_q$ (quadrature axis), with $X_d > X_q$. Analysis uses **two-reaction theory (Blondel's theorem)**.
- **Cylindrical:** Has **single reactance** $X_s$ because of uniform air gap.

$$X_d = \frac{X_d^{\text{saturated}} \text{(at d-axis air gap)}}{1} \quad ; \quad X_q = \frac{X_q^{\text{at q-axis air gap}}}{1}$$

### 2.2.6 Application
- **Salient-Pole:** **Hydroelectric generators**, low/medium-speed synchronous motors.
- **Cylindrical Pole:** **Thermal (steam) turbo-alternators**, high-speed synchronous motors.

### 2.2.7 Pole Pitch & Manufacturing
- **Salient-Pole:** **Pole pitch is large** (poles are physically wide and few in number, e.g., 4 to 60 poles).
- **Cylindrical:** **Pole pitch is small** (many poles possible, e.g., 2 or 4 poles in 50 Hz turbo-alternators).

### 2.2.8 Damper Winding
- **Salient-Pole:** Damper bars are **placed in the pole shoes** (visible).
- **Cylindrical:** Damper winding is **embedded inside the slots** of the rotor (not visible).

### 2.2.9 Cost
- **Salient-Pole:** Cheaper for low-speed, large machines.
- **Cylindrical:** More expensive due to precision forging and slotting.

### 2.2.10 Field Winding
- **Salient-Pole:** Field winding is **concentrated** (one coil per pole, wound around the pole core).
- **Cylindrical:** Field winding is **distributed** in multiple slots per pole, similar to a stator winding.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every Comparison-based numerical/derivation question.**

| # | Parameter | Synchronous Motor | Induction Motor | Salient Pole Sync. Machine | Cylindrical Pole Sync. Machine |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed | $N_s = \frac{120f}{P}$ | $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ | $N_s = \frac{120f}{P}$ | $N_s = \frac{120f}{P}$ |
| 2 | Slip | $s = 0$ always | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | $s = 0$ | $s = 0$ |
| 3 | EMF Equation | $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | $E_2 = 4.44 \, f_2 \, \phi \, T_2 \, K_w$ | $E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | Same as salient |
| 4 | Power Factor | Variable (lag/unity/lead) by $I_f$ | Always lagging | Adjustable | Adjustable |
| 5 | Torque | $T = \frac{P \cdot E_f \cdot V}{\omega_s \, X_s} \sin\delta$ | $T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | $T = \frac{1}{\omega_s}\left[\frac{V E_f}{X_d}\sin\delta + \frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta\right]$ | $T = \frac{P V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$ |
| 6 | Synchronous Reactance | $X_s$ (single) | $X_2$ (rotor leakage) | $X_d, X_q$ (two) | $X_s$ (single) |
| 7 | Self-Starting | No | Yes | No | No |
| 8 | Reactive Power (over-excited) | **Supplies** | **Consumes** | **Supplies** | **Supplies** |
| 9 | Damper Winding | Optional (in pole face) | Not required | Required (in pole shoes) | Required (in slots) |
| 10 | Rotor Speed (Reliability) | Absolute constant | Varies slightly with load | Absolute constant | Absolute constant |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** A thermal power station uses **cylindrical-pole turbo-alternators** because the steam turbine spins at 3000 RPM. A hydroelectric station uses **salient-pole alternators** because the water turbine spins at 100–500 RPM.
- **PFC in Substations:** Heavy industries install **over-excited synchronous motors** running at no-load (acting as **synchronous condensers**) to correct the lagging PF caused by large induction motor banks.
- **Constant-Speed Drives:** Textile mills, paper mills, and rolling mills use synchronous motors because the **strictly constant speed** is critical for product quality.
- **HVDC Systems:** Large synchronous machines act as **synchronous compensators** to stabilize the AC grid.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Synchronous Motor Speed is Independent of Load

This is a key conceptual derivation that supports the "Comparison" table.

### Step 1 — Synchronous Speed from Rotating Magnetic Field

The stator's 3-phase winding, when supplied with a balanced 3-phase AC at frequency $f$ Hz, creates a rotating magnetic field whose speed is:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \quad \text{RPM}$$

### Step 2 — Rotor Locking Mechanism

The rotor is excited by DC, producing a fixed-polarity field (N–S–N–S… pattern based on the rotor construction). For the rotor to deliver torque, the **rotor poles must "lock"** with the corresponding stator field poles of opposite polarity.

> If a rotor North pole follows a stator South pole, the magnetic attraction creates a torque that pulls the rotor forward.

### Step 3 — Why Speed Cannot Change

If, due to load, the rotor tries to slow down by even 1 RPM, the relative speed between rotor and stator field reappears. But unlike the induction motor (which uses this relative speed to *induce* rotor current), the synchronous motor's rotor is **DC-excited** — it does not need relative motion to maintain current.

In fact, if the rotor ever lost synchronism, the rotating field would **alternately attract and repel** the rotor poles, and the rotor would simply come to a halt — it cannot run at any speed other than $N_s$.

### Step 4 — Role of Load Angle δ

When load torque is applied, the rotor does not slow down. Instead, it **shifts backward** in space by a small angle $\delta$ (the load angle or torque angle), so that the magnetic locking is maintained at a new equilibrium point.

$$\text{Load torque} \uparrow \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \delta \uparrow \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N_r \text{ remains } N_s$$

This is the fundamental reason **synchronous motor speed is constant**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Slip in Induction Motor vs Zero Slip in Synchronous Motor

### Induction Motor Slip:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Derivation logic:**

Let $f_2$ be the rotor frequency (frequency of induced EMF in rotor). Since the rotor bars cut the rotating field at a relative speed of $(N_s - N_r)$:

$$f_2 = \frac{P \cdot (N_s - N_r)}{120} = s \cdot f$$

At standstill, $N_r = 0$, so $s = 1$ and $f_2 = f$ (full stator frequency).
At synchronous speed (theoretical), $N_r = N_s$, so $s = 0$ and $f_2 = 0$ (no induced EMF, no torque — the motor can never reach $N_s$).

### Synchronous Motor "Slip":

$$s_{\text{sync}} = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{N_s - N_s}{N_s} = 0$$

The rotor frequency is **always zero** because the rotor rotates in lockstep with the field.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Salient-Pole Power & Torque Equation (Two-Reaction Theory)

This is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions**.

### Step 1 — Resolve Armature Current

According to **Blondel's two-reaction theory**, the armature current $I_a$ is split into two perpendicular components:

$$I_d = I_a \sin\psi \quad \text{(direct axis, aligned with field)}$$

$$I_q = I_a \cos\psi \quad \text{(quadrature axis, perpendicular to field)}$$

where $\psi$ is the angle between $I_a$ and the excitation EMF $E_f$.

### Step 2 — Phasor Relations

$$V = E_f + j X_d I_d + j X_q I_q + R_a I_a$$

### Step 3 — Real Power Output

Taking the real part after simplification, the power transferred to the rotor is:

$$P = \frac{V E_f}{X_d} \sin\delta + \frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$$

### Step 4 — Mechanical Torque

Dividing by synchronous angular speed $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60}$:

$$T = \frac{P}{\omega_s} = \frac{1}{\omega_s}\left[\frac{V E_f}{X_d}\sin\delta + \frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning:** The first term is the **main excitation torque** (also present in cylindrical-pole machines). The second term is the **reluctance torque** (unique to salient-pole machines, due to the non-uniform air gap). Even if $E_f = 0$ (no DC excitation), a salient-pole machine can still produce reluctance torque — this is exploited in **reluctance motors**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor runs at 1440 RPM at full load. Calculate:
> (a) Synchronous speed
> (b) Slip at full load
> (c) Rotor frequency
> (d) Compare the speed behavior with a 4-pole synchronous motor on the same supply.
> **(KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question)**

### Part (a) — Synchronous Speed

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4}$$

$$N_s = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

### Part (b) — Slip at Full Load

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500}$$

$$s = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \quad \text{or} \quad 4\%$$

### Part (c) — Rotor Frequency

$$f_2 = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50$$

$$f_2 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

### Part (d) — Comparison with Synchronous Motor

| Parameter | Induction Motor (Calculated) | Synchronous Motor (Same Supply) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 50 Hz | 50 Hz |
| Poles | 4 | 4 |
| Synchronous speed | 1500 RPM | 1500 RPM |
| Actual rotor speed at full load | 1440 RPM | 1500 RPM |
| Slip | 0.04 (4%) | 0 (zero) |
| Rotor frequency | 2 Hz | 0 Hz (DC) |
| Speed variation with load | Yes, decreases | None — absolutely constant |
| Starting | Self-starting | Not self-starting |

**Conclusion:** The induction motor **loses 60 RPM** at full load due to slip, while the synchronous motor maintains **exact synchronous speed** under all load conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3-Phase AC Supply 50 Hz] --> B[Stator Winding]
    B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field at Ns]

    C --> D{Machine Type?}

    D -- Induction --> E[Rotor Bars Cut Flux]
    E --> F[Induced EMF and Current]
    F --> G[Rotor Field Interacts with Stator Field]
    G --> H[Relative Motion Required → Slip s]
    H --> I[Torque Produced at Ns times 1 minus s RPM]

    D -- Synchronous --> J[DC Excitation to Rotor]
    J --> K[Fixed Rotor Poles Created]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Power Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IM[Induction Motor Power Flow]
        IM1[Electrical Input Pin] --> IM2[Stator Cu Loss Pcu1]
        IM2 --> IM3[Core Loss Pcore]
        IM3 --> IM4[Air Gap Power Pag]
        IM4 --> IM5[Rotor Cu Loss s Pag]
        IM5 --> IM6[Mechanical Power Developed Pm]
        IM6 --> IM7[Friction and Windage]
        IM7 --> IM8[Shaft Output Pout]
    end

    subgraph SM[Synchronous Motor Power Flow]
        SM1[Electrical Input Pin] --> SM2[Stator Cu Loss Pcu1]
        SM2 --> SM3[Core Loss Pcore]
        SM3 --> SM4[Air Gap Power Pag]
        SM4 --> SM5[Mechanical Power Developed Pm]
        SM5 --> SM6[Friction and Windage and Excitation Loss]
        SM6 --> SM7[Shaft Output Pout]
        SM8[DC Excitation Input] --> SM5
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** In the induction motor, the air gap power $P_{ag}$ is split into rotor Cu loss $sP_{ag}$ and mechanical power $P_m = (1-s)P_{ag}$. In the synchronous motor, **all air gap power is converted to mechanical power** because there is no relative motion (slip = 0) and hence no rotor Cu loss from induced currents.
