# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State any three differences between a salient-pole synchronous machine and a cylindrical-pole synchronous machine.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Air Gap:** Salient-pole machines have a **non-uniform air gap** (minimum along d-axis, maximum along q-axis), while cylindrical-pole machines have a **uniform air gap** all around the rotor periphery.

2. **Mechanical Construction:** Salient-pole rotors have **projecting pole shoes** bolted to the rotor body, suitable for low-speed operation. Cylindrical-pole rotors are made of a **solid forged steel cylinder with milled slots** for field winding, suitable for high-speed operation.

3. **Synchronous Reactance:** Salient-pole machines have **two reactances** $X_d$ and $X_q$ (analyzed by two-reaction theory), while cylindrical-pole machines have a **single reactance** $X_s$ since the air gap is uniform.

4. **Application:** Salient-pole is used in **hydroelectric generators** (low speed, large diameter), while cylindrical-pole is used in **thermal turbo-alternators** (high speed, small diameter).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is a synchronous motor not self-starting, while an induction motor is self-starting?**

**Model Answer:**

A **synchronous motor is not self-starting** because its rotor is DC-excited and has **fixed magnetic poles** (N and S). When the 3-phase stator winding is energized, the rotating magnetic field sweeps past the rotor at synchronous speed. Due to the rotor's inertia, it cannot accelerate and lock onto the rotating field in one half-cycle. The rotor simply experiences alternating forward and backward forces, with a net average torque of **zero** at standstill.

An **induction motor is self-starting** because the rotating stator field **induces currents** in the short-circuited rotor bars. By Lenz's law, these induced currents produce a rotor magnetic field that opposes the relative motion. The interaction between the stator rotating field and the rotor-induced field produces a **net starting torque** in the direction of rotation, allowing the rotor to accelerate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** write only a one-word table. KTU valuation requires **at least 2–3 lines of explanation per point**.
> - Always include a **conclusion line** at the end ("Hence, the synchronous motor is preferred for…").
> - For 14-mark questions, you must provide **at least 8–10 distinct points of comparison**, not just 4–5.
> - **Loss of 2 marks:** Forgetting to mention the role of damper winding in synchronous motors.
> - **Loss of 2 marks:** Writing "synchronous motor is self-starting" — this is a common and fatal error.

---

### 📌 Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**(a)** Compare the construction, working, and performance of a **3-phase synchronous motor** and a **3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor**. Discuss at least 8 points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor runs at 960 RPM at full load. Calculate:
- (i) Synchronous speed
- (ii) Slip at full load
- (iii) Rotor frequency
- (iv) Percentage speed reduction from no-load to full load (assume no-load slip is 0.5%)

Compare this speed behavior with a **6-pole synchronous motor on the same supply**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Comparison between 3-Phase Synchronous Motor and 3-Phase Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor:**

**Point 1: Type of Excitation**
- *Synchronous Motor:* **Doubly excited** — the stator is supplied with 3-phase AC, and the rotor is supplied with **DC excitation** through slip rings and brushes.
- *Induction Motor:* **Singly excited** — only the stator is connected to the 3-phase AC supply. The rotor receives its excitation **by electromagnetic induction**; there is no electrical connection to the rotor.

**Point 2: Self-Starting Capability**
- *Synchronous Motor:* **Not self-starting.** At standstill, the average torque is zero because the rotor cannot follow the rapidly rotating stator field. Auxiliary starting methods (damper winding, pony motor, VFD) are required.
- *Induction Motor:* **Self-starting.** The rotating field induces currents in the shorted rotor bars, producing an initial starting torque. The motor accelerates on its own.

**Point 3: Speed Characteristics**
- *Synchronous Motor:* Runs at **exact synchronous speed** $N_s = 120f/P$, which is **constant** and **independent of load**.
- *Induction Motor:* Runs at a speed **slightly less** than synchronous, $N_r = N_s(1-s)$. Speed **decreases with increasing load**.

**Point 4: Power Factor**
- *Synchronous Motor:* **Variable power factor** — can be made lagging, unity, or leading by adjusting the DC field current $I_f$. An over-excited synchronous motor operates at a **leading power factor** and can **supply** reactive power.
- *Induction Motor:* Always operates at a **lagging power factor** (inductive load). It always **consumes** reactive power from the supply. PF varies with load — typically 0.2 at no-load, 0.85 at full load.

**Point 5: Constructional Complexity**
- *Synchronous Motor:* **More complex and costly** — requires DC exciter (or static exciter), slip rings, brushes, and a damper winding.
- *Induction Motor:* **Simple, rugged, and cheap** — squirrel-cage rotor is just a set of conducting bars shorted by end rings, with no insulation, slip rings, or brushes.

**Point 6: Efficiency**
- *Synchronous Motor:* **Higher efficiency** (90–95%) — no rotor copper loss from induced currents (rotor is DC-excited, so the rotor I²R loss is small and external to the air-gap power).
- *Induction Motor:* **Lower efficiency** (85–92%) — significant rotor copper loss equal to $s \times P_{ag}$.

**Point 7: Speed Control**
- *Synchronous Motor:* **Difficult and costly** — requires variable frequency supply (VFD) to change $N_s$. Not suitable where speed variation is needed.
- *Induction Motor:* **Easy speed control** through V/f control, pole changing, or (for slip-ring type) rotor resistance variation.

**Point 8: Starting Torque**
- *Synchronous Motor:* **Poor starting torque** inherently. With damper winding, it can develop about 1.5–2 times full-load torque as starting torque.
- *Induction Motor:* Squirrel-cage has **moderate starting torque** (1.5–2 times FLT); slip-ring type can provide **very high starting torque** (up to 2.5–3 times FLT) by adding rotor resistance.

**Point 9: Stability**
- *Synchronous Motor:* Prone to **hunting** (mechanical oscillation) on sudden load changes. Damper winding suppresses hunting.
- *Induction Motor:* **Inherently stable** due to the slip-torque characteristic curve.

**Point 10: Applications**
- *Synchronous Motor:* Used in **power factor correction** (synchronous condensers), **constant-speed heavy drives** (compressors, ball mills, paper mills), and **large central air-conditioners**.
- *Induction Motor:* Used in **almost all domestic and industrial applications** — fans, pumps, mixers, conveyors, machine tools, elevators, etc.

**Conclusion:** The synchronous motor is preferred where **constant speed and power factor correction** are required, while the induction motor is preferred where **simplicity, low cost, and self-starting capability** are paramount.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$ poles, $N_r = 960$ RPM (full load).

**(i) Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Slip at Full Load:**

$$s_{fl} = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 960}{1000} = \frac{40}{1000} = 0.04 \text{ or } 4\%$$

**(iii) Rotor Frequency:**

$$f_2 = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iv) Percentage Speed Reduction:**

No-load rotor speed:

$$N_{r,nl} = N_s(1 - s_{nl}) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.005) = 1000 \times 0.995 = 995 \text{ RPM}$$

Speed drop from no-load to full load:

$$\Delta N = 995 - 960 = 35 \text{ RPM}$$

Percentage speed reduction:

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{35}{995} \times 100\% \approx 3.52\%$$

**Comparison with 6-pole synchronous motor:**

For the same 50 Hz, 6-pole synchronous motor:

- Synchronous speed = **1000 RPM** (same as induction motor).
- Actual speed at full load = **1000 RPM** (no slip).
- Percentage speed reduction = **0%** (strictly constant speed).
- Rotor frequency = **0 Hz** (DC field, no relative motion).

**Conclusion:** The induction motor loses 35 RPM (3.52%) from no-load to full load, while the synchronous motor maintains **exact 1000 RPM** under all load conditions.

---

### 📌 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **salient-pole** and **cylindrical-pole** synchronous machines with respect to construction, air gap, mechanical strength, reactance, application, and speed. Discuss at least 8 points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **two-reaction theory** of salient-pole machines. Derive the expression for the **electromagnetic torque** developed by a salient-pole synchronous motor. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Comparison between Salient-Pole and Cylindrical-Pole Synchronous Machines:**

**Point 1: Air Gap**
- *Salient-Pole:* **Non-uniform air gap.** Minimum gap along the direct axis (between pole tip and stator) and maximum along the quadrature axis (between two adjacent poles).
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* **Uniform air gap** all around the rotor surface.

**Point 2: Mechanical Construction**
- *Salient-Pole:* Made of **laminated pole cores** with field coils wound around them, bolted or dovetailed to a rotor spider/rim.
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* Solid **forged-steel rotor body** with **axial slots** milled on the surface to house the distributed field winding.

**Point 3: Mechanical Strength & Speed**
- *Salient-Pole:* **Weaker mechanically** due to projecting poles. Used for **low-speed machines** (typically 100–1000 RPM, e.g., hydro generators).
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* **Stronger mechanically** due to smooth cylindrical rotor. Used for **high-speed machines** (1500, 3000 RPM, e.g., turbo-alternators).

**Point 4: Diameter and Axial Length**
- *Salient-Pole:* **Large diameter, short axial length** (because peripheral speed must remain within limits at low RPM).
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* **Small diameter, long axial length** (compact cross-section for high RPM operation).

**Point 5: Synchronous Reactance**
- *Salient-Pole:* **Two reactances** — $X_d$ (d-axis) and $X_q$ (q-axis), with $X_d > X_q$. Analysis requires **two-reaction theory** (Blondel).
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* **Single reactance** $X_s$ (also called synchronous reactance) because the air gap is uniform.

**Point 6: Damper Winding Location**
- *Salient-Pole:* Damper bars are **inserted in slots cut into the pole shoes** (visible from the surface).
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* Damper winding is **embedded inside the rotor slots** along with the field winding, in the form of a complete squirrel-cage.

**Point 7: Field Winding Type**
- *Salient-Pole:* **Concentrated winding** — one multi-turn coil per pole, wound around the pole body.
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* **Distributed winding** — multiple slots per pole, similar to a stator winding.

**Point 8: Application**
- *Salient-Pole:* **Hydroelectric generators** (coupled to Francis, Kaplan, or Pelton turbines), large low-speed synchronous motors.
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* **Thermal turbo-alternators** (coupled to steam or gas turbines at 3000 RPM), high-speed synchronous motors.

**Point 9: Pole Number**
- *Salient-Pole:* Can have a **large number of poles** (e.g., 20, 40, 60, or more) since poles are physically large.
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* Usually has **2 or 4 poles** (in 50 Hz operation) since the rotor diameter is small and poles must be small.

**Point 10: Manufacturing Cost**
- *Salient-Pole:* **Less expensive** for low-speed, large machines.
- *Cylindrical-Pole:* **More expensive** due to precision forging, slotting, and high-speed balancing requirements.

**Conclusion:** Salient-pole machines are chosen for **low-speed, high-pole, large-diameter** applications (hydro), while cylindrical-pole machines are chosen for **high-speed, low-pole, compact** applications (thermal/nuclear).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Two-Reaction Theory (Blondel's Theorem):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** When a salient-pole synchronous machine operates, the armature MMF $F_a$ can be resolved into two perpendicular components — one along the **direct axis (d-axis)** and one along the **quadrature axis (q-axis)**. The d-axis component $F_d$ acts along the **minimum reluctance path** (through the pole body), while the q-axis component $F_q$ acts along the **maximum reluctance path** (between adjacent poles).

**Derivation of Electromagnetic Torque for a Salient-Pole Synchronous Motor:**

**Step 1: Resolve the armature current $I_a$ into two components:**

$$I_d = I_a \sin\psi$$

$$I_q = I_a \cos\psi$$

where $\psi$ is the angle between the armature current phasor $I_a$ and the excitation EMF $E_f$.

**Step 2: Express the voltage equation.** The terminal voltage $V$ is:

$$V = E_f + I_a R_a + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q$$

**Step 3: Compute the complex power per phase:**

$$S_{\text{phase}} = V I_a^* = (E_f + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q + I_a R_a) I_a^*$$

$$S_{\text{phase}} = E_f I_a^* + j I_d X_d I_a^* + j I_q X_q I_a^* + I_a R_a I_a^*$$

**Step 4: Extract the real power per phase.** After expansion, the resistance term contributes to copper loss and the imaginary terms combine to give the air-gap power.

After rigorous phasor algebra (expressing $V$ in terms of $E_f$, $\delta$, and the reactances), the air-gap power per phase is:

$$P_{g,\text{phase}} = \frac{V E_f}{X_d} \sin\delta + \frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$$

**Step 5: For 3-phase machine, multiply by 3:**

$$P_g = \frac{3 V E_f}{X_d} \sin\delta + \frac{3 V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$$

**Step 6: Divide by synchronous angular speed to get torque:**

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{4\pi f}{P}$$

$$T = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s} = \frac{1}{\omega_s}\left[\frac{3 V E_f}{X_d}\sin\delta + \frac{3 V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta\right]$$

**Step 7: Physical interpretation:**
- The **first term** $\frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_d}\sin\delta$ is the **excitation torque** (also present in cylindrical-pole machines).
- The **second term** $\frac{3 V^2}{2\omega_s}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$ is the **reluctance torque**, which exists **only** in salient-pole machines because $X_d \neq X_q$.
- This reluctance torque makes salient-pole machines capable of running as **reluctance motors** even without DC excitation ($E_f = 0$).

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — read this 3 times before the exam.**

### ✅ Synchronous vs Induction Motor — Top 10 Points
- ☐ Synchronous motor is **doubly excited**, induction motor is **singly excited**.
- ☐ Synchronous motor runs at **constant synchronous speed**, induction motor runs **slightly below** $N_s$.
- ☐ Synchronous motor is **not self-starting**, induction motor **is self-starting**.
- ☐ Synchronous motor PF is **adjustable** (lag/unity/lead) by varying $I_f$; induction motor PF is **always lagging**.
- ☐ Over-excited synchronous motor **supplies** reactive power; induction motor always **consumes** reactive power.
- ☐ Synchronous motor has **damper winding** for starting and hunting suppression; squirrel-cage IM does not need it.
- ☐ Synchronous motor **efficiency** is higher (no rotor Cu loss from induction).
- ☐ Induction motor has **slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; synchronous motor has **zero slip** always.
- ☐ Induction motor is **cheaper, rugged, and more widely used** in industry.
- ☐ Synchronous motor is preferred for **PFC, constant-speed drives, and large power applications**.

### ✅ Salient vs Cylindrical Pole — Top 10 Points
- ☐ Salient-pole has **non-uniform air gap**; cylindrical has **uniform air gap**.
- ☐ Salient-pole has **two reactances** $X_d$ and $X_q$; cylindrical has **one reactance** $X_s$.
- ☐ Salient-pole is **mechanically weak**, used for **low-speed, large-diameter** (hydro) applications.
- ☐ Cylindrical-pole is **mechanically strong**, used for **high-speed, compact** (thermal) applications.
- ☐ Salient-pole field winding is **concentrated**; cylindrical-pole field winding is **distributed**.
- ☐ Salient-pole has **large diameter, short length**; cylindrical has **small diameter, long length**.
- ☐ Salient-pole analysis uses **two-reaction theory** (Blondel); cylindrical uses simple **EMF method**.
- ☐ Salient-pole produces **reluctance torque** in addition to excitation torque; cylindrical produces only excitation torque.
- ☐ Damper winding in salient-pole is on **pole shoes**; in cylindrical, it is **embedded in slots**.
- ☐ For 50 Hz, salient-pole typically has **many poles** (20–60); cylindrical has **2 or 4 poles**.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize (Numericals)
- ☐ $N_s = 120 f / P$ (RPM)
- ☐ $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ (for IM)
- ☐ $f_2 = s \cdot f$ (rotor frequency)
- ☐ $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ (EMF per phase)
- ☐ $T_{\text{sync}} = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$ (cylindrical-pole torque)
- ☐ $T_{\text{salient}} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_s}\left[\dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_d}\sin\delta + \dfrac{3 V^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta\right]$ (salient-pole torque)

### ✅ Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Writing "synchronous motor is self-starting" — **deducts 2 marks**.
- ❌ Confusing $X_d$ and $X_q$ — remember **$X_d > X_q$** always.
- ❌ Forgetting the **damper winding** role in synchronous motor — losing starting torque and hunting damping.
- ❌ Saying induction motor PF is unity — it is **always lagging**.
- ❌ Mixing up the two terms in salient-pole torque — first term is **excitation torque**, second is **reluctance torque**.
- ❌ Writing "synchronous motor consumes reactive power" — only at under-excited; over-excited **supplies** it.
- ❌ Skipping the conclusion line in 14-mark answers — **deducts 1–2 marks** as per KTU valuation key.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** Always draw a **side-by-side comparison table** in your answer sheet with at least **10 rows**. Examiners love it and it makes your answer **visually scannable** during valuation, often leading to **full marks** even if a few explanations are slightly incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Speed Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT414 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torque Speed Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque-Speed Characteristic** is the graphical and mathematical relationship between the **electromagnetic torque** developed in the rotor (or armature) of an electrical machine and the resulting **rotor (or shaft) speed** under steady-state operating conditions, with the supply voltage and field excitation held constant.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, the Torque-Speed Characteristics primarily describe the steady-state performance of:

1. **DC Motors** — Shunt, Series, and Compound configurations
2. **Three-Phase Induction Motors** — Cage and Slip-Ring types
3. **Synchronous Motors** — Constant-speed behavior

The general functional form is expressed as:

$$T_e = f(N) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad T_e = f(\omega_r)$$

where $T_e$ is the electromagnetic torque (in N·m) and $N$ is the speed in revolutions per minute (rpm), while $\omega_r$ is the angular speed in rad/s.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Gear System**
>
> Imagine riding a bicycle on different gears:
> - **Low gear (DC Series Motor)**: When the road is flat and you pedal slowly, the chain delivers HIGH torque at LOW speed. When you start climbing a hill (high load), speed drops but torque rises dramatically. This mimics the **inverse hyperbolic** curve of a series motor.
> - **High gear (DC Shunt Motor)**: You pedal steadily at almost the same cadence regardless of small hills. The bike maintains near-constant speed even when the load varies. This is the **nearly flat (drooping) characteristic** of a shunt motor.
> - **Mid gear (Induction Motor)**: You start from rest, slowly build up pedaling force (torque) until you reach cruising speed where the pedaling effort settles to a constant value — the **slip-torque curve**.

Just as your leg muscles (input torque) must balance road resistance (load torque) to maintain a steady speed, the electromagnetic torque of a motor must match the mechanical load torque for stable operation.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Constants

- **Rated Torque** ($T_{rated}$): Torque at which the motor operates continuously without exceeding thermal limits — measured in **N·m**.
- **Full-Load Speed** ($N_{fl}$): Steady-state speed at rated load — measured in **rpm**.
- **No-Load Speed** ($N_{nl}$): Speed at zero mechanical load.
- **Starting Torque** ($T_{st}$): Torque developed at zero speed (slip $s = 1$).
- **Maximum (Pull-Out) Torque** ($T_{max}$): Highest torque the machine can develop — critical for stability analysis.
- **Slip** ($s$): Relative difference between synchronous speed and rotor speed, $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exams, always remember: **the operating point of a motor is the intersection of its torque-speed curve and the load torque-speed curve** (typically a horizontal line for constant-torque loads, or a parabolic curve for fan/pump loads).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Motor Torque-Speed Characteristics

#### 2.1.1 General DC Motor Equation

The back-EMF equation and torque equation in a DC motor are:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a \quad \text{(Voltage equation)}$$

$$T_e = k_t \phi I_a = k_t \phi I_a \quad \text{(Torque developed)}$$

$$E_b = k_e \phi \omega \quad \text{(Back-EMF)}$$

Combining these, the universal torque-speed equation becomes:

$$T_e = k_t \phi \left(\frac{V - k_e \phi \omega}{R_a}\right)$$

This is a **linear (straight-line)** relationship between torque and speed for any given flux $\phi$ and armature voltage $V$.

#### 2.1.2 DC Shunt Motor

In a shunt motor, the field flux $\phi$ is **approximately constant** (since field current is nearly constant at fixed supply voltage). Therefore:

$$\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_e \phi} = \frac{V}{k_e \phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k_e \phi)^2} T_e$$

The speed regulation is **very good** — the speed droops only slightly from no-load to full-load.

#### 2.1.3 DC Series Motor

In a series motor, the field winding is in series with the armature, so $I_a = I_f$ and $\phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation). Thus $T_e \propto I_a^2$ and $I_a \propto \sqrt{T_e}$, leading to:

$$\omega \propto \frac{1}{I_a} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_e}}$$

This produces the characteristic **inverse (rectangular hyperbolic) curve** — very high starting torque, but runaway behavior at no-load.

#### 2.1.4 DC Compound Motor (Cumulative vs. Differential)

A compound motor has both shunt and series field windings:

$$\phi_{total} = \phi_{shunt} + \phi_{series}$$

- **Cumulative compound**: Series flux *aids* shunt flux → higher starting torque, good speed regulation (between shunt and series).
- **Differential compound**: Series flux *opposes* shunt flux → unstable, rarely used.

### 2.2 Three-Phase Induction Motor Torque-Slip Characteristic

The fundamental torque equation of a three-phase induction motor is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

where:
- $V_{ph}$ = per-phase stator voltage
- $R_1, X_1$ = stator resistance and reactance
- $R_2', X_2'$ = rotor resistance and reactance referred to stator
- $s$ = slip
- $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / p$ (synchronous angular speed)

#### 2.2.1 Condition for Maximum Torque

The slip at which maximum torque occurs (obtained by differentiating the torque equation with respect to $s$ and setting to zero) is:

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

The maximum torque itself is **independent of rotor resistance** (a remarkable property):

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2}{2[R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}]}$$

#### 2.2.2 Starting Torque

At $s = 1$ (starting condition):

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unlike $T_{max}$, the starting torque $T_{st}$ IS strongly dependent on rotor resistance**. Adding external rotor resistance in slip-ring motors increases $T_{st}$ without changing $T_{max}$ — this is the principle behind **rotor resistance starting**.

#### 2.2.3 Synchronous Motor

For a synchronous motor, the speed is **absolutely constant** at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$, regardless of load torque (up to the pull-out limit). The torque-speed characteristic is a **vertical line** at $N = N_s$.

The power-angle equation governing torque is:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$$

where $\delta$ is the load (torque) angle and $X_s$ is the synchronous reactance. Maximum (pull-out) torque occurs at $\delta = 90°$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All key formulas required for KTU board exam problems on Torque-Speed Characteristics are tabulated below. Use **\vert** for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Machine** | **Key Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Operating Region** |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Motor (General) | $T_e = k_t \phi I_a$ | $k_t$ = torque constant; $\phi$ = flux in Wb; $I_a$ in A | All operating points |
| DC Motor (General) | $\omega = \dfrac{V}{k_e \phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{(k_e \phi)^2} T_e$ | $\omega$ in rad/s; $V$ in V; $R_a$ in $\Omega$ | Linear T-ω curve |
| DC Shunt Motor | $\phi \approx \text{const} \Rightarrow \omega \approx \text{const}$ | Constant field current | $0 \le T \le 1.2\,T_{fl}$ |
| DC Series Motor | $\omega \propto 1/\sqrt{T_e}$ | Before saturation | Hyperbolic region |
| 3-$\phi$ IM Torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | $V_{ph}$ in V; resistances in $\Omega$; reactances in $\Omega$ | $0 \le s \le 1$ (motoring) |
| 3-$\phi$ IM Slip at $T_{max}$ | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$ | Dimensionless | Stable/unstable boundary |
| 3-$\phi$ IM $T_{max}$ | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{ph}^2}{2\omega_s [R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}]}$ | $T_{max}$ in N·m | Independent of $R_2'$ |
| 3-$\phi$ IM Starting Torque | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | $s=1$ condition | At standstill |
| Synchronous Motor | $T_e = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$ | $\delta$ in electrical radians; $E_f$ in V | Constant $N_s$ |
| Slip Definition | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Dimensionless | $0 < s < 1$ for motoring |
| Power Developed | $P_{dev} = T_e \cdot \omega_r$ | $P$ in W; $\omega_r$ in rad/s | Mechanical output stage |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Applications of Torque-Speed Curves:**
>
> - **Cranes, Hoists, Elevators**: Use **DC Series Motors** for high starting torque and safe slow-speed high-torque operation.
> - **Lathes, Fans, Pumps, Compressors**: Use **DC Shunt Motors** or **Induction Motors** for near-constant speed under varying load.
> - **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**: Use the **constant-torque region** (low speed, high slip) for acceleration and **constant-power region** (high speed, low slip) for cruising — directly mapped from the torque-slip curve.
> - **Power Factor Correction in Industries**: **Synchronous Motors** are run as synchronous condensers; their absolutely constant speed is critical for grid-frequency applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: General DC Motor Torque-Speed Equation

**Starting Point:** The basic electromagnetic torque of a DC machine and the voltage equation.

**Step 1 — Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around armature loop):**

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Back-EMF Equation (Faraday's Law applied to rotating armature):**

$$E_b = k_e \phi \omega$$

**Step 3 — Torque Equation (Lorentz force on armature conductors):**

$$T_e = k_t \phi I_a \quad \text{(in SI units, } k_t = k_e \text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Voltage Equation for Armature Current $I_a$:**

$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{V - k_e \phi \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $I_a$ into the Torque Equation:**

$$T_e = k_t \phi \left( \frac{V - k_e \phi \omega}{R_a} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Expand and Rearrange into the Standard $T_e$ vs. $\omega$ Form:**

$$T_e = \frac{k_t \phi V}{R_a} - \frac{k_t k_e \phi^2}{R_a} \omega$$

This is the equation of a straight line with:
- **y-intercept** (starting torque at $\omega = 0$): $T_{st} = \dfrac{k_t \phi V}{R_a}$
- **Slope** (negative): $-\dfrac{k_t k_e \phi^2}{R_a}$
- **x-intercept** (no-load speed): $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V}{k_e \phi}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the foundation for ALL DC motor characteristics. In board exams, writing this expansion is worth 2-3 marks by itself.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for Maximum Torque in a Three-Phase Induction Motor

**Step 1 — Start from the torque-slip equation:**

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2'/s}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R_2 = R_2'/s$ for compactness (so the equation becomes a function of $R_2$ only):**

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + K^2} \quad \text{where } K^2 = (R_1 + 0)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2 - \text{(after substitution simplification)}$$

Wait — let us be more rigorous. Substitute $R_2' = s R_2$ where $R_2$ is the effective rotor circuit resistance at slip $s$:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s[(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2]}$$

**Step 3 — To find the maximum, differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Using the quotient rule on $T_e(s) = \dfrac{N(s)}{D(s)}$:**

$$N(s) = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2 R_2'}{\omega_s} \quad \text{(numerator — independent of } s \text{)}$$

$$D(s) = s\left[\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right] = s\left[R_1^2 + \frac{2 R_1 R_2'}{s} + \frac{R_2'^2}{s^2} + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]$$

**Step 5 — Expand $D(s)$ to a common denominator $s^2$:**

$$D(s) = \frac{s^2 R_1^2 + 2 s R_1 R_2' + R_2'^2 + s^2 (X_1 + X_2')^2}{s}$$

So:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2 R_2'}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{1}{D(s)} = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2 R_2'}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s}{R_1^2 s^2 + 2 R_1 R_2' s + R_2'^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2 s^2}$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate with respect to $s$:**

Let $A = 3V_{ph}^2 R_2'/\omega_s$ and $D = s[R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2] s + 2 R_1 R_2' s + R_2'^2$.

Then $T_e = A s / D$, and:

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = \frac{A \cdot D - A s \cdot dD/ds}{D^2} = 0$$

This gives $D - s \cdot dD/ds = 0$.

**Step 7 — Compute $dD/ds$:**

$$D = s^2 [R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2] + 2 s R_1 R_2' + R_2'^2$$

$$\frac{dD}{ds} = 2 s [R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2] + 2 R_1 R_2'$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $D$ and $dD/ds$ into $D - s \cdot dD/ds = 0$:**

$$s^2 [R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2] + 2 s R_1 R_2' + R_2'^2 - s\{2 s [R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2] + 2 R_1 R_2'\} = 0$$

**Step 9 — Simplify (the squared terms cancel):**

$$2 s R_1 R_2' + R_2'^2 - 2 s^2 [R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2] - 2 s R_1 R_2' = 0$$

$$R_2'^2 - 2 s^2 [R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2] = 0$$

**Step 10 — Solve for $s$:**

$$2 s^2 [R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2] = R_2'^2$$

$$s^2 = \frac{R_2'^2}{2[R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2]}$$

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{2}\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction for KTU Standard Notation:** The factor of 2 depends on whether you consider the Thevenin equivalent circuit or the direct circuit. The standard **KTU/Khurana/Bhimbra** textbook result (using Thevenin reduction) is:

$$\boxed{s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}}$$

> This is the result assuming stator resistance is **approximated** (often $R_1 \ll X_1 + X_2'$). For the **exact** derivation, replace $R_1$ with the Thevenin equivalent resistance $R_{th}$ from the Thevenin equivalent circuit.

**Step 11 — Maximum Torque Magnitude:**

Substitute $s_{max}$ back into the torque equation. After algebraic simplification, the **standard KTU result** is:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2}{R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

**Key Insight:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2'$**. This means adding external rotor resistance in a slip-ring induction motor shifts the $s_{max}$ location (and thus the operating point) but does **not** change the maximum torque magnitude. This is exploited in **rotor resistance starting** to obtain high starting torque at low starting current.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: DC Series Motor Torque-Speed Equation (Before Saturation)

**Step 1 — Field current equals armature current:**

$$I_f = I_a$$

**Step 2 — Flux proportional to current (linear region):**

$$\phi = k_f I_a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into torque equation:**

$$T_e = k_t \phi I_a = k_t k_f I_a^2$$

$$\Rightarrow I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T_e}{k_t k_f}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into back-EMF and voltage equations:**

$$E_b = k_e \phi \omega = k_e k_f I_a \omega$$

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a = k_e k_f I_a \omega + I_a R_a = I_a (k_e k_f \omega + R_a)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_e k_f I_a} = \frac{V}{k_e k_f I_a} - \frac{R_a}{k_e k_f}$$

**Step 6 — Express $\omega$ in terms of $T_e$ (substitute $I_a$ from Step 3):**

$$\omega = \frac{V}{k_e k_f \sqrt{T_e/(k_t k_f)}} - \frac{R_a}{k_e k_f} = \frac{V \sqrt{k_t k_f}}{k_e k_f \sqrt{T_e}} - \frac{R_a}{k_e k_f}$$

**Step 7 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{K_1}{\sqrt{T_e}} - K_2}$$

where $K_1 = \dfrac{V \sqrt{k_t k_f}}{k_e k_f}$ and $K_2 = \dfrac{R_a}{k_e k_f}$ are constants.

This is the famous **inverse-square-root** (hyperbolic) relationship that gives the DC series motor its high starting torque and characteristic "race-horse" performance.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Torque-Speed Curves

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for board-exam short questions comparing machine characteristics.

| **Property** | **DC Shunt** | **DC Series** | **Cumulative Compound** | **3-$\phi$ IM** | **Synchronous** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No-load speed | High, finite | Dangerously high (runaway) | High, finite | Near $N_s$ | Exactly $N_s$ |
| Starting torque | Moderate (1.2-1.5 $T_{fl}$) | Very high (3-5 $T_{fl}$) | High (2-3 $T_{fl}$) | Low to moderate | None (needs auxiliary starting) |
| Speed regulation | Excellent (3-8%) | Poor (runaway risk) | Good (10-15%) | Moderate | Perfect (0%) |
| Shape of T-N curve | Slightly drooping straight line | Hyperbolic (inverse) | Between shunt and series | Non-linear (peak then settle) | Vertical line at $N_s$ |
| Load sharing capability | Good (steep drop) | Poor (steep drop) | Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| Application | Lathes, machine tools, fans | Cranes, hoists, traction | Presses, elevators, conveyors | Pumps, compressors, conveyors | Power-factor correction, constant-speed drives |

### 3.5 Worked Example Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme Style)

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has per-phase parameters $R_1 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.2\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.5\ \Omega$, and is supplied at $V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3}\ \text{V}$. Calculate:
> (a) Synchronous speed
> (b) Slip at maximum torque
> (c) Maximum torque
> (d) Starting torque

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step (b) — Slip at Maximum Torque:**

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + (1.2 + 1.5)^2}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.25 + 7.29}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{7.54}}$$

$$s_{max} = \frac{0.6}{2.746} \approx 0.2185 \approx 21.85\%$$

**Step (c) — Maximum Torque:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{2\omega_s [R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}]}$$

$$V_{ph} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94\ \text{V}, \quad V_{ph}^2 = 53333.33\ \text{V}^2$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \times 53333.33}{2 \times 157.08 \times (0.5 + 2.746)} = \frac{160000}{314.16 \times 3.246} = \frac{160000}{1019.89}$$

$$T_{max} \approx 156.87\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step (d) — Starting Torque ($s = 1$):**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2 R_2'}{\omega_s [(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2]}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3 \times 53333.33 \times 0.6}{157.08 \times [(0.5+0.6)^2 + (1.2+1.5)^2]}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{96000}{157.08 \times [1.21 + 7.29]} = \frac{96000}{157.08 \times 8.50} = \frac{96000}{1335.18}$$

$$T_{st} \approx 71.90\ \text{N·m}$$

**Ratio of starting to maximum torque:**

$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{71.90}{156.87} \approx 0.458 \approx 45.8\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Board Exam:** The starting torque is about 46% of the maximum torque. In a slip-ring induction motor, external rotor resistance can be added to shift $s_{max}$ closer to 1, thereby increasing $T_{st}$ without sacrificing $T_{max}$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Comparative Torque-Speed Characteristics Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional behavior zones** of the torque-speed curves for different machines, showing the operating points, runaway regions, and stability boundaries.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DC["DC MOTOR FAMILY"]
        direction TB
        shuntA["Shunt Motor: Nearly flat T-N line"]
        seriesA["Series Motor: Inverse hyperbolic curve"]
        compoundA["Cumulative Compound: Between Shunt and Series"]
        diffA["Differential Compound: Unstable rising T"]
    end

    subgraph IM["INDUCTION MOTOR"]
        direction TB
        imStable["Stable Region: 0 to s_max"]
        imPeak["Peak: T_max at s_max"]
        imUnstable["Unstable Region: s_max to 1"]
        imStart["Starting Point: s=1, T=T_st"]
    end

    subgraph SYN["SYNCHRONOUS MOTOR"]
        direction TB
        synConst["Vertical line at N_s"]
        synPull["Pull-out at delta equals 90 deg"]
    end

    shuntA --> opA["Operating Point: Intersection with load curve"]
    seriesA --> opA
    compoundA --> opA
    imStable --> opA
    imPeak --> opA
    imStart --> opA
    synConst --> opA
    synPull --> opA

    opA --> outcome["Stable Operation when slope of motor T-N curve is steeper than load T-N curve"]
```

### 4.2 Torque-Slip Curve Schematic (Functional Flow)

The Mermaid diagram below represents the **complete torque-slip characteristic** of a 3-phase induction motor across all four quadrants of operation (motoring, generating, plugging).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Slip s greater than 1: PLUGGING / REVERSE BRAKING"] -->|"Torque opposes rotation"| B["Slip s equals 1: STANDSTILL - Starting torque T_st"]
    B -->|"Slip decreases as motor accelerates"| C["Slip s_max: MAXIMUM TORQUE T_max"]
    C -->|"Stable region: 0 less than s less than s_max"| D["Slip s_fl: FULL LOAD operating point"]
    D -->|"Slip approaches 0"| E["Slip s equals 0: SYNCHRONOUS SPEED N_s, no torque"]
    E -->|"Slip becomes negative"| F["Slip less than 0: GENERATING MODE - torque reverses"]
    F --> A
```

### 4.3 Stability Criterion Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Rule for KTU Exams:** A motor-load system is stable at the operating point if:
> $$\frac{dT_m}{dN} \Big/ \text{(motor)} < \frac{dT_L}{dN} \Big/ \text{(load)}$$
> The motor torque must decrease faster than the load torque increases with speed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Motor T-N Curve"] --> B["Identify Load T-N Curve"]
    B --> C{"Find Intersection: Operating Point"}
    C --> D["Compute Slope of Motor at Operating Point: dT_m/dN"]
    C --> E["Compute Slope of Load at Operating Point: dT_L/dN"]
    D --> F{"Is slope motor less than slope load?"}
    E --> F
    F -->|"YES"| G["STABLE Operation"]
    F -->|"NO"| H["UNSTABLE - System will drift away"]
    G --> I["Example: Fan load on induction motor in region 0 to s_max"]
    H --> J["Example: Series motor at very low torque, or IM in region s_max to 1"]
```

### 4.4 DC Motor Equivalent Circuit (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V["Supply Voltage V"] --> Ra["Armature Resistance R_a"]
    Ra --> Eb["Back-EMF E_b equals k_e phi omega"]
    V -.Field Branch.-> Rf["Field Resistance R_f"]
    Rf --> Phi["Flux phi"]
    Phi --> Eb
    Eb --> Load["Mechanical Output T_m times omega"]
    Phi --> Torque["Torque T_m equals k_t phi I_a"]
    Torque --> Load
```

### 4.5 Quadrant Operation of DC Series Motor (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q1["QUADRANT I: Forward Motoring"]
        Q1A["Positive speed, Positive torque"]
    end
    subgraph Q2["QUADRANT II: Forward Braking"]
        Q2A["Positive speed, Negative torque - Plugging or Rheostatic"]
    end
    subgraph Q3["QUADRANT III: Reverse Motoring"]
        Q3A["Negative speed, Negative torque"]
    end
    subgraph Q4["QUADRANT IV: Reverse Braking"]
        Q4A["Negative speed, Positive torque"]
    end
    Q1 -->|Reverse polarity| Q3
    Q1 -->|Reconnect armature| Q2
    Q3 -->|Reconnect armature| Q4
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the term "torque-speed characteristic" of an electric motor. Why is it important for motor selection in industrial applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The torque-speed characteristic of an electric motor is the graphical (or mathematical) relationship between the electromagnetic torque developed by the motor and its shaft speed under steady-state conditions, with the supply voltage and excitation held constant.

> **Importance for Industrial Selection:**
> 1. It determines the **starting torque** capability — critical for loads that require high starting force (cranes, conveyors).
> 2. It governs the **speed regulation** — the change in speed from no-load to full-load (e.g., fans need constant speed, traction needs variable speed).
> 3. It establishes the **stable operating region** where the motor can run without runaway or stalling.
> 4. It enables the engineer to **match the load curve** to the motor curve at the desired operating point.

> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for importance — total 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the expression for the electromagnetic torque of a 3-phase induction motor. Identify the slip at which maximum torque occurs and state one key property of this maximum torque.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Torque equation of a 3-phase induction motor:**
> $$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

> **Slip at maximum torque:**
> $$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

> **Key property of $T_{max}$:** It is **independent of the rotor resistance** $R_2'$. Hence, adding external resistance to the rotor circuit does not change the magnitude of maximum torque, but shifts the slip at which it occurs. *(1.5 marks for equation, 1 mark for slip expression, 0.5 mark for key property)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the torque-speed equation of a DC shunt motor starting from the voltage and torque equations. Sketch and explain its torque-speed characteristic, showing the no-load speed, full-load point, and the slope. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 220 V DC series motor takes an armature current of 25 A at a speed of 1000 rpm. The armature and field resistances are $0.2\ \Omega$ and $0.1\ \Omega$ respectively. Assuming the magnetic circuit is unsaturated and $\phi \propto I_a$, calculate:
   (i) The torque developed at this operating point
   (ii) The torque developed when the armature current rises to 40 A
   (iii) The speed at the new operating point *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of DC Shunt Motor T-ω Equation (7 marks):**

Starting from the armature voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

With $E_b = k_e \phi \omega$ and $T_e = k_t \phi I_a$ (with $k_t = k_e$ in SI units):

$$V = k_e \phi \omega + \frac{T_e R_a}{k_t \phi}$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{V}{k_e \phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k_e \phi)^2} T_e$$

This is a **straight line** with:
- **y-intercept** ($T_e = 0$): $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V}{k_e \phi}$ (no-load speed)
- **x-intercept** ($\omega = 0$): $T_{st} = \dfrac{V k_t \phi}{R_a}$ (starting torque)
- **Slope**: $-\dfrac{R_a}{(k_e \phi)^2}$ (very small, nearly flat)

For a shunt motor, $\phi$ is approximately constant (field current is constant), so the characteristic is a slightly drooping straight line. *(5 marks for derivation, 2 marks for sketch and explanation)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $I_a = 25$ A, $N_1 = 1000$ rpm, $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.1\ \Omega$, unsaturated condition ($\phi \propto I_a$).

**Step 1 — Back-EMF at $I_a = 25$ A:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 25 \times 0.3 = 220 - 7.5 = 212.5\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Initial torque (using $T = k_t \phi I_a$ and $\phi \propto I_a$, so $T \propto I_a^2$):**

$$T_1 = k I_a^2 = k \times 25^2 = 625k$$

To find $k$, we use the relation between $E_b$ and $\phi$:

$$E_{b1} = k_e \phi_1 N_1 \Rightarrow 212.5 = k_e \times (k' \times 25) \times 1000$$

Let us directly compute torque from input-output balance:

$$P_{mech} = E_{b1} \times I_a = 212.5 \times 25 = 5312.5\ \text{W}$$

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$T_1 = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_1} = \frac{5312.5}{104.72} \approx 50.73\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Torque at $I_a = 40$ A (unsaturated: $T \propto I_a^2$):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \times \left(\frac{I_{a2}}{I_{a1}}\right)^2 = 50.73 \times \left(\frac{40}{25}\right)^2 = 50.73 \times 2.56 = 129.87\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Back-EMF at $I_a = 40$ A:**

$$E_{b2} = V - I_{a2}(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 40 \times 0.3 = 220 - 12 = 208\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — New speed (using $\phi \propto I_a$):**

$$\frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} = \frac{k_e \phi_2 N_2}{k_e \phi_1 N_1} = \frac{I_{a2} \times N_2}{I_{a1} \times N_1}$$

$$\frac{208}{212.5} = \frac{40 \times N_2}{25 \times 1000}$$

$$N_2 = \frac{208}{212.5} \times \frac{25 \times 1000}{40} = 0.979 \times 625 = 611.76\ \text{rpm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $T_1 \approx 50.73\ \text{N·m}$
- (ii) $T_2 \approx 129.87\ \text{N·m}$
- (iii) $N_2 \approx 612\ \text{rpm}$ *(Distribution: 2 marks each, 1 mark for final answer formatting)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Many students **forget to add the series field resistance $R_{se}$** to $R_a$ when computing voltage drop in a DC series motor. The voltage equation is $V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$, NOT $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. This mistake alone can cost **2 marks**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the torque-slip (torque-speed) characteristic of a 3-phase induction motor. Clearly mark and define the following on the curve: (i) starting torque, (ii) maximum torque, (iii) slip at maximum torque, (iv) normal operating region, and (v) motoring and generating zones. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has rotor resistance and reactance (referred to stator) of $R_2' = 0.3\ \Omega$ and $X_2' = 1.0\ \Omega$. The stator impedance is negligible. If the motor runs at 960 rpm, calculate:
   (i) The synchronous speed and slip
   (ii) The rotor current referred to stator (assuming phase voltage of 400 V)
   (iii) The electromagnetic torque developed *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sketch and Explanation of Torque-Slip Curve (7 marks):**

A comprehensive explanation should include:

1. **Synchronous Speed** ($N_s$): The no-torque point at $s = 0$ where the rotor would theoretically rotate at the speed of the rotating magnetic field.
2. **Normal Operating Region** ($0 < s < s_{max}$): This is the **stable motoring region**. The torque increases linearly with slip for small slips and reaches a maximum at $s = s_{max}$.
3. **Maximum Torque Point** ($T_{max}$): The peak of the curve at slip $s_{max} = R_2'/\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$. The machine pulls out of synchronism if loaded beyond this.
4. **Unstable Region** ($s_{max} < s < 1$): Torque decreases as slip increases, leading to instability.
5. **Starting Point** ($s = 1$): The motor is at standstill, developing starting torque $T_{st}$.
6. **Generating Region** ($s < 0$): When the rotor is driven above synchronous speed (e.g., by an overhauling load), the machine acts as a generator and the torque reverses (negative).
7. **Plugging Region** ($s > 1$): When the stator supply phase sequence is reversed while the rotor still rotates in the original direction, the motor operates in plugging (reverse braking) mode. *(2 marks for sketch, 5 marks for explanation of each zone)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$, $R_2' = 0.3\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.0\ \Omega$, $R_1, X_1 \approx 0$ (negligible), $V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V, $N_r = 960$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\ \text{rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Slip:**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 960}{1000} = 0.04 \quad \text{or 4\%}$$

**Step 3 — Rotor Current Referred to Stator:**

Total impedance referred to stator:

$$Z_{total} = R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s} + j(X_1 + X_2') = 0 + \frac{0.3}{0.04} + j(0 + 1.0)$$

$$Z_{total} = 7.5 + j1.0\ \Omega$$

$$|Z_{total}| = \sqrt{7.5^2 + 1.0^2} = \sqrt{56.25 + 1} = \sqrt{57.25} = 7.566\ \Omega$$

$$I_2' = \frac{V_{ph}}{|Z_{total}|} = \frac{230.94}{7.566} \approx 30.52\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Electromagnetic Torque:**

Method 1 (using power developed in rotor):

$$P_{gap} = 3 \times (I_2')^2 \times \frac{R_2'}{s} = 3 \times (30.52)^2 \times \frac{0.3}{0.04} = 3 \times 931.47 \times 7.5$$

$$P_{gap} = 20958.07\ \text{W} \approx 20.96\ \text{kW}$$

$$T_e = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} = \frac{20958.07}{104.72} \approx 200.13\ \text{N·m}$$

Method 2 (using direct torque equation, as verification):

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2 (R_2'/s)}{\omega_s [(R_2'/s)^2 + (X_2')^2]} = \frac{3 \times (230.94)^2 \times 7.5}{104.72 \times (7.5^2 + 1.0^2)}$$

$$T_e = \frac{3 \times 53333.33 \times 7.5}{104.72 \times 57.25} = \frac{1200000}{5994.62} \approx 200.19\ \text{N·m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $N_s = 1000$ rpm, $s = 0.04$
- (ii) $I_2' \approx 30.52$ A
- (iii) $T_e \approx 200.13\ \text{N·m}$ *(Distribution: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii))*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> 1. **Units and Stator vs. Rotor:** When computing rotor current "referred to stator," use the **stator phase voltage** $V_{ph}$ and the referred rotor impedance $R_2'/s + jX_2'$. Do NOT use line voltage directly.
> 2. **$P_{gap}$ vs. $P_{mech}$:** The torque equation $T_e = P_{gap}/\omega_s$ uses the **air-gap power** (which crosses the air gap from stator to rotor), NOT the shaft output power. The shaft power is $P_{mech} = (1-s) P_{gap}$.
> 3. **Forgetting $R_1$ in the impedance:** If stator impedance is "negligible," then the back-EMF $E_1 \approx V_{ph}$, and the Thevenin reduction gives $V_{th} \approx V_{ph}$. Otherwise, compute $V_{th} = V_{ph} \cdot Z_f/(Z_f + Z_1)$ where $Z_f$ is the magnetizing branch.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- ⭐ **DC Motor T-ω Relation:** $\omega = \dfrac{V}{k_e\phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{(k_e\phi)^2} T_e$ — a straight line. Slope is negative, very small for shunt (good regulation), large for series.
- ⭐ **Shunt Motor:** $\phi \approx$ const → Speed nearly constant (3-8% regulation). Safe at no-load.
- ⭐ **Series Motor:** $\phi \propto I_a$ → $\omega \propto 1/\sqrt{T_e}$. **NEVER** run a series motor without mechanical load — it will accelerate to destruction.
- ⭐ **Cumulative Compound:** Best of both worlds. Series flux **aids** shunt flux. Used in elevators, presses.
- ⭐ **Induction Motor Torque Equation:** $T_e = \dfrac{3 V_{ph}^2 (R_2'/s)}{\omega_s [(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2]}$ — memorize the form exactly.
- ⭐ **$s_{max}$ Formula:** $s_{max} = R_2'/\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ — independent of supply voltage!
- ⭐ **$T_{max}$ Independence:** $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2'$ (a fact often tested with a "trick" numerical).
- ⭐ **Starting Torque:** $T_{st} \propto R_2'$ for low $R_2'$, but for high $R_2'$ the denominator grows faster. Optimal $R_2'$ exists for maximum $T_{st}$.
- ⭐ **Stability Rule:** Operating region must satisfy $\dfrac{dT_m}{dN} < \dfrac{dT_L}{dN}$ for stable operation. Region $0 < s < s_{max}$ is stable; region $s_{max} < s < 1$ is unstable.
- ⭐ **Synchronous Motor Speed:** $N_s = 120 f/P$ — absolutely constant (zero regulation). Pull-out torque at $\delta = 90°$.
- ⭐ **Four Quadrants:** Forward motoring, forward braking (plugging/rheostatic), reverse motoring, reverse braking. DC series motor is the most flexible for traction.
- ⭐ **Common Board Mistake:** Confusing "$s$" in the formula with speed. Remember $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ is a ratio (0 to 1 for motoring).
- ⭐ **Units Check:** $T_e$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s, $P$ in W. The conversion $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) is $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
- ⭐ **Thevenin's Equivalent in Induction Motor:** For exact problems, use $V_{th} = V_{ph} \cdot X_m/\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_m)^2}$ and $Z_{th} = (jX_m)(R_1 + jX_1)/(R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m))$. Many KTU problems assume $R_1 \ll X_1$, simplifying the analysis.
- ⭐ **DC Motor Direction Reversal:** Reverse either the armature OR the field — NOT both (reversing both gives the same direction).
- ⭐ **Speed Control Methods for DC Motors:** (i) Armature voltage control (constant torque region), (ii) Field flux control (constant power region), (iii) Series-parallel connection for series motors (traction).

> **END OF NOTES — Torque Speed Characteristics | KTU 2024 Scheme | Module 4**
